topics.title: Power Diodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Diodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power Diode** is a two-terminal, unidirectional semiconductor switching device designed specifically to handle **high current (tens of amps to several kilo-amperes)** and **high reverse voltage (hundreds to thousands of volts)** with very low forward voltage drop and negligible switching losses. It forms the foundational building block of all power electronic converters such as rectifiers, choppers, inverters, and cyclo-converters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Highlight:**
> A power diode is a *p-n junction diode* with a heavily doped *n⁻ drift region* sandwiched between the *p⁺ anode* and the *n⁺ cathode* (forming a **p⁺-n⁻-n⁺** structure). The lightly doped *n⁻* layer is the key feature that gives the power diode its high reverse voltage blocking capability.

The standard schematic symbol and physical structure of a power diode are illustrated below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["P PLUS ANODE LAYER"] --> B["N MINUS DRIFT REGION"]
    B --> C["N PLUS CATHODE LAYER"]
    D["ANODE TERMINAL A"] --- A
    C --- E["CATHODE TERMINAL K"]
```

| Device Property | Signal Diode (1N4148) | Power Diode (1N4007) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Forward Current | ~200 mA | **1 A to 5000 A** |
| Reverse Voltage | ~75 V | **50 V to 9000 V** |
| Switching Speed | Fast (ns) | Moderate (µs) |
| Forward Drop | ~0.7 V | **0.7 V to 2.5 V** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **mechanical swing door (the p-n junction)** that only swings in one direction.
- A **small signal diode** is like a lightweight plastic swing door — it reacts *very quickly* to a push but cannot withstand a strong gust of wind or a person leaning on it.
- A **power diode** is like a heavy, reinforced **steel floodgate** used in a dam — it still swings only one way (allows current in one direction), but it can handle *massive water pressure* (high voltage) and let through a *huge volume of water* (high current) without breaking.

The thick middle "drift" region of the power diode is like the reinforced concrete wall of the floodgate — it provides structural strength (voltage blocking) but also creates a small "step" that water has to climb (forward voltage drop).

### 1.3 Why Power Diodes Are Different

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Critical Design Differences from Signal Diodes:**
> 1. **Larger die area** → to handle high currents with low on-state loss.
> 2. **Lightly doped n⁻ drift region** → to support wide depletion region and high breakdown voltage.
> 3. **Metallic heat sink / stud mount package** → to dissipate the large heat generated during conduction.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Bolded)

- **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Silicon at 300 K:** $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$
- **Bandgap energy of Si:** $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$
- **Permittivity of Si:** $\varepsilon_s = 11.8 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} \text{ F/cm}$
- **Built-in potential of Si p-n junction:** $V_{bi} \approx 0.7 \text{ V}$
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = 25.85 \text{ mV}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a Power Diode

A modern power diode has a **vertically oriented four-layer structure**:

1. **p⁺ anode layer** — heavily doped ($N_A \approx 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$)
2. **n⁻ drift region** — lightly doped ($N_D \approx 10^{13}$ to $10^{14} \text{ cm}^{-3}$), thickness $W \approx 50$–$500 \text{ µm}$
3. **n⁺ cathode layer** — heavily doped to provide ohmic contact
4. **Metal contact layers** — aluminium or molybdenum for current spreading

The vertical structure ensures **uniform current distribution** across the large cross-sectional area, which is essential for high current handling.

### 2.2 Static (V-I) Characteristics

The static V-I curve of a power diode consists of three regions:

- **Forward conduction region:** The diode begins to conduct significantly when the forward voltage exceeds the **cut-in voltage (knee voltage) $V_k \approx 0.7$ V for Si**. The current then increases exponentially.
- **Reverse blocking region:** A very small reverse saturation current $I_s$ (of the order of µA or mA) flows.
- **Reverse breakdown region:** If reverse voltage exceeds the **breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$**, the diode enters avalanche/Zener breakdown and conducts heavily in reverse — this must be avoided in operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Power diodes are *always* operated strictly in regions 1 and 2. Operating in region 3 is destructive unless the diode is specifically designed as an *avalanche diode* (TVS).

### 2.3 Switching (Dynamic) Characteristics

When a power diode transitions from ON to OFF (or vice-versa), it does *not* switch instantaneously. The transition has three distinct phases:

#### A. Turn-ON Transient
- **Delay time ($t_d$):** time for the diode voltage to fall from its initial value to near 0.
- **Rise time ($t_r$):** time for the forward current to rise to 90% of its final value.
- Total **turn-on time $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$** is typically **0.1 µs to 2 µs** for power diodes.

#### B. Reverse Recovery Transient (Most Critical!)
This is the most important dynamic behaviour and the **most-tested concept in KTU exams**.

When a forward-conducting diode is suddenly reverse-biased, it does *not* stop conducting instantly. The stored minority carriers in the drift region must be removed first.

The reverse recovery process has two stages:

1. **Storage time ($t_s$ or $t_{rr1}$):** Reverse current rises to a peak $I_{RR}$ and the stored charge is removed.
2. **Transition time ($t_t$ or $t_{rr2}$):** The reverse current falls from $I_{RR}$ back to the small leakage value $I_s$.

The total **reverse recovery time:**
$$t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse Recovery Charge ($Q_{rr}$):**
> The area under the reverse current vs. time curve is the recovered charge. It is given by:
> $$Q_{rr} = \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} i_r(t) \, dt \approx \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}$$
> A *fast-recovery* diode has $Q_{rr} \ll 1 \text{ µC}$, while a *normal* power diode has $Q_{rr}$ of several µC.

#### C. Forward Recovery Transient
When the diode is suddenly forward-biased, the voltage momentarily overshoots its steady-state value before settling. This is the **forward recovery voltage $V_{FR}$**, important in **high-frequency snubber design**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Diode current (Schockley eqn.) | $I = I_s \left(e^{V_D / \eta V_T} - 1\right)$ | A | $\eta \approx 1$ to 2 |
| 2 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = \frac{kT}{q}$ | V | 25.85 mV at 300 K |
| 3 | Breakdown voltage (avalanche) | $V_{BR} = \frac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit}^2}{2qN_D}$ | V | Depends on drift doping $N_D$ |
| 4 | Depletion width | $W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V}{qN_D}}$ | cm | In n⁻ drift region |
| 5 | Reverse recovery charge | $Q_{rr} \approx \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} t_{rr}$ | C | Area under $i_r$ vs $t$ |
| 6 | Softness factor | $S = \frac{t_{rr2}}{t_{rr1}} = \frac{t_t}{t_s}$ | — | $S = 1$: abrupt, $S > 1$: soft |
| 7 | Power dissipation | $P = V_F I_F + P_{sw}$ | W | Conduction + switching loss |
| 8 | Switching loss | $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_R I_{RR} t_{rr} f_s$ | W | Proportional to freq. |
| 9 | Thermal resistance | $R_{th} = \frac{T_J - T_A}{P}$ | °C/W | Junction to ambient |
| 10 | Junction temperature | $T_J = T_A + P \cdot R_{th(JA)}$ | °C | Should be $< 150$ °C for Si |

> [!NOTE]
> **Softness Factor (S):** KTU frequently tests this. A **soft recovery** diode ($S > 1$) is preferred in high-frequency applications to reduce EMI and voltage transients, whereas an **abrupt recovery** diode ($S = 1$) has higher switching losses and ringing.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rectifiers in SMPS & UPS:** Power diodes convert 50 Hz AC mains to DC; fast-recovery types used in high-frequency (>20 kHz) stages.
- **Freewheeling in choppers & inverters:** Diode carries inductive load current when the main switch turns off.
- **Snubber circuits:** Diodes clamp voltage spikes across switches.
- **Solar PV bypass & blocking:** Schottky and ultrafast diodes prevent reverse leakage at night and bypass shaded cells.
- **Battery reverse-polarity protection** in automotive electronics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Reverse Breakdown Voltage of a p⁺-n⁻ Diode

**Statement:** Show that the breakdown voltage of a one-sided abrupt p⁺-n⁻ junction is given by $V_{BR} = \frac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit}^2}{2 q N_D}$.

**Background:** In a power diode, the depletion region extends almost entirely into the lightly doped n⁻ side. The electric field in the depletion region is triangular, with maximum at the metallurgical junction.

**Step 1: Poisson's equation in the depletion region**

In the n⁻ drift region, charge density $\rho = qN_D$ (ionized donors), so:

$$\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_s} = \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 2: Integrate to find the electric field**

Integrating from the edge of the depletion region (where $E = 0$) at $x = W$ towards the junction at $x = 0$:

$$E(x) = \frac{qN_D}{\varepsilon_s}(W - x)$$

The maximum electric field occurs at $x = 0$ (the p⁺-n⁻ interface):

$$E_{max} = \frac{qN_D W}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 3: Relate depletion width to applied reverse voltage**

The voltage across the depletion region is the area under the E-x curve (a triangle):

$$V_R = \int_0^W E(x) \, dx = \frac{1}{2} E_{max} W = \frac{qN_D W^2}{2\varepsilon_s}$$

Solving for $W$:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s V_R}{qN_D}}$$

**Step 4: Apply the critical field condition**

Breakdown occurs when $E_{max}$ reaches the **critical electric field $E_{crit}$** of silicon ($\approx 3 \times 10^5$ V/cm). Therefore at breakdown:

$$E_{crit} = \frac{qN_D W_{BR}}{\varepsilon_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{BR} = \frac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit}}{qN_D}$$

**Step 5: Substitute to obtain $V_{BR}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{BR} &= \frac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s} \cdot W_{BR}^2 \\
&= \frac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s} \cdot \left(\frac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit}}{qN_D}\right)^2 \\
&= \frac{qN_D}{2\varepsilon_s} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_s^2 E_{crit}^2}{q^2 N_D^2} \\
&= \frac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit}^2}{2 q N_D}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{V_{BR} = \frac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit}^2}{2 q N_D}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** A *higher breakdown voltage* requires a *lower doping concentration* $N_D$, which in turn makes the drift region thicker. This is the fundamental design trade-off in power device physics — high voltage and low on-state loss are mutually conflicting.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Reverse Recovery Charge and Softness Factor

**Statement:** For a diode with linear reverse current decay, derive $Q_{rr}$ and the softness factor.

**Step 1: Define the reverse current waveform**

Assume during storage time $t_s$ the reverse current rises linearly to peak $I_{RR}$, and during transition time $t_t$ it falls linearly back to zero (idealized model).

$$i_r(t) = \begin{cases} I_{RR} \cdot \dfrac{t}{t_s}, & 0 \le t \le t_s \\[4pt] I_{RR} \cdot \dfrac{t_s + t_t - t}{t_t}, & t_s \le t \le t_s + t_t \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Compute reverse recovery charge**

The charge recovered is the integral of current over the recovery period:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{rr} &= \int_0^{t_s} \frac{I_{RR} \cdot t}{t_s} \, dt + \int_{t_s}^{t_s + t_t} I_{RR} \cdot \frac{t_s + t_t - t}{t_t} \, dt \\
&= \frac{I_{RR}}{t_s} \cdot \frac{t_s^2}{2} + \frac{I_{RR}}{t_t} \cdot \int_0^{t_t} \tau \, d\tau \\
&= \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} t_s + \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} t_t \\
&= \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} (t_s + t_t) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Softness factor definition**

By KTU convention:

$$S = \frac{t_t}{t_s} = \frac{t_{rr2}}{t_{rr1}}$$

**Step 4: Decoding the physical meaning**

- $S = 1 \Rightarrow$ abrupt recovery (square $i_r$ pulse) → high voltage spike, high EMI.
- $S > 1 \Rightarrow$ soft recovery (gradual current tail) → preferred in modern converters.
- $S < 1 \Rightarrow$ snappy recovery → worst case, very high $di/dt$ stress on the diode and switch.

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}, \quad S = \frac{t_t}{t_s}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Switching Power Loss in a Power Diode

**Statement:** Derive the average switching loss per cycle due to reverse recovery.

**Step 1: Energy lost during reverse recovery**

The energy dissipated in the diode during the reverse recovery interval is approximately:

$$E_{sw} = \int_0^{t_{rr}} v(t) \cdot i_r(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2: Assume constant reverse voltage $V_R$ during the recovery**

For a clamped inductive load, the voltage across the diode is held at $V_R$ (the DC bus voltage) during reverse recovery:

$$E_{sw} \approx V_R \int_0^{t_{rr}} i_r(t) \, dt = V_R \cdot Q_{rr}$$

Substituting $Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} t_{rr}$:

$$E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_R I_{RR} t_{rr}$$

**Step 3: Average power loss**

If the switching occurs at frequency $f_s$:

$$P_{sw} = E_{sw} \cdot f_s = \frac{1}{2} V_R I_{RR} t_{rr} f_s$$

**Step 4: Total diode power dissipation**

The diode has two loss components — conduction and switching:

$$\boxed{P_{total} = V_F I_F + \frac{1}{2} V_R I_{RR} t_{rr} f_s}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This equation is the *bible* of power electronics thermal design. As $f_s$ increases (modern converters go to 100s of kHz), the switching loss dominates, and fast-recovery or SiC Schottky diodes become essential.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Diode Junction Temperature (Thermal Model)

**Step 1: Equivalent thermal circuit**

The diode is modelled as a current source $P$ (power dissipation) feeding an RC ladder representing thermal resistance and capacitance. For DC (steady-state):

$$T_J = T_A + P \cdot R_{\theta JA}$$

**Step 2: Adding heatsink**

When a heatsink of thermal resistance $R_{\theta SA}$ is added (with interface resistance $R_{\theta CS}$):

$$R_{\theta JA} = R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA}$$

**Step 3: Substitution**

$$T_J = T_A + P(R_{\theta JC} + R_{\theta CS} + R_{\theta SA})$$

**Step 4: Required heatsink resistance**

Solving for the maximum allowable heatsink resistance:

$$R_{\theta SA}^{max} = \frac{T_{J,max} - T_A}{P} - R_{\theta JC} - R_{\theta CS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU Numerical:** Given $P = 50$ W, $T_{J,max} = 150$ °C, $T_A = 40$ °C, $R_{\theta JC} = 0.5$ °C/W, $R_{\theta CS} = 0.2$ °C/W. Compute $R_{\theta SA}^{max}$.

Solution:
$$R_{\theta SA}^{max} = \frac{150 - 40}{50} - 0.5 - 0.2 = 2.2 - 0.7 = 1.5 \text{ °C/W}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Power Diode

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    anode["ANODE TERMINAL A"]
    player["P PLUS ANODE LAYER"]
    drift["N MINUS DRIFT REGION HIGH VOLTAGE BLOCKING"]
    nlayer["N PLUS CATHODE LAYER"]
    cathode["CATHODE TERMINAL K"]
    junction["METALLURGICAL P PLUS N MINUS JUNCTION"]
    depletion["DEPLETION REGION EXPANDS IN N MINUS"]
    aval["AVALANCHE BREAKDOWN REGION"]
    forward["FORWARD CONDUCTION REGION V GREATER THAN 0.7 V"]
    reverse["REVERSE BLOCKING REGION V LESS THAN VBR"]

    anode --> player
    player --> junction
    junction --> drift
    drift --> nlayer
    nlayer --> cathode
    junction -.-> depletion
    depletion -.-> aval
    junction -.-> forward
    drift -.-> reverse
```

### 4.2 Power Diode — Static V-I Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORWARD["FORWARD BIAS REGION V GREATER THAN 0"]
        f1["I RISES EXPONENTIALLY"]
        f2["KNEE AT V K = 0.7 V FOR SI"]
        f1 --> f2
    end
    subgraph REVERSE["REVERSE BIAS REGION V LESS THAN 0"]
        r1["SMALL LEAKAGE I S IN MICRO AMPERE"]
        r2["CONSTANT UNTIL V BR"]
        r1 --> r2
    end
    subgraph BREAKDOWN["BREAKDOWN REGION V LESS THAN NEGATIVE V BR"]
        b1["AVALANCHE MULTIPLICATION"]
        b2["DESTRUCTIVE IF NOT CLAMPED"]
        b1 --> b2
    end
```

### 4.3 Reverse Recovery Waveform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["STATE 1 FORWARD CONDUCTION IF POSITIVE VF"]
    s2["STATE 2 REVERSE APPLIED AT T = 0"]
    s3["STATE 3 REVERSE CURRENT RISES TO PEAK IRR"]
    s4["STATE 4 STORAGE TIME T S"]
    s5["STATE 5 TRANSITION TIME T T"]
    s6["STATE 6 REVERSE CURRENT FALLS TO LEAKAGE I S"]
    s7["STATE 7 DIODE FULLY OFF BLOCKS VR"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7
```

### 4.4 Functional Block — Diode in a Rectifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ac["AC MAINS 50 HZ"] --> tx["STEP DOWN TRANSFORMER"]
    tx --> bridge["FULL WAVE DIODE BRIDGE 4 POWER DIODES"]
    bridge --> cap["SMOOTHING CAPACITOR"]
    cap --> load["DC LOAD RESISTOR"]
    bridge -.-> heat["HEATSINK WITH THERMAL GREASE"]
    cap -.-> heat
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Exams:**
> When asked to "draw the switching characteristics", students should always label **five points** on the time axis:
> $t_d$, $t_r$, $t_s$, $t_t$, and $t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$. Examiners specifically look for these labels.

### 4.5 Comparison of Power Diode Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pd["POWER DIODES FAMILY"]
    gp["GENERAL PURPOSE DIODES T RR 5 TO 25 USEC USED IN 50 HZ RECTIFIERS"]
    fr["FAST RECOVERY DIODES T RR LESS THAN 1 USEC USED IN 1 TO 10 KHZ"]
    ufr["ULTRAFAST DIODES T RR 25 TO 100 NSEC USED IN 10 TO 100 KHZ"]
    sch["SCHOTTKY DIODES METAL SEMICONDUCTOR VF 0.3 TO 0.5 V T RR NEAR ZERO USED IN LOW V HIGH F"]
    sic["SIC SCHOTTKY DIODES BREAKDOWN ABOVE 1200 V HIGH TEMP OPERATION"]

    pd --> gp
    pd --> fr
    pd --> ufr
    pd --> sch
    pd --> sic
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is meant by reverse recovery time of a power diode? Why is it significant in power electronic circuits?*

**Model Answer:**
Reverse recovery time $t_{rr}$ is the time interval between the instant the forward current of a diode passes through zero (when the diode is switched from forward to reverse bias) and the instant when the reverse current has decayed to 25% of its peak reverse value $I_{RR}$. It consists of two parts: storage time $t_s$ and transition time $t_t$, so $t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$.

It is significant because:
1. It causes **switching losses** proportional to $f_s$, limiting the maximum operating frequency.
2. It produces **reverse current stress** on the switching device in series, and can cause **voltage spikes and EMI**.
3. A large $t_{rr}$ may cause **shoot-through** in bridge circuits, leading to catastrophic failure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $t_{rr} = t_s$ only, *forgetting* the transition component $t_t$. This loses 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare general-purpose, fast-recovery, and Schottky power diodes in terms of reverse recovery time and forward voltage drop.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | General-Purpose | Fast-Recovery | Schottky |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $t_{rr}$ | 5 to 25 µs | 0.1 to 1 µs | $\approx 0$ (no minority carriers) |
| $V_F$ | 0.8 to 1.2 V | 0.8 to 1.5 V | **0.3 to 0.5 V** |
| Reverse voltage | up to 5000 V | up to 1500 V | up to 200 V (Si) / 1200 V (SiC) |
| Application | 50 Hz rectifiers | SMPS, choppers | Low-voltage, high-frequency DC-DC |

Schottky diodes use a metal–semiconductor junction and have *no* minority carrier storage, giving virtually zero $t_{rr}$ but a low breakdown voltage.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q3 (a) [7 Marks]** Explain with suitable diagrams the *static* and *dynamic (switching) characteristics* of a power diode. Label all important regions and time intervals.

**Model Solution:**

**Static Characteristics:**
The static V-I curve of a power diode has three regions:
1. **Forward bias region ($V > 0$):** Below the cut-in voltage $V_k \approx 0.7$ V (Si), current is negligibly small. Above $V_k$, current rises rapidly and follows the exponential law:
$$I = I_s \left(e^{V/\eta V_T} - 1\right)$$
2. **Reverse blocking region ($V < 0$, $V > -V_{BR}$):** A small saturation current $I_s$ (µA to mA) flows in the reverse direction, almost independent of reverse voltage.
3. **Reverse breakdown region ($V \le -V_{BR}$):** Avalanche multiplication causes the current to shoot up. Operation here is destructive unless the device is rated for it.

**Dynamic Characteristics:**
The switching characteristic is shown on a $v(t)$ and $i(t)$ plot vs. time. There are three transients:

- **Turn-on:** delay $t_d$ + rise $t_r$ (both small, $\approx$ 1 µs).
- **Reverse recovery (turn-off):** The most important, comprising storage $t_s$ and transition $t_t$. Total $t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$.
- **Forward recovery:** Brief voltage overshoot $V_{FR}$ when the diode is suddenly forward-biased.

```
    Forward bias
    ─────────────────
    │  V_F (on-state)
    │           ╱─────────────
    │         ╱
    │  0.7V ╱
    ─────╱──────────────────── t
    │  t_d   t_r
    │
    Reverse recovery after turn-off
    i(t): ╲
          ╲___ peak I_RR
                ╲____ (tail) ____ i_s
    │     │   t_s  │ t_t    │
    ──────┴────────┴────────┴── t
    0                t_rr
```

The areas and intervals labelled $t_d$, $t_r$, $t_s$, $t_t$, and $t_{rr}$ carry full marks.

---

**Q3 (b) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat waveforms, define **reverse recovery current $I_{RR}$**, **reverse recovery time $t_{rr}$**, **reverse recovery charge $Q_{rr}$**, and **softness factor $S$** of a power diode. How does the softness factor affect converter design?

**Model Solution:**

- **$I_{RR}$:** Peak value of the reverse current that flows through the diode immediately after reverse voltage is applied. It depends on $\frac{di}{dt}$ of the circuit and the stored charge.

- **$t_{rr}$:** Time measured from the instant the forward current crosses zero to the instant the reverse current decays to 25% of $I_{RR}$ (IEEE standard) or 10% (IEC standard).

- **$Q_{rr}$:** Total charge removed from the drift region during recovery; it is the area under the $i_r$ vs. $t$ curve:
$$Q_{rr} = \int_0^{t_{rr}} i_r(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} t_{rr}$$

- **Softness factor $S = \frac{t_t}{t_s}$:** ratio of transition time to storage time.

**Effect on converter design:**

| Softness factor | Recovery type | Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S = 1$ | Abrupt/snappy | High $di/dt$, voltage spikes, EMI, requires snubber |
| $S > 1$ | Soft | Smooth turn-off, low EMI, preferred in modern converters |
| $S < 1$ | Ultra-snappy | Worst case — very high $di/dt$ stress |

Designers prefer $S \ge 1$ for reliable, low-EMI operation. A *soft-recovery* diode reduces snubber requirements and allows higher switching frequency.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q4 (a) [7 Marks]** A power diode has the following ratings: $V_R = 600$ V, $I_F = 50$ A, $t_{rr} = 3$ µs, $I_{RR} = 8$ A. If it operates in a converter at a switching frequency of 5 kHz, calculate:
(i) the reverse recovery charge,
(ii) the switching power loss, and
(iii) the total power dissipation if the forward voltage drop is 1.2 V and average forward current is 30 A.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_R = 600$ V, $I_F = 50$ A (rated)
- $t_{rr} = 3 \text{ µs} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- $I_{RR} = 8$ A
- $f_s = 5 \text{ kHz} = 5 \times 10^3$ Hz
- $V_F = 1.2$ V, $I_{F,avg} = 30$ A

**(i) Reverse recovery charge:**
$$Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 3 \times 10^{-6} = 12 \times 10^{-6} = 12 \text{ µC}$$

**(ii) Switching power loss:**
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_R I_{RR} t_{rr} f_s$$
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \times 600 \times 8 \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^3$$
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \times 600 \times 8 \times 15 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \times 600 \times 0.12 = 36 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Conduction loss:**
$$P_{cond} = V_F \cdot I_{F,avg} = 1.2 \times 30 = 36 \text{ W}$$

**Total dissipation:**
$$\boxed{P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 36 + 36 = 72 \text{ W}}$$

This is the heat that must be removed by the heatsink.

---

**Q4 (b) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction of a power diode with a neat sketch. Why is the n⁻ drift region lightly doped, and how does its thickness affect the trade-off between breakdown voltage and on-state loss?

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (Vertical Structure):**
A power diode consists of four layers fabricated on a single silicon wafer:
1. **p⁺ anode layer** at the top — heavily doped ($N_A \approx 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$).
2. **n⁻ drift region** in the middle — lightly doped ($N_D \approx 10^{13}$ to $10^{14} \text{ cm}^{-3}$), thickness 50–500 µm.
3. **n⁺ cathode layer** at the bottom — heavily doped to provide ohmic contact.
4. **Metal contacts** on both sides — aluminium or molybdenum for current spreading and soldering to the heatsink.

**Why n⁻ is lightly doped:**
The depletion region in a p⁺-n⁻ junction extends almost entirely into the n⁻ side. To withstand a high reverse voltage, the electric field must be distributed over a *wide* depletion region. A low doping $N_D$ allows the depletion width $W = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s V_R / (qN_D)}$ to grow large, hence higher $V_{BR}$.

**Trade-off:**
- *Thicker* n⁻ region → higher breakdown voltage, but also **higher on-state voltage drop** (because the drift region acts as an ohmic resistance $R_{drift} = \rho \cdot W / A$).
- *Higher* doping $N_D$ → lower on-state drop, but lower breakdown voltage.

This is the classic **silicon limit** — high-voltage power diodes (e.g., 5 kV) suffer conduction losses of several hundred watts, while low-voltage diodes (50 V) have very low $V_F$.

Material innovations like **SiC (Silicon Carbide)** and **GaN (Gallium Nitride)** break this limit by having a much higher critical electric field, allowing thinner drift regions for the same voltage rating.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/2$ factor** in $Q_{rr}$ and $P_{sw}$ formulas — costs 1 to 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $t_{rr}$ and $t_s$** — always state $t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$.
> 3. **Not labelling the n⁻ drift region** in construction diagrams — 2 marks deducted.
> 4. **Unit mistakes** in numericals: $t_{rr}$ must be in **seconds** (not µs) when using in formulas.
> 5. **Not mentioning silicon limit** when discussing on-state vs breakdown trade-off.
> 6. **Drawing a signal-diode symbol** (with cathode bar pointing the wrong way) — examiners deduct for circuit symbol errors.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Power Diodes:**

- ☐ A power diode has a **p⁺-n⁻-n⁺** structure; the **n⁻ drift region** is its defining feature.
- ☐ Cut-in voltage for Si ≈ **0.7 V**; for Ge ≈ 0.3 V; for Schottky ≈ 0.3–0.5 V.
- ☐ Forward current rating: **A to kA**; reverse voltage: **V to kV**.
- ☐ Static V-I has three regions: **forward conduction, reverse blocking, reverse breakdown** (avoid!).
- ☐ Switching has three transients: **turn-on ($t_d + t_r$), reverse recovery ($t_s + t_t$), forward recovery ($V_{FR}$)**.
- ☐ **Reverse recovery time $t_{rr} = t_s + t_t$** is the *most important* dynamic parameter.
- ☐ **Reverse recovery charge $Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} I_{RR} t_{rr}$**.
- ☐ **Softness factor $S = t_t / t_s$** — $S \ge 1$ is preferred.
- ☐ **Switching power loss $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_R I_{RR} t_{rr} f_s$** — dominates at high $f_s$.
- ☐ **Conduction loss $P_{cond} = V_F I_F$** — dominates at low $f_s$ and high current.
- ☐ **Breakdown voltage $V_{BR} = \varepsilon_s E_{crit}^2 / (2qN_D)$** — increases as $N_D$ decreases.
- ☐ **Silicon limit trade-off:** high $V_{BR}$ ⇔ thick drift region ⇔ high $V_F$ ⇔ high conduction loss.
- ☐ **Diode types:** General-purpose, Fast-recovery, Ultrafast, Schottky, SiC Schottky.
- ☐ **Schottky diodes** have no minority carriers ⇒ $t_{rr} \approx 0$, but low $V_{BR}$.
- ☐ **Thermal model:** $T_J = T_A + P \cdot R_{\theta JA}$; always keep $T_J < 150$ °C (Si) or 200 °C (SiC).
- ☐ In a **full-bridge rectifier**, four power diodes convert 50 Hz AC to pulsating DC.
- ☐ In a **chopper/freewheeling** application, the diode carries inductive load current when the main switch opens.
- ☐ **Snubber diodes** protect switches from $L \frac{di}{dt}$ voltage transients.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
