I_C equals 0"] CHECKVBCREV -- "YES: V_BC greater than 0.7 V" --> REG4["REGION 4 REVERSE ACTIVE
EBJ Reverse and CBJ Forward
Beta very small"] CHECKVBE -- "YES: V_BE greater than 0.7 V" --> EBJFWD["EBJ is FORWARD biased"] EBJFWD --> CHECKVBCFWD{"Is V_BC greater than 0.7 V?"} CHECKVBCFWD -- "NO: V_BC less than 0.7 V
meaning V_CB greater than 0" --> REG1["REGION 1 FORWARD ACTIVE
EBJ Forward and CBJ Reverse
Amplification Mode"] CHECKVBCFWD -- "YES: V_BC greater than 0.7 V" --> REG2["REGION 2 SATURATION
Both Junctions Forward
Switch ON State"] REG1 --> OUTPUT["Output: Operating Region Identified"] REG2 --> OUTPUT REG3 --> OUTPUT REG4 --> OUTPUT ``` --- ## 4.3 Output Characteristics Curve Schematic (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AXES["OUTPUT CHARACTERISTIC PLOT I_C versus V_CE"] XAXIS["Horizontal Axis: V_CE in Volts
Range 0 to 20 V typical"] YAXIS["Vertical Axis: I_C in mA
Range 0 to 50 mA typical"] end subgraph CURVES["I_C versus V_CE CURVES at different I_B"] SAT_ZONE["SATURATION ZONE
V_CE less than V_CE sat equals 0.2 V
Steep Rise Region"] ACTIVE_ZONE["FORWARD ACTIVE ZONE
V_CE between 0.2 V and 20 V
Nearly Flat Curves
Different I_B values give different I_C levels"] BREAKDOWN["BREAKDOWN ZONE
V_CE greater than V_CEO max
Avalanche Region
Damaging Zone"] end SAT_ZONE --> ACTIVE_ZONE ACTIVE_ZONE --> BREAKDOWN ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Forward Active Region** appears as a set of **nearly horizontal lines** in the $I_C$ vs. $V_{CE}$ plot — this near-constancy of $I_C$ with $V_{CE}$ is the hallmark of **current-controlled behavior** and is the visual signature students should look for. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: List the four regions of operation of a BJT and state the biasing condition of its two junctions in each region.** **Model Answer:** The four regions of operation of a BJT are: | # | Region | EBJ Bias | CBJ Bias | |---|--------|----------|----------| | 1 | **Forward Active Region** | Forward biased | Reverse biased | | 2 | **Saturation Region** | Forward biased | Forward biased | | 3 | **Cutoff Region** | Reverse biased | Reverse biased | | 4 | **Reverse Active Region** | Reverse biased | Forward biased | The Forward Active Region is used for **linear amplification**, while the Saturation and Cutoff regions are used as the **ON** and **OFF** states in digital switching applications. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Why is the Forward Active Region preferred for analog amplification? Justify with the EBJ and CBJ biasing.** **Model Answer:** The Forward Active Region is preferred for analog amplification because: 1. The **EBJ is forward biased** → it efficiently injects majority carriers (electrons for NPN) from the emitter into the base. 2. The **CBJ is reverse biased** → it creates a strong electric field in the depletion region that sweeps the injected carriers into the collector, producing a large, well-controlled $I_C$. 3. The collector current $I_C$ is **linearly proportional** to the base current $I_B$ with gain $\beta$, enabling faithful reproduction of small input signal variations as larger output variations. 4. In Saturation and Cutoff, the $I_C$ vs. $I_B$ relationship becomes **non-linear**, making them unsuitable for amplification. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the four regions of operation of an NPN BJT. Tabulate the bias conditions and key current relationships for each region.** **Model Solution:** The four regions of operation of an NPN BJT are determined by the biasing of its two PN junctions. The classification and key properties are tabulated below: | Region | EBJ | CBJ | $V_{CE}$ Range | $I_C$ Behavior | Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Forward Active** | Forward | Reverse | $V_{CE(\text{sat})} < V_{CE} < V_{CC}$ | $I_C = \beta I_B$ (controlled by $I_B$) | Amplification | | **Saturation** | Forward | Forward | $V_{CE} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$ | $I_C$ limited by external $R_C$ | Switch ON | | **Cutoff** | Reverse | Reverse | $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}$ | $I_C \approx 0$ (only leakage) | Switch OFF | | **Reverse Active** | Reverse | Forward | $V_{EC(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$ | $I_E = \beta_R I_B$ with $\beta_R \ll \beta$ | Bidirectional switches | **Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture):** ```mermaid flowchart TD A["V_BE applied to EBJ"] --> B{"V_BE greater than 0.7 V?"} B -- "YES" --> C["EBJ FORWARD"] B -- "NO" --> D["EBJ REVERSE"] C --> E{"V_CB greater than 0?"} E -- "YES: V_C greater than V_B" --> F["REGION 1 FORWARD ACTIVE"] E -- "NO: V_C less than V_B" --> G["REGION 2 SATURATION"] D --> H{"V_C greater than V_B?"} H -- "YES" --> I["REGION 4 REVERSE ACTIVE"] H -- "NO" --> J["REGION 3 CUTOFF"] F --> APP1["Analog Amplifier"] G --> APP2["Digital Switch ON"] J --> APP3["Digital Switch OFF"] I --> APP4["Specialized Circuits"] ``` #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Q: For a BJT with $\alpha = 0.98$ and $I_{CBO} = 10\ \mu\text{A}$, calculate (i) $\beta_{DC}$, (ii) $I_{CEO}$, and (iii) $I_C$ when $I_B = 0.5\ \text{mA}$.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Calculate $\beta_{DC}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \beta_{DC} &= \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} \\ &= \dfrac{0.98}{1 - 0.98} \\ &= \dfrac{0.98}{0.02} \\ &= 49 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Calculate $I_{CEO}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{CEO} &= (\beta_{DC} + 1) \cdot I_{CBO} \\ &= (49 + 1) \times 10\ \mu\text{A} \\ &= 50 \times 10\ \mu\text{A} \\ &= 500\ \mu\text{A} = 0.5\ \text{mA} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Calculate $I_C$ for $I_B = 0.5\ \text{mA}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_C &= \beta_{DC} \cdot I_B + I_{CEO} \\ &= 49 \times 0.5\ \text{mA} + 0.5\ \text{mA} \\ &= 24.5\ \text{mA} + 0.5\ \text{mA} \\ &= 25\ \text{mA} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answers:** $\beta_{DC} = 49$, $I_{CEO} = 0.5\ \text{mA}$, $I_C = 25\ \text{mA}$. --- ### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Q: Derive the relationship $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta + 1) I_{CBO}$ starting from the fundamental current equation $I_E = I_B + I_C$ and the common-base current relation $I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CBO}$.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Start with the KCL relation:** $$ I_E = I_B + I_C \quad \text{...(i)} $$ **Step 2 — Write the common-base current relation:** $$ I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CBO} \quad \text{...(ii)} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute (i) into (ii):** $$ \begin{aligned} I_C &= \alpha (I_B + I_C) + I_{CBO} \\ I_C &= \alpha I_B + \alpha I_C + I_{CBO} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate $I_C$:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_C - \alpha I_C &= \alpha I_B + I_{CBO} \\ I_C (1 - \alpha) &= \alpha I_B + I_{CBO} \\ I_C &= \dfrac{\alpha I_B + I_{CBO}}{1 - \alpha} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $(1 - \alpha)$ and substitute $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_C &= \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} I_B + \dfrac{I_{CBO}}{1 - \alpha} \\ I_C &= \beta \cdot I_B + \dfrac{1}{1 - \alpha} \cdot I_{CBO} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Use the identity $\dfrac{1}{1 - \alpha} = \beta + 1$:** $$ \boxed{\,I_C = \beta \cdot I_B + (\beta + 1) I_{CBO}\,} $$ This completes the derivation. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Q: An NPN transistor operates in the forward active region with $V_{CE} = 5\ \text{V}$, $R_C = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_{CC} = 12\ \text{V}$, and $\beta = 100$. If $I_B = 50\ \mu\text{A}$, verify the region of operation and find $V_{CE}$.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate the unsaturated $I_C$ (assuming Forward Active):** $$ I_C = \beta \cdot I_B = 100 \times 50\ \mu\text{A} = 5\ \text{mA} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate the maximum possible $I_C$ (saturation limit):** $$ I_{C(\text{sat})} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}}{R_C} = \dfrac{12 - 0.2}{1000} = 11.8\ \text{mA} $$ **Step 3 — Compare:** Since $I_C = 5\ \text{mA} < I_{C(\text{sat})} = 11.8\ \text{mA}$, the transistor is **NOT in saturation**. Therefore, it operates in the **Forward Active Region**. ✓ **Step 4 — Calculate actual $V_{CE}$ using KVL around the output loop:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{CE} &= V_{CC} - I_C \cdot R_C \\ &= 12 - (5\ \text{mA})(1\ \text{k}\Omega) \\ &= 12 - 5 \\ &= 7\ \text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** The transistor is in the **Forward Active Region**, and $V_{CE} = 7\ \text{V}$. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Examiner's Perspective):** > > 1. **Forgetting to state both junction bias conditions** when naming a region. Simply writing "Active Region" without specifying *which* active region (Forward or Reverse) loses **1 mark** per occurrence. > 2. **Confusing $I_{CBO}$ and $I_{CEO}$** — these are *not* the same. $I_{CBO}$ is the leakage between C and B with emitter open; $I_{CEO}$ is the leakage between C and E with base open, and is $(\beta + 1)$ times larger. > 3. **Using $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ in the Saturation Region** — this is **invalid** because the relationship $I_C = \beta I_B$ holds *only* in the Forward Active Region. In Saturation, $I_C$ is limited by the external circuit, not by $\beta$. > 4. **Neglecting to verify the region before applying region-specific formulas** — the most common reason for full-mark loss. Always check whether $I_C \le I_{C(\text{sat})}$ first. > 5. **Mixing up PNP and NPN current directions** — for PNP, all currents flow *out* of the emitter (conventional current). Sign convention errors typically cost **2–3 marks**. > 6. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — write $\text{mA}$, $\mu\text{A}$, or $\text{V}$ explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam** - [x] A BJT has **two PN junctions** and therefore has **four regions of operation** based on the bias combinations. - [x] **Forward Active** = EBJ Forward + CBJ Reverse → used for **amplification** → $I_C = \beta I_B$. - [x] **Saturation** = EBJ Forward + CBJ Forward → used for **switch ON** → $V_{CE} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$. - [x] **Cutoff** = EBJ Reverse + CBJ Reverse → used for **switch OFF** → $I_C \approx 0$. - [x] **Reverse Active** = EBJ Reverse + CBJ Forward → low gain ($\beta_R \ll \beta_F$) → rarely used. - [x] **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** $I_E = I_B + I_C$ is **always true** in all four regions. - [x] The **relation $I_C = \beta I_B$ is valid ONLY in the Forward Active Region** — not in Saturation or Cutoff. - [x] **$\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$** and **$\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{\beta + 1}$** — these are the two fundamental gain conversion formulas. - [x] **$I_{CEO} = (\beta + 1) I_{CBO}$** — this is the leakage current with base open and is the primary cause of **thermal runaway**. - [x] To identify the region, **check whether $I_C$ computed from $\beta I_B$ exceeds $I_{C(\text{sat})} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}}{R_C}$**. - [x] Standard silicon BJT: **$V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$**, **$V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$**. - [x] Thermal voltage at room temperature: **$V_T \approx 26\ \text{mV}$**. - [x] For PNP transistors, **reverse the polarity** of all bias voltages and current directions. - [x] In the **Forward Active Region**, the output characteristic curves ($I_C$ vs. $V_{CE}$) are **nearly horizontal** — a visual signature of current-controlled behavior. - [x] The **Saturation Region** is the "both doors open" state — maximum current flow with minimum voltage drop. - [x] The **Cutoff Region** is the "both doors closed" state — no current flow, transistor acts as an open switch. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a numerical problem gives you $I_B$, $\beta$, $V_{CC}$, and $R_C$, **always** (1) compute $I_C = \beta I_B$, (2) compute $I_{C(\text{sat})}$, (3) compare the two, and (4) **state explicitly which region the BJT is in** *before* computing $V_{CE}$. This single habit can earn you **2–3 extra marks** on most KTU board problems.

topics.title: Switching Characteristics: Turn-On and Turn-Off Switching Waveforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Switching Characteristics: Turn-On and Turn-Off Switching Waveforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In power electronics, **switching characteristics** describe the transient electrical behavior of a power semiconductor device (BJT, MOSFET, IGBT, or GTO) as it transitions between the **ON state (conducting)** and the **OFF state (blocking)**. These transient waveforms are critical because they determine:

- The **switching speed** of the device
- The **switching power loss** per cycle
- The **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** generated
- The **safe operating area (SOA)** boundaries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Switching characteristics define the time-domain response of a power semiconductor device when triggered by a gate/base control signal. The turn-on switching waveform captures the delay time, rise time, and turn-on time, while the turn-off waveform captures the storage time, fall time, and turn-off time.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Tap Model

Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline controlled by a heavy gate valve:

- When you want to **open** the valve (turn-on), you first must overcome static friction (**delay time $t_d$**), then gradually move the handle (**rise time $t_r$**), and finally water flows fully (**turn-on time $t_{on}$**).
- When you **close** the valve (turn-off), water still flows briefly due to inertia (**storage time $t_s$**), then gradually stops (**fall time $t_f$**), until the gate is fully shut (**turn-off time $t_{off}$**).

A power transistor behaves identically. The **gate/base signal** is like your hand turning the valve, and the **collector current/voltage** is like the water flow.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Storage time $t_s$** — typically in the microsecond range for BJTs
- **Rise time $t_r$** and **Fall time $t_f$** — nanoseconds to microseconds
- **Switching frequency $f_s$** — typically **1 kHz to 1 MHz** in modern converters
- **Junction temperature $T_j$** — must be kept below **150 °C** for silicon devices

> [!NOTE]
> Modern MOSFETs and IGBTs are preferred over BJTs in high-frequency applications because they have **no storage time** (majority carrier devices), making them orders of magnitude faster.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Phases of Switching

Every power semiconductor switch has **two** transient phases:

### Phase 1 — TURN-ON Switching
Triggered by a rising edge at the gate. The device moves from blocking state to conducting state.

**Sub-stages of Turn-On:**

1. **Delay Time ($t_d$)**: Time between 10% of the gate signal and the point where collector current $i_C$ reaches 10% of its final value. During this time, the input capacitance $C_{iss}$ charges from 0 V to the threshold voltage $V_{th}$.
2. **Rise Time ($t_r$)**: Time for $i_C$ to rise from 10% to 90% of its final (saturated) value. The device moves from the **active region** into **saturation**.
3. **Turn-On Time ($t_{on}$)**: $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$

### Phase 2 — TURN-OFF Switching
Triggered by a falling edge at the gate. The device moves from conducting state to blocking state.

**Sub-stages of Turn-Off:**

1. **Storage Time ($t_s$)**: Time required to remove the excess minority carriers stored in the base region. Relevant for **bipolar devices** (BJT, IGBT, thyristor). During $t_s$, the collector current remains at its saturated value.
2. **Fall Time ($t_f$)**: Time for $i_C$ to fall from 90% to 10% of its initial value. The device moves out of saturation.
3. **Turn-Off Time ($t_{off}$)**: $t_{off} = t_s + t_f$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Turn-On Time | $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$ | Total turn-on duration | s |
| Turn-Off Time | $t_{off} = t_s + t_f$ | Total turn-off duration | s |
| Average Switching Power Loss | $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_s$ | Energy lost per switching cycle | W |
| Energy Lost per Turn-On | $E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C t_{on}$ | Single transition energy | J |
| Energy Lost per Turn-Off | $E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C t_{off}$ | Single transition energy | J |
| Total Switching Loss per Cycle | $E_{total} = E_{on} + E_{off}$ | Combined switching energy | J |
| Effective Duty Cycle Loss | $\Delta D = (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_s$ | Loss of duty ratio due to switching | dimensionless |
| Gate Charge Relation | $Q_g = C_{iss} \cdot V_{GS}$ | Gate drive energy indicator | C |
| Miller Plateau Duration | $t_{gp} = \frac{Q_{gd} \cdot R_g}{V_{GG} - V_{pl}}$ | Time spent in Miller region | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoiding Pipe Symbols in Tables**: The table above uses plain text and standard LaTeX to prevent markdown parsing errors. Notice the use of $\vert$ notation where absolute value would be needed.

## 2.3 Why These Switch Losses Matter in Real Engineering

- In a **5 kW solar inverter** switching at 50 kHz, switching losses can be **15–25%** of total losses — forcing engineers to choose wide-bandgap devices (SiC, GaN).
- In **electric vehicle traction inverters**, switching losses directly reduce **driving range**.
- In **switch-mode power supplies (SMPS)**, switching losses limit the maximum achievable power density.
- In **motor drives**, slow turn-off times cause **shoot-through** faults in half-bridge configurations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Turn-On Switching Energy

We consider an idealized linear transition where the collector current $i_C(t)$ and the collector-emitter voltage $v_{CE}(t)$ vary linearly during the turn-on interval.

**Step 1 — Define the time-domain current waveform during turn-on**

During turn-on, the collector current rises linearly from 0 to $I_C$ over the turn-on time $t_{on}$:

$$i_C(t) = \frac{I_C}{t_{on}} \cdot t \quad \text{for } 0 \leq t \leq t_{on}$$

**Step 2 — Define the time-domain voltage waveform during turn-on**

Simultaneously, the collector-emitter voltage falls linearly from the supply $V_{CC}$ to the saturation voltage $V_{CE(sat)}$:

$$v_{CE}(t) = V_{CC} - \left(\frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{t_{on}}\right) \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Neglect saturation voltage for high-voltage analysis**

Since $V_{CC} \gg V_{CE(sat)}$ in most power applications, we approximate:

$$v_{CE}(t) \approx V_{CC} \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{on}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Write the instantaneous switching power**

$$p(t) = v_{CE}(t) \cdot i_C(t) = V_{CC} \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{on}}\right) \cdot \frac{I_C}{t_{on}} \cdot t$$

$$p(t) = \frac{V_{CC} I_C}{t_{on}} \cdot t \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{on}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Integrate power over the turn-on interval to get energy**

$$E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_{on}} p(t) \, dt = \frac{V_{CC} I_C}{t_{on}} \int_{0}^{t_{on}} \left(t - \frac{t^2}{t_{on}}\right) dt$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral**

$$E_{on} = \frac{V_{CC} I_C}{t_{on}} \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} - \frac{t^3}{3 t_{on}} \right]_{0}^{t_{on}}$$

$$E_{on} = \frac{V_{CC} I_C}{t_{on}} \left( \frac{t_{on}^2}{2} - \frac{t_{on}^2}{3} \right)$$

$$E_{on} = \frac{V_{CC} I_C}{t_{on}} \cdot \frac{t_{on}^2}{6} = \frac{1}{6} V_{CC} I_C t_{on}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify using the standard engineering approximation**

The "linear overlap" model used in textbooks (Rashid, Mohan, Hart) simplifies this to:

$$\boxed{E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C t_{on}}$$

This is obtained by assuming a **rectangular voltage-current overlap** — physically valid when the device transitions through the **active region** during most of $t_{on}$.

**Step 8 — Similarly derive turn-off energy**

By symmetry of the linear overlap argument:

$$\boxed{E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C t_{off}}$$

**Step 9 — Derive total switching loss in watts**

Multiply total energy per cycle by switching frequency:

$$P_{sw} = E_{total} \cdot f_s = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_s$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Time Interval Definitions

The matrix below is a high-yield visual reference for KTU exams.

| Interval | Start Point | End Point | Physical Meaning | Applies To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $t_d$ (Delay) | 10% of $v_{GS}$ rise | $i_C$ reaches 10% | Input capacitance charging | BJT, MOSFET, IGBT |
| $t_r$ (Rise) | $i_C$ at 10% | $i_C$ at 90% | Active-region transit | All devices |
| $t_{on}$ | Gate pulse start | $i_C$ reaches 90% | Total turn-on | All devices |
| $t_s$ (Storage) | Gate pulse fall | $i_C$ begins to fall | Minority carrier removal | BJT, IGBT only |
| $t_f$ (Fall) | $i_C$ at 90% | $i_C$ at 10% | Active-region transit | All devices |
| $t_{off}$ | Gate pulse fall | $i_C$ reaches 10% | Total turn-off | All devices |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A BJT in a chopper circuit switches a load with $V_{CC} = 200 \text{ V}$ and $I_C = 50 \text{ A}$. The turn-on time is $t_{on} = 2 \, \mu s$ and turn-off time is $t_{off} = 5 \, \mu s$. The switching frequency is $f_s = 10 \text{ kHz}$. Calculate the total switching power loss.

**Step 1 — Compute turn-on energy**

$$E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C t_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 \cdot 50 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{on} = 10000 \times 10^{-6} = 0.01 \text{ J} = 10 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 2 — Compute turn-off energy**

$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C t_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 \cdot 50 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{off} = 25000 \times 10^{-6} = 0.025 \text{ J} = 25 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 3 — Total energy per cycle**

$$E_{total} = E_{on} + E_{off} = 10 + 25 = 35 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by switching frequency**

$$P_{sw} = E_{total} \cdot f_s = 35 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10 \times 10^{3}$$

$$\boxed{P_{sw} = 350 \text{ W}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** 350 W is a substantial loss — nearly the power of a small room heater. This is why high-frequency switchers use **MOSFETs** with $t_{off}$ values in the **tens of nanoseconds** range.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Switching Transistor Circuit

This block diagram maps the interactions between the gate drive, the power device, and the load during a switching transition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Gate Drive Signal Vg] --> nodeB[Gate Resistance Rg]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Device Input Capacitance Ciss]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Threshold Reached}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[Collector Current ic Begins to Rise]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Active Region Transit]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Saturation Reached]
    nodeG --> nodeH[ON State - Voltage VCE Falls to VCEsat]
    nodeD -- Falling Edge --> nodeI[Minority Carrier Removal]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Storage Time ts]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Collector Current Falls]
    nodeK --> nodeL[OFF State - Voltage VCE Rises to VCC]
    nodeH --> nodeM[Switching Loss Eon]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Switching Loss Eoff]
    nodeM --> nodeO[Total Switching Power Psw]
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP[Thermal Management Heat Sink]
```

## 4.2 Timing Diagram of Turn-On and Turn-Off Waveforms

This diagram shows the relative timing of the gate signal, collector current, and collector-emitter voltage during one full switching cycle.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Gate as Gate Signal Vg
    participant Ic as Collector Current Ic
    participant Vce as Collector Voltage Vce
    Gate->>Ic: td Delay Interval
    Ic->>Ic: tr Rise Interval
    Ic->>Vce: Vce Falls to VCEsat
    Note over Gate,Vce: ON STATE - Device Saturated
    Gate->>Ic: ts Storage Interval
    Ic->>Ic: tf Fall Interval
    Ic->>Vce: Vce Rises to VCC
    Note over Gate,Vce: OFF STATE - Device Blocking
```

## 4.3 Switching Loss Energy Map

This flowchart visualizes how the total energy dissipated per cycle is partitioned between turn-on and turn-off transitions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Total Switching Loss Psw] --> nodeB[Turn-On Energy Eon]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Turn-Off Energy Eoff]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Half of VCC times IC times ton]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Half of VCC times IC times toff]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Heat Sink Thermal Path]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Junction Temperature Tj Below 150C]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define storage time and fall time in the context of BJT turn-off switching characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**
Storage time $t_s$ is the interval between the moment the gate/base drive signal is removed (falls to 10% of its ON value) and the moment the collector current $i_C$ begins to fall from its saturated value. It represents the time required to **sweep out the excess minority carriers** stored in the base region of the BJT.

Fall time $t_f$ is the interval during which the collector current falls from 90% to 10% of its initial saturated value, corresponding to the device transitioning from the active region into cutoff.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why do MOSFETs exhibit negligible storage time compared to BJTs?**

**Model Answer:**
MOSFETs are **majority-carrier devices** — conduction occurs only via electrons (in n-channel) or holes (in p-channel). There are **no injected minority carriers** stored in the conducting channel, so when the gate voltage is removed, conduction ceases almost immediately. In contrast, BJTs are **bipolar devices** that require a **saturated base charge** of minority carriers for full turn-on. This stored charge must be removed before turn-off, producing a non-zero storage time $t_s$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the turn-on and turn-off switching waveforms of a power BJT, clearly marking the delay time $t_d$, rise time $t_r$, storage time $t_s$, fall time $t_f$, and the corresponding collector current and collector-emitter voltage transitions. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The switching waveforms consist of **three** plots against a common time axis:

**Plot 1 — Base/Gate Drive Signal $v_{BE}(t)$:**
A rectangular pulse rising from $-V_{BB}$ (reverse bias) to $+V_1$ (forward drive) at the turn-on instant, and falling back to $-V_{BB}$ at the turn-off instant.

**Plot 2 — Collector Current $i_C(t)$:**
- Remains at 0 during the OFF state.
- After a delay $t_d$ (during which the base-emitter junction capacitance charges to 0.7 V), $i_C$ rises linearly during $t_r$ from 10% to 90% of its saturation value $I_{CS}$.
- Stays at $I_{CS}$ during the ON interval.
- After the base signal falls, $i_C$ remains at $I_{CS}$ during the **storage time $t_s$** because stored minority carriers still sustain conduction.
- Falls linearly from 90% to 10% of $I_{CS}$ during $t_f$.
- Returns to 0 in the OFF state.

**Plot 3 — Collector-Emitter Voltage $v_{CE}(t)$:**
- Sits at $V_{CC}$ during the OFF state.
- Falls to $V_{CE(sat)}$ (typically 1–2 V) once saturation is reached.
- Rises back to $V_{CC}$ during the turn-off transition.

**Key Marking Points Expected by Examiner:**
- Correct identification of $t_d$, $t_r$, $t_s$, $t_f$ on the diagram
- Indication that $i_C$ and $v_{CE}$ are complementary during switching (overlap region)
- Mention of the **active region** during $t_r$ and $t_f$

---

**(b)** A power BJT is used in a step-down chopper with $V_{CC} = 100 \text{ V}$, load current $I_C = 20 \text{ A}$, turn-on time $t_{on} = 1.5 \, \mu s$, and turn-off time $t_{off} = 3 \, \mu s$. If the switching frequency is $f_s = 5 \text{ kHz}$, determine: (i) the turn-on switching energy, (ii) the turn-off switching energy, and (iii) the total switching power loss. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Turn-on switching energy:**

$$E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C t_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \cdot 20 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{on} = 1000 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 1.5 \text{ mJ}$$

**(ii) Turn-off switching energy:**

$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C t_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \cdot 20 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{off} = 1000 \times 3 \times 10^{-6} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 3 \text{ mJ}$$

**(iii) Total switching power loss:**

$$P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_s = (1.5 + 3) \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5 \times 10^{3}$$

$$P_{sw} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5 \times 10^{3} = 22.5 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{sw} = 22.5 \text{ W}}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Compare the switching characteristics of a power BJT and a power MOSFET. Explain with waveforms why MOSFETs are preferred in high-frequency applications. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Power BJT | Power MOSFET |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier type | Bipolar (minority + majority) | Unipolar (majority only) |
| Storage time $t_s$ | Significant (1–10 µs) | Negligible (< 50 ns) |
| Turn-off time $t_{off}$ | 5–20 µs | 50–200 ns |
| Drive requirement | Continuous base current | Voltage pulse at gate |
| Switching frequency limit | < 25 kHz typical | > 1 MHz possible |
| Switching loss | High | Very low |

**Waveform Explanation:**
In the BJT turn-off waveform, the collector current remains flat during the **storage time $t_s$** while excess minority carriers are removed. This plateau extends the total turn-off time significantly. In the MOSFET turn-off waveform, the drain current begins to fall **immediately** when the gate signal is removed, because there are no stored minority carriers to evacuate.

**Why MOSFETs Are Preferred at High Frequency:**
Since switching loss $P_{sw} \propto f_s \cdot t_{off}$, a 10× reduction in $t_{off}$ yields a 10× reduction in $P_{sw}$ at any given frequency. At $f_s = 100 \text{ kHz}$, a BJT with $t_{off} = 10 \, \mu s$ would dissipate **100× more** switching loss than a MOSFET with $t_{off} = 100 \text{ ns}$.

---

**(b)** An IGBT switches a load with $V_{CC} = 600 \text{ V}$ and $I_C = 100 \text{ A}$. The turn-on time is $t_{on} = 0.5 \, \mu s$ and turn-off time is $t_{off} = 1.2 \, \mu s$. If the device operates at $f_s = 20 \text{ kHz}$ with a duty cycle of 0.4, calculate: (i) the switching energy per cycle, (ii) the switching power loss, and (iii) the conduction loss if the saturation voltage is $V_{CE(sat)} = 2.5 \text{ V}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Switching energy per cycle:**

$$E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C (t_{on} + t_{off}) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 600 \cdot 100 \cdot (0.5 + 1.2) \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{sw} = 30000 \cdot 1.7 \times 10^{-6} = 5.1 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J} = 51 \text{ mJ}$$

**(ii) Switching power loss:**

$$P_{sw} = E_{sw} \cdot f_s = 51 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 20 \times 10^{3}$$

$$P_{sw} = 1020 \text{ W} = 1.02 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Conduction loss:**

The conduction loss for the ON duration is calculated as:

$$P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C \cdot D = 2.5 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.4 = 100 \text{ W}$$

**Total power dissipation:**

$$P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{cond} = 1020 + 100 = 1120 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{total} = 1120 \text{ W}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2** in the energy formula. Students often write $E = V_{CC} I_C t$, which is **3 marks lost**.
> 2. **Mixing up $t_{on}$ and $t_{off}$** symbols. Always state which is which before substituting.
> 3. **Neglecting conduction loss** when the question asks for *total* loss.
> 4. **Unit errors** — always convert microseconds to seconds ($1 \, \mu s = 10^{-6} \text{ s}$) before computing.
> 5. **In waveform questions**, failing to label axes ($v_{BE}$, $i_C$, $v_{CE}$) loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 6. **Confusing storage time with turn-off time** — $t_{off} = t_s + t_f$, not equal to $t_s$ alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- **Turn-on time:** $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$
- **Turn-off time:** $t_{off} = t_s + t_f$
- **Storage time $t_s$** exists only in **bipolar** devices (BJT, IGBT, thyristor)
- **MOSFETs** have $t_s \approx 0$ because they are **majority-carrier** devices
- **Switching energy (turn-on):** $E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C t_{on}$
- **Switching energy (turn-off):** $E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C t_{off}$
- **Switching power loss:** $P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) f_s$
- **Switching loss is proportional to frequency** — a key reason to limit $f_s$ for slow devices
- The **collector current and collector-emitter voltage overlap** during switching transitions — this overlap is the physical origin of switching loss
- **High $V_{CC}$ and high $I_C$** make switching loss worse — high-power applications demand fast devices (SiC, GaN)
- For a BJT, the **storage time** dominates the turn-off process — techniques like **Baker's clamp** and **speed-up capacitors** are used to reduce $t_s$
- IGBT combines BJT's high current capability with MOSFET's voltage control, but still has a **tail current** during turn-off due to minority carrier recombination
- The **safe operating area (SOA)** during turn-off is more restrictive than during turn-on because the device must sustain high $V_{CE}$ while still conducting — this is the classic **inductive switching** stress
- Always convert time units: $1 \, \mu s = 10^{-6} \text{ s}$, $1 \text{ ns} = 10^{-9} \text{ s}$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safe Operating Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Safe Operating Area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** of a power semiconductor device (such as a Power BJT, Power MOSFET, or IGBT) is defined as the boundary on the **collector current ($I_C$) vs. collector–emitter voltage ($V_{CE}$)** characteristic curve, within which the device can be operated without suffering **irreversible thermal or electrical damage**, either during steady-state conduction or during transient switching events.

Mathematically, SOA represents the simultaneous satisfaction of four fundamental limits:

$$
I_C \cdot V_{CE} \le P_{max}(T_j), \quad I_C \le I_{C(max)}, \quad V_{CE} \le V_{CEO(sus)}, \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Secondary Breakdown avoidance}
$$

where $P_{max}(T_j)$ is the maximum allowable power dissipation evaluated at junction temperature $T_j$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Safe Operating Area is a fundamental design specification that translates the device's electrical limits (current, voltage, power, and second breakdown) into a single graphical envelope used by circuit designers to guarantee device survivability under worst-case operating conditions.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pressure–Temperature Diagram"

Imagine a **pressure cooker** in your kitchen. The manufacturer stamps a chart on it showing the maximum **pressure** (vertical axis) and maximum **temperature** (horizontal axis) combinations under which the cooker can safely be used. If you boil water at atmospheric pressure — fine. If you crank the pressure up, you must keep the temperature low. Push both to the extremes simultaneously, and the metal fatigues and explodes.

A **power transistor's SOA plays exactly the same role**:

| Pressure Cooker Parameter | Transistor SOA Equivalent | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure | $V_{CE}$ (collector–emitter voltage) | Voltage stress across the device |
| Temperature | $I_C$ (collector current) | Heat-generating current flow |
| Material limit curve | SOA boundary | Manufacturer's guaranteed safety envelope |
| Cooking time | Pulse width ($t_p$) | Duration of stress |
| Wet/dry pot | Cooling / heatsinking | Heat removal capability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a "Region" and not a single number?** A transistor can momentarily withstand **huge currents** if the voltage is small (low $I_C \cdot V_{CE}$ dissipation). It can also momentarily withstand **high voltages** if the current is small (low leakage). It is the *simultaneous* application of high voltage and high current that destroys the device. Hence, the safety constraint is a **two-dimensional region**, not a single point.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following industry-standard quantities are used to characterize every SOA plot:

- **$V_{CEO(sus)}$** — **Collector–Emitter Sustaining Voltage** (base open-circuited): the absolute maximum voltage the device can block with the base terminal floating. Typically the right-most vertical asymptote of the SOA curve.
- **$I_{C(Max)}$** — **Maximum Continuous Collector Current**: the horizontal asymptote on the upper boundary.
- **$P_{D(Max)}$** — **Maximum Steady-State Power Dissipation**, evaluated at $T_C = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$ case temperature. Determines the **constant-power hyperbola** $I_C = P_{D(Max)}/V_{CE}$.
- **$V_{CE(sat)}$** — **Saturation Voltage**: the minimum on-state voltage drop of the device.
- **$T_{J(Max)}$** — **Maximum Junction Temperature**, typically **$150^{\circ}\text{C}$** or **$175^{\circ}\text{C}$** for silicon devices.
- **$t_p$** — **Pulse Width**: critical for **pulsed SOA**, because shorter pulses allow higher currents at the same voltage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four SOA Boundaries — Why Each Exists

A complete SOA curve is the **intersection** (logical AND) of four independent physical limits. A device survives only if **every** limit is simultaneously satisfied.

### 2.1.1 Limit 1 — Current Limit (Bond-Wire / Finger Saturation)

The metallization on the silicon die (bond wires, top metal fingers) has a finite cross-sectional area and finite fusing current. Once the collector current exceeds a certain threshold, **electromigration** and **metal fusing** cause permanent open-circuit failure.

$$
I_C \le I_{C(max)}
$$

This bound appears as a **horizontal line** at the top of the SOA diagram. **It is absolute and does NOT depend on pulse width or temperature derating.**

### 2.1.2 Limit 2 — Voltage Limit (Avalanche Breakdown)

When $V_{CE}$ exceeds $V_{CEO(sus)}$, the reverse-biased base–collector junction undergoes **avalanche breakdown**. The current avalanches and the device loses its voltage-blocking capability, leading to thermal runaway.

$$
V_{CE} \le V_{CEO(sus)}
$$

This bound appears as a **vertical line** on the right side of the SOA diagram. **It is also absolute** — the junction breaks down at the same voltage regardless of current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $V_{CEO}$ and not $V_{CBO}$?** With the base **open** (which is the worst-case for breakdown, because the hole current cannot be extracted), the transistor's gain mechanism amplifies the leakage current, so breakdown occurs at a *lower* voltage than $V_{CBO}$. Thus, $V_{CEO} < V_{CBO}$ always. Manufacturers specify $V_{CEO(sus)}$ because in switching circuits the base is often momentarily floating.

### 2.1.3 Limit 3 — Steady-State Thermal Dissipation (Power Hyperbola)

The silicon die can only dissipate so much heat before the junction temperature reaches $T_{J(Max)}$. From the thermal model:

$$
P_D = I_C \cdot V_{CE} = \frac{T_{J(Max)} - T_A}{R_{\theta JA}}
$$

where $R_{\theta JA}$ is the **junction-to-ambient thermal resistance**. Rearranged, this gives a constant-power **hyperbola**:

$$
I_C = \frac{P_{D(Max)}}{V_{CE}}
$$

For $T_C = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$, this is the right-most hyperbola. As case temperature rises, the allowed $P_D$ falls, and the hyperbola moves **downward and inward**, shrinking the safe region. **This is the primary reason for heat-sinking**.

### 2.1.4 Limit 4 — Secondary Breakdown (Forward Bias SOA only)

**Secondary breakdown (SB)** is the most subtle and most dangerous limit. It occurs when a localized "hot spot" forms on the die due to **current crowding**. The local temperature rises, the local current increases due to the negative temperature coefficient of the bipolar transistor's base–emitter voltage, which further raises the temperature — a destructive **thermal positive-feedback loop** that ends in a melted micro-short inside the silicon.

- The locus of secondary-breakdown onset is a curve that **sweeps down and to the left**, typically with a slope around $-1$ in log-log space.
- It is only present in **Forward Bias SOA (FBSOA)**.
- It is the limiting factor for **high-voltage / low-current** operation of BJTs.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Boundary / Role** | **Typical Units** | **Absolute?** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{CEO(sus)}$ | Sustaining voltage (base open) | Voltage limit (vertical line) | V | **Yes** |
| $I_{C(max)}$ | Maximum continuous current | Current limit (horizontal line) | A | **Yes** |
| $P_{D(max)}$ | DC power dissipation at $T_C = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Thermal limit (hyperbola) | W | No (derated by $T_C$) |
| $T_{J(max)}$ | Maximum junction temperature | Thermal cap | °C | **Yes** (typically $150^{\circ}\text{C}$ or $175^{\circ}\text{C}$) |
| $R_{\theta JC}$ | Junction-to-case thermal resistance | Heat path to heatsink | °C/W | No |
| $R_{\theta JA}$ | Junction-to-ambient thermal resistance | Total heat path | °C/W | No |
| $Z_{\theta JC}(t_p)$ | Transient thermal impedance | Determines **pulsed** SOA | °C/W | No (time-dependent) |
| $I_{C(pulsed)}$ | Pulsed current capability | Expands SOA for short $t_p$ | A | No (function of $t_p$, duty) |
| $V_{CE} \cdot I_C$ | Instantaneous power | Must be inside power hyperbola | W | No |

### 2.2.1 Derating Equations (Real-World Utility)

For a heatsink at $T_C \neq 25^{\circ}\text{C}$, the safe power becomes:

$$
P_{D,\text{derated}} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}}
$$

For a **single rectangular pulse** of duration $t_p$, the transient thermal impedance replaces $R_{\theta JC}$:

$$
Z_{\theta JC}(t_p) = R_{\theta JC} \cdot f(t_p)
$$

where $f(t_p) \le 1$ is the **thermal step-response factor** (read off the device's normalized $Z_{\theta}$ curve). This is what allows **pulsed SOA** to extend far beyond DC SOA at short $t_p$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** SOA is the *bridge* between a device's data-sheet parameters and its reliable use in **motor drives, switch-mode power supplies (SMPS), uninterruptible power supplies (UPS), and traction inverters**. Every power-electronics design begins by plotting the load line on top of the SOA to confirm operation stays inside the envelope under all transient and fault conditions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Constant-Power Hyperbola

**Starting premise:** The junction temperature $T_J$ must not exceed $T_{J(max)}$.

**Step 1 — Heat-flow equation (Fourier's law in 1-D thermal resistance form):**

$$
P_D = \frac{T_J - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}}
$$

**Step 2 — Set the worst case $T_J = T_{J(max)}$ and solve for $P_{D(max)}$:**

$$
P_{D(max)} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $P_D = I_C \cdot V_{CE}$:**

$$
I_C \cdot V_{CE} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $I_C$ as a function of $V_{CE}$:**

$$
I_C = \frac{1}{V_{CE}} \cdot \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize this as a hyperbola $y = k/x$:**

$$
I_C \cdot V_{CE} = \text{constant} = P_{D(max)}
$$

This is the **constant-power hyperbola** that bounds the upper-right corner of the SOA.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pulsed SOA Expansion Factor

**Starting premise:** During a short pulse, heat does not have time to propagate from the die to the case, so the *transient* thermal impedance is much smaller than the steady-state $R_{\theta JC}$.

**Step 1 — Define the transient thermal impedance:**

$$
Z_{\theta JC}(t_p) = \frac{T_J(t_p) - T_C}{P_D}
$$

**Step 2 — For a single rectangular pulse of duration $t_p$:**

$$
P_{D,pulsed} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_C}{Z_{\theta JC}(t_p)}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the dimensionless thermal factor:**

$$
k(t_p) = \frac{Z_{\theta JC}(t_p)}{R_{\theta JC}}, \quad \text{with} \quad 0 < k(t_p) \le 1
$$

**Step 4 — Express the ratio of pulsed to DC power limits:**

$$
\frac{P_{D,pulsed}}{P_{D,DC}} = \frac{1}{k(t_p)} > 1
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude that pulsed SOA current limit expands:**

$$
I_{C,pulsed}(t_p) = \frac{I_{C,DC}}{k(t_p)}
$$

**Numerical example (KTU typical value):** If $k(1\,\mu s) = 0.05$, then a DC current limit of $10\,\text{A}$ becomes a **$200\,\text{A}$** pulse capability — a $\times 20$ expansion. This is why short-circuit pulses must still be checked: $200\,\text{A} \cdot V_{CE}$ for even $1\,\mu s$ can deliver substantial energy if unchecked.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Analytical Form of the FBSOA Boundary (Piecewise)

A complete analytical model of the **Forward Bias SOA** on log-log axes uses four straight-line segments:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segment 1 (low } V_{CE}\text{):} \quad & I_{C} = I_{C(max)} = \text{const} \\
\text{Segment 2 (mid } V_{CE}\text{):} \quad & I_C \cdot V_{CE} = P_{D(max)} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \log I_C = -\log V_{CE} + \log P_{D(max)} \\
\text{Segment 3 (high } V_{CE}\text{):} \quad & I_C = K_{SB} \cdot V_{CE}^{-n} \;\; \text{(secondary breakdown, } n \approx 1\text{)} \\
\text{Segment 4 (very high } V_{CE}\text{):} \quad & V_{CE} = V_{CEO(sus)} = \text{const}
\end{aligned}
$$

The full FBSOA is the **minimum envelope** of all four:

$$
I_{C,SOA}(V_{CE}) = \min \left\{ I_{C(max)},\; \frac{P_{D(max)}}{V_{CE}},\; K_{SB} \cdot V_{CE}^{-n},\; \frac{V_{CEO(sus)}}{V_{CE}}\cdot I_{C(max)} \right\}
$$

This piecewise expression is the answer KTU examiners expect when asked *"Sketch and explain the FBSOA of a power BJT."*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — FBSOA vs. RBSOA

| **Feature** | **FBSOA (Forward Bias SOA)** | **RBSOA (Reverse Bias SOA)** |
|---|---|---|
| When active | Device turning **ON** (base driven high) | Device turning **OFF** (base reverse-biased) |
| Limits shown | All **four**: current, voltage, power, **secondary breakdown** | Only **two**: current and voltage |
| Secondary breakdown present? | **Yes** — the dominant high-voltage limit | **No** — base is reverse-biased, prevents thermal runaway |
| Typical shape | Clipped hyperbola with SB "knee" curving inward | Clean rectangular box from $I_{C,max}$ to $V_{CEO(sus)}$ |
| Largest area? | **No** — smaller due to SB | **Yes** — much larger because SB is suppressed |
| $V_{CEO(sus)}$ line | Stops at the SB curve | Extends vertically to full breakdown |
| Practical use | $t_{on}$, $t_{on}+t_{hold}$ | $t_{off}$, inductive-load switching |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** In an **inductive-load switch-off** (e.g., turning off current in a motor winding), the device is forced to simultaneously support the full DC bus voltage *and* the full load current during the turn-off transient. This is the **Reverse Bias SOA** scenario. Designers must ensure the trajectory stays inside the RBSOA rectangle — and this is usually the *binding constraint* for IGBTs in motor drives.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — SOA Boundary Engine

The following Mermaid diagram is a **functional-architecture flow** showing how the four SOA boundaries are computed and combined inside a typical device data-sheet generator / design verification tool.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device Data Sheet Inputs]:::input
    B[V_CEO_sus Limiter]:::proc
    C[I_C_max Limiter]:::proc
    D[P_D_max Thermal Hyperbola]:::proc
    E[Secondary Breakdown Curve]:::proc
    F[Minimum Envelope Combiner]:::merge
    G[FBSOA Output Curve]:::output
    H[RBSOA Output Curve]:::output
    I[Pulsed SOA Expander]:::expand
    J[Pulsed SOA Output]:::output

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    B --> F
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G
    F --> H
    D --> I
    C --> I
    I --> J

    classDef input fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#8B7500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef merge fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef expand fill:#F5E6FF,stroke:#6A0DAD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading guide:** `A` supplies the data-sheet constants; the four `proc` nodes compute each boundary; `F` takes the *minimum* of all four to form the safe envelope; the envelope is rendered as `FBSOA` and `RBSOA`; finally `I` uses the transient thermal impedance to expand the curve for short pulses, producing `J`.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Load Line Inside the SOA Box

This diagram shows the **classical KTU-style sketch** of a transistor's $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ plane with the SOA boundary and a load line drawn on it, in block-level form.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L[Log-Log I_C vs V_CE Plane]
        direction LR
        V[Vertical Axis: I_C in A]:::axis
        H[Horizontal Axis: V_CE in V]:::axis
        A1[Region 1: Low V_CE, Current-Limited Flat Top]:::r1
        A2[Region 2: Mid V_CE, Constant Power Hyperbola P_D_max]:::r2
        A3[Region 3: High V_CE, Secondary Breakdown Curve]:::r3
        A4[Region 4: V_CEO_sus Vertical Wall]:::r4
    end
    subgraph LL[Load Line Trajectory]
        direction LR
        Q1[Q-point at Turn-On: high I_C, low V_CE]:::qp
        Q2[Active Switching Path: travels through SOA]:::qp
        Q3[Q-point at Turn-Off: low I_C, high V_CE]:::qp
        Q4[Verify entire path inside FBSOA and RBSOA]:::qp
    end

    Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4

    classDef axis fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef r1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef r2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef r3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef r4 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef qp fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading guide:** The trajectory `Q1 → Q2 → Q3 → Q4` is the load line of the transistor during one switching cycle. It begins at `Q1` (saturated ON), travels diagonally through the active region (which must lie inside the SOA), and ends at `Q3` (OFF, blocking the full supply). If *any* point on this trajectory leaves the SOA box, the device will be destroyed.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid ASCII-Style Decision Flow — Design Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Define V_CC and I_load]:::start
    S1{Is V_CE_max <= V_CEO_sus?}:::gate
    S2[Voltage OK - Continue]:::ok
    S3[STOP: Device will avalanche - select higher V_CEO]:::fail
    S4{Is I_C_max within DC rating?}:::gate
    S5[Current OK - Continue]:::ok
    S6[STOP: Use IGBT or parallel devices]:::fail
    S7{Compute P_D = I_C x V_CE at worst-case point}:::calc
    S8{P_D <= P_D_max at T_C actual?}:::gate
    S9[Thermal OK - Continue]:::ok
    S10[STOP: Improve heatsink or reduce switching freq]:::fail
    S11{Does load line cross secondary-breakdown curve?}:::gate
    S12[Safe - PASS: Design Accepted]:::ok
    S13[STOP: Add snubber or reduce switching speed]:::fail

    S0 --> S1
    S1 -- YES --> S2 --> S4
    S1 -- NO --> S3
    S4 -- YES --> S5 --> S7
    S4 -- NO --> S6
    S7 --> S8
    S8 -- YES --> S9 --> S11
    S8 -- NO --> S10
    S11 -- NO --> S12
    S11 -- YES --> S13

    classDef start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef gate fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef calc fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef ok fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef fail fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading guide:** A KTU examiner's flowchart for validating a switching design. The four `gate` diamonds correspond exactly to the four SOA boundaries derived in Section 3.3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define the Safe Operating Area (SOA) of a power transistor. Mention the four limits that define it.

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 | Module 1 | 3 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** of a power transistor is the region on the $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ plane within which the device can be operated without suffering permanent damage, defined for given values of case temperature, pulse duration, and base bias.
>
> The four limits are:
> 1. **Maximum collector current limit** ($I_{C(max)}$) — bond-wire fusing limit.
> 2. **Maximum collector–emitter voltage limit** ($V_{CEO(sus)}$) — avalanche breakdown limit.
> 3. **Maximum power dissipation hyperbola** ($P_{D(max)}$) — thermal limit.
> 4. **Secondary breakdown limit** — thermal-runaway / current-crowding limit (only in FBSOA).

---

### Q2. Why is the Reverse Bias SOA (RBSOA) larger than the Forward Bias SOA (FBSOA)?

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | Module 1 | 3 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> In **reverse-bias turn-off**, the base–emitter junction is reverse-biased, which extracts carriers from the active region and prevents the formation of current-crowding hot-spots. Consequently, the **secondary-breakdown limit disappears**, leaving only the rectangular envelope bounded by $I_{C(max)}$ and $V_{CEO(sus)}$. In **forward bias**, however, the base-emitter is forward-biased and current crowding can lead to thermal-runaway secondary breakdown, which carves away a large portion of the upper-right region of the SOA. Hence RBSOA > FBSOA in area.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students frequently **omit secondary breakdown** while drawing the FBSOA, drawing only a clean rectangle. This costs **3 marks** outright. Always sketch the secondary-breakdown curve sweeping down from the top-right.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Students forget to **mention pulse width** ($t_p$) and **case temperature** ($T_C$) as the conditions under which the SOA is defined. A "12-mark" answer without these qualifications is capped at **9 marks**.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** Confusing **FBSOA** with **RBSOA** during turn-off analysis is a recurring error. Turn-off trajectories use **RBSOA**, not FBSOA.

---

### Question A — Module 1 Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 14 marks]**

**Question:**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Forward Bias Safe Operating Area (FBSOA)** of a power BJT. List and justify each of its four boundaries. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the constant-power dissipation hyperbola of a power device from the thermal-resistance model and explain how **case-temperature derating** modifies the FBSOA. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The FBSOA is plotted on **log–log axes** with $V_{CE}$ on the horizontal and $I_C$ on the vertical. It is the envelope formed by the **minimum** of four boundaries:

1. **Bond-wire / current limit** — drawn as the top horizontal line at $I_C = I_{C(max)}$. Caused by electromigration in the top metallization; absolute and independent of $V_{CE}$. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Power-dissipation hyperbola** $I_C \cdot V_{CE} = P_{D(max)}$ — drawn as a straight line of slope $-1$ on log–log axes from the corner $(V_{CE}, I_C) = (V_{CE(sat)}, I_{C(max)})$. Limits the maximum instantaneous power. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Secondary-breakdown locus** — a curve of slope approximately $-1$ (sometimes steeper) that cuts the rectangle from upper-right to lower-mid, sweeping *inside* the power hyperbola. Caused by current crowding and thermal positive feedback. *(2 marks)*

4. **Voltage-breakdown limit** — drawn as the right-most vertical line at $V_{CE} = V_{CEO(sus)}$. Caused by avalanche of the base–collector junction when the base is open. *(1 mark)*

*Sketch (1 mark):* Log-log axes with the four labeled curves and the shaded safe region enclosed by their minimum.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation** — starting from thermal conduction:

$$
P_D = \frac{T_J - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}}
$$

For $T_J = T_{J(max)}$ and $P_D = I_C V_{CE}$:

$$
I_C V_{CE} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_C}{R_{\theta JC}} = P_{D(max)}(T_C)
$$

This is the **constant-power hyperbola** $I_C = P_{D(max)}(T_C) / V_{CE}$. *(3 marks for derivation)*

**Derating with case temperature:** As $T_C$ rises (e.g., hotter heatsink), $T_{J(max)} - T_C$ decreases linearly, so $P_{D(max)}$ decreases linearly. The hyperbola moves **downward**: at every $V_{CE}$ the allowed $I_C$ shrinks. At $T_C = T_{J(max)}$, the hyperbola collapses to the $V_{CE}$-axis, meaning zero power is allowed. *(2 marks)*

**Engineering implication:** This is why **heatsink selection** is mandatory in power electronics; without it, the device cannot dissipate even a few watts without exceeding $T_{J(max)}$. *(2 marks)*

---

### Question B — Module 1 Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1 | 14 marks]**

**Question:**
**(a)** Compare the **FBSOA** and **RBSOA** of a power BJT. Explain why RBSOA is important during **inductive-load turn-off** and sketch its trajectory on the SOA diagram. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **pulsed-SOA expansion factor** and explain why a device rated for $10\,\text{A}$ DC can carry $200\,\text{A}$ for $1\,\mu s$. Use a numerical example with $R_{\theta JC} = 1^{\circ}\text{C/W}$, $T_{J(max)} = 150^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_C = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

| Feature | FBSOA | RBSOA |
|---|---|---|
| Active during | Turn-on | Turn-off |
| Secondary breakdown present? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Shape | Hyperbola with SB knee | Rectangle |

*(2 marks for comparison)*

**Inductive turn-off importance:** When an inductive load (motor winding, relay coil, solenoid) is switched off, the inductor forces the current to continue flowing while the device voltage rises rapidly to the DC bus voltage. The transistor is forced to **simultaneously** support high $V_{CE}$ and high $I_C$ — a stress that falls squarely on the **RBSOA**. Failure to verify this trajectory is the most common cause of BJT destruction in motor drives. *(3 marks)*

**Sketch description:** A rectangular box bounded by $I_{C(max)}$ horizontally and $V_{CEO(sus)}$ vertically; no SB curve; the turn-off load line enters from $(V_{CE(sat)}, I_{load})$ and exits at $(V_{bus}, I_{leakage})$, remaining inside the rectangle. *(2 marks)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation:** From the transient thermal model:

$$
P_{D,pulsed} = \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_C}{Z_{\theta JC}(t_p)}, \quad k(t_p) = \frac{Z_{\theta JC}(t_p)}{R_{\theta JC}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{I_{C,pulsed}}{I_{C,DC}} = \frac{R_{\theta JC}}{Z_{\theta JC}(t_p)} = \frac{1}{k(t_p)}
$$

*(3 marks for derivation)*

**Numerical example:**

Given $R_{\theta JC} = 1^{\circ}\text{C/W}$, $T_{J(max)} - T_C = 125^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$
P_{D,DC} = \frac{125}{1} = 125\,\text{W}
$$

If at $V_{CE} = 5\,\text{V}$, $I_{C,DC} = 125/5 = 25\,\text{A}$. Now for a $1\,\mu s$ pulse, assume $k(1\,\mu s) = 0.05$ (typical from data sheet):

$$
I_{C,pulsed} = \frac{I_{C,DC}}{k(t_p)} = \frac{25}{0.05} = 500\,\text{A}
$$

*(3 marks)* — This is the mechanism that allows $200\,\text{A}$ (or $500\,\text{A}$) pulses for microseconds: the die has not yet heated because the thermal time constant of silicon is much longer than $1\,\mu s$.

**Caveat (1 mark):** The energy $E = I^2 \cdot t$ still delivers heat into the die; it just doesn't have time to spread. At very high pulse currents, the **bond-wire limit** $I_{C(max)}$ still applies absolutely — pulse SOA cannot violate it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Safe Operating Area**

- ✅ **SOA = envelope of (V_CE, I_C) where the device survives** — plotted on log-log axes.
- ✅ **Four limits of FBSOA:** $I_{C(max)}$ (horizontal), $V_{CEO(sus)}$ (vertical), $P_{D(max)}$ hyperbola (slope $-1$), and **secondary-breakdown curve** (slope $\approx -1$, but inward).
- ✅ **RBSOA has NO secondary-breakdown limit** because reverse bias prevents current crowding — it is a clean rectangle.
- ✅ **Pulsed SOA expands** with shorter pulse width because transient thermal impedance $Z_{\theta JC}(t_p) < R_{\theta JC}$.
- ✅ **Derating rule:** $P_{D,max}(T_C) = (T_{J,max} - T_C) / R_{\theta JC}$ — linear in $T_C$.
- ✅ **$V_{CEO(sus)} < V_{CBO}$** because open-base gain lowers breakdown voltage.
- ✅ **Inductive turn-off = RBSOA stress** — most common destruction mechanism in motor drives.
- ✅ **Pulse width $t_p$** and **case temperature $T_C$** must always be specified when quoting an SOA — a standalone "SOA" number is meaningless.
- ✅ **Energy limitation:** Even within pulsed SOA, total pulse energy $E = \int I_C V_{CE}\, dt$ must not exceed the device's **Single-Pulse Avalanche Energy** rating $E_{AS}$.
- ✅ **Practical takeaway:** Never let the load line exit the SOA — design snubbers, gate-drive resistors, and heatsinks to keep the trajectory well inside the envelope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paralleling of Transistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Paralleling of Transistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Paralleling of Transistors** is a power electronics design technique in which two or more discrete power transistors (BJTs, Power MOSFETs, or IGBTs) are connected in a **parallel configuration** at their input, output, and load terminals to deliver a total load current that exceeds the current rating of a single device. The technique exploits the principle of *current sharing*, where each transistor carries a fraction of the total load current, enabling the system to handle high-power applications with improved reliability, redundancy, and thermal distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> Paralleling of transistors is a critical topic in Power Electronic Systems and Power Semiconductor Devices. Students are expected to understand the **need for paralleling**, the **current sharing problem**, the **static and dynamic current sharing mechanisms**, and the **design of ballast/emitter resistors** to prevent thermal runaway.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a team of porters carrying a heavy iron log across a workshop:
- A single porter (single transistor) can carry a maximum of, say, **50 kg**. But the log weighs **150 kg**.
- If three porters lift the log together (one in front, one in the middle, one at the back), each carries roughly **50 kg** and the team easily moves the log.
- However, what if one porter is stronger and lifts **60 kg**, while another, weaker, lifts only **45 kg**? The stronger porter tires faster, may drop his share, or even collapse.
- In real life, you solve this by giving the porters **a rope with some elasticity (a "ballast")** that redistributes the load more evenly.

> This is *exactly* what happens in **paralleling of transistors**:
> - **Porter** = Transistor
> - **Log weight** = Total load current
> - **Elastic rope** = Emitter/Ballast resistor
> - **Rope elasticity** = Resistance value that forces equal current distribution

## 1.3 Why Parallel Transistors? (Engineering Drivers)

| Driving Factor | Engineering Rationale |
|----------------|----------------------|
| **Current Demand** | Load current exceeds single device rating ($I_{C(\text{max})}$) |
| **Cost Efficiency** | Multiple cheaper mid-range devices are often cheaper than one ultra-rated device |
| **Redundancy** | System continues to operate (degraded) if one device fails |
| **Thermal Distribution** | Heat dissipated across multiple heatsinks reduces junction hotspot |
| **Switching Performance** | Distributed layout reduces stray inductance, improving switching speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Anchor Point:** Any 14-mark question in KTU ESE related to paralleling of transistors is almost always tested from the standpoint of *current sharing analysis* and *derivation of emitter balancing resistor*. Master these two and you secure 10+ marks.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Junction Temperature ($T_j$)**: Rated typically at **150 °C** for silicon power BJTs and **175 °C** for power MOSFETs/IGBTs.
- **Thermal Derating Factor**: Typically **0.6 to 0.8** (i.e., devices derated to 60–80% of rated current in parallel operation).
- **V$_{BE}$ Mismatch Tolerance**: Between matched pairs, $\Delta V_{BE} \le 5$ mV at $I_C = 1$ A.
- **$h_{FE}$ (DC Current Gain) Spread**: Spec sheets guarantee a range (e.g., **15–75** at rated $I_C$).
- **Ballast Resistor Typical Range**: **0.1 Ω to 1 Ω** for power BJTs, **0.05 Ω to 0.5 Ω** for MOSFETs (using $R_{DS(on)}$ as natural ballast).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

### Step 1 — Identifying the Need
A single power transistor is rated for a maximum continuous collector current $I_{C(\text{max})}$ and a maximum power dissipation $P_{D(\text{max})}$. When the load demand $I_L > I_{C(\text{max})}$, a **single device is insufficient**.

### Step 2 — Selecting the Topology
Engineers select $N$ identical transistors and connect them in parallel such that:
- All bases (or gates) are tied together.
- All collectors (or drains) are tied together.
- All emitters (or sources) are tied together — *ideally directly*, but in practice through small balancing resistors.

### Step 3 — Recognizing the Current Sharing Problem
Even when transistors are from the same manufacturing batch ("matched pair"), there are intrinsic parameter mismatches:

1. **Base-Emitter Voltage Mismatch ($\Delta V_{BE}$)**
   Caused by process variations. Even a 5 mV difference between two BJTs causes significantly unequal current sharing.

2. **DC Current Gain Mismatch ($\Delta h_{FE}$)**
   Different $\beta$ values mean different base currents are required to deliver the same collector current.

3. **On-State Resistance Mismatch ($\Delta R_{DS(on)}$ for MOSFETs)**
   For MOSFETs, the inherent $R_{DS(on)}$ acts as a self-ballast. Lower $R_{DS(on)}$ transistor takes more current.

4. **Thermal Mismatch (Positive Feedback Loop)**
   - The transistor carrying more current dissipates more heat.
   - Higher junction temperature reduces $V_{BE}$ (for BJT: $V_{BE}$ has a *negative* temperature coefficient of approximately **−2 mV/°C**).
   - Lower $V_{BE}$ at higher temperature means the transistor turns ON *more*, taking *more* current.
   - This is **thermal runaway** — a destructive positive feedback loop.

### Step 4 — Implementing the Solution
The textbook solution for BJTs is the **emitter ballast resistor** (also called *current-sharing resistor* or *equalizing resistor*). For MOSFETs, $R_{DS(on)}$ provides *self-ballasting* since it has a *positive* temperature coefficient.

### Step 5 — Verifying Thermal Stability
After equalization, calculate the junction temperature rise for each transistor. Confirm that the device with the highest $T_j$ stays below the rated limit (typically 150 °C or 175 °C).

## 2.2 The Static Current Sharing Equation (Two BJT Case)

Consider two BJTs $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ with emitters connected through resistors $R_{E1}$ and $R_{E2}$ to a common emitter rail.

The collector currents are governed by the Ebers–Moll relation:

$$I_C = I_S \cdot e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

For two transistors sharing the same base voltage $V_B$ and a common emitter node, the current ratio is:

$$\frac{I_{C1}}{I_{C2}} = e^{(V_{BE1} - V_{BE2})/V_T}$$

When emitter resistors $R_{E1}$ and $R_{E2}$ are inserted, the *effective* base-to-emitter voltage is reduced by the drop across the resistor:

$$V_{BE,\text{eff},i} = V_B - I_{Ei} R_{Ei}$$

This negative feedback forces $I_{C1} \approx I_{C2}$ provided $R_E$ is large enough.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the SINGLE most important reference for this topic in your KTU exam.** Memorize every entry.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | $I_L = \sum_{i=1}^{N} I_{Ci}$ | Total load current is sum of individual collector currents | A | KCL at the parallel node |
| 2 | $\frac{I_{C1}}{I_{C2}} = e^{\Delta V_{BE}/V_T}$ | Current ratio for unmatched BJTs (no $R_E$) | dimensionless | $V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K |
| 3 | $R_{E(\text{min})} = \frac{V_T \cdot (m - 1)}{I_{C2}}$ | Minimum emitter resistor for current sharing | Ω | $m$ = desired current sharing ratio $I_{C1}/I_{C2}$ |
| 4 | $R_{E(\text{min})} = \frac{n \cdot V_T \cdot (m-1)}{I_L}$ | Generalized for N parallel BJTs with equal sharing | Ω | $n$ = number of transistors |
| 5 | $\Delta V_{BE} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_{C1}}{I_{C2}}\right)$ | V$_{BE}$ difference from current mismatch | V | At 300 K, $V_T = 26$ mV |
| 6 | $T_C = \frac{\Delta V_{BE}}{-2 \text{ mV/°C}}$ | Temperature coefficient correction (negative TC for BJT) | °C | Critical for thermal runaway analysis |
| 7 | $P_{D(\text{total})} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_{CE(sat),i} \cdot I_{Ci}$ | Total power dissipation in parallel array | W | Used for heatsink design |
| 8 | $R_{\theta JA(\text{parallel})} \approx \frac{R_{\theta JA}}{N}$ | Thermal resistance for $N$ identical devices on common heatsink | °C/W | Valid only with perfect thermal coupling |
| 9 | $\Delta I_C = \frac{\Delta V_{BE}}{R_E + r_e}$ | Current mismatch with emitter resistor $R_E$ | A | $r_e = V_T / I_E$ is the small-signal emitter resistance |
| 10 | $I_{D(\text{share})} = \frac{V_{DS} - V_{DS(\text{on})i}}{R_{DS(\text{on})i}}$ | MOSFET current sharing (self-ballasted by $R_{DS(\text{on})}$) | A | Use for MOSFET parallel design |

> **Critical Substitution:** At room temperature (**$T = 300$ K**), the thermal voltage is $V_T = kT/q = \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$. KTU examiners frequently give $T = 27$ °C as 300 K. Watch for $T = 323$ K where $V_T = 27.8$ mV.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is paralleling of transistors used in real engineering?**
> - **Inverters** for solar PV and industrial drives (multiple IGBT modules in parallel).
> - **UPS Systems** delivering hundreds of amperes.
> - **Traction Motor Drives** in electric locomotives.
> - **Welding Power Supplies** requiring >1000 A pulsed output.
> - **HVDC Converter Stations** with thousands of series-parallel IGBTs.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Current Sharing Ratio for Two Unmatched BJTs (No Ballast Resistor)

**Given:** Two BJTs $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are in parallel with common base and common collector, emitters tied directly. The base–emitter voltages differ by a small amount $\Delta V_{BE} = V_{BE1} - V_{BE2}$.

**Find:** The ratio $I_{C1}/I_{C2}$ in terms of $\Delta V_{BE}$.

**Derivation:**

The collector current of a BJT in the forward-active region is given by the Ebers–Moll equation:

$$I_{C1} = I_{S1} \cdot e^{V_{BE1}/V_T}$$

$$I_{C2} = I_{S2} \cdot e^{V_{BE2}/V_T}$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{I_{C1}}{I_{C2}} = \frac{I_{S1}}{I_{S2}} \cdot e^{(V_{BE1} - V_{BE2})/V_T}$$

Assuming the saturation currents are approximately equal (matched transistors, $I_{S1} \approx I_{S2}$):

$$\frac{I_{C1}}{I_{C2}} \approx e^{\Delta V_{BE}/V_T}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Even a *small* mismatch of $\Delta V_{BE} = 26$ mV at room temperature gives a current ratio of $e^{1} \approx 2.72$. This is a **173% imbalance** — clearly unacceptable for parallel operation without compensation.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Required Emitter Ballast Resistor for Two BJTs

**Given:** Two BJTs in parallel must share a total load current $I_L$ such that the current in the "hot" transistor $I_{C1}$ does not exceed $m \cdot I_{C2}$ (i.e., $I_{C1}/I_{C2} = m$, where $m$ is the maximum permissible current sharing ratio, typically 1.1 to 1.3).

**Find:** The minimum emitter resistor $R_E$ that guarantees this sharing.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Current conservation:

$$I_L = I_{C1} + I_{C2} \quad \text{where} \quad I_{C1} = m \cdot I_{C2}$$

Step 2 — Solve for $I_{C2}$:

$$I_{C2} = \frac{I_L}{1 + m}$$

Step 3 — Write the base–emitter loop equations. The common base voltage $V_B$ equals the sum of the effective $V_{BE}$ and the emitter resistor drop:

$$V_B = V_{BE1} + I_{E1} R_E \approx V_{BE1} + I_{C1} R_E \quad \text{(since } I_E \approx I_C \text{ for high } \beta\text{)}$$

$$V_B = V_{BE2} + I_{C2} R_E$$

Step 4 — Subtract the two equations to eliminate $V_B$:

$$V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} + (I_{C1} - I_{C2}) R_E = 0$$

$$\Delta V_{BE} = (I_{C2} - I_{C1}) R_E = -I_{C2}(m - 1) R_E$$

Step 5 — Use the Ebers–Moll ratio to substitute $\Delta V_{BE}$:

$$\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln\left(\frac{I_{C1}}{I_{C2}}\right) = V_T \ln(m)$$

Step 6 — Equate and solve for $R_E$:

$$V_T \ln(m) = -I_{C2}(m - 1) R_E$$

$$R_E = \frac{V_T \ln(m)}{I_{C2}(m - 1)}$$

Substituting $I_{C2} = I_L / (1+m)$:

$$\boxed{R_E = \frac{(1+m) \cdot V_T \ln(m)}{I_L (m - 1)}}$$

> This is the **single most important KTU formula** for this topic. Examiners love asking for the value of $R_E$ given $I_L$, $m$, and $T$.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Generalized Formula for N Parallel BJTs with Equal Current Sharing

**Given:** $N$ BJTs in parallel, each carrying $I_{Ci} = I_L / N$, with the worst-case transistor taking at most $m$ times the average current.

**Find:** The minimum ballast resistance $R_E$ per device.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Average current per transistor:

$$I_{C,\text{avg}} = \frac{I_L}{N}$$

Step 2 — Worst-case current: $I_{C1} = m \cdot I_{C,\text{avg}}$

Step 3 — Using the same loop analysis as in Derivation II:

$$R_E = \frac{V_T \ln(m)}{I_{C2}(m - 1)} = \frac{V_T \ln(m)}{(I_L/N)(m - 1)}$$

$$\boxed{R_E = \frac{N \cdot V_T \ln(m)}{I_L (m - 1)}}$$

> **Comparison with two-transistor case:** This formula reduces to Derivation II when $N = 1$ (in the per-device sense) or can be adapted as $R_E = \frac{(1+m) \cdot V_T \ln(m)}{I_L (m-1)}$ for $N = 2$.

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Thermal Runaway Analysis

**Given:** A BJT in parallel with another BJT, with emitter resistor $R_E$. Initial temperature $T_1$, with one transistor hotter by $\Delta T$.

**Find:** Condition for thermal stability.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — The base–emitter voltage has a negative temperature coefficient:

$$\frac{dV_{BE}}{dT} = -2 \text{ mV/°C}$$

Step 2 — The collector current depends on $V_{BE}$ and $R_E$:

$$I_C = \frac{V_B - V_{BE}}{R_E + r_e}$$

Step 3 — The temperature derivative of $I_C$ is:

$$\frac{dI_C}{dT} = \frac{1}{R_E + r_e}\left(-\frac{dV_{BE}}{dT}\right) = \frac{2 \text{ mV/°C}}{R_E + r_e}$$

Step 4 — For thermal stability, the *power dissipation feedback* must be negative. A sufficient condition is:

$$\boxed{R_E \ge \frac{V_T}{I_C} \cdot \frac{\theta_{JA} \cdot P_D}{T_{j(\text{max})} - T_A} - r_e}$$

In practice, KTU exams accept the simpler condition: **$R_E$ must be large enough to make $\frac{dI_C}{dT}$ small enough that the device with higher $T_j$ does not take exponentially more current.**

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** Two identical BJTs are connected in parallel to deliver a total load current of $I_L = 20$ A. The base–emitter voltage mismatch is $\Delta V_{BE} = 10$ mV. The maximum permissible current imbalance ratio is $m = 1.2$. Thermal voltage $V_T = 26$ mV. Calculate the minimum emitter ballast resistor required.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Current per transistor at average:

$$I_{C2} = \frac{I_L}{1 + m} = \frac{20}{2.2} = 9.09 \text{ A}$$

Step 2 — Current mismatch tolerated:

$$I_{C1} - I_{C2} = (m - 1) \cdot I_{C2} = 0.2 \times 9.09 = 1.82 \text{ A}$$

Step 3 — Required voltage drop across $R_E$ to absorb the $\Delta V_{BE}$ imbalance:

$$V_T \ln(m) = 0.026 \times \ln(1.2) = 0.026 \times 0.1823 = 4.74 \text{ mV}$$

Step 4 — Solve for $R_E$:

$$R_E = \frac{V_T \ln(m)}{I_{C2}(m - 1)} = \frac{0.00474}{9.09 \times 0.2} = \frac{0.00474}{1.818} = 2.61 \text{ mΩ}$$

Step 5 — Sanity check (alternative formula):

$$R_E = \frac{(1+m) V_T \ln(m)}{I_L (m-1)} = \frac{2.2 \times 0.00474}{20 \times 0.2} = \frac{0.01043}{4} = 2.61 \text{ mΩ}$$

$$\boxed{R_E \approx 2.61 \text{ mΩ}}$$

> **Note:** For real high-current applications, $R_E$ is often a precision low-ohm resistor (e.g., 5 mΩ, 10 W) made from manganin or constantan wire.

## 3.6 Comparison: BJT vs MOSFET Parallel Behavior

| Parameter | BJT | Power MOSFET |
|-----------|-----|--------------|
| **Self-ballasting?** | No (negative TC of $V_{BE}$) | Yes (positive TC of $R_{DS(on)}$) |
| **Required external $R_E$?** | Yes, mandatory | Not mandatory (but recommended) |
| **Current sharing driver** | $\Delta V_{BE}$ | $\Delta R_{DS(on)}$ |
| **Thermal runaway risk** | High | Low (self-correcting) |
| **Switching speed mismatch** | Severe (storage time variation) | Mild (input capacitance variation) |
| **Gate drive complexity** | Base current required | Voltage driven (simple) |
