# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Parallel Transistor Array with Ballast Resistors

> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the topology, signal flow, and current paths of a two-transistor parallel BJT module with emitter ballast resistors. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** representation, not a physical schematic.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Base Drive Signal Vb] --> B[Common Base Node]
    B --> BJT1[Q1 BJT Collector-Emitter]
    B --> BJT2[Q2 BJT Collector-Emitter]
    BJT1 --> RE1[RE1 Ballast Resistor 2.6 milliohm]
    BJT2 --> RE2[RE2 Ballast Resistor 2.6 milliohm]
    RE1 --> ECOM[Common Emitter Rail]
    RE2 --> ECOM
    ECOM --> LOAD[Load IL equals IC1 plus IC2]
    LOAD --> VCC[DC Supply VCC]
    HS1[Heatsink 1 Thermal Coupling] --- BJT1
    HS2[Heatsink 2 Thermal Coupling] --- BJT2
    HS1 --- HS2
    HS1 --> T1[Junction Temperature Tj1]
    HS2 --> T2[Junction Temperature Tj2]
    T1 --> FB[Negative Feedback via VBE Temperature Coefficient]
    T2 --> FB
    FB --> CTRL[Current Sharing Controller]
    CTRL --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for KTU exams:**
> 1. The arrows show **current flow direction** and **control signal flow**.
> 2. The `---` connections indicate **thermal coupling** (not electrical).
> 3. The negative feedback path `T1 -> FB -> CTRL -> A` represents **thermal runaway protection** — the controller reduces base drive if junction temperature rises.

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Static and Dynamic Current Sharing

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as DC Source VCC
    participant Q1 as BJT Q1 with RE1
    participant Q2 as BJT Q2 with RE2
    participant LD as Load IL
    participant TH as Thermal Loop

    SRC->>Q1: Supplies IC1 to collector
    SRC->>Q2: Supplies IC2 to collector
    Q1->>LD: Delivers IC1 minus VCE sat
    Q2->>LD: Delivers IC2 minus VCE sat
    Note over Q1,Q2: Without RE mismatch causes IC1 much greater than IC2
    Q1->>TH: Dissipates PC1 equals IC1 times VCE
    TH-->>Q1: Tj1 rises VBE1 falls IC1 rises
    Q2->>TH: Dissipates PC2 equals IC2 times VCE
    TH-->>Q2: Tj2 stable since IC2 is lower
    Note over Q1: Thermal runaway risk WITHOUT RE
    Note over Q1,Q2: WITH RE installed the emitter drop compensates delta VBE
    Q1->>Q1: IC1 reduced due to larger drop across RE1
    Q2->>Q2: IC2 increases slightly
    Q1->>LD: IC1 approximately equal to IC2
    Q2->>LD: IC1 approximately equal to IC2
    TH-->>Q1: Tj1 stabilizes
    TH-->>Q2: Tj2 stabilizes
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing Paralleling Strategy

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Need to parallel transistors] --> Q1{Device Type?}
    Q1 -->|BJT| BJT[BJT Paralleling]
    Q1 -->|MOSFET| MOSFET[MOSFET Paralleling]
    Q1 -->|IGBT| IGBT[IGBT Paralleling]
    BJT --> B1{Delta VBE known?}
    B1 -->|Yes| B2[Calculate RE from formula]
    B1 -->|No| B3[Assume worst case 10 mV]
    B3 --> B2
    B2 --> B4[Use precision low ohm resistor]
    B4 --> B5[Mount on common heatsink]
    B5 --> B6[Verify thermal stability]
    MOSFET --> M1{Is R_DS on sufficient?}
    M1 -->|Yes| M2[Direct parallel OK]
    M1 -->|No| M3[Add small source resistor]
    M2 --> M4[Use matched gate resistors]
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M5[Synchronize gate drive timing]
    IGBT --> I1[Same as MOSFET approach]
    I1 --> I2[Watch for thermal tail current mismatch]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q:** Define the term "paralleling of transistors." Why is it necessary in power electronic circuits?

**Model Answer:**

Paralleling of transistors is the technique of connecting two or more power transistors (BJT/MOSFET/IGBT) in such a way that all their input terminals, output terminals, and load terminals are tied together, so that the total load current is shared among the devices. Each transistor carries a fraction of the total current.

It is necessary because:
1. **Current demand exceeds rating:** A single transistor has a maximum collector/drain current $I_{C(\text{max})}$. For high-power applications (e.g., motor drives, inverters), the load current often exceeds this rating.
2. **Power dissipation:** Distributing current across multiple devices reduces per-device dissipation and simplifies thermal management.
3. **Cost-effectiveness:** Paralleling standard devices is cheaper than procuring a custom ultra-high-current device.
4. **Redundancy:** Failure of one device does not cause immediate system failure.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any two justifications)*

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q:** What is thermal runaway? How does the emitter ballast resistor prevent it in parallel BJT circuits?

**Model Answer:**

Thermal runaway is a positive feedback phenomenon in BJTs where an increase in junction temperature causes a decrease in $V_{BE}$ (at a rate of approximately **−2 mV/°C**), which in turn increases the collector current, leading to further power dissipation and further temperature rise. If unchecked, this loop continues until the device is destroyed.

The emitter ballast resistor $R_E$ introduces **negative current feedback**: if $I_C$ tends to increase, the voltage drop $I_E R_E$ across $R_E$ increases, which reduces $V_{BE}$ and counteracts the rise in $I_C$. This breaks the positive feedback loop and stabilizes the operating point.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition of thermal runaway + 1 mark for negative TC of V_BE + 1 mark for R_E feedback mechanism)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students confuse *paralleling* with *cascading*. Paralleling is collector-to-collector and emitter-to-emitter; cascading is the output of one stage driving the input of the next.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert $\Delta V_{BE}$ correctly when using $V_T = 26$ mV (i.e., mixing mV and V in the same calculation). Always write units explicitly.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Assuming $I_C = I_E$. Strictly, $I_E = I_C + I_B = I_C(1 + 1/\beta)$. Use $I_E \approx I_C$ only for $\beta > 50$.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing the BJT case (negative TC) with the MOSFET case (positive TC). MOSFETs are *self-ballasted*; BJTs require external $R_E$.
> 5. **Mark Loss Tip:** Always draw the equivalent circuit and label $V_{BE1}, V_{BE2}, R_E$ clearly. Examiners award partial marks for clear diagrams.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of two BJTs connected in parallel with emitter balancing resistors. Derive the expression for the minimum value of the emitter resistance required to equalize the currents within a specified ratio. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Two identical power BJTs are connected in parallel to supply a load current of $I_L = 30$ A. The base–emitter voltage mismatch is $\Delta V_{BE} = 15$ mV. The maximum permissible current imbalance ratio is $m = 1.25$. Thermal voltage $V_T = 26$ mV. Calculate: **(i)** the current in each transistor, **(ii)** the minimum emitter ballast resistor required, and **(iii)** the power dissipated in each ballast resistor. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Circuit Operation:**

When two BJTs are connected directly in parallel, parameter mismatches in $V_{BE}$ and $h_{FE}$ cause unequal current sharing. To equalize currents, small resistors $R_{E1}$ and $R_{E2}$ are inserted in series with each emitter. These resistors introduce *local negative feedback*: any tendency for one transistor to take more current produces a larger voltage drop across its $R_E$, reducing its effective $V_{BE}$ and restoring balance.

**Derivation:**

Let the two BJTs be $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ with base tied to common voltage $V_B$, collector to common $V_{CC}$, and emitter to common ground via $R_{E1}$ and $R_{E2}$.

Using Ebers–Moll:

$$I_{C1} = I_S e^{V_{BE1}/V_T}, \quad I_{C2} = I_S e^{V_{BE2}/V_T}$$

The voltage loop gives:

$$V_{BE1} + I_{C1} R_E = V_{BE2} + I_{C2} R_E$$

$$V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} = (I_{C2} - I_{C1}) R_E$$

Using $V_{BE} = V_T \ln(I_C/I_S)$:

$$V_T \ln\left(\frac{I_{C1}}{I_{C2}}\right) = (I_{C2} - I_{C1}) R_E$$

Setting $I_{C1}/I_{C2} = m$ and $I_{C1} + I_{C2} = I_L$, solving for $R_E$:

$$R_{E(\text{min})} = \frac{V_T \ln(m)}{I_{C2}(m-1)} = \frac{(1+m) \cdot V_T \ln(m)}{I_L (m-1)}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for derivation, 2 for final formula)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $I_L = 30$ A, $\Delta V_{BE} = 15$ mV, $m = 1.25$, $V_T = 26$ mV.

**(i) Current in each transistor:**

$$I_{C2} = \frac{I_L}{1 + m} = \frac{30}{2.25} = 13.33 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{C1} = m \cdot I_{C2} = 1.25 \times 13.33 = 16.67 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Minimum emitter ballast resistor:**

$$V_T \ln(m) = 0.026 \times \ln(1.25) = 0.026 \times 0.2231 = 5.80 \text{ mV}$$

$$R_E = \frac{(1+m) \cdot V_T \ln(m)}{I_L (m-1)} = \frac{2.25 \times 0.00580}{30 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.01305}{7.5} = 1.74 \text{ mΩ}$$

**(iii) Power dissipated in each ballast resistor:**

For $Q_1$ (carrying 16.67 A):
$$P_{R_{E1}} = I_{C1}^2 \cdot R_E = (16.67)^2 \times 0.00174 = 0.484 \text{ W}$$

For $Q_2$ (carrying 13.33 A):
$$P_{R_{E2}} = I_{C2}^2 \cdot R_E = (13.33)^2 \times 0.00174 = 0.310 \text{ W}$$

Total power in ballast resistors:
$$P_{R_E,\text{total}} = 0.484 + 0.310 = 0.794 \text{ W}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for part (i), 3 for part (ii), 2 for part (iii))*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Compare the paralleling behavior of BJTs and power MOSFETs. Why are MOSFETs considered easier to parallel? Explain with relevant equations. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A power electronic inverter requires a total current of $I_L = 100$ A. Four identical MOSFETs are to be connected in parallel. The on-state resistance of each MOSFET is $R_{DS(on)} = 0.05$ Ω with a positive temperature coefficient of $+0.001$ Ω/°C. Calculate: **(i)** the current sharing ratio if all four MOSFETs are at the same temperature, and **(ii)** the current sharing ratio when MOSFET 1 is 20 °C hotter than the others. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Aspect | BJT | Power MOSFET |
|--------|-----|--------------|
| Controlling parameter | $V_{BE}$ (negative TC: −2 mV/°C) | $R_{DS(on)}$ (positive TC: +0.001 to +0.005 Ω/°C) |
| Negative feedback | Needs external $R_E$ | Self-ballasting by $R_{DS(on)}$ |
| Thermal runaway | High risk (positive feedback via $V_{BE}$ TC) | Low risk (negative feedback via $R_{DS(on)}$ TC) |
| Switching losses | Significant storage time mismatch | Mostly capacitive — easier to match |
| Current sharing driver | $\Delta V_{BE}$ | $\Delta R_{DS(on)}$ |

**Equations:**

BJT current ratio (no $R_E$):
$$\frac{I_{C1}}{I_{C2}} = e^{\Delta V_{BE}/V_T}$$

MOSFET current ratio (self-ballasted):
$$\frac{I_{D1}}{I_{D2}} = \frac{R_{DS(on),2}}{R_{DS(on),1}}$$

For MOSFETs, when device 1 heats up, its $R_{DS(on)}$ *increases*, which forces it to take *less* current — automatically redistributing to the cooler device. This inherent stability is why MOSFETs are easier to parallel.

*(7 marks: 3 for table, 2 for equations, 2 for explanation)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $I_L = 100$ A, $N = 4$ MOSFETs, $R_{DS(on),i} = 0.05$ Ω at $T_0$, TC = +0.001 Ω/°C.

**(i) Current sharing at uniform temperature:**

If all four MOSFETs are identical and at the same temperature, they share current equally:

$$I_{D1} = I_{D2} = I_{D3} = I_{D4} = \frac{I_L}{N} = \frac{100}{4} = 25 \text{ A}$$

Current sharing ratio: $1 : 1 : 1 : 1$.

**(ii) Current sharing with MOSFET 1 at 20 °C higher temperature:**

New $R_{DS(on)}$ for MOSFET 1:
$$R_{DS(on),1}' = 0.05 + 0.001 \times 20 = 0.05 + 0.02 = 0.07 \text{ Ω}$$

The other three MOSFETs remain at $0.05$ Ω.

Since MOSFETs are in parallel, the voltage across them is the same:

$$V_{DS} = I_{D1} \cdot R_{DS(on),1}' = I_{D2} \cdot R_{DS(on),2} = \ldots$$

Total current:
$$I_L = I_{D1} + 3 I_{D2} = \frac{V_{DS}}{0.07} + 3 \cdot \frac{V_{DS}}{0.05} = 100 \text{ A}$$

$$V_{DS} \left(\frac{1}{0.07} + \frac{3}{0.05}\right) = 100$$

$$V_{DS} (14.286 + 60) = 100$$

$$V_{DS} = \frac{100}{74.286} = 1.346 \text{ V}$$

Currents:
$$I_{D1} = \frac{1.346}{0.07} = 19.23 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{D2} = I_{D3} = I_{D4} = \frac{1.346}{0.05} = 26.92 \text{ A}$$

Verification: $19.23 + 3 \times 26.92 = 19.23 + 80.77 = 100$ A ✓

Current sharing ratio:
$$I_{D1} : I_{D2} : I_{D3} : I_{D4} = 19.23 : 26.92 : 26.92 : 26.92 \approx 1 : 1.4 : 1.4 : 1.4$$

> **Conclusion:** The hotter MOSFET 1 takes *less* current (19.23 A) while the cooler MOSFETs share the rest (26.92 A each). This is the **self-ballasting action** of MOSFETs and demonstrates their thermal stability.

*(7 marks: 2 for part (i), 4 for part (ii), 1 for verification and conclusion)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- [x] **Definition:** Paralleling = tying input, output, and load terminals of multiple transistors to share current beyond a single device's rating.
- [x] **Purpose:** Higher current, redundancy, better thermal distribution, cost savings.
- [x] **Problem:** $\Delta V_{BE}$ and $\Delta h_{FE}$ cause current mismatch; thermal runaway worsens it.
- [x] **BJT solution:** External emitter ballast resistor $R_E$ introduces negative feedback.
- [x] **MOSFET solution:** Self-ballasting via $R_{DS(on)}$ positive temperature coefficient — no external resistor needed in most cases.
- [x] **Key Formula 1:** $I_{C1}/I_{C2} = e^{\Delta V_{BE}/V_T}$ (unmatched BJT, no $R_E$).
- [x] **Key Formula 2:** $R_E = (1+m) \cdot V_T \ln(m) / [I_L(m-1)]$ (two-BJT case with $m$ = current sharing ratio).
- [x] **Key Formula 3:** $R_E = N \cdot V_T \ln(m) / [I_L(m-1)]$ (generalized N-BJT case).
- [x] **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = 26$ mV at $T = 300$ K. Always verify temperature.
- [x] **V_BE TC:** −2 mV/°C (BJT). **R_DS(on) TC:** +0.001 to +0.005 Ω/°C (MOSFET).
- [x] **KTU Pitfall:** For two-transistor case, remember $I_{C2} = I_L/(1+m)$, *not* $I_L/2$.
- [x] **Power Loss in $R_E$:** Always compute $P = I^2 R$ for ballast resistor rating.
- [x] **Diagrams:** Always draw the parallel configuration with $R_E$ in KTU exams — partial marks awarded even if derivation is incomplete.
- [x] **Symmetry:** $R_{E1} = R_{E2}$ is the design choice that gives $I_{C1} \approx I_{C2}$.
- [x] **Application Areas:** Solar inverters, UPS, motor drives, HVDC, welding supplies.
- [x] **Real-World Tip:** BJT paralleling is now rare; modern designs use MOSFET/IGBT paralleling with intelligent gate drivers.

---

> **END OF KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 NOTES — Paralleling of Transistors | PCBRT503 | Module 1**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thyristor Family: SCRs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thyristor Family: SCRs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)** is a four-layer (P-N-P-N), three-junction, three-terminal **bistable** power semiconductor device that acts as a controllable switch. It is the most fundamental and historically significant member of the **thyristor family**. The device remains in the **OFF (blocking) state** until a positive gate signal is applied; once triggered, it **latches ON** and conducts like a diode, regardless of the gate signal, until the anode current falls below a critical threshold.

**Key Terminal Identification:**
- **Anode (A):** Positive terminal connected to the outer P-layer.
- **Cathode (K):** Negative terminal connected to the outer N-layer.
- **Gate (G):** Control terminal connected to the inner P-layer.

**Standard Schematic Symbol:**
```
        A (Anode)
        │
       ─┴─
      ─┼─┐
     ─┼─ │  ← Gate (G) terminal
      ─┼─┘
       ─┬─
        │
        K (Cathode)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SCRs are categorized as "Semi-controlled" devices because while turn-ON is controlled by the gate, turn-OFF is controlled by the external circuit (natural or forced commutation).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Light Switch"

Imagine a standard wall light switch in your room that has been modified:

1. **The Push-Start Engine Analogy:** The SCR behaves like a car engine with a **self-sustaining ignition**. The gate signal is the ignition key. You only need a *brief turn of the key* (short gate pulse) to start the engine, and the engine will keep running on its own fuel supply (anode current) until you disconnect the fuel (reduce current below holding value) or apply the brakes (forced commutation).

2. **The Mechanical Latch Analogy:** Think of a gate that, once pushed open with a small force (gate trigger), stays open by itself due to a mechanical latch mechanism (regenerative internal feedback). The gate will only close when the spring force (anode current reduction) overcomes the latch threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Operating Insight:** The SCR's latching behavior arises from the **regenerative (positive) feedback** between its internal PNP and NPN transistor structures — a critical concept for KTU 2-mark and short-answer questions.

---

## 1.3 Layer Structure and Physical Construction

The SCR is constructed by **alternately doping four semiconductor layers**, forming three PN junctions (J₁, J₂, J₃):

$$\text{P}_1 - \text{N}_1 - \text{P}_2 - \text{N}_2$$

| Layer | Doping Level | Connected To |
|-------|--------------|--------------|
| P₁ | Heavily doped (P⁺) | Anode (A) |
| N₁ | Lightly doped (N⁻) | — |
| P₂ | Moderately doped (P) | Gate (G) |
| N₂ | Heavily doped (N⁺) | Cathode (K) |

| Junction | Location | Function |
|----------|----------|----------|
| J₁ | Between P₁ and N₁ | Forward biased during forward blocking |
| J₂ | Between N₁ and P₂ | Reverse biased during forward blocking |
| J₃ | Between P₂ and N₂ | Forward biased during forward blocking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Lightly Doped N₁?** The middle N₁ layer is lightly doped to give the SCR a **high forward and reverse blocking voltage capability**, which is essential for high-power applications like HVDC transmission and motor drives.

---

## 1.4 Position in the Thyristor Family Tree

The thyristor family is a hierarchy of semi-controlled/controllable power devices. SCRs sit at the **root** of this family:

```
Thyristor Family
│
├── 1. SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier)        ← Mother device
│
├── 2. TRIAC (TRIode for AC)
│       ↳ Bidirectional equivalent of two SCRs
│
├── 3. DIAC (DIode for AC)
│       ↳ Triggering device for TRIAC
│
├── 4. GTO (Gate Turn-Off thyristor)
│       ↳ Self-commutated SCR
│
├── 5. IGCT (Integrated Gate-Commutated Thyristor)
│
├── 6. MCT (MOS-Controlled Thyristor)
│
├── 7. SCS (Silicon Controlled Switch)
│
├── 8. PUT (Programmable Unijunction Transistor)
│
└── 9. LASCR (Light-Activated SCR)
```

**Standard Reference Value:** The typical anode-cathode breakover voltage ($V_{BO}$) for an industrial-grade SCR ranges from **50 V to 6500 V**, with current ratings from **1 A to 5000 A** at 50/60 Hz operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two-Transistor Model — The Heart of SCR Operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two-Transistor Analogy** is the most frequently tested concept in KTU exams on SCRs. The SCR can be modelled as an **interconnected PNP and NPN transistor pair** sharing a common collector-base junction.

The four-layer P-N-P-N structure can be split as follows:
- **Upper Transistor (T₁):** PNP transistor formed by layers P₁-N₁-P₂
- **Lower Transistor (T₂):** NPN transistor formed by layers N₁-P₂-N₂
- The **collector of T₁ = base of T₂** and **base of T₁ = collector of T₂** (regenerative loop).

### Mathematical Operating Conditions:

Let:
- $\alpha_1$ = common-base current gain of PNP transistor T₁
- $\alpha_2$ = common-base current gain of NPN transistor T₂
- $I_{CO}$ = reverse saturation leakage current of the collector-base junction
- $I_G$ = gate current injected externally

The total anode current is given by:

$$I_A = \frac{\alpha_2 \cdot I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Critical Insight — Why the SCR Latches:**

| Condition | Physical Meaning | Device State |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|
| $(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) < 1$ | Anode current is small; loop gain < 1 | **OFF state (blocking)** |
| $(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) = 1$ | Critical boundary condition | **Breakover / Turn-on threshold** |
| $(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \to 1$ | Regenerative action dominates | **ON state (latched)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The sum $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ increases with increasing anode current due to the rising nature of transistor current gain. Once it crosses **unity**, the loop becomes unstable and the device rapidly switches to ON.

---

## 2.2 Static V-I Characteristics of an SCR

The V-I characteristics plot the anode current ($I_A$) versus anode-cathode voltage ($V_{AK}$) under different gate-current conditions.

### 2.2.1 Forward Blocking Mode (Quadrant I)
- $V_{AK}$ is positive, $I_G = 0$
- Junctions J₁ and J₃ are forward biased; J₂ is reverse biased
- Only a tiny leakage current flows ($I_A \approx I_{CO}$)
- Device blocks forward voltage up to $V_{BO}$ (breakover voltage)

### 2.2.2 Reverse Blocking Mode (Quadrant III)
- $V_{AK}$ is negative
- Junctions J₁ and J₃ are reverse biased; J₂ is forward biased
- Only reverse leakage current flows up to $V_{BR(RR)}$ (reverse breakdown voltage)

### 2.2.3 Forward Conduction Mode
- Once $V_{AK} \geq V_{BO}$ (or gate triggered), device turns ON
- Voltage across SCR drops to **1 V to 2 V** (on-state voltage drop)
- Current is limited by external load

### 2.2.4 Reverse Breakdown Region
- Beyond $V_{BR}$, avalanche multiplication occurs; device is damaged.

---

## 2.3 Critical SCR Parameters — Definition and Physical Meaning

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range |
|-----------|--------|------------|---------------|
| Breakover Voltage | $V_{BO}$ | Forward anode voltage at which SCR turns ON without gate signal | **200 V – 4500 V** |
| Forward Blocking Voltage | $V_{DRM}$ | Maximum repetitive forward voltage the SCR can block | **100 V – 4000 V** |
| Reverse Blocking Voltage | $V_{RRM}$ | Maximum repetitive reverse voltage the SCR can block | **100 V – 4000 V** |
| Holding Current | $I_H$ | Minimum anode current to keep SCR in ON state | **10 mA – 200 mA** |
| Latching Current | $I_L$ | Minimum anode current required to turn ON and sustain the SCR | **20 mA – 500 mA** |
| On-State Voltage Drop | $V_T$ | Voltage across SCR when fully conducting | **1.0 V – 2.5 V** |
| Gate Trigger Current | $I_{GT}$ | Minimum gate current to turn ON the SCR | **1 mA – 50 mA** |
| Gate Trigger Voltage | $V_{GT}$ | Gate voltage corresponding to $I_{GT}$ | **0.5 V – 3.0 V** |
| Turn-On Time | $t_{on}$ | Time from gate pulse to full conduction (delay + rise) | **1 µs – 10 µs** |
| Turn-Off Time | $t_{off}$ | Time required for SCR to regain forward blocking | **10 µs – 200 µs** |
| Critical dv/dt | $\left.\frac{dv}{dt}\right\vert_{cr}$ | Maximum rate of rise of anode voltage to avoid false triggering | **10 V/µs – 1000 V/µs** |
| di/dt Rating | $\left.\frac{di}{dt}\right\vert_{max}$ | Maximum rate of rise of anode current at turn-on | **20 A/µs – 500 A/µs** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$I_L > I_H$ — Always.** Latching current is the minimum current to establish latching; holding current is the minimum current to *maintain* it. The relationship is: $I_L \geq 2 I_H$ typically.

---

## 2.4 Turn-On (Triggering) Methods of an SCR

An SCR can be turned ON by any of the following methods — this is a **favourite KTU Part A question**:

### Method 1: Forward Voltage Triggering
- Anode-cathode voltage exceeds $V_{BO}$
- Avalanche breakdown at J₂ initiates conduction
- **Disadvantage:** Destroys the gate junction integrity — used only in emergency.

### Method 2: Gate Triggering (Most Common)
- A small positive gate pulse (with respect to cathode) turns ON the SCR
- Gate current must be $\geq I_{GT}$ for reliable turn-on
- Gate pulse width should be $> t_{on}$ (turn-on time) of the SCR

### Method 3: dv/dt Triggering (Undesirable)
- Rapidly rising anode voltage causes **displacement current** through the reverse-biased junction J₂:
$$I_{disp} = C_j \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$
- If $I_{disp} \cdot (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \geq I_H$, the SCR turns on **falsely**.

### Method 4: Temperature Triggering
- At high temperature ($> 100°C$), thermal generation of carriers increases $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$
- $V_{BO}$ decreases, leading to unwanted turn-on
- **Mitigation:** Heat sinks and proper thermal design

### Method 5: Light Triggering (LASCR only)
- Photon energy incident on the reverse-biased junction J₂ creates electron-hole pairs
- Used in HVDC and high-voltage applications for electrical isolation

---

## 2.5 Turn-Off (Commutation) Methods of an SCR

| Method | Type | Mechanism | Application |
|--------|------|-----------|-------------|
| **Natural Commutation** | Class A (Load Commutation) | Load current naturally falls below $I_H$ due to AC supply reversal | Line-frequency inverters, AC voltage controllers |
| **Class B (Resonant Commutation)** | Forced | LC resonant circuit forces current to zero | Resonant inverters |
| **Class C (Complementary Commutation)** | Forced | An auxiliary charged capacitor reverse-biases the SCR | Choppers, DC drives |
| **Class D (External Pulse Commutation)** | Forced | External pulse source applies reverse voltage across SCR | Specialized converters |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Special Note:** For SCR-based choppers, the **Class C (complementary) commutation** is the most widely studied because it is reliable for DC-DC conversion without requiring an AC supply.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$ | Anode current (two-transistor model) | Amperes |
| 2 | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ | Turn-on criterion | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $P = V_T \cdot I_T$ | ON-state power loss | Watts |
| 4 | $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^{T} v^2(t)\,dt}$ | RMS voltage across load | Volts |
| 5 | $I_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_\alpha^{\beta} i^2(\theta)\,d\theta}$ | RMS current through SCR | Amperes |
| 6 | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)$ | Average output voltage (single-phase full converter) | Volts |
| 7 | $I_{avg} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi R}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)$ | Average load current | Amperes |
| 8 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{RMS}}{V_{avg}}$ | Form Factor | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $RF = \dfrac{V_{RMS}^2}{V_{avg}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{V_{m}/\pi}$ | Ripple Factor | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_R = \dfrac{V_{avg}}{V_{m}/\pi} \times 100\%$ | Rectification Efficiency | % |
| 11 | $t_q = t_{st} + t_{rr}$ | Turn-off time (storage + reverse recovery) | seconds |
| 12 | $E_{ON} = \int_0^{t_{on}} v(t) \cdot i(t)\,dt$ | Turn-on switching energy | Joules |
| 13 | $E_{OFF} = \int_0^{t_{off}} v(t) \cdot i(t)\,dt$ | Turn-off switching energy | Joules |
| 14 | $P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = V_T I_{T(avg)} + f(E_{ON}+E_{OFF})$ | Total power dissipation | Watts |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:** The SCR is the workhorse of:
- **HVDC transmission systems** (line-commutated converters)
- **AC-DC rectifiers** in industrial drives
- **Static VAR compensators (SVCs)**
- **Battery chargers, UPS systems, and welding equipment**
- **Traction drives and locomotive propulsion**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Two-Transistor Anode Current Equation

We shall derive the fundamental anode current expression of the SCR from first principles, as this is a **recurrent 7-mark KTU question**.

### Step 1: Define the Current Components

Consider the SCR biased with anode voltage $V_{AK} > 0$ and gate current $I_G$ injected. From the two-transistor model:
- The leakage current through junction J₂ (collector-base of both T₁ and T₂) is $I_{CO}$.
- The PNP transistor T₁ has collector current: $I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CO1}$
- The NPN transistor T₂ has collector current: $I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_A + I_{CO2}$

**Logic:** The collector currents arise from the fraction of emitter (anode) current captured by the collector plus the reverse-saturation leakage of the CB junction.

### Step 2: Apply KCL at the Common Node

At the central node (between the two transistors), the total anode current splits as:

$$I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2}$$

Substituting the transistor collector equations:

$$I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CO1} + \alpha_2 I_A + I_{CO2}$$

### Step 3: Combine Leakage Currents

Define the combined leakage current:

$$I_{CO} = I_{CO1} + I_{CO2}$$

Rearranging:

$$I_A - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) I_A = I_{CO}$$

$$I_A [1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)] = I_{CO}$$

### Step 4: Account for External Gate Current

The gate current $I_G$ is injected into the base of T₂ (NPN). This effectively increases the leakage term:

$$I_A [1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)] = \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}$$

### Step 5: Final Anode Current Expression

Solving for $I_A$:

$$I_A = \frac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Logic Check:** When $I_G = 0$ and $I_{CO}$ is very small but $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$, the denominator approaches zero and $I_A$ grows rapidly — this is the **turn-on action**. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical dv/dt for False Triggering

A high rate of rise of anode voltage can falsely trigger the SCR. The condition for false triggering is derived below.

### Step 1: Displacement Current at Reverse-Biased Junction

The reverse-biased junction J₂ has a depletion-layer capacitance $C_j$. A rapidly rising anode voltage induces a displacement current:

$$i_{disp}(t) = C_j \cdot \frac{dv_{AK}}{dt}$$

### Step 2: Condition for Turn-on

This displacement current acts like a leakage current feeding the transistor pair. Turn-on occurs when:

$$i_{disp} \cdot (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \geq I_H$$

### Step 3: Solve for Critical dv/dt

$$C_j \cdot \left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{cr} \cdot (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) = I_H$$

$$\left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{cr} = \frac{I_H}{C_j \cdot (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Engineering Implication:** To prevent false triggering, the rate of rise of anode voltage must be less than this critical value. A **snubber circuit** (RC network) across the SCR is used to limit dv/dt.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of a Single-Phase Full-Wave Controlled Rectifier

This is a **standard 14-mark KTU problem**.

### Step 1: Circuit Description

A single-phase full converter (2-pulse midpoint or bridge) uses two SCRs conducting in each half-cycle. Let the input be $v_s = V_m \sin(\theta)$.

### Step 2: Firing Delay Angle

The SCR is fired at $\theta = \alpha$ (firing angle) and conducts from $\alpha$ to $\pi + \alpha$ in the positive half-cycle.

### Step 3: Average Voltage Calculation

The instantaneous output voltage equals the input voltage during conduction:

$$v_o(\theta) = V_m \sin\theta \quad \text{for} \quad \alpha \leq \theta \leq \pi + \alpha$$

The average output voltage is:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos\theta \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha}$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi + \alpha) + \cos\alpha \right]$$

Since $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos\alpha$:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ \cos\alpha + \cos\alpha \right]$$

$$\boxed{V_{avg} = \frac{2 V_m \cos\alpha}{\pi}}$$

### Step 4: RMS Output Voltage

$$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m^2 \sin^2\theta \, d\theta}$$

Using the identity $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$V_{RMS} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2} \, d\theta}$$

$$V_{RMS} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Key Result:** For a single-phase full converter, $V_{RMS} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$, **independent of firing angle $\alpha$**. This is a very important conclusion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of SCR Operating Modes

| Mode | Condition | Anode Current | Device State | Power Dissipation |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Forward Blocking** | $0 < V_{AK} < V_{BO}$, $I_G = 0$ | $I_A \approx I_{CO}$ (µA–mA) | OFF | Negligible |
| **Forward Conduction** | $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ and $I_A > I_L$ | $I_A$ = load current (A) | ON | $P = V_T \cdot I_A$ (1–2% of rating) |
| **Reverse Blocking** | $V_{AK} < 0$, $\vert V_{AK}\vert < V_{BR}$ | $\vert I_A\vert \approx I_{CO}$ (µA) | OFF | Negligible |
| **Reverse Breakdown** | $\vert V_{AK}\vert \geq V_{BR}$ | Excessive (destructive) | Damaged | N/A (device fails) |
| **Transient Turn-on** | Gate pulse applied | Rising from 0 to load value | Switching | Switching losses dominate |
| **Commutation Interval** | Reverse bias applied | Falling below $I_H$ | Turning OFF | Reverse recovery losses |

---

## 3.5 Energy Loss During Turn-on and Turn-off Switching

### Turn-on Energy Loss:

During the turn-on interval, both voltage and current exist simultaneously, producing switching loss.

$$E_{ON} = \int_0^{t_{on}} v_{AK}(t) \cdot i_A(t) \, dt$$

Assuming linear approximations: $v_{AK}(t) \approx V_D (1 - t/t_{on})$ and $i_A(t) \approx I_L (t/t_{on})$:

$$E_{ON} = V_D \cdot I_L \int_0^{t_{on}} \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{on}}\right) \frac{t}{t_{on}} \, dt$$

$$E_{ON} = \frac{V_D \cdot I_L \cdot t_{on}}{6}$$

### Turn-off Energy Loss:

$$E_{OFF} = \frac{V_R \cdot I_R \cdot t_{off}}{6}$$

where $V_R$ and $I_R$ are the peak reverse voltage and recovered current respectively.

### Total Switching Loss:

$$P_{sw} = f \cdot (E_{ON} + E_{OFF})$$

where $f$ is the switching frequency. This is why SCRs are preferred for **low-frequency** applications (50 Hz–400 Hz) — at high frequencies, switching losses become dominant.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: SCR Layer Structure and Two-Transistor Equivalence

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[P1 Layer Heavy P] --> B[J1 Junction Forward Biased]
    B --> C[N1 Layer Light N]
    C --> D[J2 Junction Reverse Biased Blocking]
    D --> E[P2 Layer Moderate P]
    E --> F[J3 Junction Forward Biased]
    F --> G[N2 Layer Heavy N]
    
    A --> H[Anode Terminal A]
    G --> I[Cathode Terminal K]
    E --> J[Gate Terminal G]
    
    C --> K[NPN Transistor T2]
    E --> K
    G --> K
    
    A --> L[PNP Transistor T1]
    C --> L
    E --> L
    
    K --> M[Regenerative Feedback Loop]
    L --> M
    M --> N[ON State Latched]
    
    J --> O[Gate Signal Source]
    O --> P[Trigger Pulse Positive]
    P --> Q[Turn ON Action]
    Q --> N
```

**Description:** This diagram illustrates the four-layer P-N-P-N construction of the SCR, the corresponding two-transistor model, and the regenerative feedback loop that causes latching. The gate terminal injects a positive pulse that initiates the regenerative process.

---

## 4.2 V-I Characteristics Curve — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Start Anode Voltage = 0] --> B[Apply Forward VAK]
    B --> C{Junction J2 Reverse Biased}
    C --> D[Forward Blocking Region Small Leakage Current]
    D --> E{VAK reaches VBO OR Gate Triggered}
    E -->|No| F[Continue Blocking]
    E -->|Yes| G[Avalanche at J2 Initiated]
    G --> H[Regenerative Turn ON]
    H --> I[Forward Conduction Region Low VAK 1 to 2V]
    I --> J[ON State Maintained]
    J --> K{IA drops below IH}
    K -->|Yes| L[Return to OFF State]
    K -->|No| J
```

**Description:** This flowchart represents the V-I characteristics of the SCR. Starting from zero, the device remains in the forward blocking region with negligible leakage current. Once $V_{AK}$ exceeds the breakover voltage $V_{BO}$ or a gate trigger is applied, the device rapidly transitions to the conduction region. The device remains ON until the anode current falls below the holding current $I_H$.

---

## 4.3 SCR Triggering and Commutation Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[SCR Operating Cycle] --> Mode1{Need to Turn ON}
    
    Mode1 -->|Yes| Trig{Triggering Method}
    Trig --> T1[Gate Triggering Most Common]
    Trig --> T2[Forward Voltage Triggering Emergency]
    Trig --> T3[dvdt Triggering Undesirable]
    Trig --> T4[Temperature Triggering Undesirable]
    Trig --> T5[Light Triggering LASCR only]
    
    T1 --> Con[Conduction Established]
    T2 --> Con
    T3 --> Con
    T4 --> Con
    T5 --> Con
    
    Con --> Mode2{Need to Turn OFF}
    Mode2 -->|Yes| Comm{Commutation Method}
    
    Comm --> C1[Natural Class A Load Commutation]
    Comm --> C2[Resonant Class B LC Circuit]
    Comm --> C3[Complementary Class C Capacitor]
    Comm --> C4[External Pulse Class D]
    
    C1 --> Off[SCR Returns to Blocking State]
    C2 --> Off
    C3 --> Off
    C4 --> Off
    
    Off --> End[Cycle Complete]
    Mode2 -->|No| Con
```

**Description:** This decision tree outlines the complete operational cycle of the SCR — from initial turn-on through the choice of triggering mechanism, sustained conduction, and the various commutation methods available to return the device to the OFF state.

---

## 4.4 Industrial Application Architecture: HVDC Converter Station

```mermaid
graph TD
    ACSource[AC Source 50Hz] --> Transformer[Step Down Transformer]
    Transformer --> Rectifier[6 Pulse SCR Bridge Rectifier]
    Rectifier --> Filter[DC Filter Reactor]
    Filter --> DCLink[DC Transmission Link]
    DCLink --> Inverter[SCR Inverter Station]
    Inverter --> LoadAC[Load AC Network]
    
    Rectifier --> G1[Gate Pulse Generator 1]
    G1 --> CT1[Control Circuit]
    CT1 --> FB1[Feedback from DC Link]
    
    Inverter --> G2[Gate Pulse Generator 2]
    G2 --> CT2[Control Circuit 2]
    
    CT1 --> Alpha[Firing Angle Alpha Control]
    Alpha --> PowerReg[Active Power Regulation]
    Alpha --> ReacReg[Reactive Power Control]
```

**Description:** This block diagram depicts a typical HVDC transmission system using SCR-based line-commutated converters. The SCR bridge rectifier converts AC to DC, which is transmitted via the DC link, and then an inverter converts it back to AC at the receiving end. The firing angle $\alpha$ of the SCR controls both active and reactive power flow.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Latching Current and Holding Current
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **Model Answer:**
> 
> **Latching Current ($I_L$):** It is the minimum anode current that must flow through the SCR during the turn-on process to ensure that the device **transitions fully into the latched ON state**. If the anode current falls below $I_L$ before triggering is complete, the SCR will not remain ON.
> 
> **Holding Current ($I_H$):** It is the minimum anode current required to **keep the SCR in the ON state** once it has been latched. If the anode current falls below $I_H$, the SCR returns to the OFF state.
> 
> **Key Relation:** $I_L > I_H$ (always), and typically $I_L \approx 2 I_H$ for industrial SCRs.

---

### Question 2: Why is an SCR called a Semi-controlled Device?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Model Answer:**
> 
> An SCR is termed a **semi-controlled device** because:
> 
> 1. **Turn-ON is controlled** by the gate terminal — a small gate current pulse can trigger the device into conduction.
> 2. **Turn-OFF is NOT controlled by the gate** — once the SCR is ON, removing the gate signal does not turn it OFF. The device can only be turned OFF by reducing the anode current below the holding current $I_H$ through external commutation circuitry (natural or forced).
> 
> Hence, the device offers only half the controllability of a fully controlled switch (like a BJT or MOSFET).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Analysis of SCR Characteristics**

> **[a) 7 Marks]** With the help of the two-transistor model, derive the expression for anode current in terms of gate current and explain the conditions for the SCR to switch from the OFF state to the ON state.
> 
> **[b) 7 Marks]** Draw and explain the static V-I characteristics of an SCR. Clearly label the forward blocking region, forward conduction region, reverse blocking region, and the breakover voltages.

#### Model Solution:

**[a) Derivation of Anode Current (7 Marks):**

The SCR is modelled as a **PNP transistor (T₁)** and an **NPN transistor (T₂)** interconnected such that the collector of T₁ drives the base of T₂, and vice versa, creating a **regenerative feedback loop**.

**Step 1:** Define the parameters:
- $\alpha_1$ = common-base current gain of T₁ (PNP)
- $\alpha_2$ = common-base current gain of T₂ (NPN)
- $I_{CO}$ = reverse saturation current of the common CB junction
- $I_G$ = external gate current

**Step 2:** Write collector currents:

$$I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_A + I_{CO1}$$
$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_A + I_{CO2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply KCL:

$$I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2} = (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) I_A + I_{CO1} + I_{CO2}$$

**Step 4:** Include gate current contribution (gate current flows into base of T₂, adding to $I_{C2}$ term):

$$I_A = (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) I_A + \alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange:

$$\boxed{I_A = \frac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}}$$

**Conditions for turn-on:**
- When $I_G = 0$ and $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 < 1$: anode current is small (leakage only) → **OFF state**.
- When $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ OR $V_{AK} \geq V_{BO}$: $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ rises toward 1 due to increasing current gain with current → **regenerative action** begins.
- When $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$: denominator → 0, $I_A \to \infty$ (limited by load) → **ON state** latched.

**[b) V-I Characteristics (7 Marks):**

The static V-I characteristics of the SCR consist of **four distinct regions**:

1. **Forward Blocking Region (Quadrant I, lower portion):**
   - Anode voltage positive, gate current zero
   - Junctions J₁ and J₃ forward biased, J₂ reverse biased
   - Only microampere-level leakage current flows
   - Device blocks forward voltage up to $V_{BO}$

2. **Forward Conduction Region (Quadrant I, upper portion):**
   - Once $V_{AK} \geq V_{BO}$ or gate pulse applied, device turns ON
   - Anode-cathode voltage drops to $V_T \approx 1.5 V$ (on-state drop)
   - Anode current is determined by load: $I_A = V_{DC}/R_L$

3. **Reverse Blocking Region (Quadrant III):**
   - Anode negative with respect to cathode
   - Junctions J₁ and J₃ reverse biased
   - Only reverse leakage current flows
   - Device blocks reverse voltage up to $V_{BR(RR)}$

4. **Reverse Breakdown Region (Quadrant III, far right):**
   - Beyond $V_{BR(RR)}$, avalanche breakdown damages the device

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the breakover voltage $V_{BO}$ with the forward blocking voltage $V_{DRM}$. Remember: $V_{BO}$ is the **natural turn-on voltage** without gate, while $V_{DRM}$ is the **maximum repetitive blocking voltage** with gate signal $= 0$. Always: $V_{DRM} < V_{BO}$. **(Lose 1 mark for this mistake!)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Commutation and Application of SCRs**

> **[a) 7 Marks]** Explain the various methods of turning ON an SCR. Which method is preferred in practical applications and why?
> 
> **[b) 7 Marks]** A single-phase full converter feeds a highly inductive load of $R = 10 \Omega$ and $L = \infty$ from a $230 V$, $50 Hz$ AC supply. If the firing angle is $\alpha = 45°$, calculate the average output voltage, RMS output voltage, and rectification efficiency.

#### Model Solution:

**[a) Turn-ON Methods (7 Marks):**

**Method 1 — Forward Voltage Triggering:** Anode-cathode voltage exceeds $V_{BO}$, causing avalanche breakdown at J₂. **Disadvantage:** Thermal stress damages the gate junction; used only in over-voltage protection circuits.

**Method 2 — Gate Triggering (Most Preferred):** A positive gate pulse (with respect to cathode) of magnitude $\geq I_{GT}$ for a duration $\geq t_{on}$ reliably turns ON the SCR. **Advantages:** Precise control over firing angle, low power required, repeatable operation.

**Method 3 — dv/dt Triggering (Undesirable):** Rapid anode voltage change causes displacement current $i_{disp} = C_j \cdot dv/dt$ through the reverse-biased junction, which can falsely trigger the SCR. **Mitigation:** Use RC snubber circuits.

**Method 4 — Temperature Triggering:** Elevated temperature increases carrier generation, raising $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ and lowering $V_{BO}$. Undesirable; prevented by adequate heat sinking.

**Method 5 — Light Triggering (LASCR):** Photon-generated carriers at J₂ initiate turn-on. Used in HVDC valves for electrical isolation of gate drive.

**Preferred Method:** **Gate triggering** is universally used because:
- Precise, controllable firing angle
- Low power consumption
- Reliable and repeatable
- No damage to the device

**[b) Numerical Problem Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_s = 230 V$ (RMS), $f = 50 Hz$
- $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27 V$
- $R = 10 \Omega$, $L = \infty$ (highly inductive load)
- $\alpha = 45° = \pi/4$ rad

**Step 1: Average Output Voltage**

For a single-phase full converter with highly inductive load:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2 V_m \cos\alpha}{\pi}$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2 \times 325.27 \times \cos(45°)}{\pi}$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2 \times 325.27 \times 0.7071}{3.1416}$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{460.04}{3.1416} = 146.43 V$$

**Step 2: RMS Output Voltage**

For a single-phase full converter (independent of $\alpha$):

$$V_{RMS} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{325.27}{1.4142} = 230 V$$

**Step 3: Average Load Current**

$$I_{avg} = \frac{V_{avg}}{R} = \frac{146.43}{10} = 14.64 A$$

**Step 4: RMS Load Current**

$$I_{RMS} = \frac{V_{RMS}}{R} = \frac{230}{10} = 23 A$$

**Step 5: Rectification Efficiency**

$$\eta_R = \frac{V_{avg}^2 / R}{V_{RMS}^2 / R} \times \frac{1}{V_{m}/\pi} \times 100\%$$

Alternative direct formula for single-phase full converter:

$$\eta_R = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \cos^2\alpha \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_R = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \times (0.7071)^2 \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_R = 0.8106 \times 0.5 \times 100\% = 40.53\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $V_{avg}$ for full converter vs half converter** — for half converter: $V_{avg} = \dfrac{V_m(1+\cos\alpha)}{\pi}$; for full converter: $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2V_m\cos\alpha}{\pi}$. Using the wrong formula costs **3 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Using RMS value for $V_m$** — students frequently forget to convert RMS to peak: $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_{RMS}$.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting units** — always quote voltage in **V**, current in **A**, efficiency in **%**.
> 
> 4. **Angle conversion errors** — $\cos(45°)$ is NOT 0.5; it is 0.7071. Always carry 4 decimal places.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ⭐ **SCR = 4-layer (P-N-P-N)**, 3 junctions, 3 terminals: **A**node, **C**athode, **G**ate.
- ⭐ **SCR is a semi-controlled device** — gate controls turn-ON only; turn-OFF needs external commutation.
- ⭐ **Two-transistor model** divides SCR into interconnected **PNP (T₁) and NPN (T₂)** with regenerative feedback.
- ⭐ **Anode current equation:** $I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$
- ⭐ **Turn-on condition:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ → regenerative action → latched ON.
- ⭐ **Latching current ($I_L$) > Holding current ($I_H$)** — always, typically $I_L \approx 2 I_H$.
- ⭐ **Five turn-ON methods:** Gate, forward voltage, dv/dt, temperature, light (LASCR).
- ⭐ **Four turn-OFF methods:** Natural (Class A), Resonant (Class B), Complementary (Class C), External pulse (Class D).
- ⭐ **dv/dt triggering is undesirable** — leads to false turn-on; use **snubber circuits** to limit it.
- ⭐ **Single-phase full converter average output voltage:** $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2 V_m \cos\alpha}{\pi}$
- ⭐ **Single-phase full converter RMS output voltage:** $V_{RMS} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ (independent of $\alpha$).
- ⭐ **Typical on-state voltage drop:** $V_T \approx 1.0$ to $2.5 V$ (nearly constant).
- ⭐ **Turn-on time** = delay time ($t_d$) + rise time ($t_r$); typically **1–10 µs**.
- ⭐ **Turn-off time** = reverse recovery time ($t_{rr}$) + gate recovery time ($t_{gr}$); typically **10–200 µs**.
- ⭐ **Maximum junction temperature:** $125°C$ to $150°C$ (mandatory heat sink design).
- ⭐ **Forward voltage rating** $V_{DRM}$ is **less than** breakover voltage $V_{BO}$.
- ⭐ **SCR is used in:** HVDC, AC-DC converters, choppers, inverters, AC voltage controllers, traction drives.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DIAC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DIAC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **DIAC (Diode for Alternating Current)** — also formally called a **Bidirectional Trigger Diode** or **Bidirectional Breakdown Diode** — is a two-terminal, three/five-layer semiconductor device belonging to the **thyristor family** that begins to conduct only after its applied voltage (in either polarity) exceeds a sharply defined threshold called the **breakover voltage ($V_{BO}$)**. Once triggered, the device exhibits a **negative resistance region**, snapping into a low-impedance ON state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBRT503 – Module 1):** The DIAC is studied as a *triggering auxiliary* device. Its primary engineering role in the KTU 2024 scheme is as a **symmetric firing element for TRIAC-based AC phase-control circuits**, e.g., lamp dimmers, fan regulators, and heater controllers.

**Two accepted industry-standard constructions exist:**
1. **Three-layer (NPN / PNP) DIAC** — essentially a transistor chip whose base terminal is left floating.
2. **Five-layer (NPNPN / PNPNP) DIAC** — a symmetric back-to-back Shockley-diode arrangement offering sharper, more symmetric $V_{BO}$ characteristics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Picture)

Imagine a **mechanical pressure-relief valve** mounted on a water pipe. The valve is *closed* for low pressure. It does nothing whether water flows in from the left or the right. However, the **moment the pressure exceeds, say, 100 kPa in EITHER direction**, the valve snaps open and water rushes through. A DIAC behaves identically in the *electrical* domain:

| Property | Pressure Valve | DIAC |
|---|---|---|
| Terminals | 2 (inlet/outlet) | 2 (MT1, MT2) |
| Trigger threshold | 100 kPa either way | $V_{BO} \approx 30\text{ V}$ either way |
| Post-trigger state | Open & flows | Conducts with low $\Delta V$ |
| Reset condition | Pressure must drop below holding value | Current must fall below $I_H$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core takeaway for KTU exams:** A DIAC is *symmetric*, *bilateral*, and *voltage-triggered*. It has no control (gate) terminal — once you cross $V_{BO}$ in either polarity, it turns ON automatically.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Breakover Voltage ($V_{BO}$):** typically **30 V – 40 V** (industry standard ≈ 32 V for the classic DB3 / ST2 type).
- **Breakover Current ($I_{BO}$):** very small, typically **50 µA – 200 µA**.
- **Holding Current ($I_H$):** typically **5 mA – 15 mA**.
- **ON-state Voltage Drop ($V_T$):** approximately **1.5 V – 5 V** at rated current.
- **Peak Repetitive Surge Current ($I_{TSM}$):** typically **1 A – 2 A** (8.3 ms sine).
- **Power Dissipation ($P_D$):** typically **150 mW – 500 mW** continuous.
- **Operating Temperature Range:** **-40 °C to +125 °C** (industrial grade).

> [!TIP]
> **Memorisation tip for KTU 2024 board exams:** Always quote the **DB3-type DIAC** values ($V_{BO} \approx 32$ V, $I_{BO} \approx 100$ µA) when the question does not specify a device — examiners reward *standard* part-number recognition.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details (Layer-by-Layer)

The five-layer DIAC is fabricated as a single silicon die with the following doping sequence, reading from terminal **MT1 → MT2**:

$$\text{MT1} \;\; \rightarrow \;\; P_1 \, - \, N_2 \, - \, P_2 \, - \, N_1 \, - \, P_3 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{MT2}$$

Equivalently, this is a **PNPNP** structure (or NPNPN from the other terminal), giving two back-to-back Shockley diodes sharing a common anode/cathode region. The **central $P_2$ / $N_2$ region** is lightly doped to set the desired $V_{BO}$.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Forward Blocking State ($|V| < V_{BO}$):** Only a tiny leakage current ($\sim \mu\text{A}$) flows. The four p-n junctions are reverse-biased in such a way that the centre depletion regions block conduction.
2. **Approaching Breakover:** As $|V|$ rises, the electric field across the lightly-doped central region intensifies. Avalanche multiplication begins.
3. **Breakover Event ($|V| = V_{BO}$):** The reverse-biased junction undergoes **avalanche breakdown**. The resulting carrier injection flips the other junctions from reverse-biased to forward-biased.
4. **Negative Resistance Region:** A small *increase* in current produces a *decrease* in voltage drop — the device snaps to its ON state.
5. **ON State ($I > I_H$):** All junctions are forward-biased; voltage drop settles to $V_T \approx 1.5\text{–}5$ V.
6. **Commutation:** The device resets to OFF only when the current falls **below $I_H$** (naturally happens at AC zero-crossing in 50/60 Hz lines).

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" the device is symmetric:** Because the doping profile is geometrically mirror-symmetric about the centre, the same avalanche injection sequence occurs whether MT2 is positive or negative with respect to MT1.

## 2.3 Why the Negative Resistance?

The negative resistance emerges from the **regenerative feedback** of carrier multiplication. Once avalanche begins at one junction, the injected carriers forward-bias an adjacent junction, which in turn injects *more* carriers into the original junction. This **positive feedback loop** is the same physical mechanism that gives the SCR and TRIAC their latching property.

Mathematically, this is captured by the condition:

$$\alpha_1 M_1 + \alpha_2 M_2 \; \ge \; 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Breakover occurs}$$

where $\alpha_i$ are the common-base current gains of the two internal transistor halves and $M_i$ are the avalanche multiplication factors (both $\to \infty$ at the breakdown field).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relationship | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Breakover voltage | $V_{BO}$ | **30 – 40 V** (≈ 32 V for DB3) | Voltage at which device snaps ON |
| 2 | Breakover current | $I_{BO}$ | **50 – 200 µA** | Current just before breakover |
| 3 | Holding current | $I_H$ | **5 – 15 mA** | Minimum current to remain ON |
| 4 | ON-state voltage drop | $V_T$ | **1.5 – 5 V** | Voltage during conduction |
| 5 | Peak surge current | $I_{TSM}$ | **1 – 2 A** (8.3 ms) | Non-repetitive surge rating |
| 6 | Power dissipation | $P_D$ | **150 – 500 mW** | Continuous dissipation limit |
| 7 | Triggering criterion | $\alpha_1 M_1 + \alpha_2 M_2 \ge 1$ | dimensionless | Breakover condition |
| 8 | Phase firing angle | $\alpha$ | $0^\circ < \alpha < \pi$ | TRIAC firing instant w.r.t. zero-crossing |
| 9 | Firing angle from $V_{BO}$ | $\alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\bigl(V_{BO}/V_m\bigr)$ | rad / deg | Trigger lag angle |
| 10 | TRIAC conduction angle | $\theta_c = \pi - \alpha$ | rad | Time TRIAC conducts per half-cycle |
| 11 | RMS output voltage | $V_{rms} = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\!\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | V | Standard phase-control expression |
| 12 | DIAC as $R_{BO}$ equivalent | $R_{BO} = V_{BO} / I_{BO}$ | $\sim 200$ k$\Omega$ | Effective breakover resistance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table formatting rule for KTU answer scripts:** When writing the V-I characteristic, examiners expect *both* the first and third quadrants to be plotted (symmetric). Always label the four corner regions: **(i) Off state**, **(ii) Negative-resistance region**, **(iii) On state**, and the **breakover points** $(\pm V_{BO}, \pm I_{BO})$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Specific Use of DIAC |
|---|---|
| **Domestic lamp dimmers** | Generates sharp trigger pulse to TRIAC gate at adjustable phase angle |
| **Fan speed regulators** | Same as dimmer, controlling motor RMS voltage |
| **Heat-control circuits** | Phase-angle control of resistive heaters |
| **Solid-state relays (SSRs)** | Zero-voltage / random-fire switching reference |
| **Over-voltage crowbar protection** | Crowbars sensitive loads once line transient crosses $V_{BO}$ |
| **Trigger modules for SCR bridges** | Symmetric, reliable triggering source for back-to-back SCRs |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Phase Firing Angle $\alpha$ in a DIAC–TRIAC Lamp Dimmer

**Circuit description (classic KTU exam problem):** A DIAC is placed in series with a resistor $R$ and the gate of a TRIAC across an AC source $v_s(t) = V_m \sin \omega t$. The DIAC blocks conduction until the voltage across it reaches $V_{BO}$ in either half-cycle. Once it does, the DIAC suddenly conducts, dumping current into the TRIAC gate and latching the TRIAC ON for the remainder of that half-cycle.

**Step 1 — Voltage at the trigger instant.**

The voltage at the DIAC equals the source voltage minus the drop across $R$ (the TRIAC gate draws negligible current until fired). The DIAC fires when the source voltage satisfies:

$$v_s(t_1) = V_m \sin \omega t_1 \;=\; V_{BO}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the time instant $t_1$.**

$$\sin \omega t_1 \;=\; \frac{V_{BO}}{V_m}$$

This yields the firing instant:

$$\omega t_1 \;=\; \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{BO}}{V_m}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Define the firing angle $\alpha$.**

The firing angle $\alpha$ is measured from the most recent AC voltage zero-crossing, so:

$$\alpha \;=\; \omega t_1 \;=\; \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{BO}}{V_m}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Conducting (conduction) angle of the TRIAC.**

Since the TRIAC remains ON until the AC current falls to zero (at $\omega t = \pi$ for a resistive load), the conduction angle per half-cycle is:

$$\theta_c \;=\; \pi - \alpha$$

**Step 5 — RMS output voltage delivered to the load.**

For a resistive load, with the TRIAC OFF for $\omega t \in [0, \alpha]$ and ON for $\omega t \in [\alpha, \pi]$, the RMS voltage is:

$$V_{rms}^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}\omega t \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 6 — Use the trigonometric identity.**

Apply the half-angle identity $\sin^{2}\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$V_{rms}^{2} \;=\; \frac{V_m^{2}}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos 2\omega t}{2} \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$V_{rms}^{2} \;=\; \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \Big[\, (\omega t) - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\omega t \,\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute the upper and lower limits.**

$$V_{rms}^{2} \;=\; \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \left[\,(\pi - \alpha) - \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(\sin 2\pi - \sin 2\alpha\bigr)\,\right]$$

Since $\sin 2\pi = 0$:

$$V_{rms}^{2} \;=\; \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi} \left[(\pi - \alpha) + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\alpha\right]$$

**Step 9 — Final compact result.**

$$\boxed{\;V_{rms} \;=\; V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}\;}$$

This is the **standard KTU result** for a phase-controlled AC output using a DIAC–TRIAC pair, valid for $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Breakover Voltage from the Two-Transistor Analogy

Model the five-layer DIAC as **two cross-coupled transistors** $Q_1$ (PNP) and $Q_2$ (NPN) with their collectors tied to each other's bases.

**Step 1 — Write the common-base current gains.**

$$\alpha_1 = \frac{I_{C1}}{I_{E1}}, \qquad \alpha_2 = \frac{I_{C2}}{I_{E2}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the total current through the device.**

By KCL at the central terminal, the leakage current is the sum of both collector currents:

$$I \;=\; \frac{I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

where $I_{CO}$ is the reverse-saturation current of the collector junctions.

**Step 3 — Introduce the avalanche multiplication factor $M$.**

Near breakdown, the multiplication factor follows Miller's empirical relation:

$$M \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - \left(\dfrac{V}{V_{BD}}\right)^{n}}$$

where $V_{BD}$ is the bulk breakdown voltage of the central junction and $n \approx 3\text{–}6$ for silicon.

**Step 4 — Modified breakover criterion.**

Substituting $M$ for both transistor halves:

$$I \;=\; \frac{M \, I_{CO}}{1 - M(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Step 5 — Singularity gives breakover.**

$I \to \infty$ (physical interpretation: device snaps ON) when the denominator vanishes:

$$M(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \;=\; 1$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{BO}$.**

Setting $M = \dfrac{1}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2}$ and inverting Miller's relation:

$$\frac{1}{1 - (V_{BO}/V_{BD})^{n}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2}$$

$$1 - \left(\frac{V_{BO}}{V_{BD}}\right)^{n} \;=\; \alpha_1 + \alpha_2$$

$$\boxed{\;V_{BO} \;=\; V_{BD}\,\bigl[\,1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)\,\bigr]^{1/n}\;}$$

This is the **KTU board-exam-grade expression** linking the DIAC's breakover voltage to the bulk breakdown voltage and the common-base current gains of the internal transistor model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** Higher $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ (i.e., better transistor action) *lowers* $V_{BO}$. Designers tune the central-region doping to push $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ close to (but less than) 1, so that the device breaks over at a precise target $V_{BO}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DIAC vs. Other Triggering Devices

| Parameter | DIAC | UJT | SBS (Silicon Bidirectional Switch) | Neon Bulb |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Terminals | **2** | 3 | 2 | 2 |
| Polarity | **Bilateral** | Unilateral | Bilateral | Bilateral |
| $V_{BO}$ sharpness | **Sharp** | Gradual (peak-point) | Very sharp | Gradual |
| Symmetry | **Excellent** | N/A | Excellent | Good |
| Typical $V_{BO}$ | **30–40 V** | 10–20 V (Vp) | 6–10 V | ~ 80 V |
| Trigger pulse to TRIAC | **Yes (standard)** | No (needs SCR pair) | Yes | No (slow) |
| Solid-state reliability | **High** | High | High | Low (gas discharge) |
| KTU exam frequency | **High** | Medium | Low | Low |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Set B]**

Define a **DIAC**. List any **two salient features** of its V-I characteristics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **DIAC (Diode for Alternating Current)** is a two-terminal, three- or five-layer semiconductor device that remains in the OFF state until the voltage across it (in either direction) exceeds its **breakover voltage ($V_{BO}$)**, after which it conducts with a low voltage drop.

**Salient features of the V-I characteristic (any two for full marks):**

1. The V-I curve is **symmetric about the origin** — identical behaviour in quadrants I and III.
2. It exhibits a clearly defined **breakover voltage $V_{BO} \approx 30\text{–}40$ V**, with a very small breakover current $I_{BO}$ (µA range).
3. Between the OFF state and ON state lies a **negative resistance region** where voltage falls as current rises (snap action).
4. The ON-state voltage drop $V_T$ is only a few volts, independent of current direction.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]**

Why is a **DIAC used in conjunction with a TRIAC** in AC phase-control circuits? Justify in **three points**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Symmetric triggering:** A DIAC has identical breakover in both polarities, which matches the bidirectional conduction requirement of a TRIAC across an AC line.
2. **Sharp, clean trigger pulse:** The DIAC's snap action delivers a steep, well-defined gate pulse, ensuring the TRIAC fires cleanly and reduces EMI/RFI.
3. **Compensates TRIAC's poor first-quadrant sensitivity:** TRIACs have asymmetric triggering quadrants. The DIAC's bilateral breakover produces the high di/dt pulse needed for reliable firing in the less-sensitive quadrants (III & IV), making triggering symmetric and hysteresis-free.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper Slot 1]**

**(a)** With a neat **constructional diagram**, describe the **structure and operation** of a **five-layer DIAC**. Explain its **V-I characteristics**, clearly marking the **breakover voltage $V_{BO}$**, **breakover current $I_{BO}$**, and **holding current $I_H$**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Using the **DIAC–TRIAC lamp-dimmer circuit** as a reference, derive an expression for the **RMS output voltage** as a function of the **firing angle $\alpha$**. Hence calculate $V_{rms}$ for $V_m = 325$ V and $\alpha = 60^\circ$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Construction (2 marks):**

The five-layer DIAC has the doping sequence **P–N–P–N–P** (or **N–P–N–P–N** from the opposite terminal), sandwiched between the two main terminals **MT1** and **MT2**. The two end layers ($P_1$ and $P_5$) are heavily doped; the central region is lightly doped to set the desired breakover voltage. The structure is equivalent to **two Shockley diodes connected in anti-parallel** sharing a common region.

**Step 2 — Operation (3 marks):**

* **Forward blocking (Quadrant I, $0 < V < V_{BO}$):** Junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are forward-biased, but $J_2$ (the lightly-doped centre junction) is reverse-biased and blocks conduction. Only a small leakage current flows.
* **Breakover ($V = V_{BO}$):** The reverse-biased junction $J_2$ undergoes avalanche breakdown. The resulting carrier injection forward-biases the other junctions, causing the device to snap to a low-impedance ON state.
* **Negative resistance region:** Voltage across the device falls from $V_{BO}$ to $V_T$ as current rises from $I_{BO}$ to a few mA.
* **ON state:** All four junctions are effectively forward-biased; the device behaves like a forward-biased diode with a drop of 1.5–5 V.
* **Quadrant III operation:** Identical to Quadrant I due to the symmetric doping profile.
* **Commutation:** The device turns OFF only when the current drops below $I_H$ (≈ 5–15 mA) — naturally occurring at every AC zero-crossing.

**Step 3 — V-I Characteristic (2 marks):**

Plot the curve in **quadrants I and III** with the following labeled points:

* $(0, 0)$ — origin
* $(\pm V_{BO}, \pm I_{BO})$ — breakover points
* $(\pm V_T, \pm I_{max})$ — ON-state operating points
* **Holding region** at $\pm I_H$ (turn-off threshold)

Mark the **negative-resistance region** (dotted portion of the curve between breakover and ON state) clearly. Add arrows showing the snap action.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State the firing condition (1 mark):**

The DIAC fires when the source voltage equals $V_{BO}$:

$$V_m \sin \omega t_1 = V_{BO} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \omega t_1 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{BO}}{V_m}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Set up the RMS integral (2 marks):**

For a resistive load, the TRIAC is OFF for $0 \le \omega t \le \alpha$ and ON for $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$ in each positive half-cycle (mirrored in the negative half-cycle):

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^{2}\sin^{2}\omega t \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral (3 marks):**

Using $\sin^{2}\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation (1 mark):**

Given $V_m = 325$ V, $\alpha = 60^\circ = \pi/3$ rad:

* $(\pi - \alpha) = \pi - \pi/3 = 2\pi/3 \approx 2.0944$ rad
* $\sin 2\alpha = \sin(120^\circ) = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.8660$
* Bracket $= 2.0944 + 0.8660/2 = 2.0944 + 0.4330 = 2.5274$
* $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}(2.5274) = \dfrac{2.5274}{6.2832} \approx 0.4023$
* $\sqrt{0.4023} \approx 0.6343$
* $V_{rms} = 325 \times 0.6343 \approx 206.1$ V

$$\boxed{V_{rms} \approx 206 \text{ V}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper Slot 2]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **two-transistor equivalent model** of a DIAC. Using this model, derive the **breakover criterion** and the **expression for $V_{BO}$** in terms of the bulk breakdown voltage and the common-base current gains. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the **construction, operation, and triggering behaviour** of a **DIAC** with a **UJT** and an **SBS (Silicon Bidirectional Switch)**. State **two practical applications** of DIACs. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Two-transistor model (2 marks):**

The five-layer DIAC can be visualised as two cross-connected transistors:

* **$Q_1$ (PNP):** left half — $P_1$–$N_2$–$P_2$
* **$Q_2$ (NPN):** right half — $N_2$–$P_2$–$N_1$

The **base of $Q_1$ is tied to the collector of $Q_2$**, and the **base of $Q_2$ is tied to the collector of $Q_1$**, creating a **regenerative feedback loop**.

**Step 2 — Current relations (2 marks):**

The collector current of each transistor is:

$$I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I, \qquad I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I$$

Including the avalanche multiplication factor $M$ (both junctions contribute):

$$I_{C1} = M \alpha_1 I, \qquad I_{C2} = M \alpha_2 I$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the central node (1 mark):**

$$I = \frac{M I_{CO}}{1 - M(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Step 4 — Breakover criterion (1 mark):**

As $M(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \to 1$, $I \to \infty$ — the device snaps ON. Hence:

$$\boxed{M(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) = 1}$$

**Step 5 — Derive $V_{BO}$ (1 mark):**

Substitute Miller's relation $M = \dfrac{1}{1 - (V/V_{BD})^{n}}$:

$$V_{BO} = V_{BD}\,\bigl[1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)\bigr]^{1/n}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — (7 marks)

**Comparison table (5 marks):**

| Feature | DIAC | UJT | SBS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layers / Type | 5-layer bilateral | 3-terminal unijunction | 5-layer bilateral |
| Terminals | 2 (MT1, MT2) | 3 (B1, B2, E) | 2 (T1, T2) |
| Triggering property | Voltage breakover, bilateral | Voltage peak-point $V_P$ (unilateral) | Voltage breakover, bilateral |
| $V_{BO}/V_P$ range | 30–40 V | 10–20 V | 6–10 V |
| Negative resistance | Yes, in both polarities | Yes, only in forward direction | Yes, in both polarities |
| Symmetry | Excellent | N/A | Excellent |
| Trigger pulse use | Direct TRIAC gate trigger | Needs pulse transformer or SCR pair | Direct TRIAC gate trigger |
| Quadrant of operation | I and III | Forward only | I and III |

**Two practical applications of DIACs (2 marks):**

1. **Lamp dimmer / fan regulator** — DIAC supplies a sharp gate pulse to a TRIAC at a controllable phase angle, varying the RMS voltage to the load.
2. **Solid-state AC switch with adjustable firing angle** — used in heat controllers and universal motor speed controllers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DIAC Questions**
> 1. **Do not confuse TRIAC and DIAC** — students often draw the TRIAC symbol (with gate) when asked for a DIAC symbol. The DIAC has **only two terminals** and **no gate**.
> 2. **Forgetting symmetry** — V-I must be plotted in **both** quadrants I and III. Single-quadrant plots lose at least **1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping the avalanche-multiplication factor $M$** in the $V_{BO}$ derivation. Examiners specifically look for $M$ in the breakover criterion $\alpha_1 M_1 + \alpha_2 M_2 = 1$.
> 4. **Unit errors in RMS calculation** — final answer must be in **volts (V)**, not the dimensionless bracket value. Watch the square root.
> 5. **Confusing "triggering angle" with "conduction angle"** — $\alpha$ is the lag (OFF) angle; $\theta_c = \pi - \alpha$ is the ON angle. Both must appear distinctly.
> 6. **Not specifying the standard part number** (e.g., DB3) — examiners appreciate when you cite the industry-standard reference.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **DIAC = Diode for Alternating Current = 2-terminal bilateral trigger diode.**
- **Construction:** 3-layer (NPN/PNP) or 5-layer (NPNPN/PNPNP). Five-layer type has **sharper $V_{BO}$** and is the industry standard (DB3, ST2).
- **Symbol:** two triangles pointing in **opposite directions**, in series, between MT1 and MT2 — **no gate terminal**.
- **V-I characteristic:** symmetric in **quadrants I and III**; three labelled regions — **OFF state, Negative-resistance region, ON state**.
- **Key parameters:** $V_{BO} \approx 30\text{–}40$ V (≈ 32 V for DB3), $I_{BO} \approx 50\text{–}200$ µA, $I_H \approx 5\text{–}15$ mA, $V_T \approx 1.5\text{–}5$ V.
- **Breakover criterion (two-transistor model):** $M(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) = 1$.
- **$V_{BO}$ formula:** $V_{BO} = V_{BD}\,[1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)]^{1/n}$.
- **Firing angle in dimmer:** $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(V_{BO}/V_m)$.
- **Conduction angle:** $\theta_c = \pi - \alpha$.
- **RMS output formula:** $V_{rms} = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$.
- **Primary use:** symmetric trigger source for **TRIAC gate** in AC phase-control circuits (lamp dimmers, fan regulators, heater controllers).
- **Advantages over UJT triggering:** bilateral, no pulse transformer needed, sharper pulse, symmetric triggering of all four TRIAC quadrants.
- **Turn-off condition:** current must fall **below $I_H$** — naturally happens at AC zero-crossing for 50/60 Hz mains.
- **Standard part number for KTU problems:** **DB3** ($V_{BO} \approx 32$ V) — always quote this if no device is specified.
- **Polarity-symmetry origin:** the doping profile is **geometrically mirror-symmetric** about the centre, which is why the same breakover occurs in both polarities.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam tip:** Memorise **four** items and you can solve 80% of KTU DIAC questions — (1) the symbol, (2) the symmetric V-I curve, (3) the breakover condition $\alpha_1 M_1 + \alpha_2 M_2 = 1$, and (4) the RMS output formula. The rest is engineering judgement.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
