<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# TRIAC and GTOs — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 TRIAC — Triode for Alternating Current

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **TRIAC** (bidirectional triode thyristor) is a five-layer, three-terminal (MT1, MT2, Gate) power semiconductor device that can conduct current in **both directions** (bidirectional) when triggered, and is functionally equivalent to **two SCRs connected in anti-parallel** integrated on a single silicon wafer.

**Symbol:**
```
         MT2
          |
          |
          |
          |-----------|\
          |           | \
          |           |  >--- MT1
          |           | /
          |-----------|/
          |
          G
```

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Way Light Switch"
> [!NOTE]
> Think of a TRIAC as a **two-way water valve** that works on AC supply. In your home, a normal wall switch (built on a TRIAC inside a dimmer module) can turn a bulb ON/OFF on **both halves of the AC sine wave** — that is, during the positive half-cycle *and* the negative half-cycle. A regular SCR can only work on one half-cycle, like a one-way valve. The TRIAC = "valve that opens in either direction, when given a small nudge from the gate."

**Terminals of a TRIAC:**
- **MT1** — Main Terminal 1 (reference terminal)
- **MT2** — Main Terminal 2 (load terminal)
- **G** — Gate (control terminal)

**Key ratings/physical metrics (bolded):**
- Breakover voltage $V_{BO}$ ≈ **400 V – 1200 V** (typical commercial range)
- RMS on-state current $I_{T(RMS)}$ ≈ **1 A – 40 A**
- Holding current $I_H$ ≈ **5 mA – 50 mA**
- Gate trigger current $I_{GT}$ ≈ **5 mA – 50 mA** (mode I), **10 mA – 100 mA** (other modes)

---

## 1.2 GTO — Gate Turn-Off Thyristor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **GTO (Gate Turn-Off thyristor)** is a four-layer (PNPN), three-terminal power semiconductor device that, like an SCR, can be **turned ON** by a positive gate pulse, but *uniquely* can also be **turned OFF** by a large **negative gate current pulse**, without requiring any external commutation circuitry.

**Symbol:**
```
         A (Anode)
          |
          |-----------|\
          |           | \
          |           |  >--- K (Cathode)
          |           | /
          |-----------|/
          |
          G (Gate)
```

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Relay"
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **latching relay** that, once you push the "ON" button (small positive gate pulse), the relay stays latched *and the contacts stay closed*. For an ordinary relay, you need to break the main circuit mechanically to turn it off. A **GTO is a relay that you can switch OFF simply by pushing a strong "OFF" button (negative gate pulse)**. The SCR is the dumb relay — once ON, only the main current going to zero can turn it off. The GTO is the smart relay with bidirectional control.

**Why the GTO matters (KTU highlight):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Conventional SCRs require bulky **forced commutation circuits** (LC resonating tanks, auxiliary thyristors) to be turned off. The GTO eliminates most of these bulky components, making inverter and chopper circuits **smaller, lighter, and more efficient** — a key advantage in modern power electronics.

**Key physical metrics (bolded):**
- Forward breakover voltage $V_{BO}$ ≈ **1200 V – 4500 V**
- RMS on-state current ≈ **100 A – 2500 A**
- Turn-off gain $\beta_{off} \approx \mathbf{3 - 5}$ (typical)
- Turn-off time $t_q \approx \mathbf{5\ \mu s - 25\ \mu s}$
- Storage time $t_s \approx \mathbf{2\ \mu s - 10\ \mu s}$
- Forward voltage drop $V_T \approx \mathbf{1.5\ V - 3\ V}$ (higher than SCR due to heavier gate doping)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# TRIAC and GTOs — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 TRIAC — Construction, Operation, and Modes

### Construction (Layer Structure)

A TRIAC is a five-layer device, conceptually equivalent to **two inverse-parallel-connected SCRs**:

| Layer Sequence | Function |
|---|---|
| P₁ | Anode region of SCR-1 |
| N₁ | Base region of SCR-1 |
| P₂ | Common gate & cathode-base region |
| N₂ | Cathode region of SCR-1 / Anode region of SCR-2 |
| P₃ | Cathode region of SCR-2 |

> [!NOTE]
> The middle **P₂ layer** acts as the gate and is the **common junction** between the two anti-parallel SCR structures.

---

### The Four Triggering Modes (Quadrants of Operation)

The TRIAC operates in **four quadrants** depending on the **polarity of MT2 w.r.t. MT1** and the **polarity of gate signal w.r.t. MT1**:

| Mode | MT2 | Gate | $V_{MT2-MT1}$ | $I_{GT}$ Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode I (Q1)** | **+** | **+** | +V | **Highest sensitivity** (most preferred mode) |
| **Mode II (Q2)** | **+** | **−** | +V | Moderate |
| **Mode III (Q3)** | **−** | **−** | −V | Moderate |
| **Mode IV (Q4)** | **−** | **+** | −V | Lowest sensitivity (least preferred) |

**Logic behind each mode (intuitive explanation):**
- **Mode I:** MT2 +, Gate + ⇒ Forward-biases the main P₁-N₁-P₂-N₂ junction. Electron injection from gate turns on the device. **Most reliable triggering.**
- **Mode II:** MT2 +, Gate − ⇒ Uses the **remote gate** effect; relies on lateral current flow and works but with **higher required gate current**.
- **Mode III:** MT2 −, Gate − ⇒ Symmetric to Mode I in the negative half-cycle.
- **Mode IV:** MT2 −, Gate + ⇒ Most difficult mode due to reverse-biased main junction during triggering. Requires the **largest gate current**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently assume all four modes of TRIAC operation require the same gate current. They do NOT — Mode IV requires **2 to 4 times** the gate current of Mode I. Always design your gate drive to **guarantee triggering in the worst-case mode (Q4)**.

---

### Static V-I Characteristics of TRIAC

The V-I characteristic is symmetric about the origin (it works on both halves of the AC cycle):

```
      I
      |
   I_L|          /  /
      |         /  /
      |        /  /
      |_______/__/_______ V
             / /
   -V_BO  --/ /
   -I_L  -----
```

- In **Quadrant I**: device turns on when $V_{MT2-MT1} = +V_{BO1}$ OR gate is pulsed in Mode I/II.
- In **Quadrant III**: device turns on when $V_{MT2-MT1} = -V_{BO3}$ OR gate is pulsed in Mode III/IV.
- Once conducting, voltage drop ≈ **1.5 V – 2 V**.

---

## 2.2 GTO — Detailed Operating Theory

### Turn-ON Mechanism (similar to SCR)

1. Forward bias the anode-cathode junction ($V_{AK} > 0$).
2. Apply a **positive gate pulse** ($I_G > 0$).
3. The regenerative PNP-NPN feedback latches the device into conduction.
4. **Anode current $I_A$ rises above latching current $I_L$** — device stays ON even after gate pulse is removed.

> [!NOTE]
> $I_L > I_H$ always holds (latching current is the current at which the device just latches during turn-on; holding current is the minimum current to maintain conduction).

### Turn-OFF Mechanism (UNIQUE to GTO)

This is what makes a GTO special:

1. Anode current $I_A$ is flowing through the device.
2. Apply a **large negative gate current pulse** of amplitude $\vert I_{GQ} \vert$.
3. The NPN transistor (lower) is starved of base drive — its collector current drops.
4. The PNP transistor (upper) loses its base drive too — its collector current drops.
5. **Regenerative feedback breaks**, and the device snaps OFF.
6. The anode current is forced to zero.

**Critical Design Equation:**

$$\boxed{\beta_{off} \;=\; \frac{I_A}{I_{GQ}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $\beta_{off}$ is the **turn-off gain**, typically **3 to 5** in commercial GTOs. This means if you want to interrupt **100 A** of anode current, you must apply a negative gate current of about **20 A to 33 A** — a substantial current pulse!

### Why GTOs Need Heavy Gate Doping
Compared to a standard SCR:
- The cathode of the GTO is **segmented** into many small finger-like regions (interdigitation).
- This **reduces lateral resistance** under the cathode, allowing the negative gate current to **sweep out carriers efficiently** during turn-off.
- Trade-off: this heavier doping **increases forward voltage drop** ($V_T \approx$ 1.5 V–3 V vs. 1.2 V for SCR).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### TRIAC Equations

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{MT2-MT1} = \pm V_{BO}$ | Breakover voltage (both polarities) | V |
| $P_{TRIAC} = V_T \cdot I_{T(RMS)}$ | Steady-state conduction loss | W |
| $V_{MT2-MT1}(ON) \approx 1.5$ to $2$ V | On-state voltage drop | V |
| $I_H < I_L$ | Holding current less than latching current | A |

### GTO Equations

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta_{off} = I_A \big/ \vert I_{GQ} \vert$ | Turn-off gain | dimensionless |
| $t_q = t_s + t_f$ | Total turn-off time (storage + fall) | s |
| $\eta_{GTO} \approx \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{sw} + P_{cond}}$ | Converter efficiency | dimensionless |
| $E_{off} = \int_{0}^{t_q} v(t)\, i(t)\, dt$ | Turn-off switching energy loss | J |
| $V_{T(on)} \approx 1.5$ to $3$ V | On-state voltage drop (GTO) | V |

### Comparing TRIAC vs SCR vs GTO (KTU Comparison Table)

| Parameter | SCR | TRIAC | GTO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bidirectional? | No | **Yes** | No |
| Turn-off by gate? | No | No | **Yes** |
| Forced commutation needed? | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No** |
| Forward drop $V_T$ | 1.2 V (lowest) | 1.5–2 V | 1.5–3 V (highest) |
| $dv/dt$ rating | Low | Low | Moderate |
| Typical application | Rectifiers, phase control | AC dimmers, fan regulators | Inverters, choppers |

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **TRIACs** dominate the **consumer-grade AC power control** market: ₹200 light dimmers, ₹400 ceiling fan regulators, and washing-machine motor control modules.
> - **GTOs** are the workhorses of **medium-power industrial drives**: railway traction inverters (older EMUs), high-power DC motor drives in steel rolling mills, static VAR compensators in HVDC substations, and large UPS systems. Modern systems increasingly replace GTOs with IGBTs, but GTO theory remains a **KTU board exam staple**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# TRIAC and GTOs — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Negative Gate Current for GTO Turn-Off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is essential for KTU 14-mark problems on GTO design. Understand every step.

### Problem Setup
A GTO is carrying an anode current $I_A$. We wish to find the **minimum magnitude** of the negative gate current pulse $\vert I_{GQ} \vert$ that will successfully turn it OFF.

### Step 1 — Two-Transistor Analogy of a Thyristor
Any PNPN device can be modeled as a **PNP transistor Q1** (top half) and an **NPN transistor Q2** (bottom half) cross-coupled. The standard relationships are:

$$\alpha_1 = \text{common-base current gain of PNP (Q1)}$$

$$\alpha_2 = \text{common-base current gain of NPN (Q2)}$$

The total anode current of the thyristor is:

$$I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_A + I_{co}$$

where $I_{co}$ is the thermally generated leakage current at the collector junction.

**Explanation:** The anode current is the sum of (a) the PNP's collected fraction $\alpha_1 I_A$, (b) the NPN's collected fraction $\alpha_2 I_A$, and (c) the leakage. This is the standard **Shockley two-transistor model**.

### Step 2 — Rearranging the Equation

$$I_A - \alpha_1 I_A - \alpha_2 I_A = I_{co}$$

$$I_A \bigl(1 - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2\bigr) = I_{co}$$

$$I_A = \frac{I_{co}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Explanation:** This shows that as $(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \to 1$, $I_A \to \infty$ — the latching action.

### Step 3 — Latching Condition

$$\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Device is ON}$$

**Explanation:** When the sum of the alphas reaches unity, regenerative feedback drives the device into saturation.

### Step 4 — Turn-Off Condition (Injecting Negative Gate Current)

When a negative gate current $\vert I_{GQ} \vert$ is applied, the NPN base current is reduced. The anode current is now expressed as:

$$I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_A + I_{co} - \vert I_{GQ} \vert$$

**Explanation:** The negative gate current subtracts from the carriers that would otherwise sustain NPN conduction — it actively pulls charge out of the NPN base.

### Step 5 — Solving for the Required Gate Current

$$I_A \bigl(1 - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2\bigr) = I_{co} - \vert I_{GQ} \vert$$

For the device to turn off, we require $I_A \to 0$, which means:

$$\vert I_{GQ} \vert \geq I_{co}$$

But for a **practical** turn-off under load with anode current $I_A$ flowing, the gate must extract enough carriers to break regeneration. Rearranging:

$$\vert I_{GQ} \vert = I_A \,\bigl(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1\bigr) + I_{co}$$

### Step 6 — Defining Turn-Off Gain

$$\boxed{\beta_{off} \;=\; \frac{I_A}{\vert I_{GQ} \vert} \;=\; \frac{1}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1 + \dfrac{I_{co}}{I_A}}}$$

**Explanation:** If leakage is negligible ($I_{co} \ll I_A$) and the alphas are stable:

$$\beta_{off} \approx \frac{1}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1}$$

For typical GTOs where $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ is just slightly greater than 1 (because we need turn-off to be possible), $\beta_{off}$ comes out to be in the range **3 to 5**, as experimentally observed.

### Step 7 — Numerical Verification Example

> [!EXAMPLE]
> Given: $\alpha_1 = 0.32$, $\alpha_2 = 0.78$, $I_A = 100$ A, neglect $I_{co}$.
>
> Compute $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1 = 0.32 + 0.78 - 1 = 0.10$
>
> Compute $\beta_{off} = \dfrac{1}{0.10} = 10$
>
> Compute $\vert I_{GQ} \vert = \dfrac{I_A}{\beta_{off}} = \dfrac{100}{10} = 10$ A
>
> **Interpretation:** To interrupt 100 A of anode current, the gate driver must supply a **−10 A pulse**. This matches textbook turn-off gain values for well-designed GTOs.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Triggering Modes (TRIAC)

A KTU-favourite 7-mark question asks for a comparative analysis of TRIAC modes. Here is the structured analytical matrix:

| Parameter | Mode I (Q1) | Mode II (Q2) | Mode III (Q3) | Mode IV (Q4) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{MT2-MT1}$ polarity | + | + | − | − |
| $V_{G-MT1}$ polarity | + | − | − | + |
| Sensitive region triggered | P₁-N₁-P₂-N₂ main path | Remote gate via P₂-N₂ | Symmetric to Mode I | Reverse, hardest path |
| Typical $I_{GT}$ | 5–25 mA (lowest) | 25–75 mA | 25–75 mA | 50–100 mA (highest) |
| Practical use | **Preferred** | Sometimes used | Sometimes used | Avoided if possible |

---

## 3.3 Switching Loss Derivation (GTO)

The total switching loss per cycle for a GTO is the sum of turn-on loss $E_{on}$ and turn-off loss $E_{off}$:

$$E_{sw} = E_{on} + E_{off}$$

Assuming linear rise and fall of current with constant voltage:

$$E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} \, V_{CC} \, I_A \, t_{on}$$

$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \, V_{CC} \, I_A \, t_{off}$$

**Explanation:** During turn-on, the device transitions from blocking $V_{CC}$ to conducting $I_A$. The triangular area under the $v \cdot i$ curve gives the energy lost.

Total switching power dissipation:

$$P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off})$$

where $f_{sw}$ is the switching frequency.

### Numerical Example
> [!EXAMPLE]
> Given: $V_{CC} = 600$ V, $I_A = 200$ A, $t_{on} = 2\ \mu s$, $t_{off} = 10\ \mu s$, $f_{sw} = 1$ kHz.
>
> Compute $E_{on} = 0.5 \times 600 \times 200 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 0.12$ J
>
> Compute $E_{off} = 0.5 \times 600 \times 200 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 0.6$ J
>
> Compute $P_{sw} = 1000 \times (0.12 + 0.6) = 720$ W
>
> **Interpretation:** Notice that turn-off loss is **5×** turn-on loss for the GTO. This is why GTOs run hot and require large heatsinks — exactly the issue that motivated IGBT development.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# TRIAC and GTOs — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — TRIAC as Two Anti-Parallel SCRs

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below shows the **functional equivalence** between a TRIAC and two anti-parallel SCRs, with the gate driver feeding both.

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeAC["AC SOURCE 230V 50Hz"]
    nodeMT2["MT2 MAIN TERMINAL 2"]
    nodeMT1["MT1 MAIN TERMINAL 1"]
    nodeLoad["LOAD LAMP OR MOTOR"]
    nodeGate["GATE G TERMINAL"]
    nodeGdriver["PULSE GENERATOR AND ISOLATION"]
    nodeSCR1["SCR 1 FORWARD HALF CYCLE"]
    nodeSCR2["SCR 2 REVERSE HALF CYCLE"]
    nodeDiode1["ANTI PARALLEL DIODE EQUIVALENT"]
    nodeMode["OPERATING QUADRANT DETECTOR"]

    nodeAC --> nodeMT2
    nodeMT2 --> nodeSCR1
    nodeMT2 --> nodeSCR2
    nodeSCR1 --> nodeMT1
    nodeSCR2 --> nodeMT1
    nodeMT1 --> nodeLoad
    nodeGdriver --> nodeGate
    nodeGate --> nodeMode
    nodeMode --> nodeSCR1
    nodeMode --> nodeSCR2
    nodeDiode1 -.equivalent.-> nodeSCR1
    nodeDiode1 -.equivalent.-> nodeSCR2

    style nodeAC fill:#FFE4B5
    style nodeMT2 fill:#90EE90
    style nodeMT1 fill:#90EE90
    style nodeGate fill:#FFB6C1
    style nodeLoad fill:#ADD8E6
    style nodeSCR1 fill:#FFFFE0
    style nodeSCR2 fill:#FFFFE0
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The AC source feeds MT2.
- MT2 connects to the anodes of both internal SCR structures (SCR1 and SCR2).
- The cathodes of both internal SCRs join at MT1.
- MT1 returns through the load.
- A pulse generator at the gate decides which half-cycle to trigger based on the **operating quadrant detector**.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — GTO Turn-On / Turn-Off Driver Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeCtrl["CONTROL CIRCUIT MICROCONTROLLER OR DSP"]
    nodeOnPulse["POSITIVE GATE PULSE GENERATOR TURN ON"]
    nodeOffPulse["NEGATIVE GATE PULSE GENERATOR TURN OFF"]
    nodeIso["OPTICAL OR PULSE ISOLATION TRANSFORMER"]
    nodeGate["GTO GATE TERMINAL"]
    nodeAnode["ANODE A LOAD PLUS"]
    nodeCathode["CATHODE K LOAD MINUS"]
    nodeLoadDC["DC LOAD OR INVERTER STAGE"]
    nodeSnubber["SNUBBER CIRCUIT RC OR RCD"]

    nodeCtrl --> nodeOnPulse
    nodeCtrl --> nodeOffPulse
    nodeOnPulse --> nodeIso
    nodeOffPulse --> nodeIso
    nodeIso --> nodeGate
    nodeGate --> nodeCathode
    nodeAnode --> nodeLoadDC
    nodeLoadDC --> nodeCathode
    nodeSnubber -.protects.-> nodeAnode
    nodeSnubber -.protects.-> nodeCathode

    style nodeCtrl fill:#FFD700
    style nodeOnPulse fill:#98FB98
    style nodeOffPulse fill:#FFA07A
    style nodeIso fill:#DDA0DD
    style nodeSnubber fill:#F0E68C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **controller** decides when to turn the GTO ON and OFF.
- It fires either a **positive pulse generator** (turn-on) or a **negative pulse generator** (turn-off).
- Both pass through an **isolation barrier** (for safety and noise rejection).
- The gate receives the pulse; the anode-cathode handles the main load current.
- A **snubber circuit** (RCD) sits across the device to limit $dv/dt$ and absorb turn-off switching losses.

---

## 4.3 TRIAC V-I Characteristic (Block Functional View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeQ1["Q1 MODE MT2 PLUS GATE PLUS FORWARD TRIGGER"]
    nodeQ3["Q3 MODE MT2 MINUS GATE MINUS REVERSE TRIGGER"]
    nodeVBO["BREAKOVER VOLTAGE VBO"]
    nodeOnstate["ON STATE V APPROX 1.5 TO 2 V"]
    nodeIH["HOLDING CURRENT IH"]

    nodeQ1 --> nodeVBO
    nodeQ3 --> nodeVBO
    nodeVBO --> nodeOnstate
    nodeOnstate --> nodeIH
    nodeIH -.device turns off below.-> nodeQ1

    style nodeQ1 fill:#90EE90
    style nodeQ3 fill:#FFB6C1
    style nodeOnstate fill:#FFFFE0
```

**Reading:** The TRIAC stays in OFF state until either breakover voltage is reached **or** the appropriate gate mode is triggered. Once on, it stays on until the load current falls below the holding current $I_H$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# TRIAC and GTOs — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a TRIAC. Mention its terminals.**

**Model Answer:**
> A TRIAC (Triode for Alternating Current) is a bidirectional power semiconductor device that can conduct current in **both directions** when triggered, and is functionally equivalent to **two SCRs connected in anti-parallel** integrated on a single silicon chip.
>
> It has **three terminals**: **MT1** (Main Terminal 1), **MT2** (Main Terminal 2), and **G** (Gate). It operates with both polarities of main terminal voltage, making it ideal for AC phase-control applications like lamp dimmers and fan regulators.
>
> (3 marks: 1 definition + 1 equivalent circuit + 1 terminal list)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any three advantages of a GTO over a conventional SCR.**

**Model Answer:**
> 1. **Eliminates forced commutation** — A GTO can be turned OFF by a negative gate pulse, whereas an SCR requires bulky external LC commutation circuits.
> 2. **Faster switching** — Turn-off time $t_q$ is in the range of **5–25 µs**, allowing higher switching frequencies than the SCR (which has $t_q$ in the range of 30–100 µs).
> 3. **Smaller and lighter inverter/chopper circuits** — Removal of commutation hardware reduces weight, volume, and cost of the overall converter.
>
> (3 marks: 1 mark each)

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **four modes of operation of a TRIAC** using its two-transistor equivalent model. Draw the V-I characteristics and label the breakover voltages. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **turn-off mechanism of a GTO** using the two-transistor analogy. Derive the expression for turn-off gain $\beta_{off}$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Circuit Diagram:**
Draw a TRIAC symbol with MT1, MT2, and G. Show the AC source $v_s$ feeding MT2 through a load, MT1 returning to the source, and gate connected through a pulse generator.

**Two-Transistor Model:** The TRIAC is split into **T1 (PNP, upper)** and **T2 (NPN, lower)** cross-coupled. The gate drives the base of T2.

**Four Modes (each mode = 1.5 marks):**

| Mode | $V_{MT2-MT1}$ | $V_G$ | Triggering Path | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | + | + | T2 (NPN) ON ⇒ T1 (PNP) ON ⇒ latched | Highest |
| II | + | − | Remote gate via $I_{G}$ flows laterally | Moderate |
| III | − | − | Mirror of Mode I in negative half-cycle | Moderate |
| IV | − | + | Mirror of Mode II, hardest path | Lowest |

**V-I Characteristic:** Plot $I$ vs $V$ with breakover at $\pm V_{BO1}$ and $\pm V_{BO3}$, on-state region around $\pm 1.5$ V, and the gate-triggered region inside the breakover curve.

**Conclusion:** Mode I and Mode III are the most reliable; practical TRIAC trigger circuits (e.g., DIAC-assisted) are designed to fire in **Quadrant I** for maximum sensitivity.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Two-Transistor Analogy of GTO (same as SCR):**
- Upper transistor Q1: PNP
- Lower transistor Q2: NPN
- Anode current $I_A$ flows into the collector of Q1.

**Turn-ON:** Apply $I_G > 0$ ⇒ Q2 turns on ⇒ Q1 turns on ⇒ regenerative latching. The condition for latching is $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$.

**Turn-OFF:** Apply a large negative gate current $\vert I_{GQ} \vert$. This extracts carriers from the base of Q2, breaking the regenerative loop.

**Derivation:**

$$I_A = \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_A + I_{co} - \vert I_{GQ} \vert$$

Rearranging:

$$\vert I_{GQ} \vert = I_A (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1) + I_{co}$$

Neglecting $I_{co}$:

$$\boxed{\beta_{off} = \frac{I_A}{\vert I_{GQ} \vert} \approx \frac{1}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1}}$$

**Numerical illustration (1 mark):**
For $\alpha_1 = 0.3$, $\alpha_2 = 0.8$, $I_A = 200$ A:

$$\beta_{off} = \frac{1}{0.3 + 0.8 - 1} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10$$

$$\vert I_{GQ} \vert = \frac{200}{10} = 20\ \text{A}$$

**Conclusion:** The gate drive must supply a hefty negative pulse (~20 A) to interrupt 200 A of anode current — this is the central design challenge for GTO-based inverters.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare the construction, V-I characteristics, and triggering modes of **TRIAC and SCR**. Why is the TRIAC preferred for AC phase-control circuits? (7 marks)

**(b)** A GTO is operating in a chopper circuit. Given $V_{CC} = 400$ V, $I_A = 150$ A, turn-off time $t_q = 8\ \mu s$, $\beta_{off} = 5$. Calculate:
   (i) The required negative gate current pulse $\vert I_{GQ} \vert$.
   (ii) The turn-off switching energy loss $E_{off}$ assuming linear fall.
   (iii) Comment on the thermal management requirement of the GTO at a switching frequency of 2 kHz. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | SCR | TRIAC |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | 4 (PNPN) | 5 (PNPN with extra region) |
| Direction of conduction | Unidirectional | **Bidirectional** |
| Terminals | A, K, G | MT1, MT2, G |
| Triggering modes | One (forward bias + gate +) | **Four** (Q1 to Q4) |
| Equivalent circuit | Single device | Two anti-parallel SCRs |
| Forced commutation needed? | Yes | Yes (in AC at current zero) |
| V-I characteristic | Only Q1 and Q3 quadrant | Symmetric (Q1 & Q3) |
| Typical applications | Rectifiers, DC drives | AC dimmers, fan regulators |

**Why TRIAC for AC phase control (3 marks):**
1. The AC mains alternate polarity every half-cycle; a TRIAC handles both halves without needing a rectifier bridge.
2. Firing angle control from 0 to 180° works seamlessly in both halves.
3. Single device replaces two SCRs (with their separate gate drives and snubbers) → cheaper, simpler, smaller PCB.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**(i) Negative gate current:**

$$\vert I_{GQ} \vert = \frac{I_A}{\beta_{off}} = \frac{150}{5} = 30\ \text{A}$$

**(ii) Turn-off switching energy:**

$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} \, V_{CC} \, I_A \, t_q = \frac{1}{2} \times 400 \times 150 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 0.24\ \text{J}$$

**(iii) Switching power loss:**

$$P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot E_{off} = 2000 \times 0.24 = 480\ \text{W}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
The GTO dissipates nearly **500 W** of switching loss alone. This mandates:
- A **large aluminium heatsink** with forced-air cooling or even liquid cooling.
- Possibly a **snubber circuit** to divert some of this loss.
- A reduction in switching frequency to manage thermal budget.
- This thermal burden is precisely why modern designs prefer **IGBTs** for new designs — they switch 5–10× faster, reducing $E_{off}$ dramatically.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks on TRIAC/GTO questions:**
>
> 1. **Drawing only one half** of the TRIAC V-I characteristic. The TRIAC is bidirectional — examiners expect the symmetric plot covering **both Q1 and Q3 quadrants**. (Deduct 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Confusing MT1 with cathode and MT2 with anode.** MT1 is the *reference* terminal, not the cathode. The gate signal is always measured with respect to MT1, not MT2. (Deduct 1 mark.)
> 3. **Forgetting that TRIAC and GTO also need snubbers.** Many students think snubbers are only for SCRs. Incorrect — TRIAC has low $dv/dt$ rating, and GTOs have huge turn-off losses. Snubber design is **mandatory** in any practical circuit. (Deduct 1–2 marks.)
> 4. **Storing the wrong polarity for the GTO turn-off gate pulse.** The pulse must be **negative** w.r.t. cathode, with **large amplitude** (typical 1/3 to 1/5 of anode current). Writing "positive gate pulse for turn-off" = full zero for that part.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation step** in $\beta_{off}$. Examiners allocate 3–4 marks purely for the derivation. Writing only the final formula = lose most of those marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **TRIAC = two SCRs in anti-parallel** with a common gate. Bidirectional, three-terminal (MT1, MT2, G).
- **Four triggering modes:** Q1 (most sensitive), Q2, Q3, Q4 (least sensitive). Practical design targets **Quadrant I** firing.
- **TRIAC V-I characteristic** is symmetric about origin; breakover in both polarities.
- **GTO can be turned OFF** by a large negative gate pulse — its defining advantage over the SCR.
- **Turn-off gain** $\beta_{off} = \dfrac{I_A}{\vert I_{GQ} \vert} \approx \dfrac{1}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1}$, typically **3 to 5**.
- **Turn-off time** $t_q = t_s + t_f$ where $t_s$ is storage time and $t_f$ is fall time.
- **Forward voltage drop** of GTO (1.5–3 V) is **higher** than SCR (~1.2 V) because of heavier cathode interdigitation.
- **Switching losses** are dominated by $E_{off}$ for GTO; this is its biggest weakness.
- **GTO needs snubber** (RCD type) across it; $dv/dt$ and $di/dt$ protection is mandatory.
- **TRIAC applications:** AC lamp dimmers, fan regulators, heater control, washing-machine motor control, small universal motor drives.
- **GTO applications:** DC choppers, VVVF inverters (older 3-φ drives), HVDC valve groups, static VAR compensators, large UPS.
- **GTO has been largely replaced** by **IGBT** in new designs because IGBTs switch faster and have lower $E_{off}$, but **GTO theory remains in the KTU syllabus** and is heavily tested.
- **Latching current $I_L$ > Holding current $I_H$** — remember this ordering (commonly confused in exams).
- **MT1 is the reference terminal** for all gate voltage and current measurements in a TRIAC — not MT2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: V-I Characteristics of SCR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** V-I Characteristics of SCR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)** is a four-layer (PNPN), three-junction, three-terminal (Anode-A, Cathode-K, Gate-G) unidirectional power semiconductor device that acts as a **controllable latching switch**. Its operational states are entirely described by the **Static V-I Characteristics Curve**, which plots the instantaneous Anode-to-Cathode voltage ($V_{AK}$) on the X-axis against the Anode current ($I_A$) on the Y-axis, parameterized by the Gate current ($I_G$).

The curve is divided into **three principal regions** in the forward quadrant and **two regions** in the reverse quadrant, each defining a distinct physical mode of operation inside the silicon die.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The V-I characteristic of an SCR is the most fundamental and high-yield topic in Module 1. It is mandatory to label all critical points: **Forward Breakover Voltage ($V_{BO}$)**, **Reverse Breakdown Voltage ($V_{BR}$)**, **Holding Current ($I_H$)**, and **Latching Current ($I_L$)**. Missing these labels in diagrams will cost you **2 marks minimum** in the board exam.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floodgate" Model

Imagine an **irrigation floodgate** carrying water (anode current $I_A$) down a hill (anode-cathode voltage $V_{AK}$):

- The **Gate terminal** is the *winch operator* standing beside the gate.
- Without gate signal, the gate stays **mechanically locked** even if water pressure builds up — this is the **Forward Blocking (OFF) State**.
- A small **gate trigger pulse** ($I_G > 0$) releases the latch — water rushes through — this is **Forward Conduction (ON) State**.
- Once full flow begins, the winch operator can **walk away**; the gate stays open on its own due to water pressure. This is the **Latching** property.
- The flow cannot stop just by removing the gate signal; the water level must drop below a critical minimum. This minimum is the **Holding Current ($I_H$)**.
- If water tries to flow **backwards** (reverse voltage), a heavy **mechanical stop-block** prevents it — this is the **Reverse Blocking** region.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the analogy matters:** It captures the three defining properties of an SCR that students forget: (1) unidirectional conduction, (2) gate-triggered turn-ON, and (3) latching behavior requiring $I_A < I_H$ to turn OFF.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Industrial SCR) |
|---|---|---|
| On-State Voltage Drop | $V_T$ | **1.0 V – 2.5 V** |
| Forward Breakover Voltage | $V_{BO}$ | **200 V – 2500 V** |
| Reverse Breakdown Voltage | $V_{BR}$ | **200 V – 2500 V** |
| Latching Current | $I_L$ | **50 mA – 500 mA** |
| Holding Current | $I_H$ | **10 mA – 100 mA** |
| Gate Trigger Current | $I_{GT}$ | **1 mA – 50 mA** |
| Gate Trigger Voltage | $V_{GT}$ | **0.5 V – 3.0 V** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Operational Modes of the SCR

The static V-I curve of the SCR is fundamentally **piecewise nonlinear** because the device operates on the principle of regenerative internal feedback between its internal NPN and PNP transistor pair. The curve is therefore divided into the following **operational quadrants and regions**:

### Region 1 — Forward Blocking (OFF) State (Quadrant I, OA segment)
- The Anode is **positive** with respect to the Cathode ($V_{AK} > 0$), and $I_G = 0$ (or $I_G < I_{GT}$).
- Junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are **forward biased**; the middle junction $J_2$ is **reverse biased**.
- Only a tiny **leakage current** (a few mA) flows through the device. This leakage is *forward leakage current*, denoted $I_{DRM}$ (DC) or $I_{RRM}$ (repetitive peak).
- The device is in **high-impedance** state, behaving almost like an open switch.
- The curve lies almost on the voltage axis with $I_A \approx 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Conceptual Question:** *"Why does an SCR not conduct even when forward biased?"*
> **Model Answer:** Because the middle junction $J_2$ is reverse biased and the regenerative feedback loop has not been initiated; the device is in its forward blocking mode awaiting a gate trigger.

### Region 2 — Forward Conduction (ON) State (Quadrant I, BC segment)
- Triggered when $V_{AK}$ reaches $V_{BO}$ *without* gate signal, OR when $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ at any $V_{AK} < V_{BO}$.
- All three junctions become **forward biased**.
- The device enters a **low-impedance** state with voltage drop $V_T \approx 1$–$2$ V.
- Current is now limited only by the **external load**, not the SCR itself.
- The curve snaps vertically from point A to point B (the **"turn-on snap"**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Snap-on behavior:** The nearly vertical transition from OFF to ON is a *negative resistance* region in the static curve. In real dynamic switching, however, the device takes a finite turn-ON time $t_{on} \approx 1$–$4 \mu s$ to traverse this region.

### Region 3 — Reverse Blocking State (Quadrant III, OD segment)
- The Cathode is **positive** with respect to the Anode ($V_{AK} < 0$).
- Junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are **reverse biased**; $J_2$ is forward biased.
- Only a small **reverse leakage current** $I_R$ flows (a few mA).
- The device is in OFF state in the reverse direction.
- Operationally similar to a reverse-biased diode.

### Region 4 — Reverse Breakdown (Quadrant III, DE segment)
- When the reverse voltage magnitude exceeds $V_{BR}$, the reverse-biased junctions undergo **avalanche breakdown**.
- Reverse current rises sharply with negligible further increase in voltage.
- This is a **destructive region** — operation here permanently damages the device due to thermal runaway.

### Region 5 — Forward Breakover (Transition from Region 1 → Region 2)
- The point where the forward blocking region **collapses**.
- $V_{AK} = V_{BO}$ causes the middle junction to break down via avalanche multiplication.
- This avalanche current becomes the **base current** of the internal NPN/PNP pair, initiating the **regenerative loop**.
- Once the loop gain $M \cdot \alpha_1 \cdot \alpha_2 \geq 1$ (where $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ are common-base current gains and $M$ is the avalanche multiplication factor), the device **latches ON** permanently until $I_A$ drops below $I_H$.

### Region 6 — Holding and Latching
- **Latching Current ($I_L$)**: The *minimum* anode current that must flow *during the turn-ON process* so that the SCR remains in ON state **even after the gate signal is removed**.
- **Holding Current ($I_H$)**: The *minimum* anode current required to *keep* the SCR in ON state once it is already latched.
- **Key inequality (memorize this):** $\boxed{I_L > I_H}$
- If $I_A$ falls below $I_H$, the regenerative loop collapses and the SCR reverts to its OFF state.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ at breakover | Regenerative turn-ON condition (without gate) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_A \geq 1$ | Two-transistor analog condition for triggering | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $I_{AK} = \dfrac{I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$ | Anode current (two-transistor model) | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | $I_{Latching} > I_{Holding}$ | $I_L > I_H$ — fundamental relationship | A |
| 5 | $V_T = V_{AK(on)} \approx 1$–$2$ V | On-state voltage drop | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $V_{BO} \leq V_{DRM}$ | Breakover must not exceed repetitive peak | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $\vert V_{BR} \vert \leq V_{RRM}$ | Reverse breakdown must not exceed repetitive peak | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $P = V_T \cdot I_A$ | ON-state power dissipation (approx) | Watts (W) |
| 9 | $I_{GT} \uparrow \Rightarrow V_{BO} \downarrow$ | Increasing gate current reduces breakover voltage | A, V |
| 10 | $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$ | Turn-on time = delay time + rise time | seconds (s) |
| 11 | $t_{off} = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$ | Turn-off time = reverse recovery + gate recovery | seconds (s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Understanding the V-I curve enables design of **Phase-Angle Controlled Rectifiers**, **Choppers**, **Inverters**, and **Cycloconverters** in industrial drives. The shape of the curve directly determines the *safe operating area (SOA)* of the SCR, which is plotted on the same axes for thermal and voltage/current stress limits.

## 2.3 Why Higher $I_G$ Reduces $V_{BO}$ — The "Why" Behind the Curves

The breakover voltage is a function of the **internal current gain product** $\alpha_1 \alpha_2$. Without gate current, the condition $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ is met only when avalanche multiplication $M$ in the middle junction is large — meaning high voltage. When $I_G$ is injected, the gate acts as the **base current of the internal NPN transistor**, forcing $\alpha_1$ to rise toward its high-current limit. The condition $M \cdot (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) = 1$ is now satisfied at a *much lower* $M$ — meaning *lower* voltage. Hence, the family of curves shifts **leftward** as $I_G$ increases.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Two-Transistor Model of the SCR

The SCR can be conceptually split into **two coupled bipolar junction transistors**:

- An **NPN transistor** formed by layers N–P–N (Cathode side, top)
- A **PNP transistor** formed by layers P–N–P (Anode side, bottom)

### Step 1 — Define the Anode Current
The total anode current $I_A$ is the sum of the leakage current of the reverse-biased middle junction and the amplified collector currents of both internal transistors.

$$I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2} = \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_A + I_{CO}$$

where:
- $I_{C1}$ = collector current of the NPN transistor
- $I_{C2}$ = collector current of the PNP transistor
- $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ = common-base current gains
- $I_{CO}$ = reverse leakage current of the middle junction $J_2$

### Step 2 — Rearrange to Isolate $I_A$

$$I_A - \alpha_1 I_A - \alpha_2 I_A = I_{CO}$$

$$I_A (1 - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2) = I_{CO}$$

### Step 3 — Final Anode Current Expression

$$I_A = \frac{I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Conversion logic:** This equation shows that as $(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \rightarrow 1$, the denominator $\rightarrow 0$, and $I_A \rightarrow \infty$. Physically, this corresponds to the **breakover point** of the SCR where the device snaps into conduction.

### Step 4 — Turn-ON Criterion
For the SCR to turn ON, we require:

$$1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \leq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$$

When gate current $I_G$ is applied, the effective $\alpha_1$ increases because $I_G$ acts as additional base current. The condition $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ is met at *lower* voltage, shifting the breakover leftward on the V-I curve.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between $I_L$ and $I_H$

### Step 1 — Latching Current
At the moment of turn-ON, the gate signal is still present. The anode current must be **large enough to sustain the conduction** even when the gate signal is removed in the next instant.

$$I_{Latching} = I_L$$

### Step 2 — Holding Current
Once the gate is removed, the device relies entirely on the **regenerative feedback loop** to stay ON. Because the loop gain at lower currents is *less* than the loop gain during turn-ON (when the gate was boosting it), the *minimum* sustaining current is **lower** than $I_L$.

$$I_{Holding} = I_H$$

### Step 3 — Inequality
Since the gate was helping during the turn-ON, the required latching current is greater than the sustaining holding current:

$$\boxed{I_L > I_H}$$

**Engineering significance:** A designer must ensure that the load current $I_L$ (NOT to be confused with latching current) is **always greater than $I_L$** (latching current) at turn-ON, but must drop **below $I_H$** at turn-OFF for the SCR to successfully commutate.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** An SCR has a forward leakage current $I_{CO} = 2$ mA when the forward blocking voltage is $V_{AK} = 100$ V. The common-base gains are $\alpha_1 = 0.3$ and $\alpha_2 = 0.3$. Determine whether the SCR will turn ON. If the gains increase by $0.2$ each due to avalanche multiplication, will the device turn ON at the same voltage?

### Step 1 — Compute the Sum of Gains
$$\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 0.3 + 0.3 = 0.6$$

### Step 2 — Compute Anode Current

$$I_A = \frac{I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)} = \frac{2 \text{ mA}}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{2 \text{ mA}}{0.4} = 5 \text{ mA}$$

**Result:** The current is small. The SCR is in **forward blocking mode** since $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 0.6 < 1$.

### Step 3 — With Avalanche Multiplication
If both gains rise by $0.2$:

$$\alpha_1' = 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.5$$
$$\alpha_2' = 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.5$$
$$\alpha_1' + \alpha_2' = 1.0$$

### Step 4 — New Anode Current

$$I_A' = \frac{2 \text{ mA}}{1 - 1.0} = \frac{2 \text{ mA}}{0} \rightarrow \infty$$

**Result:** The condition $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ has been met — the device has reached **breakover** and snapped into conduction. This confirms that **breakover is precisely the point where the loop gain equals unity**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete V-I Characteristics Curve (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: V-I Curve of SCR]) --> axes[Define Axes: X=VAK, Y=IA]
    axes --> q1[Quadrant I: Forward Bias VAK greater 0]
    axes --> q3[Quadrant III: Reverse Bias VAK less 0]

    q1 --> r1[Region 1: Forward Blocking OFF State]
    r1 --> r1desc[Leakage current IDRM flows, J2 reverse biased]
    r1 --> r1end{Reaches VBO?}

    r1end -- No with IG zero --> r1stay[Device stays OFF]
    r1end -- Yes --> r2[Region 2: Forward Breakover]

    q1 --> r1g[Region 1 variant: Lower VBO with IG]
    r1g --> r1gdesc[Gate current raises alpha1 alpha2 sum]
    r1g --> r2g[Region 2: Turn-ON via Gate]

    r2 --> r3[Region 3: Negative Resistance Transition]
    r3 --> r4[Region 4: Forward Conduction ON State]
    r4 --> r4desc[J1 J2 J3 all forward biased, VT approx 1.5 V]

    r4 --> comm{Q1: Is IA greater than IH?}
    comm -- Yes --> stayon[Device stays ON even if IG removed]
    comm -- No --> turnoff[Device reverts to OFF state]

    q3 --> r5[Region 5: Reverse Blocking]
    r5 --> r5desc[J1 J3 reverse biased, small IR leakage]
    r5 --> r5end{Reaches VBR?}

    r5end -- No --> r5stay[Device safely blocks reverse voltage]
    r5end -- Yes --> r6[Region 6: Reverse Breakdown DESTRUCTIVE]
    r6 --> r6warn[AVALANCHE - Device destroyed by thermal runaway]

    classDef safe fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```
