# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the following terms with reference to the V-I characteristics of an SCR:**
(a) Forward Breakover Voltage ($V_{BO}$)
(b) Holding Current ($I_H$)
(c) Latching Current ($I_L$)

### Model Answer:

**(a) Forward Breakover Voltage ($V_{BO}$):** It is the minimum forward anode-to-cathode voltage at which the SCR transitions from the forward blocking (OFF) state to the forward conduction (ON) state *without* any gate signal applied ($I_G = 0$). Mathematically, it is the voltage at which the regenerative condition $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ is satisfied due to avalanche multiplication in the middle junction $J_2$.

**(b) Holding Current ($I_H$):** It is the *minimum* anode current required to *maintain* the SCR in the ON state once conduction has been established. If the anode current $I_A$ falls below $I_H$, the device reverts to the forward blocking state. Typical range: **10 mA – 100 mA**.

**(c) Latching Current ($I_L$):** It is the *minimum* anode current that must flow *during the turn-ON process* so that the SCR remains in the ON state **even after the gate trigger signal is removed**. Typical range: **50 mA – 500 mA**. Always: $I_L > I_H$.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why does an SCR have a reverse blocking capability? What happens if the reverse voltage exceeds $V_{BR}$?**

### Model Answer:

**Reverse blocking capability:** When the SCR is reverse biased (Cathode positive w.r.t. Anode), junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are reverse biased while $J_2$ is forward biased. The two reverse-biased junctions create a high-impedance path, allowing only a small reverse leakage current $I_R$ (typically a few mA) to flow. This is the **reverse blocking region** of the V-I curve.

**If $V_{AK}$ exceeds $V_{BR}$:** The reverse-biased junctions undergo **avalanche breakdown**, causing a sharp rise in reverse current. This leads to excessive localized heating and **thermal runaway**, permanently destroying the device. Hence, the SCR must always be operated within $\vert V_{AK} \vert < V_{BR}$ in the reverse direction.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat labeled V-I characteristics curve, explain the different operating modes of an SCR. Mark the regions: forward blocking, forward conduction, reverse blocking, and reverse breakdown. Clearly indicate $V_{BO}$, $V_{BR}$, $I_H$, and $I_L$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the two-transistor model expression for the anode current of an SCR and show mathematically how the breakover condition is achieved. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The V-I characteristics curve is plotted with $V_{AK}$ on the X-axis (positive to the right) and $I_A$ on the Y-axis (positive upward). The curve is symmetric in *shape* but with key differences between the forward and reverse quadrants.

**Forward Quadrant (Quadrant I, $V_{AK} > 0$):**
- **Region OA (Forward Blocking):** The curve lies almost on the voltage axis. Anode current is limited to a small forward leakage $I_{DRM}$ (typically 1–10 mA). The middle junction $J_2$ is reverse biased.
- **Point A → B (Breakover / Snap-on):** At $V_{AK} = V_{BO}$, the curve transitions vertically (negative resistance region) into the conduction state. This transition is triggered either by reaching $V_{BO}$ with $I_G = 0$ or by applying $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ at any $V_{AK} < V_{BO}$.
- **Region BC (Forward Conduction):** All three junctions are forward biased. Voltage drop across the SCR is small, $V_T \approx 1$–$2$ V. Anode current is limited by the external load.
- **Points along BC:** $I_L$ is the *latching current* (minimum to sustain ON after gate removal); $I_H$ is the *holding current* (minimum to maintain ON). $I_L > I_H$.

**Reverse Quadrant (Quadrant III, $V_{AK} < 0$):**
- **Region OD (Reverse Blocking):** Only reverse leakage $I_R$ flows; the device blocks reverse voltage.
- **Region DE (Reverse Breakdown):** At $\vert V_{AK} \vert = V_{BR}$, avalanche breakdown occurs. The current rises sharply — this is a destructive region and must be avoided.

**Labeling checklist for the diagram:** $V_{BO}$ on the X-axis at breakover, $V_{BR}$ on the negative X-axis at reverse breakdown, $I_H$ and $I_L$ on the Y-axis in the forward conduction region, and the snap-on vertical line connecting blocking to conduction regions.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The two-transistor model splits the SCR (P-N-P-N) into an **NPN** transistor (top, Cathode side) and a **PNP** transistor (bottom, Anode side). Their collectors are cross-coupled to each other's bases, creating a **regenerative feedback loop**.

Let $I_A$ be the anode current, $I_K$ be the cathode current, and $I_G$ be the gate current. By KCL at the anode node:

$$I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2} + I_{CO}$$

where $I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_K$ (collector current of NPN) and $I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_A$ (collector current of PNP). Also $I_K = I_A + I_G$ from the cathode node.

Substituting:

$$I_A = \alpha_1 (I_A + I_G) + \alpha_2 I_A + I_{CO}$$

$$I_A - \alpha_1 I_A - \alpha_2 I_A = \alpha_1 I_G + I_{CO}$$

$$I_A (1 - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2) = I_{CO} + \alpha_1 I_G$$

$$\boxed{I_A = \frac{I_{CO} + \alpha_1 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}}$$

**Breakover condition:** When $I_G = 0$ and $V_{AK}$ rises, avalanche multiplication factor $M$ increases the effective gains. Breakover occurs when the denominator vanishes:

$$1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$$

At this point, the anode current $I_A \to \infty$ (in the ideal model), causing the device to snap into conduction. This is the mathematical foundation of the V-I curve's breakover point.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Compare and contrast Latching Current ($I_L$) and Holding Current ($I_H$) in an SCR. Why is $I_L > I_H$? Explain with reference to the regenerative feedback mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SCR has the following ratings: $V_{BO} = 600$ V, $V_T = 1.8$ V, $I_L = 80$ mA, $I_H = 20$ mA, $I_{GT} = 15$ mA. The SCR is used in a circuit with load resistance $R_L = 10 \, \Omega$ and supply voltage $V_S = 200$ V DC. Determine:
   (i) Whether the SCR will turn ON at $V_S = 200$ V without gate signal. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The minimum gate current $I_G$ required to trigger the SCR. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The power dissipated in the SCR during conduction. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Parameter | Latching Current $I_L$ | Holding Current $I_H$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Min. anode current to *establish* ON state during turn-ON | Min. anode current to *maintain* ON state after latching |
| Gate signal | Gate is **still present** when this is measured | Gate is **already removed** |
| Magnitude | **Larger** (50–500 mA typical) | **Smaller** (10–100 mA typical) |
| Physical role | Overcomes initial high-impedance state | Sustains already-established low-impedance state |

**Why $I_L > I_H$:** During turn-ON, the device is transitioning from the high-impedance forward blocking state to the low-impedance forward conduction state. The internal transistor pair has not yet been fully driven into saturation. The gate signal is *actively assisting* by providing base current to the internal NPN transistor. To *replace* this external assistance, the anode current must be large enough to maintain the loop gain at unity. Once the device is already ON and the gate is removed, the device is in deep saturation, and the loop gain remains above unity even at a *lower* anode current. Hence, $I_H < I_L$.

Mathematically: at turn-ON, the regenerative condition requires $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$. Since the gate assists $\alpha_1$ during turn-ON, less anode current is needed once the device is saturated. Thus, the holding current (post-saturation) is lower.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Will the SCR turn ON at $V_S = 200$ V without gate signal?**

The supply voltage is $V_S = 200$ V. For turn-ON without gate signal, $V_{AK}$ must reach $V_{BO} = 600$ V. Since $V_S = 200$ V < $V_{BO} = 600$ V, the SCR will **NOT** turn ON without a gate signal.

**Result:** The device remains in forward blocking state. To trigger, we need either a higher supply or a gate signal.

**(ii) Minimum gate current to trigger:**

For turn-ON with gate signal, we need $I_G \geq I_{GT}$. The given gate trigger current is:

$$I_{GT} = 15 \text{ mA}$$

Therefore, the **minimum gate current** is $\boxed{I_G = 15 \text{ mA}}$.

**(iii) Power dissipated in the SCR during conduction:**

Load current:

$$I_A = \frac{V_S - V_T}{R_L} = \frac{200 - 1.8}{10} = 19.82 \text{ A}$$

Power dissipated in SCR:

$$P_{SCR} = V_T \cdot I_A = 1.8 \times 19.82 = 35.68 \text{ W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check the latching condition:** Even if $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ and the SCR turns on, students often forget to verify $I_A > I_L$. If $I_A < I_L$, the SCR will turn ON momentarily but immediately revert to OFF when the gate is removed — and you'll lose 2 marks for missing this.
> 2. **Confusing $I_L$ (latching current) with $I_L$ (load current):** Both are denoted $I_L$ in many textbooks. Always read the symbol in the *context of the sentence*. Latching current is a device property; load current is circuit-dependent.
> 3. **Drawing the V-I curve without labels:** A V-I curve without $V_{BO}$, $V_{BR}$, $I_H$, $I_L$ labeled is incomplete. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for unlabeled diagrams.
> 4. **Wrong inequality:** Writing $I_H > I_L$ instead of $I_L > I_H$ is a *very common* mistake and will cost you a mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the avalanche condition:** When asked "why does breakover occur?", many students write only "the device turns on" without mentioning the regenerative condition $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ or avalanche multiplication in $J_2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read this the night before the exam:**

- The SCR is a **4-layer PNPN, 3-junction, 3-terminal** unidirectional controllable switch.
- The V-I curve has **two quadrants** of operation: **Forward (I)** and **Reverse (III)**.
- **Forward Blocking (OFF):** $V_{AK} > 0$, $I_G < I_{GT}$, $J_2$ reverse biased, only $I_{DRM}$ leakage flows.
- **Forward Conduction (ON):** All three junctions forward biased, $V_T \approx 1$–$2$ V, snap-on behavior.
- **Reverse Blocking:** $V_{AK} < 0$, $J_1$ and $J_3$ reverse biased, only $I_R$ leakage flows.
- **Reverse Breakdown:** $\vert V_{AK} \vert \geq V_{BR}$ — **destructive**, must be avoided.
- **$V_{BO}$** = Forward Breakover Voltage (turn-ON voltage without gate).
- **$V_{BR}$** = Reverse Breakdown Voltage (max reverse voltage before destruction).
- **$I_L$** = Latching Current (min anode current at turn-ON with gate still applied).
- **$I_H$** = Holding Current (min anode current to *maintain* ON without gate).
- **$I_L > I_H$** — memorize this inequality.
- **$I_{GT}$** = Gate Trigger Current (min gate current to initiate turn-ON).
- **$V_{GT}$** = Gate Trigger Voltage.
- **Increasing $I_G$** → reduces $V_{BO}$ (curves shift left).
- **Two-transistor model:** $I_A = \dfrac{I_{CO} + \alpha_1 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$.
- **Breakover condition:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ (loop gain equals unity).
- **On-state power dissipation:** $P = V_T \cdot I_A$.
- **Turn-ON time:** $t_{on} = t_d + t_r \approx 1$–$4 \, \mu s$.
- **Turn-OFF time:** $t_{off} = t_{rr} + t_{gr} \approx 10$–$100 \, \mu s$.
- **Always operate the SCR within its Safe Operating Area (SOA).**
- **The SCR cannot be turned OFF by removing the gate** — anode current must drop below $I_H$ (natural or forced commutation).
- **Applications:** Phase-controlled rectifiers, AC voltage regulators, DC drives, choppers, inverters, cycloconverters, static switches, and overvoltage protection (crowbar circuits).
- **Family of curves:** Always mention that the V-I curve is plotted for *multiple values* of $I_G$ — it is a *parameterized family*, not a single curve.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Power Devices in Renewable Energy Systems and Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application of Power Devices in Renewable Energy Systems and Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Devices in Renewable Energy Systems & Electric Vehicles**
> *Power semiconductor devices* (such as **IGBTs**, **MOSFETs**, **SiC MOSFETs**, **GaN HEMTs**, **Thyristors/SCRs**, and **GTOs**) are the active switching elements that condition, convert, and control electrical energy between sources (solar PV, wind turbines, batteries, fuel cells) and loads (EV traction motors, grid, auxiliary systems). In the context of KTU PCBRT503, these devices form the **backbone of all power electronic converters** — DC-DC choppers, DC-AC inverters, AC-DC rectifiers, and AC-AC cycloconverters — that govern efficiency, switching frequency, power density, and reliability of modern energy systems.

In a Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) plant, the raw DC output of the PV array must be stepped up (boosted), converted to AC, filtered, and synchronized with the grid. In an Electric Vehicle (EV), the battery's DC supply must be inverted to a variable-frequency variable-voltage AC drive for the traction motor. Every one of these stages is built around a **power semiconductor switch** that operates in **cut-off (OFF)** or **saturation (ON)** states, ideally consuming negligible power in either state.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Traffic-Police" Analogy for Power Devices**
> Imagine a multi-lane highway where **electricity is the traffic**, and **power devices are traffic policemen** standing at junctions. They decide:
> - **When** current may flow (ON-state → green signal)
> - **When** current must stop (OFF-state → red signal)
> - **How fast** it flows (PWM duty-cycle → timing of signal changes)
>
> Just as a smart traffic system reduces congestion and fuel waste, a smart power device reduces **switching loss**, **conduction loss**, and **harmonic distortion**. A **solar inverter** is essentially a "roundabout controller" that converts DC traffic from PV panels into synchronized AC traffic for the grid. An **EV motor controller** is a "race-track marshal" that modulates battery DC into precisely-shaped AC waveforms to spin the motor at the exact torque and RPM demanded by the driver's accelerator pedal.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Operational Constants (must memorize for KTU exams)**
> - **Switching frequency ($f_{sw}$):** typically **10 kHz – 100 kHz** for IGBT, **> 100 kHz – 1 MHz** for SiC/GaN
> - **DC link voltage ($V_{DC}$):** **400 V** (standard EVs), **800 V** (modern fast-charging EVs like Hyundai E-GMP, Porsche Taycan)
> - **Battery nominal voltage:** **3.6 – 3.7 V/cell** (Li-ion); EV packs typically **300 – 800 V**
> - **Solar insolation constant (AM1.5G):** **1000 W/m²** at **25 °C cell temperature**
> - **Grid frequency (India):** **50 Hz** ± 0.5 Hz (per CEA regulations)
> - **Permissible Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** **< 5 %** (IEEE 519 standard)
> - **Device junction temperature limit ($T_{j,max}$):** **150 °C** for Si IGBT, **175 – 200 °C** for SiC

## 1.4 Syllabus Mapping & Course Outcomes

| Module Sub-topic | KTU 2024 CO Mapping | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| Power devices in SPV systems | CO1 – Identify power electronic components | L1 (Remember) |
| Power devices in Wind systems | CO1 / CO2 – Apply converters | L3 (Apply) |
| Power devices in EV powertrain | CO2 – Analyze EV drive architecture | L4 (Analyze) |
| Power devices in Battery chargers | CO3 – Design charger topology | L5 (Design) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Power Devices (Hierarchical Logic)

### Tier-1: Fully-Controlled Active Switches
- **Power MOSFET** — Voltage-controlled, majority carrier, used up to **600 V / 100 A**, very high $f_{sw}$
- **IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor)** — Voltage-controlled, combines MOSFET input + BJT output; **workhorse** of EV traction and grid-tied inverters up to **6.5 kV / 3.6 kA**
- **SiC MOSFET / GaN HEMT** — Wide-bandgap (WBG) devices; lower $R_{DS(on)}$, faster switching, higher temperature tolerance
- **GTO / IGCT** — Thyristor-family; used in **HVDC** and very high-power drives

### Tier-2: Semi-Controlled Devices
- **SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier)** — Latches ON once gated; turned OFF by natural commutation. Used in **PFC rectifiers, HVDC, soft-starters**

### Tier-3: Passive / Diode Family
- **Power Diode, Schottky Diode, SiC Diode** — Provide free-wheeling paths in motor drives and rectifier outputs

## 2.2 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Operating Principle: Hard Switching**
> A power device operates as a **switch** between cut-off (OFF) and saturation (ON). The goal of a *good* converter is to minimize the time spent in the **active region** (where both voltage and current are non-zero simultaneously), because this is where **switching loss ($P_{sw}$)** is dissipated as heat. Hard switching forces the device to transition through this lossy region; **soft switching** (ZVS, ZCS) techniques attempt to engineer zero-voltage or zero-current transitions to push efficiency beyond **98 %**.

### Step-by-Step Logic for IGBT in an EV Traction Inverter:
1. **Gate drive circuit** applies $+15$ V between gate and emitter → channel forms → device saturates.
2. **Collector current ($I_C$)** rises with the load (motor stator winding current).
3. **Gate signal pulled to $-15$ V** → channel pinches off → device enters cut-off.
4. **Tail current** flows briefly due to stored minority carriers in the N$^{-}$ drift region → this is the **turn-off loss** mechanism unique to IGBTs.
5. Heat is sunk to a **cold plate** using thermal interface material (TIM).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols & Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{C,avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{C,rms}^{2}$ | $V_{CE(sat)}$ = V; $I_C$ = A; $r_{CE}$ = $\Omega$ | **IGBT conduction loss** in inverter leg |
| 2 | $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$ | $V_{DC}$ = V; $I_L$ = A; $t$ = s; $f_{sw}$ = Hz | **Switching loss** in hard-switched converter |
| 3 | $P_{total} = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$ | W | Total semiconductor loss per switch |
| 4 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ (Boost) | $D$ = duty ratio (0 → 1) | DC-DC boost for PV MPPT |
| 5 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{in}}{1-D}$ (Buck-Boost) | — | EV auxiliary battery charger |
| 6 | $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$ (Buck) | — | Step-down for EV 12 V auxiliaries |
| 7 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | $n$ = ideality factor; $k$ = Boltzmann const; $q$ = electron charge | **PV cell open-circuit voltage** |
| 8 | $\eta_{MPPT} = \dfrac{P_{PV,actual}}{P_{PV,ideal}} \times 100\%$ | % | **MPPT efficiency** — typical 96 – 99 % |
| 9 | $\eta_{inv} = \dfrac{P_{AC,out}}{P_{DC,in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Inverter efficiency; modern SiC > **98.5 %** |
| 10 | $P_{mech} = T_{em} \cdot \omega_{m}$ | $T$ = Nm; $\omega$ = rad/s | EV traction motor mechanical output |
| 11 | $T_{em} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m \cdot I_q$ | $p$ = pole pairs; $\psi_m$ = flux linkage; $I_q$ = q-axis current | PMSM torque (used in most EVs) |
| 12 | $E_{batt} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$ | V | Simplified EV battery model |
| 13 | $SOC(\%) = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | % | State of Charge (BMS parameter) |
| 14 | $V_{grid,LL} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot M \cdot V_{DC}$ | $M$ = modulation index (0 – 1) | **Line-to-line RMS** of SPWM inverter output |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar String Inverters** (e.g., Huawei SUN2000, Sungrow SG125HV) use **1500 V DC-link IGBT modules** (Infineon FF1000R17IE4 or equivalent) to reach **> 99 %** CEC-weighted efficiency.
- **Tesla Model 3 / Model Y** uses a **SiC MOSFET inverter** (STMicroelectronics/特斯拉 co-design) to push inverter efficiency to **97 %** while reducing cooling system mass.
- **BYD Blade Battery EV chargers** use **650 V SiC MOSFETs** for 800 V architecture; enables 10 → 80 % charging in **< 20 minutes**.
- **Wind turbines (e.g., Vestas V236-15.0 MW)** use **press-pack IGBTs** rated **6.5 kV / 3.6 kA** for full-scale converters interfacing generator to grid.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Power Loss in an IGBT Module

We derive the **per-switch total power dissipation** combining conduction and switching contributions.

**Step 1: Conduction Loss Definition**

Conduction loss is the integral of instantaneous power $v_{CE}(t) \cdot i_C(t)$ over one switching period $T_{sw}$.

$$
P_{cond} = \frac{1}{T_{sw}} \int_{0}^{T_{sw}} v_{CE}(t) \cdot i_C(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 2: Linearize the $V_{CE}$ – $I_C$ Output Characteristic**

The IGBT saturation curve can be approximated as a straight line through the origin with slope $r_{CE}$:

$$
v_{CE}(i_C) = V_{CE(sat)} + r_{CE} \cdot i_C
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and Split the Integral**

$$
P_{cond} = \frac{1}{T_{sw}} \int_{0}^{T_{sw}} \left[V_{CE(sat)} + r_{CE} \cdot i_C(t)\right] i_C(t) \, dt
$$

$$
P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot \underbrace{\left[\frac{1}{T_{sw}}\int_{0}^{T_{sw}} i_C(t) \, dt\right]}_{I_{C,avg}} + r_{CE} \cdot \underbrace{\left[\frac{1}{T_{sw}}\int_{0}^{T_{sw}} i_C^{2}(t) \, dt\right]}_{I_{C,rms}^{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{C,avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{C,rms}^{2}}
$$

**Step 4: Switching Loss Derivation**

Switching loss occurs only during finite transition times. Energy lost per turn-on event $E_{on}$ and turn-off event $E_{off}$ are computed as the area under the $V \cdot I$ trajectory:

$$
E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_{on}} v_{CE}(t) \cdot i_C(t) \, dt
$$

Assuming idealized linear ramps (worst-case rectangular approximation):

$$
E_{on} \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot t_{on}
$$

Similarly for turn-off:

$$
E_{off} \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot t_{off}
$$

**Step 5: Total Switching Loss per Period**

There are $f_{sw}$ switching cycles per second, and each cycle dissipates $E_{on} + E_{off}$:

$$
P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot (E_{on} + E_{off}) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}
$$

**Step 6: Total Semiconductor Loss**

$$
\boxed{P_{total} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{C,avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{C,rms}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DC-DC Boost Converter Voltage Gain (for PV MPPT Front-End)

**Step 1: Define Inductor Voltage during ON-time**

When switch $S$ is ON (duration $DT$), diode $D$ is reverse-biased; inductor sees full input:

$$
v_L = V_{in} = L \cdot \frac{di_L}{dt} \Rightarrow \frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_{in}}{L}
$$

**Step 2: Inductor ON-time Change in Current**

$$
\Delta i_{L,ON} = \frac{V_{in}}{L} \cdot D \cdot T
$$

**Step 3: Define Inductor Voltage during OFF-time**

When $S$ is OFF (duration $(1-D)T$), the diode conducts and the inductor sees $V_{in} - V_{out}$:

$$
v_L = V_{in} - V_{out} = L \cdot \frac{di_L}{dt}
$$

**Step 4: Inductor OFF-time Change in Current**

$$
\Delta i_{L,OFF} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{L} \cdot (1-D) \cdot T
$$

**Step 5: Apply Volt-Second Balance (Inductor Steady-State)**

In steady state, total flux linkage change per cycle must be zero:

$$
\Delta i_{L,ON} + \Delta i_{L,OFF} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{V_{in}}{L} \cdot D \cdot T + \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{L} \cdot (1-D) \cdot T = 0
$$

**Step 6: Solve for Voltage Gain**

$$
V_{in} \cdot D + (V_{in} - V_{out})(1-D) = 0
$$

$$
V_{in} \cdot D + V_{in}(1-D) - V_{out}(1-D) = 0
$$

$$
V_{in} = V_{out}(1-D)
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1-D}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** As duty ratio $D \to 1$, gain $\to \infty$ (theoretically). Practical converters limit $D \le 0.85$ to avoid inductor saturation and MOSFET heating. For a typical 48 V solar panel feeding a 400 V DC bus, we need $D = 1 - (48/400) = 0.88$, requiring careful magnetic design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — EV PMSM Torque Equation (Why PMSM Dominates EV Drives)

For a surface-mounted PMSM (used in Tesla, Hyundai, BYD):

**Step 1: Stator d-q Reference Frame Currents**

Three-phase stator currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ are transformed to rotating reference frame:

$$
i_d = \tfrac{2}{3}\left[i_a \cos\theta + i_b \cos(\theta - 120°) + i_c \cos(\theta + 120°)\right]
$$

$$
i_q = -\tfrac{2}{3}\left[i_a \sin\theta + i_b \sin(\theta - 120°) + i_c \sin(\theta + 120°)\right]
$$

**Step 2: Air-gap Power**

$$
P_{gap} = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \omega_m \cdot \left[\psi_d i_q - \psi_q i_d\right]
$$

**Step 3: Surface PMSM Simplification**

For surface-mounted PMSM, $L_d = L_q$ and $\psi_d = \psi_m$ (permanent magnet flux), $\psi_q = 0$:

$$
P_{gap} = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \omega_m \cdot \psi_m \cdot i_q
$$

**Step 4: Electromagnetic Torque**

Using $P_{gap} = T_{em} \cdot \omega_m$:

$$
\boxed{T_{em} = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m \cdot i_q}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **EV Design Insight:** This equation shows that torque in a PMSM is controlled *purely* by the q-axis current $i_q$. The d-axis current $i_d$ is set to zero for maximum torque per ampere (MTPA) — this is the fundamental reason PMSMs deliver **> 95 % efficiency** in EVs and require **no field weakening** at low speeds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Grid-Tied Solar PV System with Power Devices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PV[PV Array 30 to 60 V string] --> DC1[DC DC Boost Converter with MPPT]
    DC1 --> DCB[DC Link Capacitor 400 to 1500 V]
    DCB --> INV[3 phase IGBT or SiC Inverter]
    INV --> L[LCL Filter]
    L --> GRID[Utility Grid 50 Hz]
    INV -.-> CTRL[Digital Controller DSP or FPGA]
    CTRL --> G1[Gate Driver IGBT]
    CTRL --> G2[MPPT Algorithm]
    PV --> SENSE[Voltage and Current Sensors]
    SENSE --> CTRL
    GRID --> SYNC[PLL Grid Synchronization]
    SYNC --> CTRL
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — EV Powertrain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BAT[Li ion Battery Pack 400 V or 800 V] --> BMS[BMS Battery Management System]
    BMS --> INV[3 phase SiC MOSFET Inverter]
    INV --> MOT[PMSM Traction Motor]
    MOT --> GEAR[Single Speed Reducer]
    GEAR --> WH[Drive Wheels]
    INV -.-> VCU[Vehicle Control Unit]
    BMS -.-> VCU
    VCU --> THR[Accelerator Pedal Sensor]
    VCU --> BRK[Brake Pedal Sensor]
    VCU --> INV
    CHG[Onboard Charger OBC] --> BAT
    CHG -.-> VCU
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WT[Wind Turbine Rotor] --> GEN[PMSG Doubly Fed Induction Generator]
    GEN --> AC-DC[Generator side AC DC Rectifier IGBT]
    AC-DC --> DC-DC[DC DC Boost Converter]
    DC-DC --> DC-LINK[DC Link]
    DC-LINK --> DC-AC[Grid side DC AC Inverter IGBT]
    DC-AC --> XFRM[Step Up Transformer]
    XFRM --> GRID[Utility Grid]
    DC-LINK -.-> CTRL[Pitch and Converter Controller]
    WT -.-> CTRL
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Onboard EV Charger (Level 2, 6.6 kW)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC[AC Mains 230 V 50 Hz] --> EMI[EMI Filter]
    EMI --> PFC[Boost PFC Stage IGBT or SiC]
    PFC --> LLC[LLC Resonant DC DC Isolated]
    LLC --> REC[Output Rectifier SiC Diode]
    REC --> BAT[High Voltage Battery]
    PFC -.-> DSP[Digital Controller]
    LLC -.-> DSP
    DSP -.-> CAN[CAN Bus to BMS]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Battery Management System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CELL[Li ion Cell Module] --> AFE[Analog Front End IC LTC6811 or similar]
    AFE --> MCU[Master MCU]
    MCU --> ISO[Isolated CAN]
    ISO --> VCU[Vehicle Control Unit]
    MCU --> BAL[Passive or Active Cell Balancer]
    BAL --> CELL
    MCU --> CHG_RLY[Charging Contactor]
    MCU --> DIS_RLY[Discharge Contactor]
    CELL --> SHUNT[Current Shunt or Hall Sensor]
    SHUNT --> MCU
    CELL --> NTC[Temperature Sensor NTC]
    NTC --> MCU
```

## 4.6 Switching Waveform Schematic — IGBT Hard-Switching

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["Turn-ON Transition"]
        A1[Gate voltage rises 0 to 15 V] --> A2[Collector current rises]
        A2 --> A3[Collector emitter voltage falls]
    end
    subgraph B["Turn-OFF Transition"]
        B1[Gate voltage falls 15 to 0 V]
        B1 --> B2[Collector emitter voltage rises]
        B2 --> B3[Tail current decays slowly]
    end
    A -.->|"Energy lost Eon"| LOSS[Switching Loss]
    B -.->|"Energy lost Eoff"| LOSS
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

### Q1. Compare the switching frequency, voltage rating, and typical application of Power MOSFET, IGBT, and SCR in renewable energy systems.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Device | Voltage Rating | $f_{sw}$ Range | Typical RE/EV Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power MOSFET** | Up to 600 V | 50 kHz – 1 MHz | Low-power PV micro-inverters, EV aux DC-DC |
| **IGBT** | 600 V – 6.5 kV | 2 kHz – 50 kHz | Solar string/central inverters, EV traction inverter, wind converters |
| **SCR** | Up to 10 kV | Line frequency (50 Hz) | HVDC, soft-starters, controlled rectifiers in large wind systems |

**Key takeaway:** MOSFET → high-frequency low-power; IGBT → mid-power medium-frequency workhorse; SCR → very-high-power line-frequency.

---

### Q2. List three major functions performed by power semiconductor devices in a grid-tied Solar PV inverter. *(KTU University Exam — Dec 2023)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **DC-DC boost conversion with MPPT** — tracks maximum power point of PV array using high-frequency switching (e.g., 20 kHz) to extract peak available power under varying irradiance.
2. **DC-AC inversion** — converts DC link voltage to grid-synchronized 50 Hz AC using Sinusoidal PWM with MOSFETs/IGBTs/SiC switches, maintaining THD < 5 %.
3. **Anti-islanding and grid protection** — under/over-voltage, under/over-frequency detection, and rapid disconnection of the inverter within ≤ 2 seconds per IEEE 1547.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU ESE Module 1 Internal Choice Question Pattern]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.** *(a)* Explain with a neat block diagram the role of IGBT and MOSFET in a **grid-tied Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) power conditioning unit (PCU)**. Discuss the converter stages and their switching frequencies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase VSI uses IGBTs with $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.8$ V, $r_{CE} = 25$ m$\Omega$, $t_{on} = t_{off} = 0.4$ µs, switching at 16 kHz from a 800 V DC bus. The motor load draws sinusoidal line current with peak 200 A. Calculate **total power loss per IGBT** and **total inverter loss for 6 switches**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 — PV Array & DC-DC Boost Converter (3 marks)**
- PV array consists of series-parallel strings; typical open-circuit voltage 30 – 60 V per string.
- **MOSFET** (e.g., Infineon IPB072N15N3 G, 150 V) drives a boost converter switching at **20 – 50 kHz**.
- Function: **MPPT** using Perturb & Observe (P&O) or Incremental Conductance algorithm. Output: regulated DC bus (e.g., 400 V).

**Stage 2 — DC-AC Inverter (3 marks)**
- 3-phase 2-level VSI using **IGBT modules** (e.g., Infineon FF600R17ME4, 1700 V/600 A) at **8 – 16 kHz** SPWM.
- SPWM reference is grid-synchronized via PLL.
- Output LCL filter smooths the PWM waveform to clean 50 Hz sinusoidal AC.

**Stage 3 — Control & Protection (1 mark)**
- Anti-islanding relay, DC reverse polarity protection, surge protection, and MPPT controller.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.8$ V
- $r_{CE} = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ $\Omega$
- $t_{on} = t_{off} = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- $f_{sw} = 16 \times 10^{3}$ Hz
- $V_{DC} = 800$ V
- $I_{peak} = 200$ A (sinusoidal)

**Step 1: Sinusoidal current averages**

For sinusoidal $i(t) = I_{peak} \sin(\omega t)$:

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{I_{peak}}{\pi} = \frac{200}{\pi} = 63.66 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{rms} = \frac{I_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{2}} = 141.42 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2: Conduction Loss**

$$
P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^{2}
$$

$$
P_{cond} = (1.8)(63.66) + (0.025)(141.42)^{2}
$$

$$
P_{cond} = 114.59 + 500.00 = 614.59 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3: Switching Loss**

$$
P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}
$$

$$
P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 800 \cdot 200 \cdot (0.4 + 0.4) \times 10^{-6} \cdot 16 \times 10^{3}
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 0.5 \cdot 800 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 16000
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 1024 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4: Total Loss per IGBT**

$$
P_{total/IGBT} = P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 614.59 + 1024 = 1638.59 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5: Total Inverter Loss (6 IGBTs)**

$$
P_{inverter} = 6 \times 1638.59 = 9831.54 \text{ W} \approx 9.83 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Per-IGBT loss ≈ **1.64 kW**; total inverter loss ≈ **9.83 kW**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** *(a)* With a block diagram, explain the **power electronic architecture of an Electric Vehicle (EV) powertrain**. Identify the role of each power device and justify why **SiC MOSFETs** are preferred over silicon IGBTs in modern 800 V EVs. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A **Bidirectional DC-DC converter** operates in boost mode (battery → DC bus) for an EV. Given: $V_{batt} = 400$ V, $V_{bus} = 800$ V, $L = 500$ µH, $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz. Compute **(i)** the steady-state duty ratio $D$, and **(ii)** the peak-to-peak inductor ripple current $\Delta i_L$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — EV Powertrain Block Diagram & Justification (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
*[Refer to SECTION 4.2 above — EV Powertrain Architecture]*

**Roles of each power device (2 marks):**

| Device | Location | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **SiC MOSFET** | Traction inverter | High-frequency (10–20 kHz) DC→AC conversion for PMSM; supports 800 V bus |
| **Si IGBT** | Onboard charger | Lower-frequency AC-DC rectification/PFC stage (line frequency + PFC switch) |
| **SiC Diode** | OBC output rectifier | Fast-recovery, low-reverse-recovery; reduces switching loss in LLC stage |
| **MOSFET (low-voltage)** | 12 V DC-DC converter | Buck converter for auxiliaries (lights, ECU) |
| **SCR / Triac** | Pre-charge circuit | Soft-charges DC link capacitor to prevent inrush current |

**Justification: SiC MOSFET vs Si IGBT (2 marks):**

- **Voltage rating:** SiC MOSFETs withstand **1200 – 1700 V** natively; Si IGBTs at 1200 V are larger and slower.
- **Switching loss:** SiC has **70 – 80 %** lower $E_{on} + E_{off}$ → enables $f_{sw}$ increase from **8 kHz → 40 kHz** → smaller magnetics.
- **Thermal:** SiC operates at $T_j \le 200$ °C → simpler liquid cooling.
- **E.g.,** Tesla Model 3 inverter uses **ST GK026 SiC MOSFET module** → **97 %** efficiency, **> 700 km** range.

**Part (b) — Bidirectional DC-DC Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_{batt} = 400$ V, $V_{bus} = 800$ V, $L = 500 \times 10^{-6}$ H, $f_{sw} = 20 \times 10^{3}$ Hz

**Step 1: Steady-State Duty Ratio**

For boost mode:

$$
V_{bus} = \frac{V_{batt}}{1 - D} \Rightarrow 800 = \frac{400}{1 - D}
$$

$$
1 - D = \frac{400}{800} = 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 0.5}
$$

**Step 2: Period of Switching**

$$
T_{sw} = \frac{1}{f_{sw}} = \frac{1}{20{,}000} = 50 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3: On-Time**

$$
t_{on} = D \cdot T_{sw} = 0.5 \times 50 \text{ µs} = 25 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 4: Inductor Ripple Current**

During ON-state, voltage across inductor = $V_{batt}$:

$$
\Delta i_L = \frac{V_{batt}}{L} \cdot t_{on} = \frac{400}{500 \times 10^{-6}} \times 25 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\Delta i_L = \frac{400 \times 25}{500} = \frac{10{,}000}{500} = 20 \text{ A}
$$

> **Final Answers:** $D = 0.5$ (50 %), $\Delta i_L = 20$ A peak-to-peak.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Avoid these!)**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 6** in 3-phase inverter loss calculations — students frequently give per-IGBT loss as the final answer, losing **1 mark**.
> 2. **Using $I_{peak}$ instead of $I_{avg}$** in conduction loss formula — must distinguish between average (for $V_{CE(sat)}$ term) and RMS (for $r_{CE}$ term).
> 3. **Mixing boost and buck-boost gain equations** — KTU frequently tests $V_{out}/V_{in} = 1/(1-D)$ vs $D/(1-D)$ vs $D$; state the topology before applying the formula.
> 4. **Omitting units** in numerical answers — examiner strictly deducts **0.5 mark** per missing unit.
> 5. **Forgetting the tail-current explanation** in IGBT turn-off loss — this is a favorite 2-mark sub-question; students must mention minority carrier storage in N$^{-}$ drift region.
> 6. **Not drawing block diagrams with labeled power devices** — generic diagrams without device names fetch only **partial credit**.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Power devices** = active switches in converters; classified as MOSFET, IGBT, SCR, GTO, SiC, GaN.
- ✅ **IGBT** is the **workhorse** for EV traction and medium-power PV inverters (up to 6.5 kV/3.6 kA).
- ✅ **SiC MOSFETs** dominate modern **800 V EVs** due to lower switching loss, higher temperature, and higher frequency.
- ✅ **Total loss** = $P_{cond} + P_{sw}$; always compute **per switch first**, then multiply by switch count.
- ✅ **Boost converter gain:** $V_{out}/V_{in} = 1/(1-D)$; **Buck:** $V_{out}/V_{in} = D$; **Buck-Boost:** $D/(1-D)$.
- ✅ **MPPT efficiency** typically 96 – 99 %; goal is to operate PV array at its **knee point**.
- ✅ **PMSM torque** $T_{em} = \tfrac{3}{2} p \psi_m i_q$ — independent of d-axis when $i_d = 0$ (MTPA strategy).
- ✅ **Inductor volt-second balance** is the fundamental tool to derive all DC-DC gain equations.
- ✅ **IEEE 519 THD limit:** < 5 % for grid-tie; LCL filter is mandatory for IGBT-based inverters.
- ✅ **Boltzmann constant** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; **electron charge** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C — required for PV cell derivations.
- ✅ **Anti-islanding** protection time ≤ 2 s; PLL synchronization is mandatory for grid tie.
- ✅ **Cell balancing** in BMS (passive vs active) directly impacts usable battery capacity in EVs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Phase Controlled Rectifiers - Half Wave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Single Phase Controlled Rectifiers - Half Wave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier** is a power electronic converter circuit that employs a single thyristor (Silicon Controlled Rectifier, SCR) to convert a single-phase AC input voltage into a unidirectional (DC) output voltage, where the magnitude of the average DC output is precisely regulated by varying the **firing angle (α)** of the thyristor within each positive half-cycle of the supply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> A controlled rectifier uses a thyristor (SCR) as the switching device. The point at which the thyristor is turned ON in each positive half-cycle is called the **firing angle (α)**, measured from the instant the supply voltage becomes positive (the **zero-crossing point** of the AC source).

## 1.2 Circuit Constituents

| Component | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| AC Voltage Source | $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | Provides single-phase sinusoidal input |
| Thyristor (SCR) | T1 / TH1 | Controlled unidirectional switching device |
| Load | R, RL, RLE | Converts rectified power into useful form |
| Gate Trigger Circuit | $i_g$ | Fires the SCR at the desired angle $\alpha$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The thyristor conducts only when **both** conditions are satisfied: (i) it is forward-biased ($v_{AK} > 0$) AND (ii) a gate pulse is applied. Once conducting, the gate loses control — the device turns OFF only when the anode current falls below the **holding current $I_H$** (typically a few mA).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚰

Think of an **SCR as a smart water tap** on a pipe carrying alternating-flow water (the AC source):

- The water **only flows in one direction** through the tap (like the thyristor's unidirectional nature).
- You can **delay opening the tap** in each cycle — that delay is the **firing angle $\alpha$**.
- The **later** you open the tap, the **less water** you collect in your bucket (the **DC output voltage drops**).
- The tap **automatically closes** when the flow tries to reverse (natural commutation at $\pi$ for R load).
- For a **bucket with inertia (RL load)**, water keeps swirling after the tap closes — the diode of an RL load "freewheels" the stored energy.

This delay-based flow control is exactly how the SCR "throttles" DC output from an AC source.

## 1.4 Key Nomenclature (KTU Board Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Firing Angle ($\alpha$):** The angle (in degrees or radians) measured from the **zero crossing** of the supply voltage to the instant the gate pulse is applied.
> - Range: $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ (for R load)
> - $\alpha = 0$: Full output (rectifier behaves like an uncontrolled diode)
> - $\alpha = \pi$: Zero output (thyristor never fires in useful region)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conduction Angle ($\gamma$):** The angle during which the thyristor is in the ON state.
> - For R load: $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$
> - For RL load: $\gamma = \beta - \alpha$ (where $\beta$ is the extinction angle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extinction Angle ($\beta$):** The angle at which the anode current naturally falls to zero and the SCR turns OFF (used for RL loads).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)

The complete operating cycle of a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with **resistive load (R)** is described below:

1. **Interval $0 \to \alpha$:** Supply voltage is positive, but the thyristor is **not yet fired**. No gate pulse is present, so the SCR remains in the forward-blocking state. The output voltage $v_o = 0$ and load current $i_o = 0$.

2. **At $\omega t = \alpha$:** A **gate pulse** $i_g$ is applied to the thyristor. The SCR turns ON (latches into conduction). The forward voltage drop across the SCR is neglected (ideal switch assumption: $V_T \approx 1.5$ V, ignored for theoretical analysis).

3. **Interval $\alpha \to \pi$:** The thyristor conducts. The output voltage follows the supply: $v_o = v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. The load current is $i_o = v_o / R$.

4. **At $\omega t = \pi$:** The supply voltage crosses zero and goes negative. The anode current $i_o$ tends to reverse, but since it cannot (unidirectional device), it falls to zero. With $i_o < I_H$, the SCR turns OFF (**natural commutation**).

5. **Interval $\pi \to 2\pi$:** The thyristor is reverse-biased and remains OFF. Output voltage $v_o = 0$. The thyristor regains its forward-blocking capability before the next cycle.

6. **At $\omega t = 2\pi + \alpha$:** The cycle repeats with a new gate pulse.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Equations

The **average output voltage** of a controlled rectifier is essentially the **DC component** of the chopped waveform. Mathematically, it is the integral of the output voltage over one full cycle, divided by the period $2\pi$.

$$V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

The **RMS value** represents the **effective heating value** (used to compute power dissipation, transformer utilization, and component ratings).

$$V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \bigl[v_o(\omega t)\bigr]^2 \, d(\omega t)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Convention:** $V_m$ = peak supply voltage, $\alpha$ = firing angle (radians unless stated), $I_m = V_m / Z$ where $Z$ is load impedance.

### Table 2.1: Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier — All Formulas

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Supply Voltage | $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | Standard sinusoidal |
| 2 | Avg. Output (R load) | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 3 | RMS Output (R load) | $V_{o(rms)} = \dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 4 | Avg. Output (RL load) | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)$ | Discontinuous mode |
| 5 | RMS Output (RL load) | $V_{o(rms)} = \dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\beta - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2}\right)}$ | Discontinuous mode |
| 6 | Avg. Current (R load) | $I_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_{o(avg)}}{R} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi R}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 7 | RMS Current (R load) | $I_{o(rms)} = \dfrac{V_{o(rms)}}{R}$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 8 | Form Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{o(rms)}}{V_{o(avg)}}$ | Always $\ge 1$ |
| 9 | Ripple Factor | $RF = \sqrt{FF^2 - 1}$ | Indicates pulsation |
| 10 | Rectification Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{DC}}{P_{AC}} = \dfrac{V_{o(avg)}^2}{V_{o(rms)}^2} \times 100\%$ | Max $\approx 40.5\%$ (R load) |
| 11 | Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) | $PIV = V_m$ | For half-wave |
| 12 | Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF) | $TUF = \dfrac{P_{DC}}{V_s I_s}$ | Secondary rating consideration |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier is Used |
|---|---|
| **DC Motor Speed Control (small fractional HP)** | Simple, low-cost firing-angle-based armature voltage control |
| **Battery Chargers** | Adjustable charging current by varying $\alpha$ |
| **Lamp Dimming Circuits** | AC phase control for theatrical/domestic lighting |
| **Heater Temperature Control** | RMS power control for resistive heating elements |
| **DC Welding Power Supplies** | Adjustable welding current via $\alpha$ control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation Alert:** Half-wave rectifiers have **poor transformer utilization** (TUF $\approx 0.286$ for R load) and inject significant **DC component** into the supply, causing saturation in transformer cores. They are thus restricted to **low-power (< 1 kW)** applications. Full-wave and bridge topologies are preferred in industrial drives.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Output Voltage (R Load) ⭐ Most Important

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the average output voltage of a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier supplying a resistive load.

**Given:** $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, thyristor fired at $\omega t = \alpha$, conduction interval $[\alpha, \pi]$.

**Step 1 —** Write the output voltage piecewise over one full cycle $[0, 2\pi]$:

$$
v_o(\omega t) = 
\begin{cases}
0, & 0 \le \omega t \lt \alpha \\
V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\
0, & \pi \lt \omega t \le 2\pi
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 —** Apply the definition of the average (DC) value over one full period $T = 2\pi$:

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 —** Substitute the piecewise expression. Only the interval $[\alpha, \pi]$ contributes a non-zero integral:

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 4 —** Pull the constant $V_m$ outside and integrate:

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}
$$

**Step 5 —** Evaluate the antiderivative at the upper and lower limits:

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi) - \bigl(-\cos(\alpha)\bigr) \right]
$$

**Step 6 —** Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos(\pi) = -1$:

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -(-1) + \cos(\alpha) \right]
$$

**Step 7 —** Simplify to obtain the **final boxed result**:

$$
\boxed{V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl(1 + \cos\alpha\bigr)}
$$

**Step 8 — Sanity Check:**
- At $\alpha = 0$: $V_{o(avg)} = V_m/\pi$ → matches the standard half-wave **uncontrolled** rectifier result. ✅
- At $\alpha = \pi$: $V_{o(avg)} = 0$ → no conduction, no output. ✅
- Monotonically decreasing in $\alpha$. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RMS Output Voltage (R Load)

**Step 1 —** Apply the RMS definition over the conduction interval:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 2 —** Apply the half-angle identity $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 3 —** Split the integral:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} d(\omega t) - \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos(2\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right]}
$$

**Step 4 —** Evaluate the first integral: $\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} d(\omega t) = \pi - \alpha$.

**Step 5 —** Evaluate the second integral: $\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos(2\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \left[\dfrac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \dfrac{\sin(2\pi) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} = -\dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}$.

**Step 6 —** Substitute back:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) - \left( -\frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right) \right]}
$$

**Step 7 —** Combine terms and simplify:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]}
$$

**Step 8 —** Factor $V_m/2$ out of the square root:

$$
\boxed{V_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_m}{2} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left( \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right)}}
$$

**Step 9 — Sanity Check:**
- At $\alpha = 0$: $V_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_m}{2}$ → matches the uncontrolled half-wave result. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Average Output Voltage (RL Load, Discontinuous Mode)

**Physical Setup:** The load contains both resistance $R$ and inductance $L$. When the SCR is fired at $\omega t = \alpha$, the load current builds up gradually (limited by $L$). The current continues past $\omega t = \pi$ due to stored inductor energy, and finally falls to zero at the **extinction angle $\beta$**.

**Load current during conduction:**

$$
i_o(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z} \left[ \sin(\omega t - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi) \, e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)} \right]
$$

where $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$, $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R)$, and $\tau = L/R$.

**Step 1 —** Output voltage during $[\alpha, \beta]$ is still $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (SCR is ON and ideal).

**Step 2 —** Apply the average definition, but now the integration limit is $[\alpha, \beta]$:

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 —** Integrate:

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\beta}
$$

**Step 4 —** Evaluate the limits:

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos\beta + \cos\alpha \right]
$$

**Step 5 —** Final result:

$$
\boxed{V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta\bigr)}
$$

**Step 6 —** To find $\beta$, apply the **boundary condition** $i_o(\beta) = 0$:

$$
\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi) \, e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/(\omega \tau)}
$$

This transcendental equation is **solved numerically** (no closed-form solution exists in general).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: RMS Output Voltage (RL Load)

**Step 1 —** Apply the RMS definition over the extended conduction interval $[\alpha, \beta]$:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 2 —** Apply $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \left[ 1 - \cos(2\omega t) \right] d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 3 —** Evaluate each integral separately:

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\beta} d(\omega t) = \beta - \alpha
$$

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \cos(2\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{\sin(2\beta) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2}
$$

**Step 4 —** Substitute:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \left[ (\beta - \alpha) - \frac{\sin(2\beta) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]}
$$

**Step 5 —** Rearrange the sign for clarity:

$$
\boxed{V_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_m}{2} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left( \beta - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2} \right)}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (R Load)

**Problem:** A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier is supplied from $v_s = 230\sqrt{2} \sin(314t)$ V and feeds a resistive load of $R = 10 \, \Omega$. If the firing angle is $\alpha = 60°$, compute:
(a) Average output voltage
(b) RMS output voltage
(c) Average and RMS load currents
(d) Power delivered to the load
(e) Form Factor and Ripple Factor

**Given:** $V_m = 230\sqrt{2} \approx 325.27$ V, $\alpha = 60° = \pi/3$ rad, $R = 10 \, \Omega$.

**(a) Average output voltage:**

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\bigl(1 + \cos\alpha\bigr) = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}\bigl(1 + \cos 60°\bigr)
$$

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{325.27}{2\pi}(1 + 0.5) = \frac{325.27 \times 1.5}{6.2832} = \frac{487.91}{6.2832} \approx 77.66 \text{ V}
$$

**(b) RMS output voltage:**

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}
$$

$$
= \frac{325.27}{2}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \frac{\pi}{3} + \frac{\sin 120°}{2}\right)}
$$

$$
= 162.635 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{2\pi}{3} + \frac{0.8660}{2}\right)}
$$

$$
= 162.635 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}(2.0944 + 0.4330)} = 162.635 \times \sqrt{0.8042}
$$

$$
V_{o(rms)} \approx 162.635 \times 0.8968 \approx 145.85 \text{ V}
$$

**(c) Currents:**

$$
I_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_{o(avg)}}{R} = \frac{77.66}{10} = 7.766 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_{o(rms)}}{R} = \frac{145.85}{10} = 14.585 \text{ A}
$$

**(d) Power:**

$$
P = I_{o(rms)}^2 \cdot R = (14.585)^2 \times 10 = 2127.2 \text{ W} \approx 2.13 \text{ kW}
$$

**(e) Form Factor & Ripple Factor:**

$$
FF = \frac{V_{o(rms)}}{V_{o(avg)}} = \frac{145.85}{77.66} \approx 1.878
$$

$$
RF = \sqrt{FF^2 - 1} = \sqrt{(1.878)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{3.528 - 1} = \sqrt{2.528} \approx 1.590
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $RF = 1.59$ means the AC ripple content is $159\%$ of the DC value — a hallmark of half-wave rectification and the reason why filtering is essential in practice.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Circuit Topology (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier across all four common load variants. Node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid parser safety rules.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ACsource[Single Phase AC Source Vm sin wt]

```

## 4.2 Operating Sequence (R Load Waveform Timing Diagram)

The Mermaid sequence chart below maps the **time-domain behaviour** of the key signals across one full AC cycle $[0, 2\pi]$. Each lifelin represents a signal trace; the comments denote critical transitions.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant VS as vs Supply
    participant IG as ig Gate Pulse
    participant IT as iT Thyristor Current
    participant VO as vo Output Voltage
    participant IO as io Load Current

    Note over VS,IO: Interval 0 to alpha  SCR is OFF
    VS-->>VS: Positive half cycle begins at 0
    VO->>VO: vo = 0 SCR forward blocked
    IO->>IO: io = 0
    IT->>IT: iT = 0

    Note over VS,IO: At wt = alpha Gate pulse applied
    IG->>IG: ig applied SCR latches ON
    VO->>VO: vo follows vs = Vm sin wt
    IO->>IO: io = vo / R ramps sinusoidally
    IT->>IT: iT = io

    Note over VS,IO: Interval alpha to pi  Conduction
    VS-->>VS: vs peaks at Vm at wt = pi by 2
    VO-->>VO: vo equals vs entire interval
    IO-->>IO: io maximum Im at pi by 2

    Note over VS,IO: At wt = pi  Natural Commutation
    VS->>VS: vs crosses zero
    IG->>IG: Gate pulse removed at pi plus alpha
    VO->>VO: vo falls to zero
    IO->>IO: io falls below IH SCR turns OFF
    IT->>IT: iT = 0

    Note over VS,IO: Interval pi to 2pi  SCR OFF
    VO->>VO: vo = 0 entire negative half
    IO->>IO: io = 0
    IT->>IT: iT = 0 reverse blocking mode
```
