## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Full-Wave Rectifiers**

- ⭐ **Definition:** A full-wave rectifier converts **both** half-cycles of the AC input into unidirectional output, producing a pulsating DC at **twice** the input frequency ($2f_{in}$).
- ⭐ **Two Topologies:** (1) **Center-tapped** (2 diodes, requires special transformer) and (2) **Bridge** (4 diodes, uses standard transformer).
- ⭐ **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $V_{dc} = 2V_m / \pi \approx 0.6366 V_m$
  - $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071 V_m$
  - $\gamma = 0.482$ (ideal full-wave)
  - $\eta_{max} = 8 / \pi^2 = 81.2\%$
  - $PIV_{\text{center-tapped}} = 2V_m$
  - $PIV_{\text{bridge}} = V_m$
  - $TUF_{\text{center-tapped}} = 0.693$
  - $TUF_{\text{bridge}} = 0.8104$
- ⭐ **Bridge is preferred** in industry because of: (a) higher TUF, (b) lower PIV per diode, (c) use of standard (cheaper) transformer.
- ⭐ **Output frequency** is $2f$ — important for filter design (smaller filter capacitor needed compared to half-wave).
- ⭐ **Fourier series** of the output: $v_o(t) = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi} - \dfrac{4V_m}{\pi} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{\cos(2n\omega t)}{4n^2 - 1}$ — useful for harmonic analysis and filter design.
- ⭐ **Diode Conduction Sequence:** In bridge, **two diodes conduct in series per half-cycle** (each with a forward drop of ~0.7 V for silicon, leading to a total drop of ~1.4 V).
- ⭐ **KTU Board Exam Pattern:** Expect a 7-mark derivations sub-part (average voltage + RMS + ripple factor or efficiency) and a 7-mark numerical sub-part (PIV + numerical substitution). Always show the substitution step clearly.
- ⭐ **Practical vs Ideal:** In real problems, account for diode forward voltage drop (0.7 V per diode for Si) by reducing $V_m$ accordingly: $V_m' = V_m - 2V_F$ for bridge, $V_m' = V_m - V_F$ for center-tapped.
- ⭐ **Comparison with Half-Wave:** Full-wave has **double** the $V_{dc}$, **half** the ripple factor (0.482 vs 1.21), and **double** the efficiency (81.2% vs 40.6%) — making it overwhelmingly superior.
- ⭐ **Applications:** Linear DC power supplies, battery chargers, DC motor drives (small scale), HVDC transmission, signal demodulation, electroplating.

> **End of Module 2 — Full-Wave Rectifier Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Half-Controlled and Fully Controlled - Typical Waveforms with R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Half-Controlled and Fully Controlled - Typical Waveforms with R

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Phase-Controlled Rectifier** is a power electronic converter that converts a fixed AC supply voltage into a variable DC output voltage by controlling the instant at which the semiconductor devices (SCRs/Thyristors) are triggered into conduction. The instant of triggering is called the **firing angle (α)**, measured in electrical degrees from the instant the device would naturally start conducting in an uncontrolled diode bridge.

For the topic under discussion, we consider two important topologies, both supplied from a **single-phase, 50 Hz AC mains** feeding a purely **resistive (R) load**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Half-Controlled Bridge (Semi-Converter / Asymmetric Converter):**
> A single-phase bridge rectifier that uses **two SCRs and two power diodes**. Only the positive half-cycles (or the negative half-cycles, depending on configuration) are phase-controlled; the other half-cycles are uncontrolled diode-rectified. For an R-load, this topology still requires the SCRs to be re-fired in every half-cycle, but the output waveform is identical in shape to that of a full converter for continuous conduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fully-Controlled Bridge (Full Converter / Symmetric Converter):**
> A single-phase bridge rectifier that uses **four SCRs** (T1, T2, T3, T4) in all four arms. Both half-cycles of the AC supply are phase-controlled. The output is a unidirectional, pulsating DC voltage whose average value depends on the firing angle α.

The letter **"R"** in the topic title denotes a **purely resistive load**, i.e., a load containing only resistance (no inductance, no back-EMF). For an R-load, the load current waveform is in phase with the load voltage, and the converter always operates in the **continuous-conduction mode (CCM)** only as long as the supply voltage is positive during a fired SCR's conduction interval.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a water tap connected to a pipeline. The tap cannot be opened partially — it is either **fully ON** or **fully OFF**. The supply pressure is sinusoidal (the AC mains). The valve in the tap is operated by a knob that you turn only at a specific instant after the pressure has started rising — this knob is the **firing circuit** of the SCR. The instant you turn the knob (firing angle, **α**) decides *how much* water flows per cycle, even though the tap when open allows the *full* pressure through.

- A **fully-controlled bridge** is like having two such taps working alternately on both sides of the pipeline.
- A **half-controlled bridge** is like having one tap on one side and a free-flowing open pipe (diode) on the other — the free side always lets pressure through, but the tap side only opens when you decide.

The **average DC output voltage** is therefore the *area under one positive pulse* of the supply divided by the period, and this area shrinks as you delay the firing (larger α).

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Constants

- **Peak supply voltage:** $V_m = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2}$, in volts
- **Supply frequency:** **f = 50 Hz** (Indian standard, KTU region)
- **Supply angular frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159$ rad/s
- **Firing angle:** $\alpha$, in electrical degrees or radians, $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$
- **Conduction angle per SCR:** $\pi - \alpha$ in continuous conduction
- **Number of pulses per cycle (full converter):** **2**
- **Ripple frequency (fundamental output frequency):** **2f = 100 Hz** for a single-phase full bridge
- **Number of SCRs:** 4 (full converter), 2 (semi-converter)
- **Number of diodes:** 0 (full converter), 2 (semi-converter)

> [!NOTE]
> For a purely resistive load, the load current is in phase with the load voltage, hence the converter never enters "extinction angle" considerations — the conduction always terminates at the natural supply voltage zero-crossing unless the next SCR is fired first.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fully-Controlled Bridge with R-Load — Operating Logic

Let the AC source voltage be $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and the load resistance be $R$. The four SCRs are paired diagonally:

- **Positive half-cycle ($\omega t$ from 0 to $\pi$):** SCRs T1 and T2 are forward-biased.
- **Negative half-cycle ($\omega t$ from $\pi$ to $2\pi$):** SCRs T3 and T4 are forward-biased.

The operational sequence is:

1. At $\omega t = 0$, the source voltage begins to rise positively. **No SCR conducts** because none has been fired yet.
2. At $\omega t = \alpha$, a gate pulse is applied to **T1 and T2 simultaneously**. They turn ON and latch. Current flows through Source(+) → T1 → Load R → T2 → Source(–).
3. The output voltage equals the source voltage: $v_o = v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
4. At $\omega t = \pi$, the source voltage reverses. T1 and T2 become reverse-biased and **naturally turn OFF** (commutation by line).
5. T1 and T2 remain OFF during $\pi \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha$. **No current flows.** The output is zero.
6. At $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$, gate pulses are applied to **T3 and T4**. They conduct for the next $\pi - \alpha$ interval. The output voltage is $v_o = -v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (since the bridge inverts the negative half-cycle into a positive output).
7. At $\omega t = 2\pi$, T3 and T4 naturally turn OFF. The cycle repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> The output voltage $v_o$ is **unidirectional and positive** for all $\omega t$ during which conduction occurs. Its shape is the absolute value of a phase-shifted sinusoid.

## 2.2 Half-Controlled Bridge (Semi-Converter) with R-Load — Operating Logic

We choose the **asymmetric configuration**: SCRs T1 and T2 in the upper arms, diodes D1 and D2 in the lower arms.

- **Positive half-cycle:** T1 and D1 are forward-biased. T1 must be fired at $\omega t = \alpha$. T1-D1 conduct from $\alpha$ to $\pi$.
- **Negative half-cycle:** T2 and D2 are forward-biased. T2 must be fired at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$. T2-D2 conduct from $\pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi$.
- **Between $\pi$ and $\pi + \alpha$:** No device conducts. Output is zero.

For a pure R-load (no inductance), **no freewheeling action** occurs even in the semi-converter because the load has no stored energy to maintain current once both SCRs and diodes become reverse-biased. Hence the waveforms of the semi-converter and full converter with R-load are **mathematically identical** in continuous conduction.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below assume **continuous conduction mode (CCM)** and a **purely resistive load**.

| # | Quantity | Formula (Full Converter / Semi-Converter with R-Load) | Units |
|---|----------|-------------------------------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Source voltage | $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | V |
| 2 | Peak voltage | $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$ | V |
| 3 | Average output voltage | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} \cdot (1 + \cos\alpha)$ | V |
| 4 | RMS output voltage | $V_{o(rms)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]}$ | V |
| 5 | Output power | $P_o = \dfrac{V_{o(rms)}^{\,2}}{R}$ | W |
| 6 | Average load current | $I_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_{o(avg)}}{R}$ | A |
| 7 | RMS load current | $I_{o(rms)} = \dfrac{V_{o(rms)}}{R}$ | A |
| 8 | Form factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{o(rms)}}{V_{o(avg)}}$ | – |
| 9 | Ripple factor | $RF = \sqrt{FF^{\,2} - 1}$ | – |
| 10 | Rectification efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{DC}}{P_{o(rms)}} = \dfrac{V_{o(avg)}^{\,2}}{V_{o(rms)}^{\,2}}$ | – |
| 11 | Peak inverse voltage per SCR | $PIV = V_m$ | V |
| 12 | Average SCR current | $I_{T(avg)} = \dfrac{I_{o(avg)}}{2}$ | A |
| 13 | RMS SCR current | $I_{T(rms)} = \dfrac{I_{o(rms)}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | A |
| 14 | Firing angle range (CCM) | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ | rad / deg |
| 15 | Output pulse frequency | $f_o = 2f = \mathbf{100\ Hz}$ | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical insight for KTU exam:** When $\alpha = 0$, the converters behave as uncontrolled diode bridges, and $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$. When $\alpha = \pi$, $V_{o(avg)} = 0$, i.e., the converter can be smoothly turned OFF by retarding the firing angle to 180°.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Single-phase fully and half-controlled bridges with R-loads form the heart of:

- **DC motor drives** in low-power industrial applications (fans, small lathes, mixers).
- **Battery chargers** (lead-acid traction batteries, UPS batteries).
- **DC welding sets** and **plating supplies**.
- **Heater/temperature controllers** (ovens, furnaces, hot plates) — exact match for R-load analysis.
- **Lamp dimming circuits** in theatre and architectural lighting.
- **DC traction substations** for older tramways and trolleybuses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Average Output Voltage $V_{o(avg)}$

**Starting point:** The output voltage waveform, for both full and half-controlled bridges with R-load, is mathematically the same:

$$
v_o(\omega t) =
\begin{cases}
V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\[2pt]
0, & \pi \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha \\[2pt]
V_m \sin(\omega t), & \pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi
\end{cases}
$$

**Definition of average value over one full period ($2\pi$):**

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \; d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the piecewise definition:**

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \;+\; 0 \;+\; \int_{\pi + \alpha}^{2\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the first integral:**

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = V_m \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = V_m \left[ -\cos(\pi) + \cos(\alpha) \right] = V_m \left[ 1 + \cos\alpha \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the second integral. Let $\theta = \omega t - \pi$:**

$$
\int_{\pi + \alpha}^{2\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = V_m \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin(\theta + \pi) \, d\theta = -V_m \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta
$$

$$
= -V_m \left[ -\cos\theta \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = V_m \left[ \cos\pi - \cos\alpha \right] = V_m \left[ -1 - \cos\alpha \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the two integrals:**

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ \big(1 + \cos\alpha\big) + \big(-1 - \cos\alpha\big) \Big]
$$

Wait — this is a beautiful result. The two half-cycle integrals contribute **identically** because of the bridge's polarity inversion. Let us re-derive more carefully by exploiting symmetry.

**Step 4 (corrected) — By symmetry:** Both pulses have the same area because the bridge inverts the negative half-cycle. So we can simply write:

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\,(1 + \cos\alpha)\,}
$$

**Sanity check at $\alpha = 0$:** $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ — the well-known average of a full-wave-rectified sine wave. ✓

**Sanity check at $\alpha = \pi$:** $V_{o(avg)} = 0$ — full extinction. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the RMS Output Voltage $V_{o(rms)}$

**Definition of RMS over one full period:**

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^{\,2}(\omega t) \; d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute piecewise $v_o$ and exploit symmetry** (two identical pulses per period):

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^{\,2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the half-angle identity** $\sin^{2}(\omega t) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 3 — Split the integral:**

$$
V_{o(rms)} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} d(\omega t) - \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos(2\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right]}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each integral:**

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} d(\omega t) = \pi - \alpha
$$

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos(2\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \left[ \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{\sin(2\pi) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} = -\frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**

$$
V_{o(rms)} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) - \left(-\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right) \right]} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]}
$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $\pi$ and use $V_{rms,s} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ is the RMS value of the AC supply. The square-root bracket quantifies how much of the supply RMS appears at the DC output.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Form Factor and Ripple Factor

**Form Factor (FF):**

$$
FF = \frac{V_{o(rms)}}{V_{o(avg)}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]}}{\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)} = \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{2}(1 + \cos\alpha)} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi-\alpha)+\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}
$$

**At $\alpha = 0$:**

$$
FF = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{1} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11
$$

**Ripple Factor (RF):**

$$
\boxed{\,RF = \sqrt{FF^{\,2} - 1} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{o(rms)}^{\,2} - V_{o(avg)}^{\,2}}}{V_{o(avg)}}\,}
$$

**Rectification Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{DC}}{P_{o}} = \frac{V_{o(avg)}^{\,2}}{V_{o(rms)}^{\,2}} = \frac{1}{FF^{\,2}}
$$

**At $\alpha = 0$:** $\eta = \dfrac{1}{(1.11)^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{1.234} = 0.8103 = 81.03\%$ — the classic textbook value for a single-phase full-wave rectifier. ✓

---

## 3.4 Numerical Verification — Worked Example

**Given:** $V_{rms} = 230$ V, $R = 10\ \Omega$, $\alpha = 60^{\circ} = \pi/3$ rad.

**Step 1 — Compute $V_m$:**

$$
V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot 230 = 325.27 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V_{o(avg)}$:**

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{325.27}{\pi}(1 + \cos 60^{\circ}) = \frac{325.27}{\pi}(1 + 0.5) = \frac{325.27 \cdot 1.5}{\pi} = 155.27 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{o(rms)}$:**

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \frac{325.27}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\left(\pi - \frac{\pi}{3}\right) + \frac{\sin 120^{\circ}}{2}\right]}
$$

$$
= 230 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{2\pi}{3} + \frac{0.8660}{2}\right]} = 230 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[2.0944 + 0.4330\right]}
$$

$$
= 230 \sqrt{\frac{2.5274}{3.1416}} = 230 \sqrt{0.8045} = 230 \cdot 0.8970 = 206.31 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Form Factor, Ripple Factor, Efficiency:**

$$
FF = \frac{206.31}{155.27} = 1.329
$$

$$
RF = \sqrt{1.329^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{0.7662} = 0.8754
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{1.329^{2}} = \frac{1}{1.766} = 0.5662 = 56.62\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Load Power and Current:**

$$
P_o = \frac{V_{o(rms)}^{2}}{R} = \frac{206.31^{2}}{10} = 4256.4 \text{ W}
$$

$$
I_{o(avg)} = \frac{155.27}{10} = 15.53 \text{ A}, \quad I_{o(rms)} = \frac{206.31}{10} = 20.63 \text{ A}
$$

**Observation:** As $\alpha$ increases from 0 to 60°, the average voltage drops from **207.07 V** to **155.27 V** (a 25% reduction), and the ripple content increases significantly. This is the **phase-angle control** in action — the heart of every dimmer, heater, and small DC drive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Topology — Block Architecture (Full Converter)

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a single-phase fully-controlled bridge. Each node corresponds to a physical block in the converter, illustrating current flow, control signal injection, and load termination.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ACSRC[AC SOURCE 230V 50Hz]:::source
    SCR1[SCR T1]:::scr
    SCR2[SCR T2]:::scr
    SCR3[SCR T3]:::scr
    SCR4[SCR T4]:::scr
    LOAD[RESISTIVE LOAD R]:::load
    FCTRL[FIREING CIRCUIT alpha CONTROLLER]:::ctrl
    GP1[GATE PULSE T1 T2 at alpha]:::pulse
    GP2[GATE PULSE T3 T4 at pi plus alpha]:::pulse

    ACSRC -- TOP WIRE --> SCR1
    ACSRC -- BOTTOM WIRE --> SCR4
    SCR1 -- A --> SCR2
    SCR2 -- A --> ACSRC
    SCR3 -- B --> SCR4
    SCR3 -- B --> ACSRC
    SCR1 -- DC POS --> LOAD
    SCR4 -- DC NEG --> LOAD
    LOAD -- DC NEG RETURN --> SCR2
    LOAD -- DC NEG RETURN --> SCR3
    FCTRL --> GP1
    FCTRL --> GP2
    GP1 --> SCR1
    GP1 --> SCR2
    GP2 --> SCR3
    GP2 --> SCR4

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef scr fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef load fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef pulse fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows show that the firing circuit (block FCTRL) generates two phase-shifted gate-pulse trains, one delayed by α (for T1, T2) and the other by π+α (for T3, T4). The four SCRs form a diamond bridge whose DC terminals feed the load R.

## 4.2 Circuit Topology — Block Architecture (Semi-Converter)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ACSRC[AC SOURCE 230V 50Hz]:::source
    SCR1[SCR T1 UPPER ARM]:::scr
    SCR2[SCR T2 UPPER ARM]:::scr
    D1[DIODE D1 LOWER ARM]:::diode
    D2[DIODE D2 LOWER ARM]:::diode
    LOAD[RESISTIVE LOAD R]:::load
    FCTRL[FIREING CIRCUIT alpha CONTROLLER]:::ctrl
    GP1[GATE PULSE T1 AT alpha]:::pulse
    GP2[GATE PULSE T2 AT pi plus alpha]:::pulse

    ACSRC -- LINE A --> SCR1
    ACSRC -- LINE B --> SCR2
    SCR1 --> D1
    SCR2 --> D2
    D1 --> ACSRC
    D2 --> ACSRC
    SCR1 -- DC POS --> LOAD
    D2 -- DC NEG --> LOAD
    FCTRL --> GP1 --> SCR1
    FCTRL --> GP2 --> SCR2

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef scr fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef diode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef pulse fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Typical Waveform Timing Diagram

The figure below renders the **key waveforms** in a sequence chart — the firing pulses, source voltage, gate pulses, and the resulting output voltage. Each lane is one waveform, time progresses left-to-right, and the markers indicate the firing events.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Time as omega t axis
    participant Vs as Vs Source Vm sin
    participant Gate12 as Gate T1 T2 pulse
    participant Gate34 as Gate T3 T4 pulse
    participant Vo as Vo Output Voltage
    participant Io as Io Load Current

    Time->>Vs: 0 to 2pi cycle
    Note over Vs: Positive half 0 to pi
    Note over Vs: Negative half pi to 2pi
    Time->>Gate12: HIGH from alpha to pi
    Time->>Gate12: LOW from pi to 2pi plus alpha
    Time->>Gate34: LOW from 0 to pi plus alpha
    Time->>Gate34: HIGH from pi plus alpha to 2pi
    Gate12->>Vo: Vo equals Vm sin from alpha to pi
    Gate12->>Io: Io equals Vm sin over R from alpha to pi
    Gate34->>Vo: Vo equals Vm sin from pi plus alpha to 2pi
    Gate34->>Io: Io equals Vm sin over R from pi plus alpha to 2pi
    Note over Vo,Io: Vo and Io are zero between pi and pi plus alpha
```

## 4.4 Sketches of the Waveforms (ASCII Reconstruction)

For KTU board-exam answer scripts, you must draw the **timing diagram** with the following labelled axes:

```
   v_s, v_o, i_o
     ▲
Vm   │      ╱‾‾╲                          ╱‾‾╲
     │     ╱    ╲                        ╱    ╲
     │    ╱      ╲                      ╱      ╲
  0  ├───╱────────╲────┬───────────────╱────────╲────┬─────► ωt
     │  0    α     π   π+α             2π
     │                 │                │
     │  v_s:  sin(ωt)  │  v_s: sin(ωt) │ v_s: sin(ωt)
     │                 │                │
     │  v_o:  0 then   │   v_o: 0 then │
     │  Vm sin(ωt)     │   Vm sin(ωt)  │
     │  from α to π    │   from π+α    │
     │                 │   to 2π        │
     │  i_o: v_o/R     │   i_o: v_o/R  │

   GATE PULSES (T1, T2):  __|‾‾‾‾‾|________|‾‾‾‾‾|______
                          0  α    π       π+α    2π
   GATE PULSES (T3, T4):  ________|‾‾‾‾‾|________|‾‾‾‾‾|__
                          0  α    π+α    2π
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the term *firing angle* in a single-phase fully-controlled bridge rectifier. State its range of operation for a purely resistive load.

**Model Answer:**
The **firing angle (α)** of a thyristor (SCR) in a phase-controlled rectifier is the electrical angle (measured in degrees or radians) between the instant at which the thyristor would naturally start conducting in an *uncontrolled* diode bridge (i.e., the instant the AC source becomes sufficiently positive to forward-bias it) and the instant at which the gate pulse is actually applied to turn it ON.

For a single-phase fully- or half-controlled bridge feeding a **purely resistive load**:
$$
\boxed{\,0 \le \alpha \le 180^{\circ}\ \text{(or}\ 0 \le \alpha \le \pi\ \text{rad)}\,}
$$

At $\alpha = 0^{\circ}$, the converter behaves as an uncontrolled diode bridge (maximum DC output). At $\alpha = 180^{\circ}$, the converter delivers zero average DC output (full extinction).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Compare a single-phase fully-controlled bridge and a single-phase half-controlled bridge in terms of the number of thyristors, complexity of the firing circuit, and output voltage for a resistive load in continuous conduction mode.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Fully-Controlled Bridge | Half-Controlled Bridge (Semi-Converter) |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Number of SCRs | 4 (T1, T2, T3, T4) | 2 (T1, T2) |
| Number of diodes | 0 | 2 (D1, D2) |
| Firing circuit complexity | Higher (2 pulse trains, 2 firing angles) | Lower (1 pulse train per SCR, simpler) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Output voltage (R-load, CCM) | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ (identical) |
| Polarity reversal capability | Yes (by shifting α beyond 90°) | No (output is always unidirectional) |
| Quadrant operation | Two-quadrant (1st and 4th) | One-quadrant (1st only) |

> [!NOTE]
> For a **purely resistive load**, the two topologies give **identical** output voltage waveforms in continuous conduction. The differences (freewheeling, inversion mode) emerge only with RL loads.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students forget the factor of **2** when integrating over a full period, then divide by $2\pi$. The correct method is to integrate over one pulse of width $(\pi - \alpha)$ and divide by $\pi$ (since there are 2 such pulses per period).
> - Students often confuse **PIV** (Peak Inverse Voltage) for an SCR in a full bridge. The PIV per SCR in a single-phase full bridge is **$V_m$** (not $2V_m$ as in a centre-tapped transformer rectifier).
> - The **Ripple Factor** formula is often mis-quoted as $RF = FF - 1$ — the correct relation is $RF = \sqrt{FF^{2} - 1}$.
> - For R-load, the **discontinuous-conduction condition** sets in when $\alpha > \pi/2$ (in some textbooks) — students forget to check this and blindly apply the CCM formula.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** For a single-phase fully-controlled bridge rectifier supplying a purely resistive load of $R = 20\ \Omega$ from a $230$ V, $50$ Hz AC source, with firing angle $\alpha = 45^{\circ}$:

**(a)** Sketch the circuit diagram and the waveforms of source voltage, gate pulses, output voltage, and output current. Mark the conduction intervals clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine the average output voltage, RMS output voltage, average and RMS load currents, ripple factor, and rectification efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Circuit and Waveforms (7 Marks)

**Circuit (drawn on answer script):**

A diamond bridge of four SCRs (T1, T2, T3, T4) is connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz AC source. The load $R = 20\ \Omega$ is connected across the DC output terminals. A firing circuit produces two pulse trains, one for the (T1, T2) pair (at $\alpha = 45^{\circ}$) and another for the (T3, T4) pair (at $180^{\circ} + 45^{\circ} = 225^{\circ}$).

**Conduction schedule:**

| Interval ($\omega t$) | Conducting devices | $v_o$ | $i_o$ |
|------------------------|--------------------|--------|--------|
| $0$ to $\alpha = 45^{\circ}$ | None | 0 | 0 |
| $\alpha$ to $\pi$ | T1, T2 | $V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | $V_m \sin(\omega t) / R$ |
| $\pi$ to $\pi + \alpha$ | None (T1, T2 turn OFF at $\pi$) | 0 | 0 |
| $\pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi$ | T3, T4 | $V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | $V_m \sin(\omega t) / R$ |

**Waveforms (to be drawn on answer script with neat axes and labels):**

- **$v_s$ (source):** A sinusoid $V_m \sin(\omega t)$ crossing zero at $0, \pi, 2\pi$.
- **Gate pulses T1, T2:** A pulse train HIGH from $45^{\circ}$ to $180^{\circ}$, and from $405^{\circ}$ to $540^{\circ}$ (in the next cycle).
- **Gate pulses T3, T4:** A pulse train HIGH from $225^{\circ}$ to $360^{\circ}$.
- **$v_o$ (output):** Two positive humps per cycle — one from $45^{\circ}$ to $180^{\circ}$ following the positive half of $v_s$, and one from $225^{\circ}$ to $360^{\circ}$ following the *inverted* negative half of $v_s$. Output is **zero** from $180^{\circ}$ to $225^{\circ}$.
- **$i_o$:** Identical in shape to $v_o$, scaled by $1/R$.

**Key numerical value:** $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27$ V.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Average output voltage:**

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) = \frac{325.27}{\pi}(1 + \cos 45^{\circ}) = \frac{325.27}{\pi}(1 + 0.7071) = \frac{325.27 \times 1.7071}{3.1416}
$$

$$
V_{o(avg)} = 176.74 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — RMS output voltage:**

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}
$$

$$
= \frac{325.27}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\left(\pi - \frac{\pi}{4}\right) + \frac{\sin 90^{\circ}}{2}\right]} = 230 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{3\pi}{4} + \frac{1}{2}\right]}
$$

$$
= 230 \sqrt{\frac{2.3562 + 0.5}{3.1416}} = 230 \sqrt{\frac{2.8562}{3.1416}} = 230 \sqrt{0.9091} = 230 \times 0.9535 = 219.30 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Average and RMS load currents:**

$$
I_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_{o(avg)}}{R} = \frac{176.74}{20} = 8.837 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_{o(rms)}}{R} = \frac{219.30}{20} = 10.965 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4 — Ripple factor:**

$$
FF = \frac{V_{o(rms)}}{V_{o(avg)}} = \frac{219.30}{176.74} = 1.2408
$$

$$
RF = \sqrt{FF^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{1.5396 - 1} = \sqrt{0.5396} = 0.7346
$$

**Step 5 — Rectification efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{FF^{2}} = \frac{1}{1.5396} = 0.6495 = 64.95\%
$$

**Step 6 — Output power:**

$$
P_o = I_{o(rms)}^{2} \cdot R = (10.965)^{2} \times 20 = 2404.6 \text{ W} \approx 2.40 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final tabulated answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $V_{o(avg)}$ | **176.74 V** |
| $V_{o(rms)}$ | **219.30 V** |
| $I_{o(avg)}$ | **8.84 A** |
| $I_{o(rms)}$ | **10.97 A** |
| Ripple factor | **0.735** |
| Rectification efficiency | **64.95 %** |
| Output power | **2.40 kW** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** A single-phase half-controlled bridge rectifier (semi-converter) with two SCRs and two diodes feeds a resistive load $R = 15\ \Omega$ from a $230$ V, $50$ Hz supply. The firing angle is $\alpha = 60^{\circ}$.

**(a)** With a neat sketch of the circuit, explain the operating principle of the converter and draw the source voltage, gate pulses, and output voltage waveforms for one complete cycle. Clearly mark the conduction intervals. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the average output voltage in terms of $V_m$ and $\alpha$. Compute the average and RMS output voltages, average and RMS load currents, and the rectification efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Circuit and Operating Principle (7 Marks)

**Circuit description:** A single-phase half-controlled bridge (semi-converter) uses **two thyristors T1 and T2** in the upper arms and **two power diodes D1 and D2** in the lower arms. The AC source is connected to the bridge through the input terminals; the resistive load R is connected across the DC output terminals. A firing circuit generates gate pulses for T1 (at $\omega t = \alpha$) and for T2 (at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$).

**Operating principle:**

- **Mode 1 ($0 \le \omega t < \alpha$):** Source voltage is positive. T1 and D1 are forward-biased, but T1 has not yet been fired. No conduction; $v_o = 0$.
- **Mode 2 ($\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$):** At $\omega t = \alpha$, T1 is fired. T1 and D1 conduct. Current flows: Source(+) → T1 → Load R → D1 → Source(–). $v_o = v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. At $\omega t = \pi$, the source voltage reverses polarity, and T1 and D1 become reverse-biased and turn OFF.
- **Mode 3 ($\pi \le \omega t < \pi + \alpha$):** Source voltage is negative. T2 and D2 are forward-biased, but T2 has not yet been fired. No conduction; $v_o = 0$. *(Note: For a pure R-load, no freewheeling occurs through D1 and D2 because the load has no inductance to sustain current.)*
- **Mode 4 ($\pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi$):** At $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$, T2 is fired. T2 and D2 conduct. The bridge inverts polarity, so $v_o = -v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (positive). At $\omega t = 2\pi$, T2 and D2 turn OFF naturally.

**Waveform sketch** (to be drawn on answer script):

- $v_s$: Standard sinusoid.
- Gate T1: Pulse HIGH from $\alpha = 60^{\circ}$ to $180^{\circ}$.
- Gate T2: Pulse HIGH from $240^{\circ}$ to $360^{\circ}$.
- $v_o$: Positive humps from $60^{\circ}$ to $180^{\circ}$ and from $240^{\circ}$ to $360^{\circ}$, **zero** in between.
- $i_o$: Scaled replica of $v_o$ ($i_o = v_o / R$).

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Derivation of $V_{o(avg)}$:**

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + 0 + \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)\right]
$$

Using the half-angle substitution $\theta = \omega t - \pi$ for the second integral:

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[V_m(1 + \cos\alpha) + V_m(1 + \cos\alpha)\right] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 V_m (1 + \cos\alpha)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)\,}
$$

**Substitute values:** $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27$ V, $\alpha = 60^{\circ}$, $\cos 60^{\circ} = 0.5$:

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{325.27}{\pi}(1 + 0.5) = \frac{325.27 \times 1.5}{3.1416} = 155.27 \text{ V}
$$

**RMS voltage** (using the same formula as the full converter for R-load):

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}
$$

$$
= 230 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\left(\pi - \frac{\pi}{3}\right) + \frac{\sin 120^{\circ}}{2}\right]} = 230 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{2\pi}{3} + \frac{0.8660}{2}\right]}
$$

$$
= 230 \sqrt{\frac{2.0944 + 0.4330}{3.1416}} = 230 \sqrt{0.8045} = 230 \times 0.8970 = 206.31 \text{ V}
$$

**Load currents:**

$$
I_{o(avg)} = \frac{155.27}{15} = 10.35 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{o(rms)} = \frac{206.31}{15} = 13.75 \text{ A}
$$

**Form factor, ripple factor, efficiency:**

$$
FF = \frac{206.31}{155.27} = 1.329
$$

$$
RF = \sqrt{1.329^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{0.7662} = 0.875
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{1.329^{2}} = 0.5663 = 56.63\%
$$

**Final answer summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $V_{o(avg)}$ | **155.27 V** |
| $V_{o(rms)}$ | **206.31 V** |
| $I_{o(avg)}$ | **10.35 A** |
| $I_{o(rms)}$ | **13.75 A** |
| $FF$ | **1.329** |
| $RF$ | **0.875** |
| $\eta$ | **56.63 %** |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Half- and Fully-Controlled Bridges with R-Load**

- **Topology counts:** Full converter = **4 SCRs**, **0 diodes**. Semi-converter = **2 SCRs**, **2 diodes**.
- **Firing-angle range for R-load:** $0 \le \alpha \le 180^{\circ}$ in continuous conduction.
- **Average output voltage (both topologies with R-load, CCM):** $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$.
- **RMS output voltage:** $V_{o(rms)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$.
- **At $\alpha = 0$:** $V_{o(avg)} = 2V_m/\pi$, $FF = \pi/(2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.11$, $\eta = 81.03\%$.
- **At $\alpha = 90^{\circ}$:** $V_{o(avg)} = V_m/\pi$ (exactly half the maximum).
- **At $\alpha = 180^{\circ}$:** $V_{o(avg)} = 0$ — full extinction.
- **Pulse train:** Two gate pulses per cycle per SCR pair, separated by $\pi$.
- **Pulse width:** From firing instant to the natural zero-crossing of the supply ($180^{\circ} - \alpha$ wide).
- **Output pulse count per AC cycle:** 2 for a single-phase bridge.
- **Output ripple frequency:** $2f = 100$ Hz (Indian supply).
- **PIV per SCR:** $V_m$ (full bridge, single-phase).
- **PIV per SCR:** $V_m$ (semi-converter as well, since each SCR blocks only the peak supply when reverse-biased).
- **Conduction:** T1-T2 (positive half), T3-T4 (negative half) for full bridge. T1-D1 and T2-D2 for semi-converter.
- **For R-load only:** Semi-converter and full converter produce **identical** output voltage waveforms (no freewheeling in the semi-converter because there is no load inductance).
- **For RL-load:** Semi-converter gives **different (higher) average output** than full converter at large α because of freewheeling action through D1-D2 — but that is **Module 3 territory**, not R-load.
- **No inversion mode** for R-load — the full converter's "inversion" capability ($\alpha > 90^{\circ}$ sending power back to AC) requires an **active load** (back-EMF source) or a **DC source**, not a pure resistor.
- **Average SCR current:** $I_{T,avg} = I_{o(avg)} / 2$ because each SCR carries current for only one of the two pulses per cycle.
- **RMS SCR current:** $I_{T,rms} = I_{o(rms)} / \sqrt{2}$ for the same reason.
- **Sizing the heat sink:** The maximum average current an SCR must handle occurs at $\alpha = 0$; the heat-sink design is based on this worst-case condition.
- **Numerical trap to avoid:** When $\alpha$ is given in degrees, **convert to radians before integration**. The factor $(\pi - \alpha)$ is in radians.
- **Sketch the waveforms first** on the answer script, label axes with **symbols and units**, and mark the **conduction intervals** with shaded regions — examiners award easy marks for a clean, labelled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RL with Diode and RL with Voltage Source - Voltage and Current Equation for Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - Power Electronics & Power Converters
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RL with Diode and RL with Voltage Source - Voltage and Current Equation for Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **half-wave controlled rectifier** is a power electronic converter circuit that converts single-phase AC supply into a variable DC output by allowing only one half-cycle of the input AC waveform to pass through a switching device (thyristor/SCR) to the load. The term "controlled" implies that the output DC voltage can be regulated by varying the **firing angle (α)** of the thyristor.

In practical industrial applications, the load is rarely purely resistive. Most real loads (DC motor armature, field winding, solenoids, inductors in SMPS filters) contain **inductance (L)** in addition to resistance (R). This inductive nature fundamentally changes the current waveform — the current does not fall to zero when the supply voltage reverses, leading to **conduction beyond π radians (180°)** until the energy stored in the inductor is fully dissipated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2:**
> The two canonical load configurations analyzed in this topic are:
> 1. **RL Load with Diode** (Uncontrolled half-wave rectifier feeding an RL load — single diode replaces SCR for uncontrolled analysis).
> 2. **RL Load with Voltage Source (E)** (Half-wave controlled rectifier with an additional back-EMF source, modeling battery charging or DC motor load).
> 
> The governing differential equation for the load side, **Vs(ωt) = L(di/dt) + iR + E**, must be solved using the boundary conditions **i(α) = 0** and **i(β) = 0**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Bucket Analogy

Imagine a water tap supplying a tilted bucket with a small hole at the bottom:

- The **diode/SCR** is the tap that you can open (turn on) at a chosen moment.
- The **resistor R** is the steady leak — water that leaves the bucket continuously.
- The **inductor L** is the bucket itself — it **stores water (energy)** and resists sudden changes in flow.
- The **battery E** (when present) is a raised platform inside the bucket — water must climb over it before any can flow out the leak.

When the tap is closed, nothing flows. When opened, water rushes in, fills the bucket, and only when the bucket's water level exceeds the platform height does it drain out. If you close the tap before the bucket empties, water keeps draining for a while — this is the **extinction angle β** in electrical terms. The **firing angle α** is exactly when you open the tap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why inductance causes β > π:**
> The inductor L stores energy in its magnetic field during the positive half-cycle. When the supply voltage goes negative, this stored energy keeps the current flowing in the same direction, forcing the diode/SCR to continue conducting **past π radians** until the inductor is fully discharged. This delayed extinction is the most important phenomenon in this topic.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:--------------|
| **Vs** | Instantaneous AC supply voltage | Volts (V) |
| **Vm** | Peak supply voltage | Volts (V) |
| **R** | Load resistance | Ohms (Ω) |
| **L** | Load inductance | Henry (H) |
| **E** | Battery / back-EMF | Volts (V) |
| **ω** | Angular supply frequency | rad/s (typically **2π × 50 = 314.16 rad/s** in India) |
| **α** | Firing angle | radians (°) |
| **β** | Extinction angle | radians (°) |
| **γ** | Conduction angle = β − α | radians (°) |
| **Z** | Load impedance = √(R² + (ωL)²) | Ohms (Ω) |
| **φ** | Load impedance angle = tan⁻¹(ωL/R) | radians (°) |
| **τ** | Load time constant = L/R | seconds (s) |
| **ωτ** | Normalized time constant = ωL/R | dimensionless |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology Analysis

### 2.1.1 Case A — Half-Wave Rectifier with RL Load (Uncontrolled, using Diode)

**Circuit Configuration:**
- Single-phase AC source: $v_s(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- One diode D in series with RL load
- No firing angle control — diode conducts automatically as soon as $v_s$ becomes positive

**Operational Stages:**

1. **Stage 1 — Diode OFF (ωt = 0 to ωt = 0⁺):**
   - For ωt < 0, vs is negative → diode is reverse-biased → current i = 0
   - All supply voltage appears across the diode as reverse voltage

2. **Stage 2 — Diode ON (ωt = 0 to ωt = β):**
   - At ωt = 0, vs becomes positive and crosses zero
   - Diode turns ON (forward biased)
   - KVL applied: $v_s = L\frac{di}{dt} + iR$
   - Current rises from 0, reaches a peak, then begins to fall
   - Diode continues to conduct past π radians because of stored inductor energy
   - At ωt = β, current finally becomes zero and diode turns OFF

3. **Stage 3 — Diode OFF (ωt = β to 2π):**
   - Diode remains reverse-biased
   - Current i = 0
   - Supply voltage reappears across diode

### 2.1.2 Case B — Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier with RL Load and Voltage Source E

**Circuit Configuration:**
- Single-phase AC source: $v_s(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- One thyristor T (SCR) in series with RL load
- A DC voltage source E (battery / motor back-EMF) in series with the load
- Controlled by firing angle α

**Operational Stages:**

1. **Stage 1 — SCR OFF (0 to α):**
   - Even though vs may be positive, the SCR is not yet triggered
   - Gate pulse not applied → i = 0
   - Supply voltage appears across the SCR

2. **Stage 2 — SCR ON (α to β):**
   - Gate pulse applied at ωt = α
   - KVL: $v_s = L\frac{di}{dt} + iR + E$
   - Current rises from 0, peaks, then decays
   - SCR continues to conduct as long as current i > 0 and vs > E (typically)
   - At ωt = β, i returns to 0 and SCR turns OFF naturally (commutation)

3. **Stage 3 — SCR OFF (β to 2π + α):**
   - Current i = 0
   - Voltage across SCR = vs − E (when vs is reverse, SCR sees negative voltage — natural commutation confirmed)

## 2.2 Why We Need a Differential Equation Solution

Because L is present, the relationship between voltage and current is **not algebraic** — it is a **first-order linear ordinary differential equation (ODE)**. The general solution to any first-order linear ODE of the form:

$$\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{R}{L}i = \frac{f(t)}{L}$$

is the sum of:
- **Forced (steady-state) response** — sinusoidal, following the source
- **Natural (transient) response** — exponential decay, governed by the time constant L/R

This physical principle — superposition of forced sinusoidal and natural exponential modes — is the bedrock of all power electronics transient analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Battery Chargers:** RL with voltage source models the battery being charged — E is the battery EMF, R is internal resistance, L is cable/choke inductance.
> - **DC Motor Drives:** The armature of a DC motor is exactly modeled as R + L + back-EMF E. The rectifier feeds variable DC to control speed.
> - **UPS Systems:** Inverter-fed RL loads use the same mathematical framework in reverse.
> - **Welding Rectifiers:** Highly inductive welding transformers require this analysis to size the freewheeling path.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM-CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize these for KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary Condition Used |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------------------------|
| 1 | $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$ | Load impedance magnitude | — |
| 2 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega L}{R}\right)$ | Load impedance angle (0 < φ < 90°) | — |
| 3 | $\omega\tau = \frac{\omega L}{R} = \tan\phi$ | Normalized time constant | — |
| 4 | $i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\omega t - \phi) + \sin(\phi)\,e^{-\omega t / \omega\tau}\right]$ | **Current equation — RL with Diode** | $i(0) = 0$ |
| 5 | $\sin(\beta - \phi) + \sin(\phi)\,e^{-\beta/\omega\tau} = 0$ | **Extinction equation — RL with Diode** | $i(\beta) = 0$ |
| 6 | $i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m} - \left(\sin(\alpha - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m}\right)e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/\omega\tau}\right]$ | **Current equation — RL with E** | $i(\alpha) = 0$ |
| 7 | $\sin(\beta - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m} - \left(\sin(\alpha - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m}\right)e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\omega\tau} = 0$ | **Extinction equation — RL with E** | $i(\beta) = 0$ |
| 8 | $V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\beta} (V_m \sin\omega t - E)\,d(\omega t)$ | Average output voltage (RL with E) | — |
| 9 | $I_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\beta} i(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)$ | Average output current | — |
| 10 | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\beta} v_o^2(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)}$ | RMS output voltage | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall — Pipe Symbol Rule:**
> When writing **|sin(β − φ)|** or similar absolute-value expressions in your exam answer, **always use the `\vert` or `\mid` LaTeX command**. Do **NOT** use the raw keyboard pipe `|`, as it collides with markdown table syntax and renders as a broken cell separator. This has been a recurring typographical penalty in scanned answer sheets.

## 2.4 Physical Intuition Summary of Key Angles

- **Firing angle α (alpha):** The moment we command the SCR to turn ON. We control this. Higher α → lower output voltage.
- **Extinction angle β (beta):** The moment the SCR naturally turns OFF because current has decayed to zero. We **cannot** control β directly; it depends on α, R, L, and E.
- **Conduction angle γ = β − α:** The duration the SCR actually conducts per cycle. For pure R load, γ = π − α. For RL load, γ > π − α.
- **Impedance angle φ (phi):** A fixed parameter of the load. It is the phase shift between voltage and current that would exist in steady-state AC operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current Equation for Half-Wave Rectifier with RL Load (Uncontrolled, Diode)

### 3.1.1 Circuit Equation Setup

When the diode D is forward biased and conducting, applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the closed loop:

$$v_s(\omega t) = L\frac{di}{dt} + iR$$

Substituting $v_s(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$V_m \sin(\omega t) = L\frac{di}{dt} + iR$$

### 3.1.2 Standard Form Conversion

Dividing throughout by L to obtain standard first-order linear ODE form:

$$\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{R}{L}\,i = \frac{V_m}{L}\sin(\omega t)$$

This is of the form $\frac{di}{dt} + P \cdot i = Q(t)$ where $P = R/L$ and $Q(t) = \frac{V_m}{L}\sin(\omega t)$.

The **integrating factor** is:

$$IF = e^{\int P\,dt} = e^{(R/L)t} = e^{t/\tau}$$

Multiplying both sides by the integrating factor:

$$e^{t/\tau}\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{R}{L}e^{t/\tau}\,i = \frac{V_m}{L}\sin(\omega t)\,e^{t/\tau}$$

The left side is the exact derivative of $(i \cdot e^{t/\tau})$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(i\,e^{t/\tau}\right) = \frac{V_m}{L}\sin(\omega t)\,e^{t/\tau}$$

### 3.1.3 Integration Step

Integrating both sides with respect to t:

$$i\,e^{t/\tau} = \frac{V_m}{L}\int \sin(\omega t)\,e^{t/\tau}\,dt + C$$

The integral $\int \sin(\omega t)\,e^{t/\tau}\,dt$ is a standard form. Using the identity:

$$\int e^{at}\sin(bt)\,dt = \frac{e^{at}(a\sin(bt) - b\cos(bt))}{a^2 + b^2}$$

With $a = 1/\tau$ and $b = \omega$:

$$\int e^{t/\tau}\sin(\omega t)\,dt = \frac{e^{t/\tau}\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\sin(\omega t) - \omega\cos(\omega t)\right)}{\frac{1}{\tau^2} + \omega^2}$$

Simplify the denominator: $\frac{1}{\tau^2} + \omega^2 = \frac{1 + \omega^2\tau^2}{\tau^2}$

Therefore:

$$\int e^{t/\tau}\sin(\omega t)\,dt = \frac{e^{t/\tau}\,\tau^2\left(\frac{\sin(\omega t)}{\tau} - \omega\cos(\omega t)\right)}{1 + \omega^2\tau^2} = \frac{e^{t/\tau}\left(\tau\sin(\omega t) - \omega\tau^2\cos(\omega t)\right)}{1 + \omega^2\tau^2}$$

### 3.1.4 Substituting Back

$$i\,e^{t/\tau} = \frac{V_m}{L}\cdot\frac{e^{t/\tau}\left(\tau\sin(\omega t) - \omega\tau^2\cos(\omega t)\right)}{1 + \omega^2\tau^2} + C$$

Cancel $e^{t/\tau}$ from both sides:

$$i = \frac{V_m}{L(1 + \omega^2\tau^2)}\left(\tau\sin(\omega t) - \omega\tau^2\cos(\omega t)\right) + C\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

Note that $\frac{\tau}{L(1 + \omega^2\tau^2)} = \frac{L/R}{L(1 + \omega^2 L^2/R^2)} = \frac{1}{R + \omega^2 L^2/R} = \frac{R}{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2} = \frac{R}{Z^2}$

Similarly, $\frac{\omega\tau^2}{L(1 + \omega^2\tau^2)} = \frac{\omega L}{Z^2}$

Thus:

$$i = \frac{V_m R}{Z^2}\sin(\omega t) - \frac{V_m \omega L}{Z^2}\cos(\omega t) + C\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

### 3.1.5 Combining Sinusoidal Terms Using Phasor Identity

Factor out $\frac{V_m}{Z}$:

$$i = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\frac{R}{Z}\sin(\omega t) - \frac{\omega L}{Z}\cos(\omega t)\right] + C\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

Using $\frac{R}{Z} = \cos\phi$ and $\frac{\omega L}{Z} = \sin\phi$:

$$i = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\cos\phi\sin(\omega t) - \sin\phi\cos(\omega t)\right] + C\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

Apply the trigonometric identity $\sin(A - B) = \sin A\cos B - \cos A\sin B$ in reverse form:

$$\cos\phi\sin(\omega t) - \sin\phi\cos(\omega t) = \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Therefore:

$$i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi) + C\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

### 3.1.6 Applying Boundary Condition — i(0) = 0

At ωt = 0, current is zero (diode just turned on, inductor opposes sudden change):

$$0 = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(-\phi) + C \cdot 1$$

$$0 = -\frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\phi) + C$$

$$C = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\phi)$$

### 3.1.7 Final Current Equation

$$\boxed{\,i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\omega t - \phi) + \sin(\phi)\,e^{-\omega t / \omega\tau}\right]\,}$$

Converting t to ωt using $t = \omega t/\omega$ so that $t/\tau = \omega t / (\omega\tau)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of Two Terms:**
> - $\frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$ → **Forced (steady-state) response** — would exist if circuit had been operating forever.
> - $\frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\phi)\,e^{-\omega t/\omega\tau}$ → **Natural (transient) response** — decays with time constant τ = L/R. The presence of $\sin(\phi)$ (which is positive) means the transient term is positive and tries to keep current flowing.

### 3.1.8 Extinction Angle Equation

Setting i(β) = 0 in the current equation:

$$0 = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\beta - \phi) + \sin(\phi)\,e^{-\beta/\omega\tau}\right]$$

Since $V_m/Z \neq 0$:

$$\boxed{\,\sin(\beta - \phi) + \sin(\phi)\,e^{-\beta/\omega\tau} = 0\,}$$

This is a **transcendental equation** — it cannot be solved in closed form for β. In KTU exams, β is usually obtained by:
- **Trial and error** (Newton-Raphson method)
- **Graphical intersection** of $\sin(\phi)e^{-\beta/\omega\tau}$ and $-\sin(\beta - \phi)$
- **Read from a textbook graph** of β vs α for given ωτ

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Current Equation for Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier with RL + Voltage Source E

### 3.2.1 Circuit Equation Setup

When the SCR is conducting (between α and β), the KVL is:

$$v_s(\omega t) = L\frac{di}{dt} + iR + E$$

$$V_m \sin(\omega t) = L\frac{di}{dt} + iR + E$$

### 3.2.2 Standard Form Conversion

Rearranging:

$$\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{R}{L}i = \frac{V_m \sin(\omega t) - E}{L}$$

This is again first-order linear. The **integrating factor** remains $e^{t/\tau}$.

### 3.2.3 Forced Response (Steady-State)

For the sinusoidal portion, we can treat E as a constant DC offset. The forced response is the sum of:
- Sinusoidal response to $V_m \sin(\omega t)$: $\frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$ (same as before)
- DC response to $-E$: This is found by setting $di/dt = 0$, giving $iR = -E$, so $i = -E/R$

Therefore the **total forced response** is:

$$i_f = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \frac{E}{R}$$

### 3.2.4 Natural Response (Transient)

The natural response is the homogeneous solution:

$$i_n = A\,e^{-t/\tau} = A\,e^{-\omega t/\omega\tau}$$

### 3.2.5 Total Response

$$i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \frac{E}{R} + A\,e^{-\omega t/\omega\tau}$$

### 3.2.6 Applying Boundary Condition — i(α) = 0

At ωt = α, the SCR has just been triggered and the current was zero before:

$$0 = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi) - \frac{E}{R} + A\,e^{-\alpha/\omega\tau}$$

$$A = \left[\frac{E}{R} - \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi)\right]e^{\alpha/\omega\tau}$$

Note: $\frac{E}{R} = \frac{E}{Z\cos\phi}$. To make expressions cleaner, divide the entire current equation by considering it in terms of $E/V_m$ ratio. Define $m = E/V_m$. Then:

$$\frac{E}{R} = \frac{E\,Z}{RZ} = \frac{E}{Z\cos\phi} = \frac{mV_m}{Z\cos\phi}$$

Substituting A back and multiplying by $1 = (V_m/Z)/(V_m/Z)$:

$$i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m}\cdot\frac{Z}{R} + \frac{1}{V_m/Z}\left(\frac{E}{R} - \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi)\right)e^{\alpha/\omega\tau}\,e^{-\omega t/\omega\tau}\right]$$

Since $Z/R = 1/\cos\phi$ and $E/R = (E/V_m)(V_m/R) = (E/V_m)(V_m/Z)\cdot(Z/R)$:

$$i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m\cos\phi}\cdot\cos\phi\cdot\frac{Z}{V_m}\cdot V_m + \cdots\right]$$

This is getting algebraically intensive. A cleaner derivation uses the substitution $E/R = (E/V_m)\cdot(V_m/R) = (E/V_m)\cdot(V_m/(Z\cos\phi))$ and after algebraic manipulation:

$$\boxed{\,i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m} - \left(\sin(\alpha - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m}\right)e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/\omega\tau}\right]\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\frac{E}{V_m}$ appears because E is a DC quantity while $V_m$ is the peak of the AC source. The ratio is dimensionless and acts as a "back-EMF strength" parameter. When E = 0, the equation correctly reduces to the pure RL case (with α replacing 0 in the exponent).

### 3.2.7 Extinction Angle Equation for RL + E

Setting i(β) = 0:

$$0 = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\beta - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m} - \left(\sin(\alpha - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m}\right)e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\omega\tau}\right]$$

Dividing by $V_m/Z$:

$$\boxed{\,\sin(\beta - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m} - \left(\sin(\alpha - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m}\right)e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\omega\tau} = 0\,}$$

This transcendental equation must be solved for β given α, E, $V_m$, and the load parameters R, L.

## 3.3 Numerical Verification Example

**Problem:** A single-phase half-wave rectifier feeds an RL load with R = 10 Ω, L = 0.05 H, from a 230 V, 50 Hz supply. There is no back-EMF (E = 0). The diode conducts from 0 to β. Calculate β and peak current.

**Step 1 — Compute Parameters:**

$$V_m = 230\sqrt{2} = 325.27\,\text{V}$$

$$\omega = 2\pi \cdot 50 = 314.159\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega L = 314.159 \cdot 0.05 = 15.708\,\Omega$$

$$Z = \sqrt{10^2 + 15.708^2} = \sqrt{100 + 246.74} = \sqrt{346.74} = 18.62\,\Omega$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{15.708}{10}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.5708) = 57.52° = 1.004\,\text{rad}$$

$$\omega\tau = \frac{\omega L}{R} = \frac{15.708}{10} = 1.5708$$

**Step 2 — Extinction Equation:**

$$\sin(\beta - 1.004) + \sin(1.004)\,e^{-\beta/1.5708} = 0$$

Iterating:
- Try β = 3.5 rad (≈ 200.5°): $\sin(2.496) + \sin(1.004)\cdot e^{-2.228} = 0.5988 + 0.8441 \cdot 0.1080 = 0.5988 + 0.0912 = 0.6900$ (positive, β must be larger)
- Try β = 4.5 rad (≈ 257.8°): $\sin(3.496) + \sin(1.004)\cdot e^{-2.864} = -0.2819 + 0.8441 \cdot 0.0570 = -0.2819 + 0.0481 = -0.2338$ (negative)
- Try β = 4.2 rad (≈ 240.6°): $\sin(3.196) + \sin(1.004)\cdot e^{-2.674} = -0.0588 + 0.8441 \cdot 0.0688 = -0.0588 + 0.0581 = -0.0007$ ✓

**β ≈ 4.2 rad ≈ 240.6°**

This confirms β > π (180°), as expected for an RL load.

**Step 3 — Peak Current:**

The peak occurs where $di/d(\omega t) = 0$. Differentiating the current equation and setting to zero yields a complex condition. A simpler approach: evaluate i(ωt) at the point where $\frac{V_m}{Z}\cos(\omega t - \phi) - \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\phi)\cdot\frac{1}{\omega\tau}\cdot e^{-\omega t/\omega\tau} = 0$.

Iterating numerically: peak occurs near ωt ≈ π/2 + φ = 1.571 + 1.004 = 2.575 rad (147.5°).

$$i_{peak} = \frac{325.27}{18.62}\left[\sin(2.575 - 1.004) + \sin(1.004)\cdot e^{-2.575/1.5708}\right]$$

$$= 17.47 \cdot [\sin(1.571) + 0.8441 \cdot e^{-1.639}] = 17.47 \cdot [1.0000 + 0.8441 \cdot 0.1940]$$

$$= 17.47 \cdot [1.0000 + 0.1638] = 17.47 \cdot 1.1638 = 20.33\,\text{A}$$

**Verification:** A pure R load would give $V_m/R = 325.27/10 = 32.5$ A peak. Our RL peak of 20.33 A is sensibly lower, confirming the load impedance is doing its job.

> - The **supply voltage** is a pure sinusoid, independent of load.
> - The **output voltage** follows the supply during conduction (α to β) but is offset by E.
