# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q:** Define **firing angle (α)** and **extinction angle (β)** for a half-wave controlled rectifier with RL load. Why does β depend on α in the RL load case but is fixed at π in the pure R load case?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Firing angle α (1 mark):** It is the angular instant (measured from the zero-crossing of the supply voltage) at which the gate pulse is applied to the thyristor to turn it ON. For ωt < α, the SCR remains in the forward blocking mode.

**Extinction angle β (1 mark):** It is the angular instant at which the thyristor current falls to zero naturally and the device turns OFF. It marks the end of conduction within a cycle.

**Why β depends on α in RL case (1 mark):** In a pure R load, the current is in phase with voltage and naturally goes to zero at ωt = π, so β = π regardless of α. In an RL load, the inductor stores energy during conduction and releases it after voltage reversal, keeping the SCR conducting **past π radians**. The longer the SCR was ON (i.e., the larger γ = β − α), the more energy is stored in L, and hence β is delayed further. Thus β is a function of α, R, L, and E — solved from the transcendental extinction equation.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q:** For a half-wave controlled rectifier supplying an RL load, write the **general form of the load current equation** and identify the two terms physically.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The current equation is:

$$i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi) + \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\phi)\,e^{-\omega t/\omega\tau}$$

**Physical identification (2 marks):**
- **First term** $\frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$: The **forced/steady-state response** — this is the current that would flow if the circuit had been energized for an infinitely long time. It is sinusoidal, lagging the voltage by angle φ.
- **Second term** $\frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\phi)\,e^{-\omega t/\omega\tau}$: The **natural/transient response** — this accounts for the initial condition of the inductor (current was zero at ωt = 0). It decays exponentially with time constant τ = L/R and represents the dissipation of the initial stored energy in the inductor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and **must contain an internal choice** (a) OR (b). Below we present two parallel questions — both are model solutions.

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the **expression for load current** in a single-phase half-wave rectifier with RL load (uncontrolled, using a diode). Clearly state the boundary conditions and the extinction angle equation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier feeds an RL load with R = 5 Ω, L = 20 mH from a 230 V, 50 Hz source. The firing angle is α = 30°. The load also has a back-EMF source E = 50 V in series. Compute the **extinction angle β** and the **peak load current**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — KVL during conduction (1 mark):**

$$V_m \sin(\omega t) = L\frac{di}{dt} + iR$$

**Step 2 — Convert to standard form and apply integrating factor (2 marks):**

$$\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{R}{L}i = \frac{V_m}{L}\sin(\omega t)$$

Integrating factor: $IF = e^{t/\tau}$ where τ = L/R

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(i\,e^{t/\tau}\right) = \frac{V_m}{L}\sin(\omega t)\,e^{t/\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate and simplify (2 marks):**

$$i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi) + C\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary condition i(0) = 0 (1 mark):**

$$C = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin\phi$$

**Step 5 — Final equation and extinction condition (1 mark):**

$$i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\omega t - \phi) + \sin(\phi)\,e^{-\omega t/\omega\tau}\right]$$

Setting i(β) = 0:

$$\sin(\beta - \phi) + \sin(\phi)\,e^{-\beta/\omega\tau} = 0$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Calculate parameters (2 marks):**

$$V_m = 230\sqrt{2} = 325.27\,\text{V}, \quad \omega = 314.159\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega L = 314.159 \cdot 0.020 = 6.2832\,\Omega, \quad Z = \sqrt{5^2 + 6.2832^2} = \sqrt{25 + 39.48} = 8.011\,\Omega$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(6.2832/5) = \tan^{-1}(1.2566) = 51.49° = 0.8987\,\text{rad}$$

$$\omega\tau = \omega L/R = 6.2832/5 = 1.2566, \quad E/V_m = 50/325.27 = 0.1537$$

**Step 2 — Extinction equation (1 mark):**

$$\sin(\beta - 0.8987) - 0.1537 - (\sin(30° - 51.49°) - 0.1537)e^{-(\beta - 30°)/1.2566} = 0$$

$$\sin(\beta - 0.8987) - 0.1537 - (-0.5746 - 0.1537)e^{-(\beta - 0.5236)/1.2566} = 0$$

$$\sin(\beta - 0.8987) - 0.1537 + 0.7283\,e^{-(\beta - 0.5236)/1.2566} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve by iteration (2 marks):**

- Try β = 3.0 rad (171.9°): $\sin(2.1013) - 0.1537 + 0.7283 e^{-1.9705} = 0.8627 - 0.1537 + 0.7283(0.1394) = 0.7090 + 0.1015 = 0.8105$ (positive)
- Try β = 3.5 rad (200.5°): $\sin(2.6013) - 0.1537 + 0.7283 e^{-2.3692} = 0.5150 - 0.1537 + 0.7283(0.0937) = 0.3613 + 0.0682 = 0.4295$ (positive)
- Try β = 4.0 rad (229.2°): $\sin(3.1013) - 0.1537 + 0.7283 e^{-2.7686} = 0.0489 - 0.1537 + 0.7283(0.0628) = -0.1048 + 0.0457 = -0.0591$ (negative)
- Try β = 3.95 rad: $\sin(3.0513) - 0.1537 + 0.7283 e^{-2.7267} = 0.0807 - 0.1537 + 0.7283(0.0654) = -0.0730 + 0.0476 = -0.0254$
- Try β = 3.90 rad: $\sin(3.0013) - 0.1537 + 0.7283 e^{-2.6853} = 0.1126 - 0.1537 + 0.7283(0.0682) = -0.0411 + 0.0497 = 0.0086$
- Try β = 3.92 rad: between, $\sin(3.0213) - 0.1537 + 0.7283 e^{-2.6999} = 0.1034 - 0.1537 + 0.7283(0.0673) = -0.0503 + 0.0490 = -0.0013$ ✓

**β ≈ 3.92 rad ≈ 224.5°**

**Step 4 — Peak current (2 marks):**

Peak occurs where $di/d(\omega t) = 0$. Differentiating the current equation and setting to zero:

$$\cos(\omega t - \phi) - \frac{1}{\omega\tau}\left(\sin(\alpha - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m}\right)e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/\omega\tau} = 0$$

Iterating from α = 30° = 0.5236 rad upward:
- ωt = 1.2 rad: $\cos(0.3013) - 0.7958 \cdot (-0.7283) \cdot e^{-0.5379} = 0.9550 + 0.5793 \cdot 0.5842 = 0.9550 + 0.3385 = 1.2935 > 0$
- ωt = 1.8 rad: $\cos(0.9013) - 0.7958 \cdot (-0.7283) \cdot e^{-1.0147} = 0.6204 + 0.5793 \cdot 0.3626 = 0.6204 + 0.2101 = 0.8305 > 0$
- ωt = 2.4 rad: $\cos(1.5013) - 0.7958 \cdot (-0.7283) \cdot e^{-1.4915} = 0.0672 + 0.5793 \cdot 0.2250 = 0.0672 + 0.1303 = 0.1975 > 0$
- ωt = 2.6 rad: $\cos(1.7013) - 0.7958 \cdot (-0.7283) \cdot e^{-1.6501} = -0.1274 + 0.5793 \cdot 0.1920 = -0.1274 + 0.1112 = -0.0162 < 0$

So peak is near ωt ≈ 2.55 rad:

$$i_{peak} = \frac{325.27}{8.011}\left[\sin(2.55 - 0.8987) - 0.1537 - (-0.7283)e^{-(2.55 - 0.5236)/1.2566}\right]$$

$$= 40.60 \cdot [\sin(1.6513) - 0.1537 + 0.7283 \cdot e^{-1.6126}]$$

$$= 40.60 \cdot [0.9967 - 0.1537 + 0.7283 \cdot 0.1990] = 40.60 \cdot [0.8430 + 0.1449] = 40.60 \cdot 0.9879$$

$$\boxed{i_{peak} \approx 40.11\,\text{A}}$$

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** For a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier feeding an **RL load with a back-EMF source E**, derive the complete **load current expression** starting from the KVL equation. Clearly state the **two boundary conditions** used. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the **conduction sequence** of a single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with RL load, marking the firing angle α, extinction angle β, and the conduction angle γ. Discuss what happens if a **freewheeling diode (FWD)** is added across the load. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — KVL during conduction (1 mark):**

When the SCR conducts (between α and β):

$$V_m \sin(\omega t) = L\frac{di}{dt} + iR + E$$

**Step 2 — Standard form ODE (1 mark):**

$$\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{R}{L}i = \frac{V_m \sin(\omega t) - E}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Identify forced and natural responses (2 marks):**

**Forced response** (particular integral) — solve the steady-state for the sum of the AC and DC source contributions:
- AC contribution: $i_{f,AC} = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$
- DC contribution (from −E): $i_{f,DC} = -E/R$

So $i_f = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \frac{E}{R}$

**Natural response** (homogeneous solution):
$$i_n = A\,e^{-t/\tau} = A\,e^{-\omega t/\omega\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary condition i(α) = 0 (1 mark):**

$$0 = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi) - \frac{E}{R} + A\,e^{-\alpha/\omega\tau}$$

$$A = \left[\frac{E}{R} - \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi)\right]e^{\alpha/\omega\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Final equation (1 mark):**

$$i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \frac{E}{R} + \left[\frac{E}{R} - \frac{V_m}{Z}\sin(\alpha - \phi)\right]e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/\omega\tau}$$

Factoring out $V_m/Z$ and noting $E/R = (E/V_m)(V_m/(Z\cos\phi))$:

$$\boxed{\,i(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z}\left[\sin(\omega t - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m} - \left(\sin(\alpha - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m}\right)e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/\omega\tau}\right]\,}$$

**Step 6 — Extinction equation (1 mark):** The second boundary condition is i(β) = 0:

$$\sin(\beta - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m} - \left(\sin(\alpha - \phi) - \frac{E}{V_m}\right)e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\omega\tau} = 0$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Conduction sequence description (3 marks):**

| Interval | SCR State | Current | Voltage across SCR |
|:--------:|:---------:|:-------:|:------------------:|
| 0 to α | OFF (forward blocking) | i = 0 | $V_s$ (positive, less than breakover) |
| α to β | ON (conducting) | i(ωt) per derived equation | Small (≈ 1.5 V drop) |
| β to 2π + α | OFF (reverse blocking) | i = 0 | $V_s - E$ (negative during π to 2π) |

- **Firing angle α** = ωt at which gate pulse is applied
- **Extinction angle β** = ωt at which load current naturally falls to zero
- **Conduction angle γ** = β − α

**Freewheeling Diode Discussion (4 marks):**

When an FWD is connected across the RL load (cathode to positive, anode to negative of load):

1. **During α to β:** The SCR carries the load current; the FWD is reverse-biased (since the load voltage tries to keep it reverse-biased).
2. **At ωt = β (or before, depending on when load voltage reverses):** When the load voltage reverses polarity (i.e., $v_o = V_m\sin\omega t - E$ becomes negative), the FWD becomes forward-biased and starts conducting. The load current is **commutated from the SCR to the FWD**.
3. **During freewheeling period (β to 2π + α):** The FWD carries the decaying load current, with the load terminals short-circuited (so $v_o = 0$). The current decays exponentially with time constant L/R.

**Benefits of FWD:**
- **β is forced to be exactly π + α for the SCR** (no more delayed extinction from inductor into negative voltage region).
- **No negative output voltage region** in $v_o$ waveform, improving average output voltage.
- **Reduces reactive power** drawn from AC source (no overlap with negative half).
- **Improves power factor** of the converter.
- **Reduces voltage transients** across the SCR.

**Demerit:**
- The load current flows continuously through the FWD after β, even when the supply is in the negative half-cycle. This means the source delivers current for only part of the cycle — **conduction is discontinuous from the source side** but **continuous from the load side**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES — Read before writing your exam.**

1. **Confusing the integration variable:** Many students write $dt$ when they mean $d(\omega t)$. The correct KVL form on a per-radian basis is $V_m \sin(\omega t) = \omega L \frac{di}{d(\omega t)} + iR$. The factor ω must be retained when converting time to angle. **Lose 1 mark per occurrence.**

2. **Missing the $\omega\tau$ in the exponent:** When you convert $e^{-t/\tau}$ to angular form, you MUST write $e^{-\omega t/\omega\tau}$, not $e^{-t/\tau}$. The dimensionless ratio $\omega t / \omega\tau$ is what is plotted on textbook graphs. **Lose 0.5 mark per occurrence.**

3. **Forgetting the constant-of-integration sign:** When applying i(α) = 0, students often write $A = \sin(\alpha - \phi)e^{\alpha/\omega\tau}$ but **miss the $E/V_m$ term** in the RL+E case. The correct form is $A = \left(\frac{E}{V_m} - \sin(\alpha - \phi)\right)e^{\alpha/\omega\tau}$ (with proper sign handling). **Lose 2 marks if wrong.**

4. **State units in numerical answers:** Writing "i_peak = 40.11" without "A" (Amperes) loses 0.5 mark. Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme deduct strictly for missing units in numerical answers.

5. **Conduction angle formula confusion:** Some students write γ = α for the conduction angle. The correct formula is **γ = β − α**, the difference between extinction and firing angles. **Lose 1 mark.**

6. **Forgetting i = 0 in the OFF region:** The output current is **identically zero** (not $i_o = V_s/Z$) during 0 to α and β to 2π. Drawing a non-zero "decaying tail" in the OFF region costs 1 mark.

7. **Misapplication of freewheeling diode:** If asked, students often forget that the FWD carries current only **after β**, not from α. The FWD is reverse-biased during SCR conduction. **Lose 2 marks for the wrong timing diagram.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ The fundamental governing equation for the **ON state** of any half-wave rectifier is the **KVL**: $V_m\sin(\omega t) = L\frac{di}{dt} + iR + E$. This single equation covers all three cases (R, RL, RL+E) — just set the missing terms to zero.
- ✅ The **integrating factor** is always $e^{t/\tau}$ where $\tau = L/R$. This appears in **every** power electronics transient problem.
- ✅ The general solution structure is always: **i(ωt) = Forced Response + Natural Response = sinusoidal + exponential**.
- ✅ **Two boundary conditions** are required for a first-order ODE: one for the constant of integration and one for the extinction angle.
  - For diode (uncontrolled): $i(0) = 0$ and $i(\beta) = 0$.
  - For SCR (controlled): $i(\alpha) = 0$ and $i(\beta) = 0$.
- ✅ The **extinction equation is transcendental** — it cannot be solved in closed form. Expect numerical or graphical methods in exams.
- ✅ For pure **R load** (no L): $\beta = \pi$ always, $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$, $i_{peak} = V_m\sin\alpha / R$ (note: starts at $\alpha$, not 0).
- ✅ For **RL load** (no E): $\beta$ lies between $\pi$ and $(\pi + \phi)$ approximately, with longer γ for higher ωτ.
- ✅ For **RL + E load**: When E increases, β approaches π + α and γ shortens. When E = $V_m$ (full back-EMF), no current flows.
- ✅ The **freewheeling diode** is the single most common add-on component in KTU exam questions. Remember: FWD **shortens the conduction angle** of the SCR and **eliminates negative voltage** in the output.
- ✅ **Average output voltage** for RL+E: $V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta) - \frac{E(\beta - \alpha)}{2\pi}$.
- ✅ **Power delivered to load** = $I_{rms}^2 R$. The inductor stores but does not dissipate energy; the resistor dissipates everything.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Weightage:** Module 2 (Phase-Controlled Rectifiers) typically carries **20–25%** of the ESE total marks. RL load analysis alone is a frequent 14-mark question.

---

**END OF NOTES — PCBRT503 / Module 2 / Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier with RL and RL+E Loads**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC-DC Converters: Basic Chopper Operation Using Semiconductor Switches
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# DC-DC Converters: Basic Chopper Operation Using Semiconductor Switches

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - Power Electronics & Renewable Energy Conversion
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DC-DC Converters: Basic Chopper Operation Using Semiconductor Switches

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **DC-DC Converter** (also called a **Chopper**) is a static power electronic switching device that converts a fixed magnitude DC input voltage into a variable magnitude DC output voltage. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, a chopper operates by rapidly switching a semiconductor power switch (BJT, Power MOSFET, IGBT, GTO, or force-commutated thyristor) between ON and OFF states, thereby "chopping" the input DC waveform into a pulsed waveform whose average value is controlled by varying the **duty cycle (α)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Lock:** A chopper is essentially a high-speed ON/OFF switch connecting source to load. The output voltage is regulated by controlling the **ON time (T_on)** relative to the **total chopping period (T)**.

Mathematically, the **average output voltage** of an ideal chopper is expressed as:

$$V_{0(avg)} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt = \frac{T_{on}}{T} \cdot V_s = \alpha \cdot V_s$$

where the **duty ratio (or duty cycle)** is:

$$\alpha = \frac{T_{on}}{T} = T_{on} \cdot f$$

with **f** being the chopping frequency in **Hz** (typically 1 kHz to 100 kHz for modern MOSFET/IGBT choppers).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a water tap connected to a water tank. The water tank has a constant water level (constant DC source), but you want to fill a bucket at a controlled, variable rate.

| Analogy Element | Real Chopper Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Water tank (constant level) | DC Source $V_s$ (battery) |
| Tap being turned ON/OFF rapidly | Semiconductor switch (MOSFET/IGBT) |
| ON duration of tap | $T_{on}$ (chopper switch ON) |
| OFF duration of tap | $T_{off}$ (chopper switch OFF) |
| Average water flow to bucket | Average output voltage $V_0$ |
| Frequency of tap toggling | Chopping frequency $f$ |

If you keep the tap open for **half** the time, the bucket receives roughly **half** the maximum flow. This intuitive picture is exactly how a **step-down (Buck) chopper** works.

---

## 1.3 Why Are Choppers Used? (KTU 2024 Industrial Relevance)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Choppers form the backbone of:
> - **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** — Traction motor speed control
> - **Solar MPPT Charge Controllers** — Boost/buck conversion from PV panels
> - **SMPS** (Switched Mode Power Supplies) for laptops, mobile chargers
> - **DC Motor Drives** in robotics and CNC machines
> - **Battery-operated systems** (drones, forklifts, e-bikes)

Key performance metrics highlighted in the KTU syllabus:
- **Efficiency (η)**: Modern choppers achieve **> 95%** efficiency.
- **Power density**: 1–10 kW/kg (vastly superior to linear regulators).
- **Switching frequency**: 20 kHz – 500 kHz (above audible range for SMPS).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Choppers (KTU 2024 Module Mapping)

The KTU syllabus classifies choppers into two main families based on **direction of power flow** and **direction of current**:

### 1.4.1 Based on Quadrant Operation (Voltage-Current Plane)

| Class | Q-I | Q-II | Q-III | Q-IV | Power Flow | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Source → Load | Buck (Step-down) |
| **Class B** | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Load → Source (regen) | Boost braking |
| **Class C** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Both (forward motoring + regen) | DC drives |
| **Class D** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | Both (forward + reverse motoring) | Reversible drives |
| **Class E** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | All four quadrants | High-performance servo drives |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick for KTU exams:** Class A = 1 quadrant, B = 1 quadrant, C = 2 quadrants, D = 2 quadrants, E = 4 quadrants.

### 1.4.2 Based on Conversion Function

- **Step-Down (Buck) Chopper** — Output voltage < Input voltage
- **Step-Up (Boost) Chopper** — Output voltage > Input voltage
- **Buck-Boost Chopper** — Output can be above or below input
- **Cuk Chopper** — Inverted output, smooth input/output current
- **Full-Bridge (H-Bridge) Chopper** — Used in bidirectional DC motor control

---

## 1.5 Basic Chopper Operation Using Semiconductor Switches

The **generic chopper circuit** consists of four essential building blocks:

```
   V_s ---+------[ SWITCH S ]------+-----> To Load
          |                        |
          |                       [LOAD]
          |                        |
          +----[ FREEWHEEL DIODE D ]+
```

**Component Roles:**

1. **DC Source (V_s):** Provides unregulated DC input (battery, rectifier output, PV panel).
2. **Semiconductor Switch (S):** Controls power flow. Modern choices:
   - **BJT** (Bipolar Junction Transistor) — Used in low-frequency, high-current choppers (≤ 10 kHz).
   - **Power MOSFET** — Dominant in SMPS and low-voltage applications (100 kHz – 1 MHz).
   - **IGBT** (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor) — High voltage + high current (rail traction, EV drives).
   - **GTO / IGCT** — Very high power (> 1 MW) industrial drives.
3. **Freewheeling Diode (D_F):** Provides a path for **inductive load current** when the switch opens, preventing destructive voltage spikes.
4. **Load (R, R-L, or DC Motor):** The application being driven.

### 1.5.1 Working Principle (Step-Down Chopper)

**Mode 1 — Switch S is ON (duration T_on):**
- Switch S closes, connecting source $V_s$ directly to load.
- Load voltage $v_0 = V_s$.
- Load current rises (exponentially for R-L load).
- Diode D_F is reverse-biased and OFF.

**Mode 2 — Switch S is OFF (duration T_off):**
- Switch S opens, disconnecting source from load.
- For an **inductive load**, the stored magnetic energy forces current to continue.
- This current freewheels through diode D_F.
- Load voltage $v_0 \approx 0$ (only diode forward drop).
- Load current decays.

**Mode 3 (Steady-State):** After several cycles, the system reaches **periodic steady-state** where the energy stored in the inductor at the end of one cycle equals that at the beginning of the next.

---

## 1.6 Control Strategies for Choppers (KTU 2024 High-Weightage)

### 1.6.1 Time Ratio Control (TRC)

The **switching frequency is kept constant**, and the duty cycle α is varied.

- **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM):** $T_{on}$ varied, $T$ constant.
- **Frequency Modulation Control:** $T_{on}$ constant, frequency varied.

### 1.6.2 Current Limit Control (CLC)

- Switch turns ON when load current falls to a lower reference.
- Switch turns OFF when current rises to upper reference.
- Variable frequency, variable duty cycle.
- Used in **DC traction drives**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked:** "Compare TRC and CLC."
> **Answer:** TRC gives smoother output and predictable harmonics; CLC inherently limits current but produces variable frequency spectrum, making filter design harder.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Operation of the Step-Down (Buck) Chopper with R-L Load

Consider a buck chopper feeding a **series R-L load** (representative of a DC motor armature or a heavily inductive load).

### Circuit Topology
- Source $V_s$, controllable switch S (MOSFET/IGBT/BJT), freewheeling diode $D_F$, load $R-L$ in series.

### Mode-by-Mode Analysis (Continuous Conduction Mode)

**Mode 1: Switch ON (0 ≤ t ≤ T_on)**
- Diode $D_F$ is reverse-biased (OFF).
- The loop equation is:
$$V_s = v_0(t) = R \cdot i_L(t) + L \frac{di_L(t)}{dt}$$
- Solving the first-order ODE with initial current $I_{min}$:
$$i_L(t) = I_{min} \cdot e^{-t/\tau} + \frac{V_s}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$
- At the end of Mode 1 (t = T_on), the current reaches peak value $I_{max}$.

**Mode 2: Switch OFF (T_on ≤ t ≤ T)**
- The source is disconnected; load current freewheels through $D_F$.
- The loop equation becomes (assuming $D_F$ ideal):
$$0 = R \cdot i_L(t) + L \frac{di_L(t)}{dt}$$
- Current decays from $I_{max}$ back to $I_{min}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** In steady-state, **volt-second balance** must hold for the inductor (fundamental principle enabling duty cycle equations):
> $$\int_0^{T} v_L(t)\, dt = 0$$
> This is the cornerstone of every switched-mode power supply analysis in KTU exams.

---

## 2.2 Step-Up (Boost) Chopper — Detailed Analysis

In a **boost chopper**, the output voltage is always **greater than or equal to** the input source voltage.

### Circuit Topology
- Source $V_s$ in series with inductor L, switch S in shunt (to ground), diode D in series with output capacitor C and load R.

### Mode 1: Switch ON (0 ≤ t ≤ T_on)
- Diode D is reverse-biased.
- Inductor charges from source: $V_s = L \frac{di_L}{dt}$.
- Current rises linearly: $i_L(t) = I_{min} + \frac{V_s}{L} t$.

### Mode 2: Switch OFF (T_on ≤ t ≤ T)
- Diode D is forward-biased.
- Inductor current flows through D into the output capacitor and load.
- Energy stored in L during Mode 1 is delivered to load.

**Applying volt-second balance on the inductor:**

$$V_s \cdot T_{on} + (V_s - V_0) \cdot T_{off} = 0$$

Solving for $V_0$:

$$V_0 = \frac{V_s}{1 - \alpha}$$

This is the **famous boost chopper equation** appearing in virtually every KTU end-semester exam.

---

## 2.3 Buck-Boost Chopper

The buck-boost chopper provides an output that is **inverted** with respect to the input, with magnitude:

$$V_0 = \frac{\alpha \cdot V_s}{1 - \alpha}$$

- If α < 0.5 → $|V_0| < V_s$ (buck mode)
- If α > 0.5 → $|V_0| > V_s$ (boost mode)
- At α = 0.5 → $|V_0| = V_s$

---

## 2.4 Cuk Chopper (KTU Module-2 Important Topic)

The Cuk converter provides:
- **Inverted output voltage** (like buck-boost)
- **Non-pulsating input and output currents** (smooth waveforms)
- Better for high-power applications where input ripple is critical.

**Output voltage equation:**

$$V_0 = -\frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} \cdot V_s$$

The negative sign indicates **polarity inversion**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Comprehensive)

| # | Parameter | Step-Down (Buck) | Step-Up (Boost) | Buck-Boost | Cuk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Output Voltage $V_0$ | $\alpha V_s$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-\alpha}$ | $\dfrac{\alpha V_s}{1-\alpha}$ | $-\dfrac{\alpha V_s}{1-\alpha}$ |
| 2 | RMS Output Voltage $V_{0,rms}$ | $\sqrt{\alpha}\ V_s$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-\alpha}\sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}+\alpha}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-\alpha}\sqrt{\alpha}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-\alpha}\sqrt{\alpha}$ |
| 3 | Duty Cycle $\alpha$ | $\dfrac{V_0}{V_s}$ | $1 - \dfrac{V_s}{V_0}$ | $\dfrac{V_0}{V_0 + V_s}$ | $\dfrac{\vert V_0\vert}{V_s + \vert V_0\vert}$ |
| 4 | Critical Inductance $L_{c}$ | $\dfrac{R(1-\alpha)}{2f}$ | $\dfrac{R \alpha (1-\alpha)^2}{2f}$ | $\dfrac{R(1-\alpha)^2}{2f}$ | $\dfrac{(1-\alpha)^2 R}{2f\alpha}$ |
| 5 | Average Load Current $I_0$ | $\dfrac{\alpha V_s}{R}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{R(1-\alpha)}$ | — | — |
| 6 | Continuous Conduction | $\alpha L > 0$ (always for ideal) | $L \geq L_c$ | $L \geq L_c$ | $L_1, L_2 \geq L_{c1}, L_{c2}$ |
| 7 | Switch Peak Voltage | $V_s$ | $V_0$ | $V_0 + V_s$ | $V_0 + V_s$ |
| 8 | Switch Peak Current | $I_{max}$ | $I_{L,max}$ | $I_{L,max}$ | $I_{L1,max}+I_{L2,max}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Lock:** The $\dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$ pattern appears in **buck-boost, cuk, and SEPIC** converters. Memorize this and the rest follows logically.

### 2.5.1 Auxiliary Formulas (All Converters)

**Switching frequency:**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$

**Duty cycle in terms of time:**

$$\alpha = \frac{T_{on}}{T} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$

**Effective (RMS) input current — Buck:**

$$I_{s,rms} = \sqrt{\alpha} \cdot I_0$$

**Average switch current — Buck:**

$$I_{S,avg} = \alpha \cdot I_0$$

**Freewheeling diode average current — Buck:**

$$I_{D,avg} = (1-\alpha) \cdot I_0$$

**Continuous Conduction Condition (General):**

$$L > L_c \quad \text{where} \quad L_c = \frac{R(1-\alpha)}{2f}\ \text{(buck)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In a 48V EV battery system, a buck chopper with α = 0.5 steps it down to 24V for auxiliary loads. A boost chopper with α = 0.75 boosts 200V solar panel input to 800V DC link for grid-tied inverter.

---

## 2.6 Continuous vs. Discontinuous Conduction — Boundary Definition

The boundary between **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** and **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)** is defined by the **critical load current**:

$$I_{0,c} = \frac{V_0 (1-\alpha)}{2fL} \quad \text{(buck chopper)}$$

- If $I_0 > I_{0,c}$ → **CCM** (inductor current never reaches zero)
- If $I_0 < I_{0,c}$ → **DCM** (current falls to zero before next cycle)
- If $I_0 = I_{0,c}$ → **Boundary mode**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When problem says "R-L load with very low R" → almost always **DCM**. When R is moderate to high → **CCM**. Default assumption unless otherwise stated: **CCM**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Output Voltage of Step-Down Chopper

We derive the **average output voltage** for an ideal buck chopper from first principles using the volt-second balance principle.

### Step 1: Define the Output Voltage Waveform

The output voltage $v_0(t)$ across the load alternates between $V_s$ (when switch is ON) and $0$ (when switch is OFF, with ideal freewheel diode).

$$v_0(t) = \begin{cases} V_s, & 0 \leq t < T_{on} \\ 0, & T_{on} \leq t < T \end{cases}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Definition of Average Value

By definition, the **average value over one period T** is:

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v_0(t)\, dt$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Piecewise Function

Since $v_0(t)$ is non-zero only during $T_{on}$:

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{T}\left[\int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s\, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0\, dt\right]$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Integrals

The first integral is $V_s \cdot T_{on}$, and the second is zero:

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{T}\left[V_s \cdot T_{on}\right] = V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

### Step 5: Substitute Duty Cycle Definition

Using $\alpha = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T}$:

$$\boxed{V_0 = \alpha \cdot V_s}$$

This is the **fundamental KTU 2024 equation** appearing in nearly every chopper problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Output Voltage of Step-Up (Boost) Chopper

### Step 1: Identify Inductor Voltage in Each Mode

**Mode 1 (Switch ON, 0 ≤ t ≤ T_on):**
- Diode reverse-biased, output isolated.
- The full source voltage appears across the inductor.
- $v_L(t) = V_s$

**Mode 2 (Switch OFF, T_on ≤ t ≤ T):**
- Inductor current flows through diode into output.
- Inductor sees: $v_L(t) = V_s - V_0$

### Step 2: Apply Volt-Second Balance (Steady-State)

In periodic steady state, the average voltage across L must be zero (otherwise current would ramp to infinity):

$$\int_0^T v_L(t)\, dt = 0$$

$$V_s \cdot T_{on} + (V_s - V_0) \cdot T_{off} = 0$$

### Step 3: Substitute $T_{on} = \alpha T$ and $T_{off} = (1-\alpha)T$

$$V_s \cdot \alpha T + (V_s - V_0) \cdot (1-\alpha)T = 0$$

Dividing by $T$:

$$V_s \alpha + (V_s - V_0)(1 - \alpha) = 0$$

### Step 4: Expand and Simplify

$$V_s \alpha + V_s (1 - \alpha) - V_0(1 - \alpha) = 0$$

$$V_s [\alpha + (1 - \alpha)] = V_0 (1 - \alpha)$$

$$V_s = V_0(1 - \alpha)$$

### Step 5: Solve for $V_0$

$$\boxed{V_0 = \frac{V_s}{1-\alpha}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Inductance for Continuous Conduction (Buck)

### Step 1: Determine Load Current Waveform

The current ripple $\Delta I_L$ in a buck chopper with R-L load is determined by the ON-state inductor voltage:

$$\Delta I_L = I_{max} - I_{min} = \frac{V_s - V_0}{L} \cdot T_{on} = \frac{V_s(1-\alpha)}{L} \cdot \alpha T = \frac{V_s \alpha(1-\alpha)}{fL}$$

### Step 2: Find Average and Boundary Current

In CCM, the average current is:

$$I_0 = \frac{I_{max} + I_{min}}{2}$$

The **boundary condition** for CCM is $I_{min} = 0$:

$$I_{0,c} = \frac{I_{max}}{2} = \frac{\Delta I_L}{2} = \frac{V_s \alpha(1-\alpha)}{2fL}$$

### Step 3: Substitute $V_0 = \alpha V_s$ to Get $V_s$ in Terms of $V_0$

Since $V_s = \dfrac{V_0}{\alpha}$:

$$I_{0,c} = \frac{\frac{V_0}{\alpha} \cdot \alpha(1-\alpha)}{2fL} = \frac{V_0(1-\alpha)}{2fL}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $V_0 = R I_{0,c}$ to Find $L_c$

Since $R = \dfrac{V_0}{I_{0,c}}$, we have $I_{0,c} = \dfrac{V_0}{R}$:

$$\frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{V_0(1-\alpha)}{2fL_c}$$

$$\boxed{L_c = \frac{R(1-\alpha)}{2f}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This critical inductance is what **KTU examiners love to ask** in Part B questions: "For a given chopper feeding an R-L load, find the minimum inductance for continuous conduction."

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Buck-Boost Chopper Output Voltage

### Step 1: Mode Analysis

**Mode 1 (Switch ON, 0 ≤ t ≤ T_on):**
- Inductor charges from source: $v_L = V_s$.
- Load is isolated by reverse-biased diode.

**Mode 2 (Switch OFF, T_on ≤ t ≤ T):**
- Inductor current freewheels through diode into output (with inverted polarity).
- Inductor sees: $v_L = -V_0$ (note: output is inverted).

### Step 2: Apply Volt-Second Balance

$$V_s \cdot T_{on} + (-V_0) \cdot T_{off} = 0$$

$$V_s \cdot \alpha T = V_0 \cdot (1-\alpha)T$$

### Step 3: Solve

$$\boxed{V_0 = \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} \cdot V_s}$$

The negative polarity (inversion) is implicit in the circuit topology — KTU often writes this as $|V_0|$ with the polarity specified separately.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Switch and Diode Stresses

For **all four basic chopper types**, here is the consolidated stress analysis table frequently asked in KTU exams:

| Converter | Switch Voltage Stress | Switch Current Stress | Diode Voltage Stress | Diode Current Stress |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Buck** | $V_s$ | $I_{max}$ | $V_s$ | $I_{max}$ |
| **Boost** | $V_0 = \dfrac{V_s}{1-\alpha}$ | $I_{L,max}$ | $V_0$ | $I_0$ |
| **Buck-Boost** | $V_s + V_0$ | $I_{L,max}$ | $V_s + V_0$ | $I_{L,max}$ |
| **Cuk** | $V_s + \vert V_0\vert$ | $I_{L1}+I_{L2}$ | $V_s + \vert V_0\vert$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget that in a **buck-boost or Cuk chopper**, the switch must block **the sum** of source and output voltages — not just one. This leads to under-specification of the device rating in design problems.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A step-down chopper has $V_s = 200\text{ V}$, $R = 10\ \Omega$, $L = 5\text{ mH}$, chopping frequency $f = 1\text{ kHz}$, and duty cycle $\alpha = 0.4$. Find:
(a) Average output voltage
(b) Average output current
(c) Whether the converter operates in CCM or DCM
(d) Critical inductance

### Part (a): Average Output Voltage

$$V_0 = \alpha \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 200 = 80\ \text{V}$$

### Part (b): Average Output Current

$$I_0 = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{80}{10} = 8\ \text{A}$$

### Part (c): CCM or DCM?

First, find critical inductance:

$$L_c = \frac{R(1-\alpha)}{2f} = \frac{10 \times (1 - 0.4)}{2 \times 1000} = \frac{10 \times 0.6}{2000} = \frac{6}{2000} = 3 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{H} = 3\ \text{mH}$$

Since $L = 5\text{ mH} > L_c = 3\text{ mH}$, the converter operates in **CCM**.

### Part (d): Critical Inductance

Already computed: $\boxed{L_c = 3\ \text{mH}}$

This is a **classic KTU numerical** — examiners expect you to compute all four parts sequentially and comment on conduction mode.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic Chopper System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Source V_s] -->|Unregulated DC| B[Input Filter L_in C_in]
    B --> C[Semiconductor Switch S]
    C -->|Pulsed DC| D[Output Filter L C]
    D --> E[DC Load R or R-L]
    E -->|Sensed Voltage/Current| F[Control Circuit PWM]
    F -->|Gate Drive Signal| C
    G[Reference V_ref] --> F
    H[Feedback H_s] --> F
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style D fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style E fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,color:#000
    style F fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** The DC source is filtered, chopped by S, re-filtered, and delivered to the load. Feedback is taken from the output, compared with a reference, and used to generate the PWM gate signal for S.

---

## 4.2 Step-Down (Buck) Chopper — Circuit Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    Vsrc[DC Source V_s] --- Sw1[Switch S - MOSFET or IGBT]
    Sw1 --- Nd1[Node A]
    Nd1 --- Lout[Inductor L]
    Lout --- Nd2[Node B]
    Nd2 --- Rout[Resistor R]
    Rout --- Gnd1[Ground]
    Nd1 --- Dfw[Freewheel Diode D_F]
    Dfw --- Gnd2[Ground]
    Nd2 --- Cload[Filter Capacitor C]
    Cload --- Gnd3[Ground]
    style Vsrc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99,color:#000
    style Sw1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style Dfw fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000
    style Lout fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style Cload fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066,color:#000
    style Rout fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333333,color:#000
```

> **Component roles:** V_s = source; S = chopping switch; D_F = freewheel diode; L = filter inductor; C = filter capacitor; R = load.

---

## 4.3 Step-Up (Boost) Chopper — Circuit Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    Vsrc[DC Source V_s] --- Lind[Inductor L]
    Lind --- Nd1[Node X]
    Nd1 --- Sw1[Switch S]
    Sw1 --- Gnd1[Ground]
    Nd1 --- Dfwd[Diode D]
    Dfwd --- Nd2[Node Y]
    Nd2 --- Cload[Capacitor C]
    Cload --- Gnd2[Ground]
    Nd2 --- Rout[Load R]
    Rout --- Gnd3[Ground]
    style Vsrc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99,color:#000
    style Lind fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style Sw1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style Dfwd fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000
    style Cload fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066,color:#000
    style Rout fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333333,color:#000
```

> **Key insight:** Switch S is in **shunt** (parallel with output), unlike buck where it is in **series**. This topology difference is what produces voltage step-up.

---

## 4.4 Operating Waveform — Step-Down Chopper (CCM, R-L Load)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TIME AXIS] --> B[0 to T_on]
    A --> C[T_on to T]
    B --> D[Switch S is ON]
    D --> E[v_0 equals V_s]
    D --> F[i_L rises from I_min to I_max]
    C --> G[Switch S is OFF]
    G --> H[v_0 equals 0]
    G --> I[i_L decays from I_max to I_min]
    G --> J[D_F conducts freewheel current]
    style A fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffe1cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style E fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000
    style F fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style G fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099,color:#000
    style H fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000
    style I fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style J fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066,color:#000
```

> **Interpretation:** A complete chopping period T is split into T_on (switch ON, source feeds load) and T_off (switch OFF, inductor freewheels through diode D_F). The output voltage waveform is a rectangular pulse train with amplitude V_s and duty α.

---

## 4.5 Quadrant Operation Diagram — Chopper Classification

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Chopper Quadrant Operation Map
    x-axis Reverse Current --> Forward Current
    y-axis Reverse Voltage --> Forward Voltage
    quadrant-1 Class A and C forward motoring
    quadrant-2 Class B and E regeneration
    quadrant-3 Class D and E reverse motoring
    quadrant-4 Class C and E reverse regeneration
    Class A Chopper: [0.85, 0.75]
    Class B Chopper: [0.25, 0.75]
    Class C Chopper: [0.85, 0.30]
    Class D Chopper: [0.50, 0.20]
    Class E Chopper: [0.65, 0.55]
```

> **How to read:** X-axis is current direction; Y-axis is voltage polarity. Each class of chopper operates in a specific combination of quadrants, defining its power flow capability.

---

## 4.6 Buck-Boost Chopper — Circuit Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    Vsrc[DC Source V_s] --- Sw1[Switch S]
    Sw1 --- Gnd1[Ground]
    Vsrc --- Lind[Inductor L]
    Lind --- Nd1[Node P]
    Nd1 --- Dfwd[Diode D]
    Dfwd --- Nd2[Node Q - Negative Output]
    Nd2 --- Cload[Capacitor C]
    Cload --- Gnd2[Ground]
    Nd2 --- Rout[Load R]
    Rout --- Gnd3[Ground]
    style Vsrc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99,color:#000
    style Lind fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style Sw1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style Dfwd fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000
    style Cload fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066,color:#000
    style Rout fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333333,color:#000
```

> **Note:** Output is taken across the capacitor in **inverted polarity** — negative terminal at the top relative to input ground. This polarity inversion is a defining feature of buck-boost and Cuk converters.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Chopper and State its Two Main Control Strategies
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A chopper is a static power electronic device that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage by rapidly switching a semiconductor device (BJT, MOSFET, IGBT) ON and OFF.

The two main control strategies are:

1. **Time Ratio Control (TRC):** The switching frequency is held constant, and the duty cycle α is varied. This includes **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** where $T_{on}$ is varied at constant period T.
2. **Current Limit Control (CLC):** The switch is turned ON when the load current falls below a lower reference and turned OFF when it rises above an upper reference. This produces variable frequency and variable duty cycle.

> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for TRC explanation, 1 mark for CLC explanation.

---

### Question 2: What is the Role of the Freewheeling Diode in a Chopper Circuit?
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The freewheeling diode (D_F) is connected across the load (with cathode at the positive output terminal) and serves three critical functions:

1. **Provides a path for inductive load current** when the chopper switch turns OFF, preventing sudden current interruption and the resulting high induced voltage ($v = L \frac{di}{dt}$) that would damage the switching device.
2. **Dissipates the energy stored in the load inductance** during the OFF period, transferring it to the load as useful work or returning it to the source.
3. **Maintains continuity of load current** during switch OFF time, leading to smoother output waveforms and reduced current ripple.

> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for path provision, 1 mark for energy dissipation, 1 mark for continuity/smoothness.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question Choice A: Full Buck Chopper Analysis
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module 2]`**

**Q. (a) [7 marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a step-down chopper feeding an R-L load. Draw the associated voltage and current waveforms for continuous conduction mode. Derive the expression for average output voltage.

**Q. (b) [7 marks]** A step-down chopper operates from a 220 V DC source and supplies an R-L load with R = 5 Ω and L = 10 mH. The chopping frequency is 2 kHz and the duty cycle is 0.6. Calculate:
(i) Average output voltage
(ii) Average load current
(iii) RMS output voltage
(iv) Critical inductance and state whether the converter is in CCM or DCM.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (a):

**Step 1: Circuit Description (1 mark)**

A step-down (buck) chopper consists of a DC source $V_s$, a controllable switch S (MOSFET/IGBT), a freewheeling diode D_F connected in reverse across the load, and the R-L load in series.

**Step 2: Mode 1 — Switch ON (2 marks)**

When S is closed (time $T_{on}$):
- Source $V_s$ is connected directly across the load.
- Output voltage $v_0 = V_s$.
- Current $i_L$ rises exponentially from $I_{min}$ to $I_{max}$.
- Diode D_F is reverse-biased (OFF).

The equation governing the current is:
$$V_s = R i_L(t) + L \frac{di_L(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Mode 2 — Switch OFF (2 marks)**

When S is open (time $T_{off} = T - T_{on}$):
- Source is disconnected; load current freewheels through D_F.
- Output voltage $v_0 \approx 0$ (ideal diode).
- Current $i_L$ decays from $I_{max}$ to $I_{min}$.

The equation is:
$$0 = R i_L(t) + L \frac{di_L(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4: Derivation of Average Output Voltage (2 marks)**

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^{T_{on}} V_s\, dt = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{T} = \alpha V_s$$

(Include waveform sketch showing rectangular $v_0$ and triangular $i_L$.)

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (b):

**Given:** $V_s = 220$ V, R = 5 Ω, L = 10 mH, f = 2 kHz, α = 0.6

**(i) Average Output Voltage (1.5 marks)**

$$V_0 = \alpha \cdot V_s = 0.6 \times 220 = 132\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Average Load Current (1.5 marks)**

$$I_0 = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{132}{5} = 26.4\ \text{A}$$

**(iii) RMS Output Voltage (2 marks)**

$$V_{0,rms} = \sqrt{\alpha} \cdot V_s = \sqrt{0.6} \times 220 = 0.7746 \times 220 \approx 170.4\ \text{V}$$

**(iv) Critical Inductance and Conduction Mode (2 marks)**

$$L_c = \frac{R(1-\alpha)}{2f} = \frac{5 \times (1 - 0.6)}{2 \times 2000} = \frac{5 \times 0.4}{4000} = \frac{2}{4000} = 0.5\ \text{mH}$$

Since $L = 10\text{ mH} > L_c = 0.5\text{ mH}$, the converter operates in **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)**.

---

### Question Choice B: Boost Chopper Design and Operation
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Module 2]`**

**Q. (a) [7 marks]** Explain the working principle of a step-up (boost) chopper with a circuit diagram and waveforms. Derive the expression for average output voltage in terms of source voltage and duty cycle.

**Q. (b) [7 marks]** A boost chopper is fed from a 48 V DC source and delivers 96 V across a 20 Ω load. The switching frequency is 10 kHz. Calculate:
(i) Required duty cycle
(ii) Average load current
(iii) Average source current
(iv) Critical value of inductance for continuous conduction

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (a):

**Step 1: Circuit Description (1 mark)**

A boost chopper has the switch S in shunt (parallel to output) and a series inductor L at the input. The diode D connects the inductor to the output capacitor C and load R.

**Step 2: Mode 1 — Switch ON (2 marks)**

When S is closed (time $T_{on}$):
- Diode D is reverse-biased; output is isolated.
- Source charges the inductor: $V_s = L \frac{di_L}{dt}$.
- Current rises linearly from $I_{min}$ to $I_{max}$.
- Load is supplied by capacitor C.

**Step 3: Mode 2 — Switch OFF (2 marks)**

When S is open (time $T_{off}$):
- Diode D becomes forward-biased.
- Inductor releases stored energy to load and capacitor.
- Inductor voltage: $v_L = V_s - V_0$.

**Step 4: Derivation (2 marks)**

Applying volt-second balance on inductor:
$$V_s \cdot T_{on} + (V_s - V_0) \cdot T_{off} = 0$$
$$V_s \alpha + (V_s - V_0)(1-\alpha) = 0$$
$$V_s = V_0(1-\alpha)$$
$$\boxed{V_0 = \frac{V_s}{1-\alpha}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q. (b):

**Given:** $V_s = 48$ V, $V_0 = 96$ V, R = 20 Ω, f = 10 kHz

**(i) Required Duty Cycle (1.5 marks)**

From $V_0 = \dfrac{V_s}{1-\alpha}$:

$$96 = \frac{48}{1 - \alpha} \Rightarrow 1 - \alpha = \frac{48}{96} = 0.5 \Rightarrow \alpha = 0.5$$

**(ii) Average Load Current (1.5 marks)**

$$I_0 = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{96}{20} = 4.8\ \text{A}$$

**(iii) Average Source Current (2 marks)**

By power balance (ideal chopper): $V_s I_s = V_0 I_0$:

$$I_s = \frac{V_0 I_0}{V_s} = \frac{96 \times 4.8}{48} = \frac{460.8}{48} = 9.6\ \text{A}$$

**(iv) Critical Inductance (2 marks)**

For boost chopper:
$$L_c = \frac{R \alpha (1-\alpha)^2}{2f} = \frac{20 \times 0.5 \times (0.5)^2}{2 \times 10000} = \frac{20 \times 0.5 \times 0.25}{20000} = \frac{2.5}{20000} = 0.125\ \text{mH} = 125\ \mu\text{H}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign error in boost chopper derivation:** Students often write $(V_0 - V_s) \cdot T_{off}$ instead of $(V_s - V_0) \cdot T_{off}$. The inductor voltage during OFF state is $V_s - V_0$, NOT $V_0 - V_s$. This single sign error leads to **wrong duty cycle** and costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting critical inductance check:** Many students compute $V_0$ and $I_0$ correctly but skip the CCM/DCM verification. Examiners **specifically deduct 1 mark** for failing to state the conduction mode.
> 3. **Confusing RMS and average values:** In numerical problems, students sometimes use $\alpha V_s$ for RMS. The correct RMS for buck is $\sqrt{\alpha} V_s$. Mixing these up loses 1.5 marks.
> 4. **Misidentifying switch and diode positions:** A common error is drawing the buck chopper with the switch in shunt and diode in series — this is actually a **boost** topology. Always verify the placement before starting the derivation.
> 5. **Forgetting polarity in buck-boost:** The buck-boost and Cuk outputs are **inverted** with respect to input. KTU expects you to explicitly mention this with a negative sign or a clearly labeled diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 — Choppers**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- ✅ Chopper = DC-DC converter using semiconductor switch
- ✅ Duty cycle: $\alpha = T_{on}/T$, where $T = 1/f$
- ✅ **Buck:** $V_0 = \alpha V_s$ (output < input)
- ✅ **Boost:** $V_0 = V_s/(1-\alpha)$ (output > input, always)
- ✅ **Buck-Boost:** $V_0 = \alpha V_s/(1-\alpha)$ (inverted polarity)
- ✅ **Cuk:** $V_0 = -\alpha V_s/(1-\alpha)$ (inverted, low ripple)
- ✅ Freewheel diode prevents $L \cdot di/dt$ voltage spikes

### Key Derivations
- ✅ Average output voltage by direct integration
- ✅ Boost equation by volt-second balance
- ✅ Critical inductance $L_c = R(1-\alpha)/(2f)$ for buck
- ✅ RMS output voltage $V_{0,rms} = \sqrt{\alpha} V_s$ for buck

### Quadrant Operation (1-Mark Frequently Asked)
- ✅ Class A: Q1 only (forward motoring)
- ✅ Class B: Q2 only (regenerative braking, reverse current)
- ✅ Class C: Q1 + Q2 (forward motoring + regen)
- ✅ Class D: Q1 + Q4 (forward + reverse motoring)
- ✅ Class E: all four quadrants

### Numerical Problem Steps (Always Follow)
1. Identify converter type from circuit
2. Write given data
3. Compute $V_0$ using appropriate formula
4. Compute $I_0 = V_0/R$
5. Compute $L_c$ for the specific converter
6. Compare $L$ with $L_c$ → declare CCM or DCM

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing RMS and average voltage
- ❌ Sign error in boost volt-second balance
- ❌ Forgetting polarity inversion in buck-boost/Cuk
- ❌ Skipping the CCM/DCM check
- ❌ Mixing up switch voltage and current stresses
- ❌ Drawing the wrong switch/diode placement

### Real-World Applications (Examiner Loves These)
- 🚗 EV traction motor drives (IGBT chopper)
- ☀️ Solar MPPT boost converters
- 💻 SMPS in laptop/mobile chargers
- 🚆 DC traction in Indian Railways (Class C choppers)
- 🏭 DC motor soft starters in CNC machines

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
