<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 : Buck Converter (Step-Down DC-DC Chopper)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Buck Converter**, also known as a **Step-Down Chopper** or **Class-A Chopper**, is a DC-to-DC power electronic switching converter that produces an average output voltage **lower than** the input DC source voltage. It employs forced commutation through a controlled semiconductor switch (historically a BJT, but modernly a MOSFET, IGBT, or GaN device) and an uncontrolled switch (typically a Power Diode) to periodically chop the input DC waveform and reconstruct a lower average DC level using a low-pass LC filter.

The fundamental control variable is the **duty cycle** (often denoted as **k** in older KTU textbooks and **D** in modern notations), defined as the ratio of switch ON-time to the total switching period:

$$D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} = t_{ON} \cdot f_s$$

where $T_{ON}$ is the ON duration, $T$ is the switching period, and $f_s$ is the switching frequency (typically **20 kHz to 500 kHz** in commercial SMPS designs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** *"A step-down chopper is a power electronic circuit that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage, where the output voltage is always less than or equal to the input voltage, by varying the duty ratio of the chopper."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water Tap with Bucket" Analogy:**
> Imagine a continuous high-pressure water source (the **DC input $V_s$**) connected to a pipe with a fast on/off valve (the **chopper switch**). Downstream of the valve is an accumulator tank with a floating ball-cock (the **LC filter**) that averages out the splashes into a steady trickle. By controlling how long the valve stays open (the **duty cycle**), you control the *average* flow into the bucket, but the bucket's outlet can only ever deliver water at a pressure *lower* than the inlet — never higher. You can also think of it like a **regulator tap on a high-pressure cylinder**: no matter how hard you turn it, the outlet pressure can never exceed the cylinder pressure.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Design Metrics & Standard Symbols

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| $V_s$ | Source / Input DC Voltage | 12 V – 400 V |
| $V_0$ | Average Output Voltage | 0 V to $V_s$ |
| $I_0$ | Average Load Current | mA – hundreds of A |
| $f_s$ | Switching Frequency | **20 kHz – 500 kHz** (audio noise avoidance > 20 kHz) |
| $D$ | Duty Cycle | $0 < D < 1$ (ideally) |
| $L_c$ | Critical Inductance (Boundary) | µH – mH range |
| $L$ | Filter Inductance | > $L_c$ for CCM |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 Module 2, the Buck converter forms the *foundation* for analyzing other topologies (Boost, Buck-Boost, Cuk, SEPIC). Mastery of Buck analysis guarantees ~7–8 marks guaranteed in any ESE/University exam on DC-DC converters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology (Canonical Form)

The Buck converter consists of **four essential power elements** plus a load:

1. **Switch (S):** Controlled device — MOSFET/IGBT/BJT. Located between source positive and the inductor node.
2. **Freewheeling Diode (D_f):** Uncontrolled device — Power Diode. Cathode at the switch node, anode at ground.
3. **Filter Inductor (L):** Placed in series with the load. Stores energy when switch is ON.
4. **Filter Capacitor (C):** Placed in parallel with the load. Smooths the switched waveform into a near-DC level.
5. **Load (R):** Resistive load (the *why* of the whole converter).

### 2.2 Principle of Operation — Two Distinct States

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The circuit's behavior alternates between **two linear sub-circuits**. The state is determined by whether the controlled switch is ON or OFF.

#### **State 1 : Switch ON (Duration = $T_{ON} = DT$)**

- The diode $D_f$ is **reverse-biased** (cathode is at $+V_s$, anode at 0 V).
- Current flows: $V_s \rightarrow S \rightarrow L \rightarrow C \parallel R \rightarrow$ back to source.
- Inductor voltage: $V_L = V_s - V_0 = L \dfrac{di_L}{dt}$
- Since $V_s > V_0$, $\dfrac{di_L}{dt} > 0$ → **Inductor current rises linearly.**
- The inductor acts as a *load* (absorbing energy, magnetic field builds).

#### **State 2 : Switch OFF (Duration = $T_{OFF} = (1-D)T$)**

- The diode $D_f$ becomes **forward-biased** and conducts (provides a path for inductor current — *commutation*).
- Current path: $L \rightarrow C \parallel R \rightarrow D_f \rightarrow$ back to L.
- Inductor voltage: $V_L = -V_0 = L \dfrac{di_L}{dt}$
- Since $V_0 > 0$, $\dfrac{di_L}{dt} < 0$ → **Inductor current falls linearly.**
- The inductor acts as a *source* (releasing stored energy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the diode is mandatory:** When the switch opens, the inductor (which *cannot* allow instantaneous current discontinuity, since $v = L \, di/dt$) must find a closed path or its voltage will spike destructively. The diode "freewheels" this current, preventing an inductive kick that would otherwise destroy the switch.

### 2.3 Steady-State Volt-Second Balance (The Master Equation)

In **steady state**, the average voltage across the inductor over one complete switching period must be **zero** (otherwise the inductor current would either ramp to infinity or decay to zero — violating steady-state). Applying KVL over $T$:

$$\int_{0}^{T} v_L(t)\, dt = 0$$

$$\underbrace{(V_s - V_0) \cdot T_{ON}}_{\text{State 1}} + \underbrace{(-V_0) \cdot T_{OFF}}_{\text{State 2}} = 0$$

$$(V_s - V_0) \cdot D \cdot T - V_0 \cdot (1-D) \cdot T = 0$$

Solving for the **voltage transfer ratio**:

$$\boxed{\frac{V_0}{V_s} = D}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important result** in the entire Buck converter theory. Output voltage is *linearly* controlled by duty cycle D. Since $0 \le D \le 1$, we always have $V_0 \le V_s$ — hence the name "step-down."

### 2.4 Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) vs Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)

| Parameter | **CCM** | **DCM** |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Inductor Current | Always $> 0$ | Falls to zero during part of $T_{OFF}$ |
| Inductor behavior | Always conducting | Idle interval exists |
| Transfer ratio | $\dfrac{V_0}{V_s} = D$ (linear, load-independent) | $\dfrac{V_0}{V_s} = f(D, R, L, f_s)$ (nonlinear, load-dependent) |
| Practical usage | Most power supplies | Light-load / standby modes |

The boundary between them is defined by the **critical inductance** $L_c$, derived from setting the minimum inductor current to zero.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Buck Converter Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the starred ($\star$) equations verbatim — they appear in 95% of KTU Numerical Problems.**

| $\#$ | Formula | Description | Domain |
|------|---------|-------------|--------|
| $\star 1$ | $\dfrac{V_0}{V_s} = D$ | Voltage transfer ratio (CCM) | Ideal, CCM |
| $\star 2$ | $V_0 = \dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1}$ | Output voltage | Ideal, CCM |
| 3 | $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \, D \, (1-D)}{f_s \, L}$ | Peak-to-peak inductor ripple current | CCM |
| $\star 4$ | $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_0 \, (1-D)}{f_s \, L}$ | Alternate form using $V_0$ | CCM |
| 5 | $I_{L,\text{avg}} = I_0 = \dfrac{V_0}{R}$ | Average inductor current = load current | CCM |
| 6 | $I_{L,\text{max}} = I_0 + \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2}$ | Maximum (peak) inductor current | CCM |
| 7 | $I_{L,\text{min}} = I_0 - \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2}$ | Minimum inductor current | CCM |
| $\star 8$ | $L_c = \dfrac{R \, (1-D)}{2 \, f_s}$ | Critical (boundary) inductance | CCM/DCM boundary |
| $\star 9$ | $\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{V_0 \, (1-D)}{8 \, L \, C \, f_s^2}$ | Peak-to-peak output voltage ripple | CCM |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\Delta V_0}{V_0} = \dfrac{1-D}{8 \, L \, C \, f_s^2}$ | Normalized ripple | CCM |
| 11 | $I_{S,\text{avg}} = D \cdot I_0$ | Average switch current | CCM |
| 12 | $I_{S,\text{peak}} = I_{L,\text{max}}$ | Peak switch current | CCM |
| 13 | $I_{D_f,\text{avg}} = (1-D) \cdot I_0$ | Average diode current | CCM |
| 14 | $V_{S,\text{max}} = V_{S,\text{peak}} = V_s$ | Peak voltage across switch | CCM |
| 15 | $V_{D_f,\text{max}} = V_s$ | Peak reverse voltage across diode | CCM |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table Notation Rule:** In KTU answer sheets, write absolute values as `|x|` in plain text — **never** use LaTeX vertical pipes inside handwritten expressions. In these typed notes, we use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid markdown table corruption.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Laptop / Mobile Chargers (SMPS):** The 19 V / 20 V laptop adapter uses a Buck converter to step down rectified mains to charge a 11.1 V – 12.6 V battery.
- **USB-PD Chargers:** Step-down from 20 V bus to 5 V / 9 V / 15 V / 20 V negotiated voltages.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Auxiliaries:** Step down 400 V traction battery to 12 V accessory bus.
- **Solar PV MPPT:** Battery charge controllers use Buck topology to step panel voltage ($V_{mpp} \approx 30$ V) to battery voltage (12 V – 48 V).
- **Telecom DC Plants:** –48 V bus regulation from higher DC rails.
- **Point-of-Load (POL) Converters in CPUs:** VRMs (Voltage Regulator Modules) on motherboards are multi-phase synchronous Buck converters providing 0.6 V – 1.5 V at hundreds of amps to microprocessors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 : Voltage Transfer Ratio in CCM (Volt-Second Balance)

**Statement to Prove:** $\dfrac{V_0}{V_s} = D$

**Given:** Ideal components — switch, diode, inductor, capacitor are lossless; capacitor is large enough that $V_0$ is constant DC.

**Step 1 — Identify the two sub-circuits and the inductor voltage in each.**

When switch is ON: the inductor is connected directly across $(V_s - V_0)$.

$$v_L^{\text{ON}} = V_s - V_0 \quad \text{(constant for duration } T_{ON} = DT\text{)}$$

When switch is OFF: the inductor sees $-V_0$ (diode freewheels the current).

$$v_L^{\text{OFF}} = -V_0 \quad \text{(constant for duration } T_{OFF} = (1-D)T\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply steady-state volt-second balance on the inductor.**

In one switching cycle, the average voltage across an inductor in steady state is zero. Hence:

$$\int_{0}^{T} v_L(t) \, dt = 0$$

$$\int_{0}^{DT} (V_s - V_0)\, dt + \int_{DT}^{T} (-V_0)\, dt = 0$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$(V_s - V_0)(DT) + (-V_0)\left[T - DT\right] = 0$$

$$(V_s - V_0)(DT) - V_0 \cdot T(1 - D) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Expand and simplify.**

$$V_s \cdot DT - V_0 \cdot DT - V_0 \cdot T + V_0 \cdot DT = 0$$

$$V_s \cdot DT - V_0 \cdot T = 0$$

**Step 5 — Factor out T and divide.**

$$T\left[V_s D - V_0\right] = 0$$

Since $T \neq 0$:

$$V_s \cdot D = V_0$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{V_0}{V_s} = D} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 : Inductor Current Ripple $\Delta I_L$

**Statement:** $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_0 \, (1 - D)}{f_s \, L}$

**Step 1 — Recall the inductor V-I equation.**

$$v_L = L \dfrac{di_L}{dt}$$

During switch OFF (where $v_L = -V_0$, a constant):

$$-V_0 = L \cdot \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{T_{OFF}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\Delta I_L$.**

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{-V_0 \cdot T_{OFF}}{L} = \dfrac{V_0 \cdot T_{OFF}}{L} \quad \text{(taking magnitude)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $T_{OFF} = (1 - D)T = (1 - D)/f_s$.**

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_0 \cdot (1 - D) / f_s}{L} = \dfrac{V_0 (1 - D)}{f_s L}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_0 (1 - D)}{f_s L}} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Cross-verification:** Using the alternative derivation from State 1, with $V_L = V_s - V_0$:

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{(V_s - V_0) \cdot T_{ON}}{L} = \dfrac{V_s(1 - D) \cdot DT}{L} = \dfrac{V_s D(1 - D)}{f_s L}$$

Both are equivalent since $V_0 = D \cdot V_s$. $\checkmark$

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 : Critical (Boundary) Inductance $L_c$

**Physical Meaning:** At the boundary between CCM and DCM, the minimum inductor current just touches zero.

$$I_{L,\text{min}} = I_0 - \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = 0$$

**Step 1 — Set the boundary condition.**

$$I_0 = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\Delta I_L$ and $I_0 = V_0/R$.**

$$\dfrac{V_0}{R} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{V_0 (1 - D)}{f_s L_c}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $V_0$ from both sides.**

$$\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{(1 - D)}{2 f_s L_c}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $L_c$.**

$$L_c = \dfrac{R \, (1 - D)}{2 f_s}$$

$$\boxed{L_c = \dfrac{R (1 - D)}{2 f_s}} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** For guaranteed continuous conduction at a minimum load resistance $R_{\min}$, choose $L > L_c$ (typically $L \approx 1.25 \cdot L_c$ to provide a 25% safety margin).

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4 : Output Voltage Ripple $\Delta V_0$

**Step 1 — Identify the source of ripple.**

During $T_{ON}$, the inductor current is rising above the load current. The *excess* current $\Delta i_C = i_L - I_0$ charges the output capacitor. During $T_{OFF}$, the capacitor discharges the deficit. The peak-to-peak voltage ripple equals the integral of this excess current over half a cycle, divided by $C$.

**Step 2 — Compute the charge delivered to capacitor over $T_{ON}$.**

The average excess current flowing into C during $T_{ON}$ is half the peak ripple:

$$\overline{i_C}_{ON} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2}$$

The charge accumulated:

$$\Delta Q = \overline{i_C}_{ON} \cdot T_{ON} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} \cdot DT$$

**Step 3 — Relate $\Delta Q$ to $\Delta V_0$.**

Since $Q = C \cdot V$:

$$\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L \cdot D \cdot T}{2 C}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_0(1 - D)}{f_s L}$ and $T = 1/f_s$.**

$$\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{1}{2 C} \cdot \dfrac{V_0 (1 - D)}{f_s L} \cdot \dfrac{D}{f_s}$$

$$\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{V_0 \, D \, (1 - D)}{2 \, L \, C \, f_s^2}$$

**Step 5 — Worst-case ripple (occurs at $D = 0.5$).**

To find maximum, set $\dfrac{d}{dD}\left[D(1 - D)\right] = 0 \Rightarrow 1 - 2D = 0 \Rightarrow D = 0.5$.

Maximum value: $D(1-D) = 0.25$. Therefore, the **worst-case ripple**:

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{0,\text{max}} = \dfrac{V_0}{8 \, L \, C \, f_s^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{V_0 \, (1 - D)}{8 \, L \, C \, f_s^2}} \quad \text{(at } D = 0.5\text{, worst case)} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> Many KTU textbooks present the formula in this "worst-case at $D = 0.5$" form for simplicity. Either form is acceptable, but always **state the assumption**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

Below is a **block-level state-machine flow** showing the operational sequence of the Buck converter, including the two sub-circuits, the inductor current waveform, and the switching logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([DC Input Vs Arrives]) --> check1{Switch S Position?}
    check1 -- ON --> state1[STATE 1: Switch ON Duration TON]
    state1 --> state1desc["Diode Reverse Biased OFF. Inductor Voltage VL equals Vs minus Vo. Inductor Current iL RISES linearly. Capacitor C charges toward Vo."]
    state1desc --> inductor1["iL t = Imin to Imax during TON"]
    check1 -- OFF --> state2[STATE 2: Switch OFF Duration TOFF]
    state2 --> state2desc["Diode Forward Biased ON freewheeling. Inductor Voltage VL equals minus Vo. Inductor Current iL FALLS linearly. Capacitor C supplies load."]
    state2desc --> inductor2["iL t = Imax to Imin during TOFF"]
    inductor1 --> vbalance[Volt Second Balance Applied over one period T]
    inductor2 --> vbalance
    vbalance --> vbalanceeq["Equation: Vs minus Vo times TON equals Vo times TOFF"]
    vbalanceeq --> voResult["Result: Vo equals D times Vs"]
    voResult --> check2{Is L greater than Lc?}
    check2 -- YES --> ccmMode[CCM Continuous Conduction Mode]
    check2 -- NO --> dcmMode[DCM Discontinuous Conduction Mode]
    ccmMode --> ccmOut["Vo over Vs equals D constant. iL never touches zero."]
    dcmMode --> dcmOut["Vo over Vs depends on D, R, L, fs. iL hits zero during TOFF."]
    ccmOut --> end1([Regulated DC Output Vo Delivered to Load])
    dcmOut --> end1
```

## 4.2 Power Stage Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid schematic shows the **functional block-level connections** between the four power components. (Note: For actual circuit schematics with diodes/MOSFETs, students should always draw the textbook pictorial circuit in their answer scripts — KTU examiners expect the *standard circuit diagram* for full marks.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vin[DC Source Vs] --> sw1[Controlled Switch S MOSFET IGBT]
    sw1 --> node1[Switch Node SW]
    node1 --> L1[Filter Inductor L]
    node1 --> D1[Freewheeling Diode Df cathode at node anode at ground]
    L1 --> node2[Output Node]
    node2 --> C1[Filter Capacitor C]
    node2 --> R1[Load Resistor R]
    C1 --- R1
    node1 -.commutation path.-> D1
    L1 -.current freewheels.-> D1
    node2 --> Vout[Output Vo across Load]
```

## 4.3 Operating Waveforms (Conceptual Block Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    w1[Waveform 1 Gate Drive gS] --> w2[Square wave alternating high low at period T]
    w3[Waveform 2 Switch Node Voltage VSW] --> w4["Equals Vs when S ON, equals 0 when S OFF, equals minus diode drop ~ minus 0.7 V during freewheel"]
    w5[Waveform 3 Inductor Voltage VL] --> w6["Positive value Vs minus Vo during TON, negative value minus Vo during TOFF"]
    w7[Waveform 4 Inductor Current iL] --> w8["Ramps up during TON, ramps down during TOFF, triangular ripple around DC average I0"]
    w9[Waveform 5 Output Voltage vo] --> w10["DC level Vo with small triangular ripple Delta Vo superimposed"]
    w2 --- w4
    w4 --- w6
    w6 --- w8
    w8 --- w10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "draw the waveforms," always label **all five waveforms** on a single time axis with: amplitude, period $T$, $T_{ON}$, $T_{OFF}$, peak-to-peak ripple $\Delta I_L$, $\Delta V_0$, and the average DC levels $V_0$ and $I_0$. Partial credit is awarded per correctly labeled waveform.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A : Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions are direct, definitional, and test conceptual clarity. Answer length: **3–4 sentences + one equation/diagram**.

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Q: Define a step-down chopper. Derive its average output voltage in terms of duty cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **step-down chopper** (or **Buck converter**) is a DC-DC power electronic circuit that delivers an average output voltage *less than* the input DC voltage by periodically connecting and disconnecting the load from the source using a controlled switch.

In steady state, applying **volt-second balance** on the filter inductor:

$$V_0 \cdot T_{OFF} = (V_s - V_0) \cdot T_{ON}$$

$$\therefore V_0 = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \cdot V_s = D \cdot V_s$$

Hence $\dfrac{V_0}{V_s} = D$, the **voltage transfer ratio**, where $D = T_{ON}/T$ is the duty cycle. $(3 \text{ marks})$

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: What is meant by Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) and Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)? When does boundary conduction occur?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **CCM:** The inductor current flows *continuously* throughout the entire switching period, never reaching zero. The transfer ratio is linear: $V_0/V_s = D$.
- **DCM:** The inductor current falls to zero and *remains* zero for a finite interval within $T_{OFF}$. The transfer ratio becomes nonlinear, dependent on $D, R, L, f_s$.
- **Boundary Condition:** CCM and DCM are separated by the critical inductance condition $L = L_c = \dfrac{R(1-D)}{2f_s}$, where the inductor current just touches zero at the end of the OFF period. If $L > L_c$, operation is in CCM; if $L < L_c$, operation is in DCM. $(3 \text{ marks})$

---

## 5.2 Part B : Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions for Module 2 carry **14 marks**, typically split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must attempt **exactly one full question** out of the choice given. Working sketches/diagrams are **mandatory** for full marks.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`

**Q: With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a Buck converter in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM). Derive expressions for (i) output voltage, (ii) inductor current ripple, and (iii) output voltage ripple.**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Output Voltage (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Draw the circuit.**

The Buck converter consists of a DC source $V_s$, a controlled switch S (MOSFET/IGBT/BJT), a freewheeling diode $D_f$, an inductor $L$, a filter capacitor $C$, and a resistive load $R$, all connected in the canonical step-down topology (refer Section 4.2 schematic).

**Step 2 — State ON mode ($0 < t < T_{ON}$).**

The switch S is gated ON; the diode $D_f$ is reverse-biased and blocks. Current builds up in $L$ from $I_{\min}$ to $I_{\max}$, while the capacitor $C$ charges. The voltage across $L$ is:

$$v_L = V_s - V_0$$

**Step 3 — State OFF mode ($T_{ON} < t < T$).**

The switch S is gated OFF; the inductor forces the diode $D_f$ into conduction (freewheeling). Current circulates through $L \rightarrow R \parallel C \rightarrow D_f$. The voltage across $L$ is:

$$v_L = -V_0$$

**Step 4 — Apply volt-second balance.**

$$\int_{0}^{T} v_L \, dt = (V_s - V_0)T_{ON} + (-V_0)T_{OFF} = 0$$

$$\boxed{V_0 = D \cdot V_s} \quad (4 \text{ marks for circuit + balance})$$

**Step 5 — Add waveforms.**

Sketch the gate signal $v_{GS}$, the switch-node voltage $v_{SW}$, the inductor voltage $v_L$, the inductor current $i_L$ (triangular ripple around $I_0$), and the output voltage $v_0$ (DC level $V_0$ with small triangular ripple). $(3 \text{ marks})$

#### Part (b) — Inductor Ripple Current and Output Voltage Ripple (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Derive $\Delta I_L$.**

From $v_L = L \dfrac{di_L}{dt}$ during the OFF interval:

$$-V_0 = L \cdot \dfrac{-\Delta I_L}{T_{OFF}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_0 (1 - D)}{f_s L}}$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of $V_s$.**

Since $V_0 = D \cdot V_s$:

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s D (1 - D)}{f_s L}$$

**Step 3 — Derive $\Delta V_0$ using the charge-balance method.**

The average excess current entering $C$ during $T_{ON}$ is $\Delta I_L / 2$. Charge accumulated:

$$\Delta Q = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} \cdot T_{ON} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L \cdot D}{2 f_s}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{C} = \dfrac{V_0 (1 - D)}{8 L C f_s^2}}$$

(assuming worst-case $D = 0.5$).

**Step 4 — Add critical inductance condition for CCM operation.**

$$\boxed{L_c = \dfrac{R(1 - D)}{2 f_s}} \quad \text{Choose } L > L_c \text{ for CCM}$$

$(7 \text{ marks for full derivation with numerical units})$

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model]`

**Q: A Buck converter has $V_s = 24 \text{ V}$, $L = 20 \text{ µH}$, $C = 100 \text{ µF}$, $R = 5 \, \Omega$, and switching frequency $f_s = 50 \text{ kHz}$, operating with duty cycle $D = 0.4$. Determine: (a) Average output voltage, average load current, and verify CCM operation. (b) Inductor current ripple, output voltage ripple, and the peak and minimum inductor currents.**

#### Part (a) — Output Voltage, Load Current, and CCM Verification (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate $V_0$.**

$$V_0 = D \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 24 = \boxed{9.6 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate average load current.**

$$I_0 = \dfrac{V_0}{R} = \dfrac{9.6}{5} = \boxed{1.92 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate critical inductance.**

$$L_c = \dfrac{R(1 - D)}{2 f_s} = \dfrac{5 \times (1 - 0.4)}{2 \times 50{,}000} = \dfrac{3}{100{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{L_c = 30 \text{ µH}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify CCM.**

Since $L = 20 \text{ µH} < L_c = 30 \text{ µH}$, the converter **operates in DCM**, not CCM. $(7 \text{ marks})$

#### Part (b) — Inductor Ripple, Output Ripple, Peak/Minimum Currents (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Inductor current ripple.**

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_0(1 - D)}{f_s L} = \dfrac{9.6 \times 0.6}{50{,}000 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{5.76}{1} = \boxed{5.76 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 2 — Check whether this ripple is even physically realizable.**

$I_{L,\min} = I_0 - \Delta I_L/2 = 1.92 - 2.88 = -0.96 \text{ A}$

A **negative** current confirms that $i_L$ falls to zero *before* the cycle ends → **DCM confirmed**. $\checkmark$

**Step 3 — Output voltage ripple (using CCM formula — gives upper bound).**

$$\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{V_0(1 - D)}{8 L C f_s^2} = \dfrac{9.6 \times 0.6}{8 \times 20{\times}10^{-6} \times 100{\times}10^{-6} \times (50{,}000)^2}$$

$$= \dfrac{5.76}{8 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.5 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$= \dfrac{5.76}{400} = \boxed{0.0144 \text{ V} = 14.4 \text{ mV}}$$

**Step 4 — Peak and minimum inductor currents.**

$$I_{L,\text{peak}} = I_0 + \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = 1.92 + 2.88 = \boxed{4.8 \text{ A}}$$

$$I_{L,\text{min}} = 1.92 - 2.88 = \boxed{-0.96 \text{ A (unphysical → DCM)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Recommendation:** To force CCM operation, increase $L$ to at least $1.25 \cdot L_c = 37.5 \text{ µH}$, or reduce $f_s$, or reduce $R$ (heavier load).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Buck Converter Problems**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify CCM** — Students blindly apply $V_0 = D V_s$ without checking $L > L_c$. If the circuit is actually in DCM, the *transfer ratio is wrong* and all downstream answers collapse. Always verify in **Step 1**.
>
> 2. **Sign convention errors on $\Delta V_0$** — The expression $\frac{V_0 (1-D)}{8LCf_s^2}$ is a **peak-to-peak** ripple, *not* RMS. Examiners deduct 1 mark if "RMS" or "peak" is mistakenly attached.
>
> 3. **Missing freewheeling diode** — A sketch *without* the freewheeling diode loses 1–2 marks outright. The diode is mandatory and is the *defining* element that distinguishes a chopper from a switch.
>
> 4. **Switch current vs. diode current confusion** — Average switch current is $D I_0$, average diode current is $(1-D) I_0$. Examiners love testing this.
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — $L$ in mH vs µH, $f_s$ in kHz vs MHz. Convert **before** substituting. KTU valuations mark conversion errors as full-step deductions.
>
> 6. **Duty cycle $D = V_0/V_s$ assumed for DCM** — In DCM this is invalid. Students must derive the DCM transfer ratio separately (out of syllabus for most KTU modules but occasionally asked as a 7-mark twist).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist. Memorize it. Win the exam.**

- [ ] **Buck converter** = step-down chopper; $V_0 \le V_s$ always.
- [ ] **Voltage transfer ratio in CCM:** $\dfrac{V_0}{V_s} = D$ (single most important equation).
- [ ] **Duty cycle** $D = T_{ON}/T = t_{ON} \cdot f_s$.
- [ ] **Two sub-circuits:** Switch ON → $v_L = V_s - V_0$ (positive, $i_L$ rises). Switch OFF → $v_L = -V_0$ (negative, $i_L$ falls).
- [ ] **Freewheeling diode** is *mandatory* — prevents inductive voltage spike.
- [ ] **Inductor current ripple:** $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_0 (1-D)}{f_s L} = \dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{f_s L}$.
- [ ] **Output voltage ripple:** $\Delta V_0 = \dfrac{V_0 (1-D)}{8 L C f_s^2}$ (worst case at $D = 0.5$).
- [ ] **Critical inductance:** $L_c = \dfrac{R(1-D)}{2 f_s}$. Choose $L > L_c$ for CCM.
- [ ] **Always verify CCM** before applying the linear transfer ratio $V_0 = D V_s$.
- [ ] **Switch ratings:** $I_{S,\text{avg}} = D I_0$; $V_{S,\text{peak}} = V_s$.
- [ ] **Diode ratings:** $I_{D_f,\text{avg}} = (1-D) I_0$; $V_{D_f,\text{peak}} = V_s$.
- [ ] **Waveforms to draw (always 5):** Gate drive, switch-node voltage, inductor voltage, inductor current, output voltage.
- [ ] **Real-world applications:** SMPS, EV auxiliaries, USB-PD chargers, solar charge controllers, CPU VRMs.
- [ ] **Volt-second balance** is the fundamental analysis tool — average inductor voltage = 0 in steady state.
- [ ] **Synchronous Buck:** Replace diode with a low-$R_{DS(\text{on})}$ MOSFET for higher efficiency (topology variant; not in basic syllabus but mentioned in advanced modules).
- [ ] **Efficiency assumption:** KTU Buck problems are usually *ideal* unless explicitly stated otherwise (no conduction/switching losses).
- [ ] **Numerical safety check:** If $\Delta I_L / 2 > I_0$, the converter is **automatically in DCM** — alert the reader in your answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra:** *"Duty cycle controls output voltage. Inductor size controls current continuity. Capacitor size controls voltage ripple. Frequency controls everything smaller."* Memorize this single sentence and you can derive every other relationship on the fly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boost and Buck-Boost Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boost and Buck-Boost Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Boost Converter (Step-Up Chopper)

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Boost Converter** is a class of **switch-mode DC-to-DC power electronic converter** in which the output DC voltage is **greater in magnitude** than the input DC source voltage. It is realized by inserting an inductor in series with the source and using a controlled semiconductor switch (typically a MOSFET, IGBT, or BJT) that periodically stores energy in the magnetic field of the inductor and then releases it to the load through a flyback diode and a smoothing capacitor. The output voltage magnitude is regulated by varying the **duty cycle (D)** of the switching signal, defined as $D = t_{on}/T$ where $T$ is the total switching period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Study of Boost and Buck-Boost converters must include steady-state analysis in **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)**, derivation of output voltage transfer ratio, inductor ripple current, output voltage ripple, and determination of the **critical (boundary) values of inductance and duty cycle**. The boundary between CCM and DCM is treated as a high-weightage problem.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are **blowing up a balloon with a small pump that has a one-way valve**:

- **Phase 1 (Switch ON, $t_{on}$):** The pump pushes air into the balloon. The inlet valve is closed to the atmosphere, so the balloon *stores* the energy. This is analogous to the inductor storing energy while the switch is closed and the diode is reverse-biased.
- **Phase 2 (Switch OFF, $t_{off}$):** The pump is now connected to the atmosphere. The air inside the balloon (stored energy) plus the additional air from the pump rushes out to the user at a *higher pressure* than the pump alone could provide. This corresponds to the inductor releasing its stored energy *in series* with the source voltage, pushing current into the load.

The **average output pressure** (voltage) is always higher than the **input pump pressure** (source), hence the name "**Step-Up**" or "**Boost**."

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Switching Frequency | $f_s$ | **kHz** (typically 20–500 kHz) |
| Duty Cycle | $D$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Inductance | $L$ | **Henry (H)** or milli-Henry (mH) |
| Capacitance | $C$ | **Farad (F)** or micro-Farad (µF) |
| Load Resistance | $R$ | **Ohm (Ω)** |

---

## 1.2 Buck-Boost Converter (Step-Up/Down Chopper)

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Buck-Boost Converter** is a **single-stage, non-isolated DC-DC converter** whose output voltage magnitude can be made either **greater than or less than** the input source voltage, depending upon the switching duty cycle. It is constructed by cascading the inductor section of a buck converter with the output filter of a boost converter, sharing a common switching element. The output is **inverted** (negative polarity) with respect to the input, and its magnitude is given by the classical transfer ratio $M(D) = \dfrac{D}{1-D}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** A Buck-Boost converter is essentially a **boost converter whose output polarity is reversed**. It provides *both step-down* (when $D < 0.5$) and *step-up* (when $D > 0.5$) operation from a single topology, at the cost of an inverted output voltage.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **two-way water pump system** that can either suck water from a tank (step-down) or push water uphill (step-up), and the water always comes out of the *opposite side* of where it entered.

- **Phase 1 (Switch ON, $t_{on}$):** The pump pulls water into an intermediate storage tank (the inductor magnetizes), disconnecting the output. The output capacitor *alone* supplies the load.
- **Phase 2 (Switch OFF, $t_{off}$):** The intermediate tank releases its stored water in the *reverse direction* relative to the source, pushing current through the load in the opposite polarity. The source contributes nothing; only the inductor's energy drives the load.

Because the energy transfer is **in the reverse direction** during discharge, the output is always **negative** with respect to ground.

### Key Electrical Symbols (KTU Notation)

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU Standard |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_s$ | Source Voltage | **DC Input** (V) |
| $V_o$ | Output Voltage | **DC Output** (V), negative for buck-boost |
| $i_L$ | Inductor Current | Amperes (A) |
| $i_s$ | Source Current | Amperes (A) |
| $i_o$ | Load Current | Amperes (A) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Boost Converter — Circuit Topology & CCM Operation

### 2.1.1 Circuit Description
A boost converter consists of the following components connected in a specific topology:
- **Input DC Source** $V_s$
- **Boost Inductor** $L$ in series with the source
- **Controlled Switch** $S$ (MOSFET/IGBT) connected between the inductor's right terminal and ground
- **Diode** $D_f$ (freewheeling/boost diode) connected from the switching node to the output
- **Output Filter Capacitor** $C$ in parallel with the load $R$
- **Load Resistor** $R$

### 2.1.2 Modes of Operation in CCM

**Mode 1: Switch ON ($0 \le t \le DT$)**

- Switch is **closed**; the inductor is connected directly across $V_s$.
- Diode $D_f$ is **reverse-biased** and OFF (the output is isolated from the input).
- The inductor **stores energy** (current rises linearly).
- The output capacitor $C$ **discharges** into the load $R$ to maintain $V_o$.

The voltage across the inductor is:
$$V_L = V_s = L \dfrac{di_L}{dt}$$

The peak-to-peak ripple in inductor current over the ON time:
$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \cdot D \cdot T}{L}$$

**Mode 2: Switch OFF ($DT \le t \le T$)**

- Switch is **opened**; the inductor's stored energy plus the source voltage appear across the load.
- Diode $D_f$ is **forward-biased** and ON.
- The inductor **releases energy** to both the load and the capacitor.
- Current through the inductor falls linearly.

The voltage across the inductor (now opposing source) is:
$$V_L = V_s - V_o = L \dfrac{di_L}{dt}$$

The peak-to-peak ripple (over OFF time):
$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{(V_o - V_s)(1-D)T}{L}$$

### 2.1.3 The 'Why' Behind Voltage Step-Up
During the ON state, the inductor *refuses* to allow sudden current change and **pumps up** the magnetic flux. When the switch opens, the inductor's current path must continue, which forces current through the diode into the capacitor. Because the inductor voltage *adds* to the source voltage during this discharge phase, the capacitor is charged to a value higher than $V_s$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The boost converter cannot operate at $D = 1$ in practice. As $D \to 1$, the OFF time approaches zero, and the theoretical $V_o \to \infty$. Real components have parasitic resistances that limit the practical maximum to typically 3–4× the source voltage.

### 2.1.4 Boundary Condition (CCM-to-DCM Threshold)
The converter operates at the **boundary of CCM and DCM** when the inductor current just touches zero at the end of the OFF period. The **critical inductance** is:

$$L_c = \dfrac{R(1-D)^2}{2f}$$

The critical duty cycle (for given $L$) is:
$$D_{crit} = 1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{2Lf}{R}}$$

- If $L > L_c$ (or equivalently $D < D_{crit}$): **CCM**
- If $L < L_c$ (or equivalently $D > D_{crit}$): **DCM**

---

## 2.2 Buck-Boost Converter — Circuit Topology & CCM Operation

### 2.2.1 Circuit Description
A buck-boost converter is a hybrid of the buck and boost topologies. It uses a **single inductor**, a **single switch**, a **single diode**, and an **output capacitor**. Critically, the load is connected such that the output voltage is **inverted** in polarity.

### 2.2.2 Modes of Operation in CCM

**Mode 1: Switch ON ($0 \le t \le DT$)**

- Switch $S$ is **closed**; diode $D_f$ is **reverse-biased**.
- The inductor is connected across $V_s$ and charges linearly.
- Output capacitor $C$ supplies load current $I_o$.
- **Output is isolated** from the input.

Inductor voltage: $V_L = V_s$, ripple current:
$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \cdot D \cdot T}{L}$$

**Mode 2: Switch OFF ($DT \le t \le T$)**

- Switch $S$ is **opened**; diode $D_f$ is **forward-biased**.
- The inductor releases energy into the **load and capacitor** in reverse direction.
- Source current $I_s = 0$; only inductor drives the load.

Inductor voltage: $V_L = V_o$ (note: $V_o$ is negative), ripple:
$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_o(1-D)T}{L}$$

### 2.2.3 Polarity Inversion — The 'Why'
When the switch is OFF, the inductor current has been flowing left-to-right (assuming the switch is on the right side of the inductor). When the switch opens, the inductor maintains current direction, but now this current is forced through the diode and into the *negative* terminal of the output capacitor. The capacitor therefore charges with its **top plate negative** and **bottom plate positive** (assuming conventional buck-boost orientation), yielding a **negative $V_o$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The negative sign in $V_o = -\dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$ is **NOT** an approximation — it is a fundamental feature of the converter. Do not omit it during calculations. Many students lose marks by writing $V_o = +\dfrac{DV_s}{1-D}$.

### 2.2.4 Boundary Condition
The critical inductance for the buck-boost converter is:
$$L_c = \dfrac{R(1-D)^2}{2f}$$

The critical value of the dimensionless parameter $k = 2L/RT$ is:
$$k_{crit} = (1-D)^2$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING:** All absolute value notations use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Boost Converter | Buck-Boost Converter | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Output Voltage | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | $V_o = -\dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$ | V |
| 2 | Voltage Transfer Ratio | $M = \dfrac{V_o}{V_s} = \dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | $M = \dfrac{V_o}{V_s} = -\dfrac{D}{1-D}$ | — |
| 3 | Average Inductor Current | $I_L = \dfrac{V_s}{(1-D)^2 R}$ | $I_L = \dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{(1-D)^2 R}$ | A |
| 4 | Inductor Current Ripple | $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \cdot D}{f \cdot L}$ | $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \cdot D}{f \cdot L}$ | A |
| 5 | Average Source Current | $I_s = \dfrac{I_L}{1} = \dfrac{V_s}{(1-D)^2 R}$ | $I_s = D \cdot I_L = \dfrac{D^2 V_s}{(1-D)^2 R}$ | A |
| 6 | Average Load Current | $I_o = (1-D) \cdot I_L = \dfrac{V_s}{(1-D)R}$ | $I_o = \dfrac{D \cdot I_L}{1} = \dfrac{D^2 V_s}{(1-D)R}$ | A |
| 7 | Output Voltage Ripple | $\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{D}{R \cdot C \cdot f}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{D}{R \cdot C \cdot f}$ | — |
| 8 | Critical Inductance | $L_c = \dfrac{R(1-D)^2}{2f}$ | $L_c = \dfrac{R(1-D)^2}{2f}$ | H |
| 9 | Critical Duty Cycle | $D_{crit} = 1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{2Lf}{R}}$ | $D_{crit} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2Lf/R}}{1+\sqrt{2Lf/R}}$ | — |
| 10 | CCM-DCM Boundary $k_c$ | $k_c = \dfrac{1}{2}(1-D)^2\sqrt{\dfrac{2fL}{R}}$ | $k_c = (1-D)^2$ | — |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Boost Converters** are ubiquitous in:
  - **Photovoltaic (PV) MPPT systems** (stepping up low PV voltage to DC-link voltage).
  - **Hybrid/Electric vehicle DC-link stages.**
  - **LED drivers** requiring constant current at higher voltage.
  - **Battery-powered portable electronics** (boosting Li-ion 3.7 V to USB 5 V).
- **Buck-Boost Converters** are used in:
  - **Battery management systems** where battery voltage varies above and below the desired output (e.g., 12 V output from a 9–16 V supply).
  - **Power factor correction (PFC) front ends.**
  - **SEPIC and Ćuk topologies** are derived from this basic structure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Boost Converter — Exhaustive Derivation of $V_o$ in CCM

### Step 1: Apply Volt-Second Balance on the Inductor
In **steady-state CCM**, the average voltage across the inductor over one switching period must be zero (otherwise the inductor current would ramp up indefinitely).

$$\langle V_L \rangle = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T V_L(t) \, dt = 0$$

### Step 2: Split the Integral into ON and OFF Intervals
During the ON interval ($0 \le t \le DT$), $V_L = V_s$.
During the OFF interval ($DT \le t \le T$), $V_L = V_s - V_o$.

$$\dfrac{1}{T} \left[ \int_0^{DT} V_s \, dt + \int_{DT}^T (V_s - V_o) \, dt \right] = 0$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Integrals
$$\dfrac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot DT + (V_s - V_o)(T - DT) \right] = 0$$

### Step 4: Simplify
$$V_s \cdot D + (V_s - V_o)(1 - D) = 0$$

$$V_s D + V_s - V_s D - V_o(1 - D) = 0$$

$$V_s - V_o(1 - D) = 0$$

### Step 5: Solve for $V_o$
$$\boxed{V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D}}$$

### Step 6: Derivation of Average Inductor Current
Applying **charge (amp-second) balance** on the output capacitor: the average capacitor current is zero in steady state, so the average diode current equals the average load current.

$$I_D = I_o$$

The average diode current is related to the average inductor current by:
$$I_D = (1 - D) I_L$$

Therefore:
$$(1 - D) I_L = I_o = \dfrac{V_o}{R}$$

Substituting $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$:
$$(1 - D) I_L = \dfrac{V_s}{(1 - D) R}$$

$$\boxed{I_L = \dfrac{V_s}{(1-D)^2 R}}$$

### Step 7: Derivation of Critical Inductance
At the **boundary of CCM/DCM**, the inductor current ripple equals twice the average inductor current (the current just touches zero):

$$\Delta I_L = 2 I_L$$

Substituting the ripple formula:
$$\dfrac{V_s \cdot D}{f L_c} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{V_s}{(1-D)^2 R}$$

Simplifying:
$$\dfrac{D}{f L_c} = \dfrac{2}{(1-D)^2 R}$$

$$\boxed{L_c = \dfrac{D R (1 - D)^2}{2 f}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Alert:** The critical inductance is commonly expressed in terms of $R$, $f$, and $D$ alone. Using $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$, one can equivalently write:
> $$L_c = \dfrac{R (1-D)^2}{2 f}$$
> Both forms are valid; the second is preferred when the design starts with a specified output voltage rather than a specific $D$.

---

## 3.2 Buck-Boost Converter — Exhaustive Derivation of $V_o$ in CCM

### Step 1: Apply Volt-Second Balance on the Inductor
As with the boost converter, the average inductor voltage over a complete switching cycle must equal zero in steady state.

$$\dfrac{1}{T} \left[ \int_0^{DT} V_s \, dt + \int_{DT}^T V_o \, dt \right] = 0$$

### Step 2: Evaluate Each Integral
- During ON: $V_L = V_s$, integrated over $DT$ gives $V_s \cdot DT$.
- During OFF: $V_L = V_o$ (a negative quantity), integrated over $(1-D)T$ gives $V_o (1-D)T$.

$$V_s \cdot D + V_o (1 - D) = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for $V_o$
$$V_o (1 - D) = -V_s D$$

$$\boxed{V_o = -\dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1 - D}}$$

### Step 4: Derivation of Average Inductor Current
Apply **amp-second balance** on the capacitor. The average diode current equals the average load current.

The diode conducts only during the OFF interval. The average diode current is:
$$I_D = (1 - D) I_L$$

The average load current is:
$$I_o = \dfrac{V_o}{R} = -\dfrac{D V_s}{(1 - D) R}$$

Equating the magnitudes:
$$(1 - D) I_L = \dfrac{D V_s}{(1 - D) R}$$

$$\boxed{I_L = \dfrac{D V_s}{(1 - D)^2 R}}$$

### Step 5: Derivation of Critical Inductance
At the CCM/DCM boundary: $\Delta I_L = 2 I_L$.

The peak-to-peak ripple during the ON interval is:
$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \cdot D}{f L_c}$$

Setting this equal to $2 I_L$:
$$\dfrac{V_s D}{f L_c} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{D V_s}{(1 - D)^2 R}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{f L_c} = \dfrac{2}{(1 - D)^2 R}$$

$$\boxed{L_c = \dfrac{R (1 - D)^2}{2 f}}$$

### Step 6: Derivation of Output Voltage Ripple
During the ON interval, the output capacitor alone supplies the load. The change in capacitor charge over $DT$ is:
$$\Delta Q = I_o \cdot D T = C \cdot \Delta V_o$$

Therefore:
$$\Delta V_o = \dfrac{I_o \cdot D \cdot T}{C} = \dfrac{I_o \cdot D}{f C}$$

Dividing both sides by $V_o$ and using $I_o = V_o/R$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{D}{R C f}}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A boost converter operates in CCM with $V_s = 12$ V, $D = 0.4$, $L = 100$ µH, $C = 100$ µF, $R = 10$ Ω, and $f = 50$ kHz. Find $V_o$, $I_L$, $\Delta I_L$, and verify the mode of operation.

### Solution

**Step A: Output Voltage**
$$V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D} = \dfrac{12}{1 - 0.4} = \dfrac{12}{0.6} = 20 \text{ V}$$

**Step B: Average Inductor Current**
$$I_L = \dfrac{V_s}{(1 - D)^2 R} = \dfrac{12}{(0.6)^2 \cdot 10} = \dfrac{12}{0.36 \cdot 10} = \dfrac{12}{3.6} = 3.333 \text{ A}$$

**Step C: Inductor Current Ripple**
$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s D}{f L} = \dfrac{12 \cdot 0.4}{50{,}000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{4.8}{5} = 0.96 \text{ A}$$

**Step D: Mode of Operation Check**
For CCM: $I_L > \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2}$
$$\dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = 0.48 \text{ A}$$

Since $3.333 \text{ A} \gg 0.48 \text{ A}$, the converter operates in **CCM**. ✔

**Step E: Output Voltage Ripple**
$$\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{D}{RCf} = \dfrac{0.4}{10 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50{,}000} = \dfrac{0.4}{50} = 0.008 = 0.8\%$$

$$\Delta V_o = 0.008 \times 20 = 0.16 \text{ V}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boost Converter — Power Flow Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SourceNodeA[DC Source Vs] --> InductorNodeB[Inductor L]
    InductorNodeB --> SwitchNodeC[Controlled Switch S]
    SwitchNodeC --> GroundNodeD[Reference Ground]
    InductorNodeB --> DiodeNodeE[Boost Diode Df]
    DiodeNodeE --> OutputNodeF[DC Output Vo]
    OutputNodeF --> CapacitorNodeG[Filter Capacitor C]
    CapacitorNodeG --> LoadNodeH[Load Resistor R]
    LoadNodeH --> GroundNodeD
    SourceNodeA --> GateNodeI[PWM Gate Driver]
    GateNodeI --> SwitchNodeC
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **DC Source** feeds the **Inductor**, which is the energy storage element.
- The **Switch** shorts the inductor to ground when ON, storing energy.
- The **Diode** is forward-biased only when the switch is OFF, channeling inductor current to the output.
- The **Capacitor** smooths the output voltage ripple.
- The **PWM Gate Driver** controls the switch timing (sets the duty cycle $D$).

---

## 4.2 Boost Converter — Two-Mode Operation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    StartNodeA[Start of Switching Period] --> DecisionNodeB{Switch State?}
    DecisionNodeB --|Switch ON 0 to DT| --> Mode1NodeC[Mode 1: Energy Storage]
    Mode1NodeC --> Action1NodeD[Inductor charges linearly from Vs]
    Action1NodeD --> Action2NodeE[Diode reverse biased OFF]
    Action2NodeE --> Action3NodeF[Capacitor discharges into load R]
    Action3NodeG[End of Mode 1] --> DecisionNodeB

    DecisionNodeB --|Switch OFF DT to T| --> Mode2NodeH[Mode 2: Energy Release]
    Mode2NodeH --> Action4NodeI[Inductor releases energy to load and C]
    Action4NodeI --> Action5NodeJ[Diode forward biased ON]
    Action5NodeJ --> Action6NodeK[Vo greater than Vs]
    Action6NodeL[End of Mode 2] --> EndPeriodNodeM[End of Switching Period]
    EndPeriodNodeM --> StartNodeA
```

---

## 4.3 Buck-Boost Converter — Power Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    SourceNodeA[DC Source Vs] --> InductorNodeB[Inductor L]
    InductorNodeB --> SwitchNodeC[Controlled Switch S]
    SwitchNodeC --> GroundNodeD[Reference Ground]
    SourceNodeA --> SwitchNodeC
    InductorNodeB --> DiodeNodeE[Flyback Diode Df]
    DiodeNodeE --> OutputNodeF[Negative Output Terminal]
    GroundNodeD --> ReturnNodeG[Positive Output Terminal]
    OutputNodeF --> CapacitorNodeH[Output Capacitor C]
    CapacitorNodeH --> ReturnNodeG
    ReturnNodeG --> LoadNodeI[Load R]
    LoadNodeI --> OutputNodeF
    PWMNodeJ[PWM Controller] --> SwitchNodeC
```

**Key Architectural Difference:** The buck-boost's diode is connected to the *negative* output rail, and the *positive* output rail is the ground reference. This causes the **inverted polarity** characteristic.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Function Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BoostConverter[Boost Converter Features]
        BoostFeatA[Step Up Only]
        BoostFeatB[Same Polarity as Input]
        BoostFeatC[Continuous Input Current]
    end

    subgraph BuckBoostConverter[Buck-Boost Converter Features]
        BBFeatA[Step Up or Step Down]
        BBFeatB[Inverted Output Polarity]
        BBFeatC[Discontinuous Input Current]
    end

    BoostFeatA --> CommonFeatureD[Uses Inductor for Energy Storage]
    BoostFeatB --> CommonFeatureD
    BBFeatA --> CommonFeatureD
    BBFeatB --> CommonFeatureD
```

**Engineering Takeaway:** Both topologies use the *same fundamental principle* — temporarily storing energy in a magnetic field and then releasing it — but they differ in *when* and *where* the inductor's stored energy is delivered to the load.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Q: Derive the expression for the output voltage of a boost converter operating in continuous conduction mode.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 mark) State the **volt-second balance** condition on the inductor: average inductor voltage over one switching period is zero in steady state.
- (1 mark) Set up the equation: $V_s \cdot DT + (V_s - V_o)(1 - D)T = 0$.
- (1 mark) Solve to obtain the final expression: $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Q: What is the critical value of inductance for a boost converter? Explain the condition for continuous conduction mode.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 mark) Define the boundary condition: at the CCM/DCM boundary, $\Delta I_L = 2 I_L$.
- (1 mark) Substitute the expressions for ripple and average current to obtain $L_c = \dfrac{R(1-D)^2}{2f}$.
- (1 mark) State the CCM condition: $L > L_c$ or equivalently $D < D_{crit}$ where $D_{crit} = 1 - \sqrt{2Lf/R}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The exam offers an **internal choice** — you must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a boost converter in continuous conduction mode. Derive the expression for its output voltage in terms of duty cycle. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for Q(a):**

**Step 1: Circuit Diagram Description (2 Marks)**
A boost converter consists of a DC source $V_s$ feeding a series inductor $L$. A controlled switch $S$ (MOSFET/IGBT) is connected from the inductor's right terminal to ground. A boost diode $D_f$ connects the switching node to the positive output terminal. A filter capacitor $C$ is placed in parallel with the load $R$.

**Step 2: Mode 1 — Switch ON (2 Marks)**
- Switch is closed; inductor is connected directly across $V_s$.
- $V_L = V_s$, inductor current rises linearly: $i_L(t) = I_{L,min} + \dfrac{V_s}{L}t$.
- Diode is reverse-biased; output capacitor supplies load current.

**Step 3: Mode 2 — Switch OFF (2 Marks)**
- Switch is opened; inductor current must continue, now flowing through the diode.
- $V_L = V_s - V_o$ (negative), inductor current falls linearly.
- Diode is forward-biased; inductor releases stored energy to load and capacitor.

**Step 4: Derivation of Output Voltage (1 Mark)**
Applying volt-second balance:
$$V_s \cdot D + (V_s - V_o)(1 - D) = 0 \implies V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D}$$

---

**Q(b)** A boost converter has $V_s = 24$ V, $D = 0.5$, $L = 250$ µH, $C = 200$ µF, $R = 20$ Ω, $f = 25$ kHz. Calculate:
- (i) Output voltage $V_o$ (2 marks)
- (ii) Average inductor current $I_L$ (2 marks)
- (iii) Inductor current ripple $\Delta I_L$ and mode of operation (2 marks)
- (iv) Output voltage ripple $\Delta V_o$ (1 mark)

**Model Answer for Q(b):**

**(i) Output Voltage:**
$$V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D} = \dfrac{24}{1 - 0.5} = \dfrac{24}{0.5} = 48 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Average Inductor Current:**
$$I_L = \dfrac{V_s}{(1 - D)^2 R} = \dfrac{24}{(0.5)^2 \cdot 20} = \dfrac{24}{5} = 4.8 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Inductor Current Ripple & Mode:**
$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s D}{f L} = \dfrac{24 \cdot 0.5}{25{,}000 \cdot 250 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{12}{6.25} = 1.92 \text{ A}$$

Check: $\dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = 0.96$ A. Since $I_L = 4.8$ A $> 0.96$ A, the converter operates in **CCM**. ✔

**(iv) Output Voltage Ripple:**
$$\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{D}{RCf} = \dfrac{0.5}{20 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 25{,}000} = \dfrac{0.5}{100} = 0.005$$

$$\Delta V_o = 0.005 \times 48 = 0.24 \text{ V} = 240 \text{ mV}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q(a)** Explain the operation of a buck-boost converter in continuous conduction mode with a circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for Q(a):**

**Step 1: Circuit Description (1 Mark)**
A buck-boost converter consists of an input source $V_s$, a series inductor $L$, a switch $S$, a flyback diode $D_f$, and an output capacitor $C$ with load $R$. The output is taken across the capacitor with the polarity **inverted** relative to the input.

**Step 2: Mode 1 — Switch ON (2 Marks)**
- Switch closed; diode reverse-biased and OFF.
- Inductor charges linearly: $V_L = V_s$, current rises.
- Capacitor supplies load in isolation from the source.

**Step 3: Mode 2 — Switch OFF (2 Marks)**
- Switch opened; diode forward-biased.
- Inductor releases energy *in reverse* to load and capacitor: $V_L = V_o$ (negative).
- Output polarity is **negative** with respect to ground.

**Step 4: Derivation of Output Voltage (2 Marks)**
Volt-second balance:
$$V_s \cdot D + V_o(1 - D) = 0$$
$$V_o = -\dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1 - D}$$

The **negative sign** is the defining feature of the buck-boost converter.

---

**Q(b)** A buck-boost converter has $V_s = 15$ V, $D = 0.6$, $L = 150$ µH, $C = 220$ µF, $R = 10$ Ω, $f = 40$ kHz. Determine:
- (i) Output voltage $V_o$ (2 marks)
- (ii) Average inductor current $I_L$ (2 marks)
- (iii) Average source current $I_s$ and average load current $I_o$ (2 marks)
- (iv) Critical inductance $L_c$ and verify the mode of operation (1 mark)

**Model Answer for Q(b):**

**(i) Output Voltage:**
$$V_o = -\dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1 - D} = -\dfrac{0.6 \cdot 15}{1 - 0.6} = -\dfrac{9}{0.4} = -22.5 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Average Inductor Current:**
$$I_L = \dfrac{D V_s}{(1 - D)^2 R} = \dfrac{0.6 \cdot 15}{(0.4)^2 \cdot 10} = \dfrac{9}{1.6} = 5.625 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Source and Load Currents:**
$$I_s = D \cdot I_L = 0.6 \cdot 5.625 = 3.375 \text{ A}$$
$$I_o = (1 - D) \cdot I_L \cdot (-1) = 0.4 \cdot 5.625 = 2.25 \text{ A}$$

Verification: $P_{in} = V_s \cdot I_s = 15 \cdot 3.375 = 50.625$ W; $P_{out} = \vert V_o \vert \cdot I_o = 22.5 \cdot 2.25 = 50.625$ W. ✔ Power balance satisfied.

**(iv) Critical Inductance:**
$$L_c = \dfrac{R(1 - D)^2}{2f} = \dfrac{10 \cdot (0.4)^2}{2 \cdot 40{,}000} = \dfrac{1.6}{80{,}000} = 20 \text{ µH}$$

Since $L = 150$ µH $> L_c = 20$ µH, the converter operates in **CCM**. ✔

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Read Carefully Before Exam):**
>
> 1. **Missing the negative sign in buck-boost output voltage** (-2 to -3 marks). The expression $V_o = -\dfrac{D V_s}{1 - D}$ must carry the negative sign; writing $V_o = +\dfrac{D V_s}{1 - D}$ is treated as a *conceptual error*, not a typo.
>
> 2. **Confusing the boost and buck-boost transfer ratios.** Boost is $\dfrac{1}{1-D}$, buck-boost is $\dfrac{D}{1-D}$. At $D = 0.5$, boost gives $M = 2$ and buck-boost gives $M = 1$. Many students mix these up under exam pressure.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to verify the mode of operation.** KTU examiners often award 1–2 marks for explicitly stating whether the converter is in CCM or DCM and showing the inequality $I_L > \Delta I_L / 2$.
>
> 4. **Wrong units in numerical problems.** Always write units (V, A, µH, µF) in the final answer. A correct number without units is penalized (-0.5 mark).
>
> 5. **Using $V_s$ and $V_o$ interchangeably** in inductor voltage expressions. They are *different* during the OFF state of the boost converter; the inductor sees $V_s - V_o$, not $V_s$.
>
> 6. **Not sketching the inductor current waveform** in descriptive answers. A labeled waveform showing $I_{L,max}$ and $I_{L,min}$ earns easy 1–2 marks and demonstrates understanding of CCM vs DCM.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Boost Converter — Key Takeaways
- **Transfer function in CCM:** $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D}$; output is *always greater* than input.
- **Voltage gain** ranges from 1 (at $D = 0$) to $\infty$ (as $D \to 1$).
- **Output polarity** is the *same* as input.
- **Switch stress:** The switch must block the full output voltage $V_o$ when OFF.
- **Diode** must carry the average load current when ON.
- **Critical inductance** $L_c = \dfrac{R(1-D)^2}{2f}$; design with $L \ge 1.25 L_c$ for safe CCM operation.
- **Output voltage ripple** $\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{D}{RCf}$; choose $C$ to keep ripple below 1–5%.
- **Inductor current ripple** $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s D}{fL}$; typical design has $\Delta I_L = 20\%\text{--}40\%$ of $I_L$.

### 🔄 Buck-Boost Converter — Key Takeaways
- **Transfer function in CCM:** $V_o = -\dfrac{D V_s}{1 - D}$; output is *inverted* in polarity.
- **Voltage gain magnitude** $\vert M \vert = \dfrac{D}{1-D}$; equals 1 at $D = 0.5$ (unity gain point).
- **Step-down mode:** $D < 0.5$; **Step-up mode:** $D > 0.5$.
- **Source current is discontinuous** (pulsed during ON time only) — this can cause EMI issues.
- **Critical inductance** $L_c = \dfrac{R(1-D)^2}{2f}$ — *same form as boost, but with different $I_L$*.
- **The negative sign is sacred** — never omit it.
- **Used in battery management** where input can be above or below desired output.

### 📚 Universal KTU 2024 Concepts to Memorize
- ✅ **Volt-second balance** → derives output voltage transfer ratio.
- ✅ **Amp-second balance** → derives average inductor and source currents.
- ✅ **Boundary condition** $\Delta I_L = 2 I_L$ → defines CCM/DCM threshold.
- ✅ **Steady-state assumption** → all voltages and currents are periodic with the switching period.
- ✅ **Switch is ideal** in first-order analysis (no voltage drop, no switching losses).
- ✅ **Continuous input current** in boost; **discontinuous input current** in buck-boost.
- ✅ **Power balance** $P_{in} = P_{out}$ can be used to verify numerical answers.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, converters are usually tested as a 14-mark Part B question combining a 7-mark *descriptive/derivation* part and a 7-mark *numerical* part. Practice at least 3–4 numerical problems from previous KTU papers (Dec 2022, May 2023, Dec 2023, May 2024) to internalize the standard problem format and the marks-to-steps ratio.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Phase Half-Wave and Full-Wave Controlled Rectifier with R Load - Waveforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - Power Electronics
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Three Phase Half-Wave and Full-Wave Controlled Rectifier with R Load - Waveforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Phase Controlled Rectifier:** A power electronic converter that uses **thyristors (SCRs)** to convert a fixed-magnitude, fixed-frequency three-phase AC supply into a variable DC output voltage. The magnitude of the DC output is controlled by adjusting the **firing angle (α)**, which is the delay angle between the natural commutation point and the actual gate pulse to the thyristor.

There are two principal configurations studied in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Three-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier (3-Pulse Converter):** Uses **3 thyristors**, one connected to each phase. Output pulses repeat 3 times per AC cycle.
2. **Three-Phase Full-Wave Controlled Rectifier (6-Pulse Converter):** Uses **6 thyristors** in a bridge configuration. Output pulses repeat 6 times per AC cycle, producing smoother DC.

For an **R-load (purely resistive)**, the output voltage and current waveforms are in phase, and conduction ceases at the zero-crossing of the phase voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Parameter — Firing Angle (α):** The delay in electrical degrees after the natural commutation instant at which the gate pulse is applied. **Range for continuous R-load operation: 0° ≤ α ≤ 90°**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a three-phase supply as a relay race with three runners (R, Y, B) running on a circular track. Each runner is "fastest" (highest voltage) for a stretch of **120°**. In an uncontrolled diode rectifier, runners take over the baton (conduct) the moment they become fastest. 

In a **controlled rectifier**, we install a **gate at the start of each runner's stretch**. We can delay the runner's start by an angle **α**. The further we delay, the smaller the average voltage the load receives. 

- **Three-Phase Half-Wave:** Only one runner carries the baton at a time (3 runners, 3 handovers per cycle).
- **Three-Phase Full-Wave:** The track has **two lanes** — when one runner is finishing on the positive lane, another starts on the negative lane, giving **6 handovers** per cycle (smoother flow, less ripple).

The standard **mains frequency in India is 50 Hz**, so a 3-pulse converter gives **150 pulses/sec** and a 6-pulse converter gives **300 pulses/sec**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three-Phase Half-Wave (3-Pulse) Controlled Rectifier with R-Load

**Circuit Configuration:**
- Supply: 3-phase, 4-wire system, phase voltages $v_{an}, v_{bn}, v_{cn}$.
- Thyristors: $T_1$ (R-phase), $T_2$ (Y-phase), $T_3$ (B-phase), with cathodes tied together to the load.
- Load: Pure resistance $R$ connected between common cathode and neutral.

**Natural Commutation Points (Uncontrolled Firing):**
- $T_1$ would conduct naturally from **30° to 150°** (R-phase positive).
- $T_2$ would conduct naturally from **150° to 270°** (Y-phase positive).
- $T_3$ would conduct naturally from **270° to 390°** (B-phase positive).

**Operation with Firing Angle α:**
- $T_1$ is fired at $\omega t = 30° + \alpha$.
- $T_2$ is fired at $\omega t = 150° + \alpha$.
- $T_3$ is fired at $\omega t = 270° + \alpha$.
- Each thyristor conducts for **120°** once triggered (until the next phase becomes more positive).
- Conduction interval of $T_1$: from $30° + \alpha$ to $150° + \alpha$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why conduction stops at 150°+α for T₁?** Because at $\omega t = 150° + \alpha$, phase B voltage $v_{bn}$ becomes greater than phase R voltage $v_{an}$, forward-biasing $T_3$ (which was triggered earlier or simultaneously) and reverse-biasing $T_1$. Natural commutation occurs.

**Waveform Description (R-Load):**
- Output voltage $v_o$ follows the respective phase voltage during each 120° conduction.
- Load current $i_o = v_o / R$ — same shape as $v_o$ for R-load.
- Each thyristor current is a 120° segment of the sinusoidal phase voltage divided by R.
- Maximum firing angle for R-load: **α = 90°** (output average becomes zero at α = 90° for purely R load, and discontinuous conduction begins for α > 30°... wait — for R-load with 3-phase half-wave, conduction remains continuous up to **α = 30°** only; for α > 30° the conduction becomes discontinuous. Let me correct this in the KTU notes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Correction (KTU Examiner Pattern):** For a **3-pulse half-wave rectifier with R-load**, the boundary between continuous and discontinuous conduction is at **α = 30°**, not 90°. For α > 30°, a thyristor may not receive sufficient forward voltage to turn ON (because the previous thyristor has already commutated at the natural crossover of the phase voltages). This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

### 2.2 Three-Phase Full-Wave (6-Pulse) Controlled Rectifier with R-Load

**Circuit Configuration:**
- Six thyristors arranged in a bridge: $T_1, T_3, T_5$ (positive group, anodes to phases R, Y, B; cathodes tied to positive DC terminal) and $T_4, T_6, T_2$ (negative group, cathodes to phases R, Y, B; anodes tied to negative DC terminal).
- Load: $R$ connected between + and − DC terminals.

**Conduction Sequence (60° per thyristor):**
$T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_4 \rightarrow T_5 \rightarrow T_6 \rightarrow T_1$

Each thyristor conducts for **60°**, but **two thyristors conduct at any instant** (one from positive group, one from negative group). The output voltage is the line-to-line voltage of the two conducting phases.

**Conduction Pair Mapping:**
- $T_1$–$T_6$ → output = $v_{ab}$ (R-Y line voltage, positive).
- $T_2$–$T_1$ wait, let me rewrite correctly. Standard 6-pulse sequence with R-load:

| Interval (degrees) | Conducting Pair | Output Voltage |
|---|---|---|
| 30° + α to 90° + α | $T_1, T_6$ | $v_{ab}$ |
| 90° + α to 150° + α | $T_1, T_2$ | $v_{ac}$ |
| 150° + α to 210° + α | $T_3, T_2$ | $v_{bc}$ |
| 210° + α to 270° + α | $T_3, T_4$ | $v_{ba}$ |
| 270° + α to 330° + α | $T_5, T_4$ | $v_{ca}$ |
| 330° + α to 390° + α | $T_5, T_6$ | $v_{cb}$ |

**Range of α for R-Load continuous conduction:** **0° ≤ α ≤ 60°**. For α > 60°, conduction becomes discontinuous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why α ≤ 60° for R-load 6-pulse?** At α = 60°, the conduction ends exactly at the natural zero-crossing of the relevant line voltage. If α > 60°, the line voltage would have already crossed zero and become negative before the next firing pulse, causing discontinuous conduction.

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Three-Phase Half-Wave (3-pulse) | Three-Phase Full-Wave (6-pulse) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pulses per cycle | **3** | **6** |
| 2 | Thyristor conduction angle | **120°** | **60°** |
| 3 | Average output voltage $V_{dc}$ | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{2\pi} \cos\alpha$ | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$ |
| 4 | $V_{dc}$ in terms of RMS phase voltage $V_{ph}$ | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{6}\, V_{ph}}{2\pi} \cos\alpha$ | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{6}\, V_{ph}}{\pi} \cos\alpha$ |
| 5 | RMS output voltage $V_{rms}$ | $V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left[\dfrac{\pi}{3} + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\cos 2\alpha\right]^{1/2}}$ | $V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[\dfrac{\pi}{6} + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\cos 2\alpha\right]^{1/2}}$ |
| 6 | Maximum average voltage (α = 0) | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{2\pi} \approx 0.827\, V_m$ | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi} \approx 1.654\, V_m$ |
| 7 | Continuous conduction limit (R-load) | $\alpha \leq 30°$ | $\alpha \leq 60°$ |
| 8 | Firing sequence | $T_1, T_2, T_3$ (every 120°) | $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, T_5, T_6$ (every 60°) |
| 9 | Output frequency ripple (Hz) | $3 f_s = 150$ Hz | $6 f_s = 300$ Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In all formulas above:** $V_m$ = peak phase voltage, $V_{ph}$ = RMS phase voltage, $V_L$ = RMS line voltage, $f_s$ = supply frequency.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC motor drives** in industrial applications (rolling mills, paper machines, mine hoists) — speed control of large DC motors.
- **HVDC transmission** systems use 6-pulse and 12-pulse converters at both ends.
- **Battery charging** stations.
- **Electrochemical processes** (aluminum smelting, electroplating).
- **Magnet power supplies** in particle accelerators and MRI machines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of 3-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier (R-Load)

**Given:** Phase voltages referenced from neutral:
$$v_{an} = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$v_{bn} = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$
$$v_{cn} = V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$

During the conduction interval of $T_1$ (from $\omega t = 30° + \alpha$ to $150° + \alpha$), the output voltage equals $v_{an}$:
$$v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t), \quad \text{for } 30° + \alpha \leq \omega t \leq 150° + \alpha$$

By symmetry, the same average value is produced over each 120° interval. The average output voltage is:
$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi/3} \int_{30° + \alpha}^{150° + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3}{2\pi} \int_{30° + \alpha}^{150° + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

Performing the integration:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3 V_m}{2\pi} \left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{30° + \alpha}^{150° + \alpha}$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3 V_m}{2\pi} \left[-\cos(150° + \alpha) + \cos(30° + \alpha)\right]$$

Applying the cosine addition identities. We know:
- $\cos(A + B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$
- $\cos(150° + \alpha) = \cos 150° \cos\alpha - \sin 150° \sin\alpha = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha - \frac{1}{2}\sin\alpha$
- $\cos(30° + \alpha) = \cos 30° \cos\alpha - \sin 30° \sin\alpha = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha - \frac{1}{2}\sin\alpha$

Subtracting:
$$-\cos(150° + \alpha) + \cos(30° + \alpha) = \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha + \frac{1}{2}\sin\alpha\right) + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha - \frac{1}{2}\sin\alpha\right)$$

$$= \sqrt{3}\cos\alpha$$

Therefore:
$$V_{dc} = \frac{3 V_m}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{3}\cos\alpha = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha$$

In terms of RMS phase voltage $V_{ph} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$:
$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{2}\, V_{ph}}{2\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{3\sqrt{6}\, V_{ph}}{2\pi}\cos\alpha$$

At $\alpha = 0$ (maximum):
$$V_{dc,\max} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \cdot V_m}{2\pi} \approx 0.827\, V_m$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of 3-Phase Full-Wave Controlled Rectifier (R-Load)

The output voltage follows the **line-to-line voltage** during each 60° conduction interval. For the interval where $T_1$ and $T_6$ conduct (30°+α to 90°+α):
$$v_o = v_{ab} = \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin(\omega t + 30°)$$

> Note: Line-to-line peak voltage is $\sqrt{3}\, V_m$, and it leads the phase voltage by 30°.

Average output voltage (each 60° interval contributes equally):
$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{30° + \alpha}^{90° + \alpha} \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin(\omega t + 30°)\, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi} \int_{30° + \alpha}^{90° + \alpha} \sin(\omega t + 30°)\, d(\omega t)$$

Performing the integration:
$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi} \left[-\cos(\omega t + 30°)\right]_{30° + \alpha}^{90° + \alpha}$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi} \left[-\cos(120° + \alpha) + \cos(60° + \alpha)\right]$$

Apply identities:
- $\cos(120° + \alpha) = -\frac{1}{2}\cos\alpha - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\alpha$
- $\cos(60° + \alpha) = \frac{1}{2}\cos\alpha - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\alpha$

Adding the two:
$$-\cos(120° + \alpha) + \cos(60° + \alpha) = \left(\frac{1}{2}\cos\alpha + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\alpha\right) + \left(\frac{1}{2}\cos\alpha - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\alpha\right) = \cos\alpha$$

Therefore:
$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

In terms of RMS phase voltage:
$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{6}\, V_{ph}}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

In terms of RMS line voltage $V_L = \sqrt{3}\, V_{ph}$:
$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\, V_L}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

At $\alpha = 0$:
$$V_{dc,\max} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi} \approx 1.654\, V_m$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2023 pattern]:**
A 3-phase, full-wave controlled rectifier is supplied from a 415 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase AC source. The load is purely resistive with $R = 10\,\Omega$. If the firing angle is $\alpha = 30°$, calculate:
(i) Average output voltage.
(ii) Average load current.
(iii) RMS output voltage.
(iv) Output power delivered to the load.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify quantities:**
- Line voltage $V_L = 415$ V, so $V_{ph} = 415 / \sqrt{3} \approx 239.6$ V (RMS)
- Peak phase voltage: $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot 239.6 \approx 338.86$ V
- Peak line voltage: $V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot 338.86 \approx 586.9$ V

**Step 2 — Average output voltage (α = 30°):**
$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\cos 30° = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \cdot 338.86}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$
$$V_{dc} = \frac{3 \cdot 3 \cdot 338.86}{2\pi} = \frac{3049.74}{6.2832} \approx 485.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Average load current:**
$$I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R} = \frac{485.4}{10} = 48.54 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — RMS output voltage (using 6-pulse formula):**
$$V_{rms} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\cos 60°\right]^{1/2}}$$

Wait — let me carefully reapply the standard 6-pulse RMS formula for R-load. The classical formula is:
$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{\pi}\int_{30° + \alpha}^{90° + \alpha} (\sqrt{3} V_m \sin\theta)^2\, d\theta}$$

Computing for α = 30°:
$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{9 V_m^2}{\pi}\int_{60°}^{120°} \sin^2\theta\, d\theta}$$

$$\int_{60°}^{120°} \sin^2\theta\, d\theta = \int_{60°}^{120°} \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2} d\theta = \frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{8}$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{9 V_m^2}{\pi}\left(\frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{8}\right)} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{3}{2} + \frac{9\sqrt{3}}{8\pi}}$$

Substituting $V_m = 338.86$ V:
$$V_{rms} = 338.86 \cdot \sqrt{1.5 + 0.6204} = 338.86 \cdot \sqrt{2.1204} \approx 338.86 \cdot 1.4562 \approx 493.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Output power:**
$$P = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R} = \frac{(493.5)^2}{10} \approx 24,354 \text{ W} = 24.35 \text{ kW}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | 3-Phase Half-Wave (3-pulse) | 3-Phase Full-Wave (6-pulse) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of thyristors | 3 | 6 |
| Pulse rectifier transformer needed? | **Yes** (to handle DC component) | **No** (bridge isolates DC) |
| Average voltage magnitude (α = 0) | $0.827\, V_m$ | $1.654\, V_m$ |
| Ripple frequency | $3 f_s$ | $6 f_s$ |
| Filter size required | Larger | Smaller |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Utilization factor (transformer) | Lower (0.675) | Higher (0.955) |
| DC current path goes through | 1 thyristor | 2 thyristors in series |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Phase Half-Wave (3-Pulse) Controlled Rectifier — Circuit Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    src1[3-Phase Supply R Phase van] --> T1[Thyristor T1]
    src2[3-Phase Supply Y Phase vbn] --> T2[Thyristor T2]
    src3[3-Phase Supply B Phase vcn] --> T3[Thyristor T3]
    T1 --> node1[Common Cathode Node]
    T2 --> node1
    T3 --> node1
    node1 --> RLOAD[Resistive Load R]
    RLOAD --> NEUTRAL[Neutral N]
    GP[Gate Pulse Generator firing at alpha] -.fires.-> T1
    GP -.fires.-> T2
    GP -.fires.-> T3
```

### 4.2 Three-Phase Full-Wave (6-Pulse) Controlled Rectifier — Circuit Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    RA[Phase R] --> T1A[T1 Positive Group]
    YA[Phase Y] --> T3A[T3 Positive Group]
    BA[Phase B] --> T5A[T5 Positive Group]
    T1A --> PDCP[Positive DC Bus P]
    T3A --> PDCP
    T5A --> PDCP
    RA --> T4B[T4 Negative Group]
    YA --> T6B[T6 Negative Group]
    BA --> T2B[T2 Negative Group]
    T4B --> NDCN[Negative DC Bus N]
    T6B --> NDCN
    T2B --> NDCN
    PDCP --> RLOADF[Resistive Load R]
    RLOADF --> NDCN
    SEQA[Firing Sequence T1 T2 T3 T4 T5 T6 at 60 deg interval] -.-> T1A
    SEQA -.-> T3A
    SEQA -.-> T5A
    SEQA -.-> T4B
    SEQA -.-> T6B
    SEQA -.-> T2B
```

### 4.3 Waveform Sequence — 3-Phase Half-Wave with R-Load (α = 30°)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[van R Phase 0 to 360 deg] --> A2[Thyristor T1 conducts 60 to 180 deg output follows van]
    A2 --> A3[Thyristor T2 conducts 180 to 300 deg output follows vbn]
    A3 --> A4[Thyristor T3 conducts 300 to 420 deg output follows vcn]
    A4 --> A5[Output Voltage vo consists of 3 humps per cycle average Vdc]
    A5 --> A6[Output Current io equals vo divided by R pure R load]
    A6 --> A7[Thyristor current iT1 is 120 deg segment of vo divided by R]
```

### 4.4 Waveform Sequence — 3-Phase Full-Wave with R-Load (α = 30°)

```mermaid
graph LR
    B1[Line Voltages vab vbc vca 6 segments] --> B2[Thyristors fire at 30 plus alpha 90 plus alpha 150 plus alpha 210 plus alpha 270 plus alpha 330 plus alpha]
    B2 --> B3[Output vo shows 6 positive humps per cycle smoother DC]
    B3 --> B4[Output current io in phase with vo for R load]
    B4 --> B5[Each thyristor conducts for 60 deg consecutive pair forms output]
    B5 --> B6[Ripple frequency 300 Hz for 50 Hz supply]
```

### 4.5 Functional Architecture of Firing Circuit

```mermaid
graph TB
    SYNC[Synchronization from 3 Phase Supply] --> ZCD[Zero Crossing Detector]
    ZCD --> REF[Ramp Reference Generator]
    REF --> CMP[Comparator with DC Control Voltage Vc]
    DCV[DC Control Voltage Vc] --> CMP
    CMP --> PULSE[Gate Pulse Train at alpha]
    PULSE --> ISO[Pulse Transformer Isolation]
    ISO --> GATE1[Gate of T1]
    ISO --> GATE2[Gate of T2]
    ISO --> GATE3[Gate of T3]
    ISO --> GATE4[Gate of T4]
    ISO --> GATE5[Gate of T5]
    ISO --> GATE6[Gate of T6]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Description of waveforms (textual representation since Mermaid cannot render true waveforms):**
>
> **3-Phase Half-Wave R-Load at α = 30°:**
> - Output voltage $v_o$ = segments of $v_{an}$ (60° to 180°), $v_{bn}$ (180° to 300°), $v_{cn}$ (300° to 420°), each spanning 120°.
> - Each segment starts at the natural commutation point + α and ends at the next natural commutation point + α.
> - Output current $i_o$ has the same shape (scaled by 1/R).
>
> **3-Phase Full-Wave R-Load at α = 30°:**
> - Output voltage $v_o$ = segments of line voltages $v_{ab}, v_{ac}, v_{bc}, v_{ba}, v_{ca}, v_{cb}$, each spanning 60°.
> - Six positive humps per cycle, ripple frequency = 300 Hz.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define firing angle in a 3-phase controlled rectifier. Why is the maximum firing angle for continuous conduction in a 3-phase half-wave rectifier with R-load only 30°?

**Model Answer:**

The **firing angle (α)** is the delay (in electrical degrees) between the **natural commutation point** (where an incoming phase voltage becomes greater than the outgoing phase voltage) and the instant at which the gate pulse is applied to the incoming thyristor.

For a 3-phase half-wave rectifier with R-load, the boundary of continuous conduction is **α = 30°**. This is because at α > 30°, the next phase voltage becomes greater than the presently conducting phase voltage **before** the incoming thyristor is fired. Consequently, when the gate pulse arrives, the incoming thyristor is reverse-biased and cannot turn ON, leading to discontinuous conduction and a discontinuous output waveform.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List any three differences between a 3-phase half-wave and a 3-phase full-wave controlled rectifier with R-load.

**Model Answer:**
