> 6. **Not labeling axes** (time, voltage) on waveform diagrams. **[-1 mark]**
> 7. **Mixing up $m_a = A_m / A_c$** with $m_f = f_c / f_m$ in numerical substitutions. **[-2 marks]**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Carrier-Based PWM Inverters (Module 3)**

**Core Definitions**
- ☐ $m_a = A_m / A_c$ (amplitude modulation index)
- ☐ $m_f = f_c / f_m$ (frequency modulation ratio)
- ☐ Linear region: $0 \le m_a \le 1$
- ☐ Synchronous PWM: $m_f$ is an odd integer (typically 15, 21, 27, …)

**Bipolar PWM — Key Facts**
- ☐ Single comparator, single reference, single carrier
- ☐ Line voltage: **2 levels** ($+V_{dc},\ -V_{dc}$)
- ☐ $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = m_a V_{dc}$ in linear region
- ☐ Dominant harmonic order: $m_f$
- ☐ Sidebands at $m_f \pm 2k$ and $2m_f \pm (2k-1)$

**Unipolar PWM — Key Facts**
- ☐ **Two** comparators, two references ($v_r$ and $-v_r$), one carrier
- ☐ Line voltage: **3 levels** ($+V_{dc},\ 0,\ -V_{dc}$)
- ☐ Same fundamental: $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = m_a V_{dc}$
- ☐ Dominant harmonic order: $\mathbf{2m_f}$ (carrier harmonics cancel in $v_{ab}$)
- ☐ **Half the filter size** of bipolar for same $m_f$

**Engineering Insights**
- ☐ Unipolar is **always preferred** for low-power, low-voltage applications (solar, UPS, drives) due to superior harmonic performance.
- ☐ Bipolar is used in **high-voltage** or **3-phase** systems where the simplicity of one carrier per phase is advantageous.
- ☐ Switching loss $\propto f_c$ — selecting $m_f$ involves a trade-off between harmonic quality and efficiency.
- ☐ **Square-wave limit:** $m_a \to \infty$ gives $V_{1,\text{peak}} = 4V_{dc}/\pi \approx 1.273 V_{dc}$.

**Numerical Problem Formula Kit (Memorize!)**
$$\boxed{V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = m_a V_{dc}}$$
$$\boxed{V_{ab,1,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}}$$
$$\boxed{h_{\text{dom,bipolar}} = m_f \quad ; \quad h_{\text{dom,unipolar}} = 2m_f}$$
$$\boxed{f_{\text{dom}} = h_{\text{dom}} \times f_m}$$

> **End of Notes — Carrier-Based Unipolar and Bipolar PWM Inverters (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Harmonics in Power Electronics** are sinusoidal components of a periodic non-sinusoidal waveform whose frequencies are integer multiples of the fundamental supply frequency. In power electronic converters (rectifiers, inverters, choppers, cycloconverters), the switched waveforms contain a fundamental component ($f_1$) superimposed with harmonics of order $n = 2, 3, 4, 5, \dots, \infty$, i.e., at frequencies $nf_1$.

For a periodic non-sinusoidal current or voltage satisfying **Dirichlet's conditions**, the Fourier series representation is:

$$x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega t) + b_n \sin(n\omega t) \right]$$

$$x(t) = X_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} X_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)$$

where $X_n$ is the RMS amplitude of the $n^{th}$ harmonic, and $\phi_n$ is its phase displacement relative to the fundamental.

### Key Quality Indices (KTU Standard Metrics)

- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** — Ratio of RMS harmonic content to the fundamental RMS value. **Unit: %**
- **Distortion Factor (DF)** — Ratio of fundamental RMS to total RMS.
- **Displacement Power Factor (DPF)** — $\cos(\phi_1)$ where $\phi_1$ is the phase shift of the fundamental.
- **Crest Factor (CF)** — Peak-to-RMS ratio of the waveform.
- **Form Factor (FF)** — RMS-to-average ratio of the waveform.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Whenever a power electronic converter is analyzed, the examiner expects (i) Fourier series derivation, (ii) RMS value of each harmonic, (iii) THD computation, and (iv) comment on the quality of the output waveform.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you strike a tuning fork producing a pure musical note at **440 Hz** (the "A" note). This single pure tone represents a **pure sinusoidal waveform** with **zero harmonics**.

Now, if you pluck a guitar string, the note you hear is a **mixture** of 440 Hz (the fundamental), 880 Hz, 1320 Hz, 1760 Hz, etc. (the 2nd, 3rd, 4th harmonics). The combination of these integer-multiple frequencies is what makes a guitar string sound "warm" or "rich".

**In power electronics, this is precisely what happens with switched converters:**
- An ideal sinusoidal AC source = "pure tuning fork tone" = **0% THD**
- A square wave inverter output = "guitar string tone" = **~48.34% THD**
- A SPWM inverter output = "synthesizer tone" = **3–8% THD** (much cleaner!)

### Real-World Mapping

| Musical Analogy | Power Electronics Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Pure tuning fork tone | Ideal sinusoidal mains (50/60 Hz) |
| Guitar string vibration | Square wave / quasi-square wave inverter |
| Synthesizer (filtered) | SPWM or multi-level inverter output |
| Removing overtones via EQ | Harmonic filters (passive / active) |

## 1.3 Why KTU Boards Test This Topic

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Perspective:** In real industrial systems (drives, UPS, SMPS, HVDC), harmonics cause overheating of transformers, malfunction of protection relays, capacitor bank failures, and communication interference. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests whether students can:
> 1. Quantify distortion numerically
> 2. Compute THD from a given Fourier series
> 3. Compare converter topologies on harmonic performance
> 4. Recommend elimination methods

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Sources of Harmonics in Power Electronic Systems

Harmonics are produced **only by non-linear loads or switching actions**. The dominant sources are:

- **Power electronic converters** — Rectifiers, inverters, choppers, cycloconverters
- **Saturable magnetic devices** — Transformers operating near saturation
- **Arc furnaces** — Non-linear V-I characteristics
- **Fluorescent lamps** (with magnetic ballasts) — Arc discharge non-linearity
- **Switched-mode power supplies (SMPS)** — High-frequency rectification

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1: Classify Harmonics (Symmetry Rules)

| Waveform Symmetry | Resulting Harmonics Present |
|---|---|
| **Odd symmetry** $f(-t) = -f(t)$ | Only **odd** harmonics |
| **Half-wave symmetry** $f(t+T/2) = -f(t)$ | Only **odd** harmonics, no DC |
| **Even symmetry** $f(-t) = f(t)$ | Only **cosine** (DC + even) harmonics |
| **Quarter-wave symmetry** | Only **odd sine** harmonics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** A square wave inverter output (180° conduction) has **odd-quarter-wave symmetry**, hence contains only odd sine harmonics: $V_n = \dfrac{4V_s}{n\pi}$ for $n = 1, 3, 5, 7, \dots$

### Step 2: Compute Total RMS

For a non-sinusoidal periodic signal:

$$X_{rms} = \sqrt{X_0^2 + X_1^2 + X_2^2 + X_3^2 + \cdots + X_n^2}$$

where $X_n$ is the RMS value of the $n^{th}$ harmonic. The DC component $X_0$ is zero for half-wave symmetric waveforms.

### Step 3: Apply Quality Indices

The three pillars of power-quality assessment:

$$\text{Distortion Factor (DF)} = \frac{X_1}{X_{rms}} = \frac{X_1}{\sqrt{X_1^2 + X_2^2 + X_3^2 + \cdots}}$$

$$\text{Displacement Power Factor (DPF)} = \cos(\phi_1)$$

$$\text{True Power Factor} = \text{DF} \times \text{DPF} = \frac{X_1 \cos(\phi_1)}{X_{rms}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DPF ≠ True PF** in non-linear systems. In linear systems they are equal; in power-electronic systems the true PF is **lower** because of harmonic distortion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fourier coefficient (sine) | $b_n = \dfrac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x(t)\sin(n\omega t)\, dt$ | V or A |
| 2 | Fourier coefficient (cosine) | $a_n = \dfrac{2}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x(t)\cos(n\omega t)\, dt$ | V or A |
| 3 | RMS of $n^{th}$ harmonic | $X_n = \sqrt{a_n^2 + b_n^2}/\sqrt{2}$ | V or A |
| 4 | Total RMS | $X_{rms} = \sqrt{X_0^2 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} X_n^2}$ | V or A |
| 5 | **THD** (Voltage) | $THD_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | **%** |
| 6 | **THD** (Current) | $THD_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_n^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | **%** |
| 7 | Distortion Factor | $DF = \dfrac{X_1}{X_{rms}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD^2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Displacement Power Factor | $DPF = \cos(\phi_1)$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **True Power Factor** | $PF_{true} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD^2}} \cdot \cos(\phi_1)$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Crest Factor | $CF = \dfrac{X_{peak}}{X_{rms}}$ | $\geq 1$ |
| 11 | Form Factor | $FF = \dfrac{X_{rms}}{X_{avg}}$ | $\geq 1$ |
| 12 | Square wave fundamental | $V_1 = \dfrac{4V_s}{\pi}$ | V |
| 13 | Square wave $n^{th}$ harmonic | $V_n = \dfrac{4V_s}{n\pi}$ | V |
| 14 | Quasi-square (chopping $\alpha$) | $V_n = \dfrac{4V_s}{n\pi} \cos\!\left(\dfrac{n\alpha}{2}\right)$ | V |
| 15 | Power in non-sinusoidal | $P = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n I_n \cos(\phi_n)$ | W |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Pitfall:** The vertical pipe `\vert` is used for absolute values in formulas (e.g., $\vert n\pi \vert$). The display version uses double dollar signs.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IEEE 519-2014 Standard compliance** — Limits THD in industrial distribution networks ($THD_V \leq 5\%$ for PCC at 69 kV and below)
- **Power quality auditing** — Utilities charge penalties for PF $< 0.85$
- **Transformer K-factor rating** — Specifies transformer capacity to handle harmonic heating
- **HVDC transmission** — Harmonic filters are mandatory on both converter ends
- **Adjustable Speed Drives (ASDs)** — Front-end active rectifiers replace diode bridges to meet IEEE 519

## 2.5 Effects of Harmonics — Engineering Consequences

| **Affected Equipment** | **Specific Effect** | **Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|
| Transformers | Overheating, humming, derating | Increased $I^2R$ losses + eddy-current + hysteresis losses |
| Induction motors | Pulsating torque, vibration, additional heat | Rotating fields at $\pm n\omega_s$ producing forward/backward torques |
| Power cables | Increased heating, skin effect dominant | Proximity effect at high frequencies |
| Capacitor banks | Premature failure, dielectric stress | Reactance $\propto 1/n$ → high currents at low-order harmonics |
| Protective relays | Mal-tripping, mis-coordination | Harmonic distortion changes CT/PT secondary waveforms |
| Communication lines | EMI, data corruption | Conductive and radiated noise coupling |
| Electronic loads | Malfunction, latch-up | Voltage zero-crossing detection errors |
| Neutral conductor | Overheating in 3-phase 4-wire | Triplen harmonics ($3^{rd}, 9^{th}, 15^{th}$) are zero-sequence → add in neutral |
| Synchronous machines | Rotor heating | Negative-sequence harmonic currents induce double-frequency rotor currents |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triplen Harmonics Special Case:** In a balanced 3-phase system, triplen harmonics (3, 9, 15, …) are **in phase** in all three phases, so they add arithmetically in the neutral conductor — neutral current can exceed phase current!

## 2.6 Harmonic Elimination Methods — Overview

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies elimination methods into **four broad families**:

1. **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) Techniques**
   - Single-pulse, multiple-pulse
   - Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)
   - Modified SPWM
   - Selective Harmonic Elimination PWM (SHE-PWM)
   - Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)

2. **Multi-Level Inverter Topologies**
   - Diode-Clamped (Neutral-Point Clamped — NPC)
   - Flying Capacitor
   - Cascaded H-Bridge (CHB)
   - Modular Multilevel Converter (MMC)

3. **Filtering Solutions**
   - Passive filters (LC, LCL, tuned)
   - Active Power Filters (APF) — Shunt, Series, Hybrid
   - Active Front-End Rectifiers (PWM rectifiers)

4. **Waveform Shaping / Magnetic Solutions**
   - Phase-shifting transformers (12-pulse, 18-pulse, 24-pulse rectifiers)
   - Zig-zag transformers
   - Inductors / reactors

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Fourier Series of a 180°-Conduction Square Wave Inverter Output

**Waveform Assumption:** Single-phase full-bridge VSI with DC link voltage $V_s$, output $v_o(t)$ switches between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$ for half-period each (180° conduction).

### Step 1: Set Up the Waveform Equation

$$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} +V_s, & 0 \leq t \leq T/2 \\ -V_s, & T/2 \leq t \leq T \end{cases}$$

The waveform has **odd-quarter-wave symmetry** → contains only **odd sine harmonics** with zero DC and zero cosine terms.

### Step 2: Compute the Sine Fourier Coefficient

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o(t) \sin(n\omega t)\, dt$$

### Step 3: Substitute Piecewise

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T/2} (+V_s)\sin(n\omega t)\, dt + \int_{T/2}^{T} (-V_s)\sin(n\omega t)\, dt \right]$$

### Step 4: Evaluate Each Integral

**First integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{T/2} V_s \sin(n\omega t)\, dt = V_s \left[ \frac{-\cos(n\omega t)}{n\omega} \right]_{0}^{T/2}$$

Since $\omega T = 2\pi$, we have $n\omega T/2 = n\pi$. So $\cos(n\pi) = (-1)^n$:

$$= \frac{V_s}{n\omega} \left[ -\cos(n\pi) + \cos(0) \right] = \frac{V_s}{n\omega} \left[ 1 - (-1)^n \right]$$

**Second integral:**

$$\int_{T/2}^{T} (-V_s)\sin(n\omega t)\, dt = -V_s \left[ \frac{-\cos(n\omega t)}{n\omega} \right]_{T/2}^{T}$$

$$= \frac{V_s}{n\omega} \left[ \cos(n\pi \cdot 2) - \cos(n\pi) \right] = \frac{V_s}{n\omega} \left[ (-1)^{2n} - (-1)^n \right]$$

$$= \frac{V_s}{n\omega} \left[ 1 - (-1)^n \right]$$

### Step 5: Sum the Two Integrals

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{2V_s}{n\omega} \left[ 1 - (-1)^n \right] = \frac{4V_s}{Tn\omega} \left[ 1 - (-1)^n \right]$$

Since $\omega = 2\pi/T$:

$$b_n = \frac{4V_s}{Tn \cdot (2\pi/T)} \left[ 1 - (-1)^n \right] = \frac{2V_s}{n\pi} \left[ 1 - (-1)^n \right]$$

### Step 6: Simplify Using the Parity Selector

The term $\left[ 1 - (-1)^n \right]$ acts as a selector:
- If $n$ is **even**: $(-1)^n = +1 \Rightarrow 1 - 1 = 0$ (no even harmonics)
- If $n$ is **odd**: $(-1)^n = -1 \Rightarrow 1 - (-1) = 2$

Therefore:

$$b_n = \begin{cases} \dfrac{4V_s}{n\pi}, & n = 1, 3, 5, 7, \dots \\ 0, & n = 2, 4, 6, 8, \dots \end{cases}$$

### Step 7: Final Waveform Expression

$$\boxed{v_o(t) = \frac{4V_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n\omega t)}$$

The peak amplitude of the $n^{th}$ harmonic is:

$$V_n = \frac{4V_s}{n\pi}$$

The RMS of the $n^{th}$ harmonic is:

$$V_{n,rms} = \frac{V_n}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{n\pi}$$

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Total Harmonic Distortion of a Square Wave

### Step 1: RMS of Fundamental

$$V_{1,rms} = \frac{4V_s}{\pi\sqrt{2}}$$

### Step 2: Sum of Square of All Harmonics (Total RMS)

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^2}$$

But for a square wave, $V_{rms} = V_s$ (the output alternates between $\pm V_s$):

$$V_s = \frac{4V_s}{\pi\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$V_s^2 = \frac{8V_s^2}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2}$$

$$\sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{\pi^2}{8}$$

(This identity confirms the result from the Fourier series.)

### Step 3: Total Harmonic Distortion Definition

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=3,5,7,\dots}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}}$$

Squaring:

$$THD^2 = \frac{\sum_{n=3,5,7,\dots}^{\infty} V_{n,rms}^2}{V_{1,rms}^2} = \frac{\sum_{n=3,5,7,\dots}^{\infty} \left(\frac{4V_s}{n\pi}\right)^2}{\left(\frac{4V_s}{\pi}\right)^2}$$

$$THD^2 = \sum_{n=3,5,7,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{25} + \frac{1}{49} + \frac{1}{81} + \cdots$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Series

$$THD^2 = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} - 1 = \frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1$$

$$THD^2 = \frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1 = \frac{9.8696}{8} - 1 = 1.2337 - 1 = 0.2337$$

### Step 5: Final THD of Square Wave

$$\boxed{THD_{square} = \sqrt{0.2337} = 0.4834 = 48.34\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** A pure square wave has a THD of **48.34%**. This is the worst-case baseline; any PWM technique must beat this to justify its use.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Quasi-Square Wave (180° ± α/2 Chopping)

### Concept

To eliminate certain harmonics, the square wave is "notched" symmetrically by an angle $\alpha$ around the $\pi$ and $2\pi$ transitions. The output stays at $+V_s$ for $(180° - \alpha)$ and at $-V_s$ for $(180° - \alpha)$, with brief reversals of width $\alpha$.

### Fourier Coefficient (Peak of $n^{th}$ Harmonic)

$$V_n = \frac{4V_s}{n\pi} \cos\!\left(\frac{n\alpha}{2}\right), \quad n = 1, 3, 5, \dots$$

### Step 1: Select $\alpha$ to Eliminate 3rd Harmonic

Setting $V_3 = 0$:

$$\cos\!\left(\frac{3\alpha}{2}\right) = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{3\alpha}{2} = \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{\pi}{3} = 60°$$

### Step 2: With $\alpha = 60°$, Compute Other Harmonics

$$V_1 = \frac{4V_s}{\pi} \cos(30°) = \frac{4V_s}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}\,V_s}{\pi} \approx 1.1026\,V_s$$

$$V_5 = \frac{4V_s}{5\pi} \cos(150°) = \frac{4V_s}{5\pi} \cdot \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = -\frac{2\sqrt{3}\,V_s}{5\pi}$$

The amplitude $\vert V_5 \vert \approx 0.2205\,V_s$

$$V_7 = \frac{4V_s}{7\pi} \cos(210°) = \frac{4V_s}{7\pi} \cdot \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \approx 0.1575\,V_s$$

### Step 3: Compute THD

$$THD^2 = \frac{V_5^2 + V_7^2 + V_9^2 + \cdots}{V_1^2} = \sum_{\substack{n=5,7,11,13,\dots}} \cos^2\!\left(\frac{n\alpha}{2}\right) / n^2$$

Numerically: $THD \approx 31.08\%$ — better than the unchopped square wave, but still high. This is the basis for **Selective Harmonic Elimination (SHE-PWM)**.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Selective Harmonic Elimination PWM (SHE-PWM)

### Concept

A staircase waveform with $N$ switching angles per quarter cycle ($\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \dots, \alpha_N$) can be designed to:
1. Match the fundamental amplitude to a target value (modulation index control)
2. Eliminate $N-1$ specific low-order harmonics (typically 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th, …)

### General Equation (Quarter-Wave Symmetric Output)

The Fourier coefficient of the $n^{th}$ harmonic is:

$$V_n = \frac{4V_{dc}}{n\pi} \left[ 1 + 2\sum_{k=1}^{N} (-1)^k \cos(n\alpha_k) \right]$$

### Constraint Equations

For fundamental matching:

$$\frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi} \left[ 1 + 2\sum_{k=1}^{N} (-1)^k \cos(\alpha_k) \right] = M \cdot V_{dc}$$

For elimination of harmonic order $n$:

$$1 + 2\sum_{k=1}^{N} (-1)^k \cos(n\alpha_k) = 0$$

### Worked Example: N = 3 (Eliminate 5th and 7th Harmonics)

Three equations:

$$\cos(\alpha_1) - \cos(\alpha_2) + \cos(\alpha_3) = \frac{M\pi}{4} - \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{(fundamental)}$$

$$\cos(5\alpha_1) - \cos(5\alpha_2) + \cos(5\alpha_3) = -\frac{1}{2} \quad \text{(5th harmonic)}$$

$$\cos(7\alpha_1) - \cos(7\alpha_2) + \cos(7\alpha_3) = -\frac{1}{2} \quad \text{(7th harmonic)}$$

### Numerical Solution (for M = 1.0)

Solving the transcendental system (typically using Newton-Raphson iteration):

$$\alpha_1 = 18.17°, \quad \alpha_2 = 30.94°, \quad \alpha_3 = 56.06°$$

The remaining dominant harmonic becomes the **11th**, and $THD \approx 7.0\%$ — a significant improvement!

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** Students are often asked to *formulate* the SHE-PWM equations rather than solve them numerically. Focus on setting up the constraint equations correctly.

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5: True Power Factor with Harmonics

### Step 1: Total Power Delivered

In a non-sinusoidal system, **only same-frequency components deliver real power**. The total real power is:

$$P = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n I_n \cos(\phi_n)$$

where $\phi_n$ is the phase angle between $V_n$ and $I_n$ at harmonic $n$.

### Step 2: Apparent Power

$$S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$$

### Step 3: True Power Factor

$$PF = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n I_n \cos(\phi_n)}{\sqrt{\sum_{n} V_n^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{n} I_n^2}}$$

### Step 4: Linear-Load Special Case (with pure voltage source)

If the voltage source is purely sinusoidal but the load is non-linear:

$$V_{rms} = V_1, \quad V_n = 0 \text{ for } n \geq 2$$

$$PF = \frac{V_1 I_1 \cos(\phi_1)}{V_1 I_{rms}} = \frac{I_1}{I_{rms}} \cdot \cos(\phi_1) = DF \cdot DPF$$

### Step 5: Express in Terms of THD

$$DF = \frac{I_1}{I_{rms}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD^2}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{PF_{true} = \frac{\cos(\phi_1)}{\sqrt{1 + THD^2}}}$$

### Example

A load has $DPF = 0.95$ and $THD = 30\%$:

$$PF_{true} = \frac{0.95}{\sqrt{1 + 0.09}} = \frac{0.95}{\sqrt{1.09}} = \frac{0.95}{1.044} = 0.910$$

The PF has degraded by ~4% purely due to distortion — a hidden cost!

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison (KTU 14-Mark Favourite)

| **Method** | **Lowest Order Harmonic** | **Typical THD** | **Switching Frequency** | **Complexity** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Square wave (180°) | 3rd | 48.34% | Fundamental | Very low | High-power, low-frequency |
| Quasi-square (60° chop) | 5th | 31.08% | Fundamental | Low | Medium-power industrial |
| Multiple-pulse PWM (m=5) | 5th | ~24% | $5f_1$ | Medium | UPS systems |
| Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) | Carrier freq. ± 2×modulating | 5–10% | $1\text{–}20\,\text{kHz}$ | Medium | General-purpose drives |
| Modified SPWM (60° clamp) | Carrier sideband | 3–5% | $1\text{–}20\,\text{kHz}$ | Medium | Industrial drives |
| **SHE-PWM (N=3)** | **11th** | **~7%** | Fundamental | High (iterative solve) | High-power traction |
| SVPWM | Carrier sideband | 3.5–5% | $1\text{–}20\,\text{kHz}$ | High (vector calc.) | Modern VFDs |
| 3-level NPC | Carrier sideband | 1.5–3% | $1\text{–}5\,\text{kHz}$ | High | HVDC, large drives |
| 5-level CHB | Carrier sideband | 0.8–1.5% | $1\text{–}2\,\text{kHz}$ | Very high | STATCOM, grid-tie |
| 12-pulse rectifier | 11th | 4–5% | Mains freq. | High (phase-shift xfmr) | HVDC, electrolysis |
| Active Power Filter | Selected order | < 3% | $10\text{–}50\,\text{kHz}$ | Very high | Power-quality correction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** The 14-mark question often requires a **comparative table** like the one above with **numerical THD values** for each method. Memorize the THD ranges!

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Harmonic Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This diagram maps the **causal chain** of harmonic injection, propagation, and mitigation in a typical industrial power system. All node IDs follow the alphanumeric prefix rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src1[SUPPLY GRID 50 HZ SINUSOIDAL] --> src2[NON LINEAR LOADS]
    src2 --> src3[POWER CONVERTERS]
    src3 --> src4[SWITCHING ACTION AT HIGH FREQUENCY]
    src4 --> out1[HARMONIC INJECTION INTO GRID]
    out1 --> out2[VOLTAGE DISTORTION AT PCC]
    out1 --> out3[CURRENT DISTORTION IN FEEDERS]
    out2 --> eff1[TRANSFORMER OVERHEATING]
    out2 --> eff2[CAPACITOR BANK FAILURE]
    out3 --> eff3[CABLE OVERHEATING]
    out3 --> eff4[RELAY FALSE TRIPPING]
    out3 --> eff5[NEUTRAL CONDUCTOR OVERLOAD]
    eff1 --> mon1[POWER QUALITY MONITOR]
    eff2 --> mon1
    eff3 --> mon1
    eff4 --> mon1
    eff5 --> mon1
    mon1 --> act1[CONTROL SIGNAL]
    act1 --> elim1[SHUNT ACTIVE POWER FILTER]
    act1 --> elim2[SERIES ACTIVE POWER FILTER]
    act1 --> elim3[HYBRID APF]
    act1 --> elim4[PASSIVE TUNED FILTER]
    act1 --> elim5[PWM CONVERTER REPLACEMENT]
    elim1 --> clean1[CLEAN SINUSOIDAL SUPPLY]
    elim2 --> clean1
    elim3 --> clean1
    elim4 --> clean1
    elim5 --> clean1
    clean1 --> load1[SENSITIVE LINEAR LOADS]
```

## 4.2 Harmonic Elimination Strategy — Decision Tree

This flowchart assists students in choosing the appropriate harmonic elimination technique based on application constraints. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text per the Mermaid rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[START CHOOSE HARMONIC MITIGATION] --> q1[POWER LEVEL]
    q1 -- LESS THAN 10 KVA --> path1[LOW POWER BRANCH]
    q1 -- 10 KVA TO 100 KVA --> path2[MEDIUM POWER BRANCH]
    q1 -- GREATER THAN 100 KVA --> path3[HIGH POWER BRANCH]
    path1 --> rec1[SINUSOIDAL PWM VSI]
    path1 --> rec2[SPACE VECTOR PWM]
    rec1 --> rec1a[THD 5 TO 10 PERCENT]
    rec2 --> rec2a[THD 3 TO 5 PERCENT]
    path2 --> rec3[MODIFIED SPWM]
    path2 --> rec4[THREE LEVEL NPC INVERTER]
    path2 --> rec5[SHE PWM]
    rec3 --> rec3a[THD 3 TO 5 PERCENT]
    rec4 --> rec4a[THD 1.5 TO 3 PERCENT]
    rec5 --> rec5a[THD 5 TO 8 PERCENT]
    path3 --> rec6[CASCADED H BRIDGE MULTILEVEL]
    path3 --> rec7[12 PULSE RECTIFIER WITH PHASE SHIFT XFMR]
    path3 --> rec8[MODULAR MULTILEVEL CONVERTER MMC]
    path3 --> rec9[ACTIVE POWER FILTER]
    rec6 --> rec6a[THD LESS THAN 1.5 PERCENT]
    rec7 --> rec7a[THD 4 TO 5 PERCENT]
    rec8 --> rec8a[THD LESS THAN 1 PERCENT]
    rec9 --> rec9a[THD LESS THAN 3 PERCENT]
    rec1a --> stop[END OPTIMAL TECHNIQUE SELECTED]
    rec2a --> stop
    rec3a --> stop
    rec4a --> stop
    rec5a --> stop
    rec6a --> stop
    rec7a --> stop
    rec8a --> stop
    rec9a --> stop
```

## 4.3 Multi-Level Inverter Harmonic Progression

Visualizing how **adding levels** reduces the harmonic content by approximating the sinusoidal waveform. The block diagram shows the staircase approach.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lvl1[TWO LEVEL VSI] --> s1[STEPS 2]
    s1 --> h1[HARMONICS 3RD 5TH 7TH 9TH]
    lvl2[THREE LEVEL NPC] --> s2[STEPS 3]
    s2 --> h2[HARMONICS 5TH 7TH 11TH]
    lvl3[FIVE LEVEL CHB] --> s3[STEPS 5]
    s3 --> h3[HARMONICS NEAR CARRIER FREQUENCY]
    lvl4[NINE LEVEL MMC] --> s4[STEPS 9]
    s4 --> h4[HARMONICS FAR FROM FUNDAMENTAL]
    h1 --> imp1[THD 48 PERCENT]
    h2 --> imp2[THD 20 TO 30 PERCENT]
    h3 --> imp3[THD 5 TO 10 PERCENT]
    h4 --> imp4[THD LESS THAN 2 PERCENT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each additional voltage level in a multi-level inverter **doubles the equivalent PWM pulses** per fundamental cycle. The dominant harmonic order shifts to higher frequencies, where filtering becomes trivial with small LC filters.

## 4.4 Active Power Filter Topology (Schematic Block)

Mapping the architecture of a **Shunt Active Power Filter** — the most widely deployed harmonic mitigation device in industry.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    load1[NON LINEAR LOAD] --> meas1[CURRENT SENSOR]
    meas1 --> ref1[REFERENCE HARMONIC COMPUTATION]
    ref1 --> ref2[INVERSE OF HARMONIC COMPONENT]
    ref2 --> ctrl1[HYSTERESIS OR PWM CONTROLLER]
    ctrl1 --> inv1[VOLTAGE SOURCE INVERTER VSI]
    pcc1[PCC POINT OF COMMON COUPLING] --> inv1
    inv1 --> inj1[COMPENSATING CURRENT INJECTION]
    inj1 --> grid1[CLEAN SINUSOIDAL GRID CURRENT]
    dc1[DC LINK CAPACITOR] --> inv1
    ctrl1 --> dc1
    meas2[VOLTAGE SENSOR AT PCC] --> sync1[PLL SYNCHRONIZATION]
    sync1 --> ref1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle:** The shunt APF detects the harmonic current drawn by the non-linear load, computes the **inverse** of the harmonic component, and injects it at the PCC such that the **grid current remains purely sinusoidal**. The Karnaugh/McMath textbook formulation uses $i_{grid} = i_{load} - i_{filter}$ to cancel distortion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define Total Harmonic Distortion (THD). Derive the relationship between Distortion Factor (DF) and THD.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **THD Definition (2 marks):** Total Harmonic Distortion is the ratio of the RMS sum of all harmonic components (from the 2nd order onwards) to the RMS value of the fundamental component, expressed as a percentage.
> 
> $$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

**Derivation (1 mark):**

The total RMS of a non-sinusoidal signal:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}$$

The Distortion Factor:

$$DF = \frac{V_1}{V_{rms}} = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $V_1$:

$$DF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots)/V_1^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD^2}}$$

$$\boxed{DF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD^2}}}$$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**List any six harmful effects of harmonics on power system equipment.**

**Model Answer (1 mark each, any 6):**

1. **Transformers:** Increased copper losses (I²R), stray flux losses, and audible hum due to magnetostriction. K-factor derating is required.
2. **Induction motors:** Pulsating torques from harmonic rotating fields cause vibration, mechanical stress, and additional copper losses in rotor bars.
3. **Power cables:** Higher $I^2R$ losses and aggravated skin/proximity effects at high harmonic frequencies.
4. **Capacitor banks:** Reactance $\propto 1/n$ → low-order harmonics cause large currents, leading to dielectric failure.
5. **Protective devices (relays, MCBs):** Mal-tripping due to distorted current waveforms, especially in peak-sensing relays.
6. **Communication systems:** Inductive coupling of harmonic noise into telephone lines and data cables (TIF — Telephone Influence Factor).
7. **Neutral conductor:** Triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th) are zero-sequence and add in the neutral, leading to overheating in 3-phase 4-wire systems.
8. **Electronic equipment:** False triggering of zero-crossing detectors, latch-up, and data corruption in PLCs/DCS systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice (Q1 OR Q2). Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each. Students must answer **one** full question per slot.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the Fourier series expression for the output voltage of a single-phase full-bridge square wave inverter. Hence determine the THD of the output waveform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pulse diode rectifier feeds a DC load of 50 A. Calculate the RMS value of the 5th and 7th harmonic currents. Assume the supply is purely sinusoidal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Fourier Series Derivation:** (See SECTION 3.1 for full derivation. Key steps:)

- Waveform has **odd quarter-wave symmetry** → only odd sine harmonics.
- $V_n = \dfrac{4V_s}{n\pi}$, $n = 1, 3, 5, 7, \dots$
- Output: $v_o(t) = \dfrac{4V_s}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\omega t) + \dfrac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \dfrac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \cdots \right]$

**THD Calculation:**

- $THD^2 = \dfrac{1}{3^2} + \dfrac{1}{5^2} + \dfrac{1}{7^2} + \dfrac{1}{9^2} + \cdots = \dfrac{\pi^2}{8} - 1 = 0.2337$
- $THD = \sqrt{0.2337} = 0.4834 = \boxed{48.34\%}$

#### Model Solution for (b):

For a **6-pulse diode rectifier** fed by a 3-phase sinusoidal supply, the characteristic harmonics are of order:

$$h = 6k \pm 1, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

So harmonics present: 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th, 17th, 19th, …

The amplitude of the $n^{th}$ harmonic current in a 6-pulse rectifier:

$$I_n = \frac{I_1}{n}$$

**Step 1: Fundamental Current**

$$I_1 = \frac{I_{dc}}{\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi}} \approx \frac{\pi I_{dc}}{3\sqrt{3}}$$

Wait — for a 6-pulse rectifier, the **DC output current** $I_{dc} = 50$ A. The RMS value of the fundamental AC line current is:

$$I_{1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} I_{dc} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} \times 50 = 38.97\,\text{A}$$

**Step 2: 5th Harmonic (n = 5)**

$$I_5 = \frac{I_1}{5} = \frac{38.97}{5} = 7.79\,\text{A}$$

**Step 3: 7th Harmonic (n = 7)**

$$I_7 = \frac{I_1}{7} = \frac{38.97}{7} = 5.57\,\text{A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **line current harmonics** with **DC-side ripple harmonics**. For a 6-pulse rectifier:
> - **AC line current harmonics:** $6k \pm 1$ → 5, 7, 11, 13, …
> - **DC output voltage harmonics:** $6k$ → 6, 12, 18, …
> Mark deductions occur if these are mixed up.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a **Selective Harmonic Elimination PWM (SHE-PWM)** technique for a single-phase inverter. Formulate the constraint equations for eliminating the 5th and 7th harmonics while controlling the fundamental. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a circuit and phasor diagram, explain the operation of a **12-pulse rectifier** for harmonic reduction. Show that the dominant harmonic on the AC side is the 11th. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Block Diagram of SHE-PWM (Verbal Description for Written Exam):**

The SHE-PWM technique uses a **pre-computed lookup table** of switching angles $(\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \dots, \alpha_N)$ that are stored in EPROM. The control circuit:
1. Generates a reference fundamental $V_1^* = M \cdot V_{dc}$ (modulation index $M$).
2. Looks up the angles corresponding to the desired $M$ from the lookup table.
3. Generates gating pulses to the inverter switches at these precise instants.
4. Result: Output waveform matches fundamental exactly, with selected low-order harmonics eliminated.

**Constraint Equations (Quarter-Wave Symmetric Waveform):**

General Fourier coefficient for a staircase output with $N$ switching angles per quarter cycle:

$$V_n = \frac{4V_{dc}}{n\pi} \left[ 1 + 2\sum_{k=1}^{N} (-1)^k \cos(n\alpha_k) \right]$$

**Three conditions for N = 3:**

1. **Fundamental amplitude control:**

$$\frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi} \left[ 1 - 2\cos(\alpha_1) + 2\cos(\alpha_2) - 2\cos(\alpha_3) \right] = M \cdot V_{dc}$$

$$\cos(\alpha_1) - \cos(\alpha_2) + \cos(\alpha_3) = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{M\pi}{4}$$

2. **5th harmonic elimination ($V_5 = 0$):**

$$\cos(5\alpha_1) - \cos(5\alpha_2) + \cos(5\alpha_3) = \frac{1}{2}$$

3. **7th harmonic elimination ($V_7 = 0$):**

$$\cos(7\alpha_1) - \cos(7\alpha_2) + \cos(7\alpha_3) = \frac{1}{2}$$

These transcendental equations are solved using **iterative numerical methods** (Newton-Raphson) for each value of $M \in [0, 1.15]$.

**Advantages of SHE-PWM:**
- Lowest switching frequency → lowest switching loss (suitable for high-power GTO/IGCT inverters)
- Specific harmonic elimination as per design
- Direct digital implementation possible

#### Model Solution for (b):

**12-Pulse Rectifier Concept:**

A 12-pulse rectifier uses **two 6-pulse bridges** fed by a **phase-shifting transformer** (typically Y-Δ / Y-Y) that introduces a **30° phase shift** between the two sets of 3-phase supplies.

**Circuit Topology:**

- **Transformer 1 (Y-Y):** Provides 3-phase supply to Bridge 1 with 0° phase shift
- **Transformer 2 (Y-Δ):** Provides 3-phase supply to Bridge 2 with 30° phase shift (or vice versa)
- Both bridges feed a common DC load in **series** (or parallel)

**Phasor Diagram (Verbal):**

The two AC supply systems have fundamental components displaced by 30°. The 5th and 7th harmonics of Bridge 1 are cancelled by the corresponding harmonics of Bridge 2 because they are equal in magnitude but **opposite in phase** (the 30° shift causes a $5 \times 30° = 150°$ shift in 5th harmonic, with $\sin(150°)/\sin(30°) \cdot \sin(180° - 150°) = $ cancellation).

**Mathematical Proof — Dominant Harmonic is 11th:**

For a 6-pulse rectifier, the AC line current contains harmonics of order $6k \pm 1$:
- Bridge 1: $5^{th}, 7^{th}, 11^{th}, 13^{th}, 17^{th}, 19^{th}, \dots$
- Bridge 2 (shifted by 30°): $5^{th}, 7^{th}, 11^{th}, 13^{th}, 17^{th}, 19^{th}, \dots$

The $n^{th}$ harmonic current of Bridge 2 has phase:

$$\phi_{2,n} = \phi_{1,n} + n \cdot 30°$$

**For 5th harmonic ($n = 5$):**

$$5 \times 30° = 150°$$

The two 5th harmonic currents are 150° apart, but they are also displaced due to the 30° fundamental shift. The phasor sum of the 5th harmonics in the two transformers (as seen by the primary) is:

$$I_{5,total} = I_1 \left[ e^{j(5 \cdot 0°)} \cdot T_1 + e^{j(5 \cdot 30°)} \cdot T_2 \right]$$

where $T_1, T_2$ are the transformer turns ratios. With $T_1 = T_2 = T$ and proper winding orientation:

$$I_{5,total} \propto e^{j0°} - e^{j150°} = 0 \quad \text{(cancellation)}$$

**For 7th harmonic ($n = 7$):**

$$7 \times 30° = 210°$$

$$I_{7,total} \propto e^{j0°} - e^{j210°} = 0 \quad \text{(cancellation)}$$

**For 11th harmonic ($n = 11$):**

$$11 \times 30° = 330° \equiv -30°$$

The two 11th harmonic currents are 360° + 30° apart, which means they **add constructively** (not cancel):

$$I_{11,total} \propto e^{j0°} + e^{-j30°} = 2\cos(15°)$$

**For 13th harmonic ($n = 13$):**

$$13 \times 30° = 390° \equiv 30°$$

$$I_{13,total} \propto e^{j0°} + e^{j30°} = 2\cos(15°)$$

**Conclusion:** The 5th and 7th harmonics cancel, while the **11th and 13th harmonics add**. Therefore, the **dominant AC-side harmonic is the 11th** (and 13th). This is the theoretical basis for 12-pulse rectification in HVDC systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Do not confuse **AC line current harmonics** ($6k \pm 1$) with **DC voltage harmonics** ($6k$).
> 2. The cancellation of 5th and 7th depends on the **transformer vector group** (Dy11 vs Dy1) — getting this wrong loses 2 marks.
> 3. Students forget that 12-pulse rectifiers still have a significant 11th and 13th harmonic; **passive filters tuned to 11th and 13th** are required in HVDC stations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP MARKS REQUIRE THE FOLLOWING — MISTAKES THAT COST 2-3 MARKS PER QUESTION:**
> 
> 1. **Units are mandatory** — Always state THD in %, current in A, frequency in Hz.
> 2. **Formulas must precede numbers** — Never write a numerical answer without showing the formula used.
> 3. **Symmetry arguments save time** — Always state the symmetry (odd, even, half-wave, quarter-wave) before starting Fourier integration. This earns easy 1 mark.
> 4. **Diagrams are compulsory** — A circuit or waveform sketch is expected in Part B; missing it loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Distinguish between RMS and peak** — Fourier coefficients $b_n$ give PEAK amplitudes; divide by $\sqrt{2}$ for RMS.
> 6. **Show intermediate steps** — Even if the final THD value is correct, skipping the summation series will lose partial marks.
> 7. **Final numerical answer with 2-3 decimal places** — Don't round off prematurely; carry 4 decimal places through calculation.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY BULLET SUMMARY — READ THIS BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL:**

### Core Concepts (Must Memorize)

- [x] **THD Formula:** $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} X_n^2}}{X_1} \times 100\%$
- [x] **DF Formula:** $\text{DF} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \text{THD}^2}}$
- [x] **True PF:** $\text{PF}_{true} = \text{DF} \times \text{DPF} = \dfrac{\cos(\phi_1)}{\sqrt{1 + \text{THD}^2}}$
- [x] **Square wave harmonics:** $V_n = \dfrac{4V_s}{n\pi}$, $n = 1, 3, 5, \dots$
- [x] **THD of square wave:** $\approx 48.34\%$
- [x] **Quasi-square wave:** $V_n = \dfrac{4V_s}{n\pi} \cos\!\left(\dfrac{n\alpha}{2}\right)$
- [x] **6-pulse AC line harmonics:** $h = 6k \pm 1$ (i.e., 5, 7, 11, 13, …)
- [x] **12-pulse AC line harmonics:** $h = 12k \pm 1$ (i.e., 11, 13, 23, 25, …)
- [x] **Triplen harmonics order:** $h = 3, 9, 15, 21, \dots$ (zero-sequence, add in neutral)

### Symmetry Rules (Memorize)

- [x] **Odd symmetry** → only odd harmonics
- [x] **Even symmetry** → only even harmonics (cosine terms)
- [x] **Half-wave symmetry** → only odd harmonics, no DC
- [x] **Quarter-wave symmetry** → only odd sine harmonics
- [x] **Waveform with DC offset** → $a_0 \neq 0$

### Elimination Methods (Know at Least 4)

- [x] **SPWM:** Sinusoidal reference vs triangular carrier → low THD at high switching frequency
- [x] **SHE-PWM:** Pre-computed switching angles to eliminate specific harmonics
- [x] **Multi-level inverters:** Diode-clamped, flying capacitor, cascaded H-bridge
- [x] **12/18/24-pulse rectifiers:** Phase-shifting transformers cancel low-order harmonics
- [x] **Active Power Filters (APF):** Inject compensating harmonic currents
- [x] **Passive filters:** LC tuned to absorb specific harmonic frequencies

### Effects on Equipment (List 6+ in Exam)

- [x] Transformer overheating (K-factor)
- [x] Motor torque pulsation
- [x] Cable skin effect amplification
- [x] Capacitor dielectric failure
- [x] Relay mal-tripping
- [x] Communication interference
- [x] Neutral conductor overload (triplen harmonics)
- [x] Resonant over-voltages (system + capacitor interaction)

### Standards & Limits (Important for Viva)

- [x] **IEEE 519-2014:** Voltage THD limit at PCC = 5% (general), 3% (individual)
- [x] **IEC 61000-3-2 / 3-12:** Harmonic current limits for equipment
- [x] **IEEE 519 TDD limit:** 5%–20% depending on $I_{sc}/I_L$ ratio

### Quick-Recall Numerical Facts

- [x] $\pi^2/8 \approx 1.2337$
- [x] $\pi^2/8 - 1 = 0.2337$ → $THD^2$ of square wave
- [x] $\sqrt{0.2337} = 0.4834$ → Square wave THD
- [x] $\cos(30°) = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.8660$ → Quasi-square with $\alpha = 60°$
- [x] $4/\pi = 1.2732$ → Square wave fundamental coefficient
- [x] $\sqrt{6}/\pi = 0.7794$ → 6-pulse rectifier line current conversion factor

### Common Exam Traps (Avoid These!)

- [x] ❌ Forgetting to **square** the harmonics before summing for THD
- [x] ❌ Confusing **peak** vs **RMS** values in Fourier coefficients
- [x] ❌ Using the **wrong harmonic order formula** for multi-pulse rectifiers
- [x] ❌ Forgetting the **displacement factor** $\cos(\phi_1)$ when computing true PF
- [x] ❌ Missing the **$\sqrt{2}$ factor** in RMS-to-peak conversion
- [x] ❌ Treating a **square wave** as a sinusoidal wave in power calculations
- [x] ❌ Computing the **DF** as $V_{rms}/V_1$ instead of $V_1/V_{rms}$

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Effect of Harmonics and Its Elimination**
> 
> **Total Coverage:** 5 Sections, ~3,800 words, 12+ formulas, 4 Mermaid diagrams, 2 fully solved 14-mark questions, 2 model 3-mark answers, and 1 high-yield analytical matrix.
> 
> **Recommended Next Module Topics to Study:**
> 1. PWM Techniques (SPWM, SVPWM, Modified PWM)
> 2. Multi-Level Inverter Topologies (NPC, CHB, MMC)
> 3. Active and Passive Filter Design
> 4. Power Quality Standards (IEEE 519, IEC 61000)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sinusoidal PWM Inverters
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3: Sinusoidal PWM Inverters

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - Power Electronics
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sinusoidal PWM Inverters

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)** is a sophisticated modulation technique employed in voltage-source inverters (VSI) wherein a high-frequency **triangular carrier wave** is continuously compared against a low-frequency **sinusoidal reference wave** (also called the *modulating signal* or *control signal*). The instantaneous intersection points of these two waves determine the precise switching instants of the power semiconductor switches (IGBTs/MOSFETs) in the inverter bridge, thereby synthesizing a quasi-sinusoidal output voltage from a DC bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** SPWM is the most fundamental and frequently tested PWM scheme in Power Electronics. Students must master the concepts of **modulation index ($m_a$)**, **frequency modulation ratio ($m_f$)**, and the resulting **harmonic spectrum** for both single-phase and three-phase configurations.

Mathematically, the comparator logic is defined as:

$$S_k(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & v_{control}(t) > v_{tri}(t) \\ 0, & v_{control}(t) \leq v_{tri}(t) \end{cases}$$

where $S_k(t)$ is the gating signal for the $k^{th}$ switch, $v_{control}(t)$ is the sinusoidal reference, and $v_{tri}(t)$ is the triangular carrier.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stencil Painting" Approach

Imagine you are an artist who must paint a smooth, continuous sine wave on a wall, but the only colors available to you are a bucket of **white** and a bucket of **black** — and you can only paint in straight, wide horizontal stripes. The final viewer looking from far away will see a blended *gray* tone.

- The **sine wave reference** is your *desired painting* (the smooth gray gradient).
- The **triangular carrier wave** is your *stencil or guide ruler* that determines how wide each black or white stripe should be.
- The **comparator** is the artist's *hand* that decides, at every instant, whether to dip the brush in black or white.
- The **DC bus voltage ($V_s$)** is the *fixed intensity* of the paint.

When viewed at a distance, the rapid black-and-white stripes blur into a smooth sinusoidal shape — this is exactly how SPWM synthesizes a sine wave from a fixed DC source. The wider the reference wave at any instant, the longer the "ON" pulse, and the narrower it gets, the shorter the "ON" pulse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why triangular and not sawtooth or square carrier?**
> A triangular carrier is used because it has a **linear, symmetric rise and fall**, which guarantees that the resulting pulse widths vary **sinusoidally** with the modulating signal. This produces the lowest harmonic distortion among natural-sampled PWM schemes.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Carrier Frequency ($f_c$ or $f_{tri}$):** Typically **1 kHz to 20 kHz** for industrial drives; higher than 20 kHz pushes into the ultrasonic range (used in EV and aerospace drives to avoid audible noise).
- **Fundamental Output Frequency ($f_o$ or $f_1$):** Typically **0 Hz to 400 Hz** for variable-frequency drives (VFDs).
- **DC Bus Voltage ($V_s$ or $V_{dc}$):** The fixed input DC link, e.g., **$\pm$ 400 V to 800 V** in industrial three-phase systems.
- **Switching Frequency ($f_{sw}$):** Equals the carrier frequency $f_c$ in bipolar SPWM.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of SPWM

The generation of SPWM signals follows a strict, sequential logic:

1. **Reference Wave Generation:** A low-frequency sinusoidal reference $v_c(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f_o t)$ is generated, where $V_m$ is the peak amplitude and $f_o$ is the desired fundamental output frequency.
2. **Carrier Wave Generation:** A high-frequency triangular carrier $v_{tri}(t)$ with peak amplitude $\hat{V}_{tri}$ and frequency $f_c$ is generated. This wave oscillates linearly between $+\hat{V}_{tri}$ and $-\hat{V}_{tri}$.
3. **Comparator Intersection:** An analog comparator (or digital equivalent) continuously compares $v_c(t)$ with $v_{tri}(t)$.
   - If $v_c(t) > v_{tri}(t)$ → the upper switch of the inverter leg is turned **ON**, and the lower switch is **OFF** → output = $+V_s$.
   - If $v_c(t) < v_{tri}(tri)$ → the upper switch is **OFF**, and the lower switch is **ON** → output = $-V_s$ (in bipolar PWM) or $0$ (in unipolar PWM).
4. **Pulse Width Modulation:** The width of each output pulse is **proportional to the instantaneous amplitude** of the sine wave at the carrier period center. Wider pulses occur near the peaks; narrower pulses occur near the zero crossings.
5. **Output Synthesis:** The resulting pulse train, when passed through the inductive load (motor), produces a current waveform that closely approximates a pure sinusoid due to the natural low-pass filtering action of $L$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this produce a sine wave?**
> The fundamental component of the switched output is proportional to the *area under each pulse*, which is itself proportional to $v_c(t)$ (the reference) at the instant of comparison. Since $v_c(t)$ is sinusoidal, the average output per cycle varies sinusoidally — a beautiful application of the **Fourier integral identity**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning / Range** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modulation Index (Amplitude Ratio) | $m_a$ | $m_a = \dfrac{V_m}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$ | $0 \le m_a \le 1$ (linear); $m_a > 1$ = overmodulation |
| Frequency Modulation Ratio | $m_f$ | $m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_o}$ | Usually odd integer (15, 21, 27...) for 3-$\phi$ |
| Peak Fundamental Output (Linear Region) | $V_{o1,peak}$ | $V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot \dfrac{V_s}{2}$ | Valid for single-phase full-bridge bipolar SPWM |
| RMS Fundamental Output (Linear Region) | $V_{o1,rms}$ | $V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{m_a \cdot V_s}{2\sqrt{2}}$ | Bipolar single-phase H-bridge |
| Peak Line-to-Line Output (3-$\phi$) | $V_{LL1,peak}$ | $V_{LL1,peak} = m_a \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot V_{dc}$ | Standard 3-$\phi$ VSI output |
| Overmodulation Peak Output | $V_{o1,peak}$ | $V_{o1,peak} = \dfrac{4 \cdot V_s}{\pi}$ (six-step limit) | As $m_a \to \infty$, approaches square wave |
| Harmonic Order in Output | $n$ | $n = j \cdot m_f \pm k$ | $j$ is multiple of carrier, $k = 1,2,3...$ |
| Switching Losses (per switch) | $P_{sw}$ | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_s \cdot I_o \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_c$ | Linear in $f_c$ |
| Total Harmonic Distortion | $THD$ | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1}$ | Lower $m_f$ with proper selection = lower THD |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

SPWM is the **workhorse modulation technique** behind nearly every modern motor drive and grid-tied inverter. Its real-world applications include:

- **Industrial Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs):** Controlling induction motors in pumps, fans, and compressors in factories across Kerala's industrial belts.
- **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Inverters:** Converting solar DC to grid-synchronized AC with maximum power point tracking (MPPT).
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS):** Providing clean backup power for hospitals and data centers.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Traction Inverters:** Driving 3-phase PMSM or induction motors in EVs (e.g., Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3).
- **Active Power Filters (APF):** Compensating harmonics and reactive power in distribution networks.
- **HVDC Converters:** High-voltage DC transmission systems use variants of PWM for grid interfacing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"Why use SPWM instead of square-wave inversion?"*, the canonical answer is: **SPWM places the lowest-order harmonics at high frequencies (around $m_f$), allowing small, cheap LC filters to remove them, whereas square-wave inverters place the 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th... harmonics directly in the load current, requiring massive and expensive filters.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Output Voltage ($V_{o1}$)

The single-phase full-bridge inverter with **bipolar SPWM** produces an output that switches between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$. We wish to find the amplitude of the fundamental component.

### Step 1: Defining the Switching Function

The output of the comparator $S(t)$ can be written as a periodic function of time. Within each carrier interval of duration $T_c = 1/f_c$, the duty cycle $D(\tau)$ (where $\tau$ is the local time) is:

$$D(\tau) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \frac{v_c(\tau)}{\hat{V}_{tri}}\right]$$

**Reasoning:** When $v_c = +\hat{V}_{tri}$, the reference is always greater, so $D = 1$ (full $+V_s$). When $v_c = -\hat{V}_{tri}$, $D = 0$ (full $-V_s$). Linear interpolation gives the above formula.

### Step 2: Local Average of Output

The average output voltage over one carrier period is:

$$\bar{v}_o(t) = V_s \cdot D(\tau) + (-V_s) \cdot [1 - D(\tau)]$$

Substituting the duty cycle:

$$\bar{v}_o(t) = V_s \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{v_c}{\hat{V}_{tri}}\right) \right] - V_s \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{v_c}{\hat{V}_{tri}}\right) \right]$$

### Step 3: Simplification

$$\bar{v}_o(t) = V_s \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{2} + \frac{v_c}{2\hat{V}_{tri}} - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{v_c}{2\hat{V}_{tri}} \right]$$

$$\bar{v}_o(t) = V_s \cdot \frac{v_c(t)}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$$

### Step 4: Substituting the Sinusoidal Reference

Let $v_c(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f_o t)$:

$$\bar{v}_o(t) = \frac{V_s \cdot V_m}{\hat{V}_{tri}} \cdot \sin(2\pi f_o t)$$

### Step 5: Recognizing the Modulation Index

By definition, $m_a = V_m / \hat{V}_{tri}$, so:

$$\boxed{\,v_{o1}(t) = m_a \cdot V_s \cdot \sin(2\pi f_o t)\,}$$

The **peak fundamental output voltage** is therefore:

$$V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot V_s \quad \text{(for half-bridge)}$$

$$V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_s}{2} \quad \text{(for full-bridge bipolar SPWM)}$$

The **RMS fundamental** is:

$$V_{o1,rms} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_s}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

## 3.2 Harmonic Spectrum Derivation

For natural-sampled SPWM, the Fourier analysis yields that the significant harmonic clusters appear at carrier multiples. The general harmonic order is:

$$n = j \cdot m_f \pm k$$

where $j = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ and $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ (with $k$ even for even $j$ and vice versa in certain cases).

The **double Fourier integral** analysis gives the harmonic amplitude at frequency $nf_o$ as:

$$V_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi} \cdot \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \left[ J_{2m+1}\left(\frac{n\pi m_a}{2}\right) \cdot \text{coefficient} \right]$$

A simplified result for the **principal carrier harmonics** ($n = m_f, 2m_f, 3m_f, \ldots$) shows that:

$$V_{m_f} \approx \frac{4 V_s}{\pi m_f} \cdot J_1(m_a \pi) \cdot (\text{scaling factor})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **In the linear modulation region ($m_a \le 1$):** No low-order harmonics ($n < m_f$) exist — they are all pushed to **$f_c$ and beyond**. This is the key advantage of SPWM.

## 3.3 Three-Phase SPWM — Analytical Matrix

For a balanced three-phase inverter, the three reference signals are $120°$ phase-shifted:

$$v_{cA}(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f_o t)$$
$$v_{cB}(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f_o t - 120°)$$
$$v_{cC}(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f_o t + 120°)$$

All three share the **same triangular carrier**. The critical condition is:

> **$m_f$ must be chosen as an odd multiple of 3** (i.e., $m_f = 9, 15, 21, 27, \ldots$) for **odd symmetry and quarter-wave symmetry** to be preserved. This eliminates triplen harmonics ($n = 3, 9, 15, 21, \ldots$) in the **line-to-line** output.

The **line-to-line** fundamental output is:

$$V_{LL1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot V_{dc}$$

This derivation is critical for KTU problems involving three-phase VSI design.

## 3.4 Numerical Example — Worked Out

**Problem:** A single-phase full-bridge inverter uses bipolar SPWM with $V_s = 400$ V, $m_a = 0.8$, and $f_o = 50$ Hz. The carrier frequency is $f_c = 2$ kHz. Calculate:
(a) Peak fundamental output voltage
(b) RMS fundamental output voltage
(c) Frequency modulation ratio
(d) Order of the first significant harmonic cluster

**Solution:**

**(a)** $V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_s}{2} = 0.8 \cdot \frac{400}{2} = 0.8 \cdot 200 = \mathbf{160 \text{ V}}$

**(b)** $V_{o1,rms} = \frac{V_{o1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{160}{\sqrt{2}} = \mathbf{113.14 \text{ V}}$

**(c)** $m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_o} = \frac{2000}{50} = \mathbf{40}$

**(d)** First significant harmonic cluster: $n = m_f \pm 2 = 40 \pm 2$, i.e., at $\mathbf{n = 38}$ and $\mathbf{n = 42}$. The next cluster is at $2m_f \pm 1 = 79, 81$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SPWM Generation — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Bus Source VDC]:::supply --> B[Three Phase Inverter Bridge]
    B --> C[Load Motor or Grid]
    
    D[Sinusoidal Reference Generator 50 Hz]:::ref --> E[Three Phase Sine References A B C 120 deg apart]
    E --> F1[Comparator Phase A]
    E --> F2[Comparator Phase B]
    E --> F3[Comparator Phase C]
    
    G[Triangular Carrier Generator 2 kHz]:::carrier --> F1
    G --> F2
    G --> F3
    
    F1 --> H1[Gating Signal for Leg A]
    F2 --> H2[Gating Signal for Leg B]
    F3 --> H3[Gating Signal for Leg C]
    
    H1 --> B
    H2 --> B
    H3 --> B
    
    B -.Feedback.-> I[Output Voltage Sensor]
    I -.Control Loop.-> D
    
    classDef supply fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef carrier fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** The architecture shows three parallel signal-processing lanes (one per phase) sharing a single triangular carrier. The reference generator, modulated by the output feedback, dynamically adjusts the sine reference amplitude to regulate output voltage — this is the heart of **V/Hz control** in VFDs.

## 4.2 SPWM Output Waveform Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT WAVES"]
        A1[Sinusoidal Reference vm sin wt]
        A2[Triangular Carrier vtri t]
    end
    
    INPUT --> B[Comparator Logic]
    
    B --> C{If vref greater than vtri}
    C -->|YES| D[Upper Switch ON, Lower OFF]
    C -->|NO| E[Upper Switch OFF, Lower ON]
    
    D --> F[Output Pulses to Inverter Leg]
    E --> F
    
    F --> G[Pulse Width Modulated Output]
    G --> H[Filtered Quasi Sinusoid at Load]
    
    style A1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style A2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B4513
```

## 4.3 Harmonic Spectrum Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Output Frequency Spectrum of SPWM] --> B[Fundamental at fo 50 Hz]
    A --> C[Carrier Cluster at mf times fo]
    A --> D[Second Carrier Cluster at 2mf times fo]
    A --> E[Third Carrier Cluster at 3mf times fo]
    
    B --> B1[Amplitude proportional to ma]
    C --> C1[Sidebands at mf minus 2 mf plus 2]
    D --> D1[Sidebands at 2mf minus 1 2mf plus 1]
    E --> E1[Sidebands at 3mf minus 2 3mf plus 2]
    
    style A fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#8B8B00
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Spectrum:** Notice how the fundamental has high amplitude, while the carrier clusters have **sideband structure**. In three-phase SPWM with $m_f$ as an odd multiple of 3, the **triplen harmonics** (3, 9, 15, 21...) are eliminated from the line-to-line voltage, further improving the THD.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define modulation index ($m_a$) and frequency modulation ratio ($m_f$) in SPWM. What is the practical range of $m_a$ for linear operation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Modulation Index ($m_a$):** It is the ratio of the peak amplitude of the sinusoidal reference wave ($V_m$) to the peak amplitude of the triangular carrier wave ($\hat{V}_{tri}$):

$$m_a = \frac{V_m}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$$

It controls the **amplitude** of the fundamental output voltage. In the linear region of operation, $m_a$ ranges from **0 to 1**.

**Frequency Modulation Ratio ($m_f$):** It is the ratio of the triangular carrier frequency ($f_c$) to the sinusoidal reference frequency ($f_o$):

$$m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_o}$$

It determines the **harmonic spectrum** of the output. For three-phase inverters, $m_f$ is typically chosen as an odd multiple of 3 (e.g., 15, 21, 27) to eliminate triplen harmonics in the line-to-line voltage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse $m_a$ with $m_f$. Remember: **$m_a$ → amplitude control**, **$m_f$ → frequency content / harmonic placement**. Marks are lost if these are interchanged in derivations.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**State two advantages of sinusoidal PWM over square-wave inversion in VSI.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Low Harmonic Distortion:** SPWM shifts the dominant harmonics to high frequencies around $f_c$ and its multiples, allowing the use of small, low-cost LC filters. Square-wave inverters have dominant 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th... harmonics, requiring bulky filters.

2. **Independent Control of Output Voltage and Frequency:** By varying $m_a$, the fundamental output voltage amplitude can be controlled; by varying $f_o$, the frequency is controlled. This is essential for **V/Hz control** of AC motors, enabling smooth speed regulation from zero to rated speed.

3. **Reduced Torque Pulsations:** In motor drives, high-frequency harmonics produce less torque ripple compared to low-order harmonics in square-wave operation, leading to smoother mechanical operation.

> [!NOTE]
> Any two of the above are sufficient for full marks. A common error is to write only one advantage — KTU requires *two distinct points* for a complete 3-mark answer.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - July 2023]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the fundamental RMS output voltage of a single-phase full-bridge inverter using bipolar sinusoidal PWM, in terms of the modulation index $m_a$ and DC bus voltage $V_s$. State the condition for linear operation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A three-phase VSI uses SPWM with $V_{dc} = 600$ V, $m_a = 0.85$, $f_o = 50$ Hz, and $f_c = 1050$ Hz. Calculate:
  (i) The peak and RMS line-to-line fundamental voltage
  (ii) The frequency modulation ratio
  (iii) The order of the first significant harmonic cluster in the line-to-line output
  (iv) The switching frequency of each IGBT **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation of gating signals using sinusoidal PWM technique for a three-phase voltage source inverter. Discuss the role of $m_f$ as an odd multiple of 3. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase half-bridge VSI uses SPWM with $V_s = 200$ V (total DC bus), $m_a = 0.9$, $f_o = 50$ Hz, and $f_c = 1050$ Hz. Calculate:
  (i) Peak fundamental output voltage
  (ii) RMS fundamental output voltage
  (iii) Frequency modulation ratio
  (iv) Whether this configuration is suitable for linear modulation; justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

## Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A:

**(a) Derivation:**

Consider a single-phase full-bridge inverter with bipolar SPWM. The output switches between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$. The comparator logic produces a duty cycle within each carrier period:

$$D(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \frac{v_c(t)}{\hat{V}_{tri}}\right]$$

where $v_c(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f_o t)$.

The average output over one carrier cycle:

$$\bar{v}_o(t) = V_s \cdot D(t) - V_s \cdot [1 - D(t)] = V_s \cdot \frac{v_c(t)}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$$

Substituting the sine reference:

$$\bar{v}_o(t) = \frac{V_s \cdot V_m}{\hat{V}_{tri}} \cdot \sin(2\pi f_o t) = m_a \cdot V_s \cdot \sin(2\pi f_o t)$$

**For full-bridge bipolar PWM (output swings between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$):** The amplitude must be halved for the peak-to-peak symmetry, giving:

$$V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_s}{2}$$

The **RMS** fundamental output:

$$\boxed{V_{o1,rms} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_s}{2\sqrt{2}}}$$

**Condition for linear operation:** $0 \le m_a \le 1$. Beyond this ($m_a > 1$), **overmodulation** occurs, and the relationship becomes non-linear, eventually saturating to the six-step square-wave limit of $V_{o1,peak} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi}$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{dc} = 600$ V, $m_a = 0.85$, $f_o = 50$ Hz, $f_c = 1050$ Hz.

**(i) Peak line-to-line fundamental:**

$$V_{LL1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot V_{dc} = 0.85 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot 600$$

$$V_{LL1,peak} = 0.85 \cdot 0.866 \cdot 600 = \mathbf{441.66 \text{ V}}$$

**RMS line-to-line fundamental:**

$$V_{LL1,rms} = \frac{V_{LL1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{441.66}{\sqrt{2}} = \mathbf{312.34 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Frequency modulation ratio:**

$$m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_o} = \frac{1050}{50} = \mathbf{21}$$

Note: $m_f = 21$ is an **odd multiple of 3** (specifically $3 \times 7$), which is the **ideal choice** for three-phase SPWM to eliminate triplen harmonics in the line-to-line voltage.

**(iii) First significant harmonic cluster:**

For a three-phase system, the first carrier cluster in the line-to-line voltage appears at:

$$n = m_f \pm 2 = 21 \pm 2 = \mathbf{19 \text{ and } 23}$$

So the first significant harmonics are at $19^{th}$ ($19 \times 50 = 950$ Hz) and $23^{rd}$ ($23 \times 50 = 1150$ Hz). These are the **most troublesome harmonics** and must be filtered.

**(iv) Switching frequency of each IGBT:**

$$\boxed{f_{sw} = f_c = 1050 \text{ Hz}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly use $V_{LL1,peak} = m_a \cdot V_{dc}$ (forgetting the $\sqrt{3}/2$ factor for line-to-line conversion in 3-$\phi$ systems). The correct relation is $V_{LL1,peak} = (m_a \cdot \sqrt{3}/2) \cdot V_{dc}$, NOT $m_a \cdot V_{dc}$. **Losing 2 marks for this common error.**

---

### Solution to Question B:

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation:**

The generation of three-phase SPWM signals involves a common **triangular carrier generator** producing a high-frequency wave at $f_c$. This carrier is fed to **three comparators** simultaneously. Each comparator also receives one of the three **sinusoidal references** that are $120°$ phase-shifted:

$$v_{cA} = V_m \sin(\omega_o t)$$
$$v_{cB} = V_m \sin(\omega_o t - 120°)$$
$$v_{cC} = V_m \sin(\omega_o t + 120°)$$

The comparator outputs (gating pulses) drive the upper and lower switches of each inverter leg. To prevent shoot-through, a small **dead time** (typically 1–5 μs) is inserted between complementary switching transitions.

**Why $m_f$ must be an odd multiple of 3:**

- **Odd $m_f$:** Ensures **odd quarter-wave symmetry**, which eliminates **even harmonics** in the output.
- **Multiple of 3 ($m_f = 3, 9, 15, 21, 27, \ldots$):** Since the three reference waves are $120°$ apart, the carrier comparison waveforms are also phase-displaced. The triplen harmonics ($3^{rd}$, $9^{th}$, $15^{th}$, $21^{st}$, $27^{th}$, $\ldots$) in each phase are **co-phasal** (i.e., in phase with each other) and thus cancel in the **differential (line-to-line)** output. This significantly reduces the THD of the three-phase system.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** Half-bridge, $V_s = 200$ V, $m_a = 0.9$, $f_o = 50$ Hz, $f_c = 1050$ Hz.

**(i) Peak fundamental output voltage:**

For a half-bridge, the output swings between $+V_s/2$ and $-V_s/2$:

$$V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_s}{2} = 0.9 \cdot \frac{200}{2} = 0.9 \cdot 100 = \mathbf{90 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) RMS fundamental output voltage:**

$$V_{o1,rms} = \frac{V_{o1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{90}{\sqrt{2}} = \mathbf{63.64 \text{ V}}$$

**(iii) Frequency modulation ratio:**

$$m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_o} = \frac{1050}{50} = \mathbf{21}$$

**(iv) Suitability for linear modulation:**

Since $m_a = 0.9 \le 1.0$, the inverter operates in the **linear modulation region**. The output fundamental voltage varies **linearly** with $m_a$, and the harmonic spectrum is well-behaved with dominant harmonics at $m_f \pm 2 = 19, 23$ and higher multiples. **Yes, this configuration is fully suitable for linear modulation.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly apply the full-bridge formula $V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot V_s$ to a half-bridge. **The half-bridge has half the DC bus swing**, so the correct formula is $V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot (V_s/2)$. Always read the question carefully to identify the inverter topology.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Viva and ESE**

- **SPWM Core Idea:** Compare a low-frequency sine reference with a high-frequency triangular carrier → comparator generates switching pulses.
- **Modulation Index ($m_a = V_m / \hat{V}_{tri}$):** Controls output voltage amplitude. **Linear range: $0 \le m_a \le 1$.** Beyond 1 → overmodulation → six-step limit.
- **Frequency Modulation Ratio ($m_f = f_c / f_o$):** Controls harmonic placement. **For 3-$\phi$: use odd multiples of 3** (15, 21, 27, 33...).
- **Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Single-phase half-bridge peak: $V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot V_s/2$
  - Single-phase full-bridge (bipolar) peak: $V_{o1,peak} = m_a \cdot V_s/2$
  - Three-phase line-to-line peak: $V_{LL1,peak} = m_a \cdot \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{dc}/2$
  - Overmodulation/six-step limit: $V_{o1,peak} = 4 V_s / \pi$
- **Harmonic Locations:** First cluster at $n = m_f \pm 2$ (or $m_f \pm 1$ depending on topology). Higher clusters at $2m_f \pm 1, 3m_f \pm 2$, etc.
- **Bipolar vs. Unipolar PWM:**
  - *Bipolar:* output toggles between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$ → higher ripple, simpler control.
  - *Unipolar:* output toggles between $0$ and $+V_s$ (or $0$ and $-V_s$) → lower ripple, doubles effective switching frequency, harmonic cancellation.
- **Why $m_f$ as odd multiple of 3 in 3-$\phi$?** Eliminates even harmonics (odd $m_f$) and triplen harmonics in line-to-line output (multiple of 3).
- **Trade-off:** Higher $m_f$ → better harmonic spectrum, lower filter size, **BUT** higher switching losses. Industrial VFDs typically use $f_c$ between 2 kHz to 16 kHz.
- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** $THD = \sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2} / V_1$. SPWM with proper $m_f$ achieves THD typically below 5% with small filters.
- **Dead Time:** Always required between complementary switch transitions to prevent short circuit of DC bus. Causes small voltage distortion at low fundamental frequencies.
- **Third Harmonic Injection (Advanced):** Adding a $V_3 \sin(3\omega t)$ component to the reference extends linear range to $m_a \le 1.15$ — improves DC bus utilization by 15%. (Often asked as a higher-order question in KTU 2024 Scheme.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Solar Power Plant** is an integrated electrical power generation facility that converts solar radiation—either directly via the **Photovoltaic (PV) effect** or indirectly through concentrated solar thermal cycles—into usable electrical energy, complete with balance-of-system (BOS) components, grid-tie or storage infrastructure, and protection apparatus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A solar power plant is a system of engineered components (collectors/modules, power conditioning units, mounting structures, inverters, transformers, switchgear, and grid interconnects) engineered to harvest incident solar irradiance ($G$) measured in **W/m²** and convert it into dispatchable electrical energy with a target *Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)* between **15% – 25%** depending on geography.

The two principal classifications are:

1. **Solar Photovoltaic (SPV) Power Plants** – Direct conversion using semiconductor p-n junctions.
2. **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) Plants** – Indirect conversion via thermal fluid heating and Rankine/Brayton cycles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
> Think of a **solar plant like a giant artificial leaf system**: a **PV plant is like a digital calculator's solar cell**—each photon striking a silicon wafer "knocks loose" an electron, generating a tiny DC current. Billions of these wafers (modules) connected together act like an *enormous electrical nursery* producing electrons. Meanwhile, a **concentrated solar plant behaves like a kitchen magnifying glass** that focuses sunlight onto a tiny spot to boil water—here, parabolic mirrors focus sunlight to heat molten salt, which then boils water to spin a turbine, much like a conventional coal plant, but with sunshine as the fuel.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are mandatory for KTU numerical problem-solving:

> - **Solar Constant (G$_{SC}$)** = **1367 W/m²** (extraterrestrial irradiance at AM 1.5)
> - **Air Mass (AM 1.5)** = Spectral irradiance at zenith 48.2°
> - **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** Irradiance = **1000 W/m²**, Cell Temp = **25 °C**, AM = 1.5
> - **Sun's Surface Temperature** ≈ **5778 K**
> - **Speed of Light in vacuum** = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - **Band gap of Silicon** = **1.12 eV** (at 300 K)
> - **Plant Availability Factor (PAF) target** = **≥ 95%**
> - **CUF threshold for PPA viability in India** = **≥ 19%**

## 1.4 Categorization of Solar Power Plants

| Plant Type | Energy Conversion Path | Typical Capacity Range | Plant Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grid-Tied SPV Plant | Sunlight → DC → AC → Grid | 1 MW – 600 MW | 15% – 22% |
| Off-Grid SPV Plant | Sunlight → DC → Battery → Load | < 1 MW | 12% – 18% |
| Solar Thermal (Parabolic Trough) | Sunlight → Heat → Steam → Turbine | 50 MW – 400 MW | 30% – 40% |
| Solar Tower (Central Receiver) | Sunlight → Molten Salt → Steam → Turbine | 10 MW – 200 MW | 35% – 45% |
| Solar Dish-Stirling | Sunlight → Stirling Engine | 5 kW – 50 kW | 31% |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "list and explain types," always classify BOTH the **PV family** and the **Thermal/CSP family**. Marks are awarded for showing breadth of knowledge.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Direct PV Conversion

The PV cell is a **p-n junction diode** fabricated from doped semiconductor material (typically **monocrystalline** or **polycrystalline silicon**). When photons with energy $E_{ph} = h\nu \geq E_g$ strike the cell:

1. Photons are absorbed in the depletion region.
2. Electron-hole pairs are generated (photogeneration).
3. The built-in electric field at the junction separates the charges.
4. Free electrons flow through the external load, producing DC current.

The **fundamental open-circuit voltage** of a single silicon cell is limited to approximately **0.5 V – 0.7 V** under STC, which is why modules are constructed by series-connecting **60 / 72 / 144 cells** to obtain usable voltages.

## 2.2 Solar Geometry & Irradiance Estimation

The amount of solar energy striking a tilted surface depends on the **declination angle (δ)**, **hour angle (ω)**, **latitude (φ)**, and **surface tilt (β)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU problems:** Numerical questions frequently ask to compute solar position angles or available irradiance on a tilted collector. Mastering these equations is non-negotiable.

### Declination Angle (δ)

The declination angle represents the angular position of the sun at solar noon relative to the equatorial plane. It varies seasonally due to Earth's axial tilt of **23.45°**.

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right)$$

where $n$ = day of the year (1 to 365).

### Hour Angle (ω)

The hour angle measures the angular displacement of the sun from local solar noon, with **+15° per hour** before/after noon (negative in morning, positive in afternoon).

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (t_{solar} - 12)$$

### Solar Altitude Angle (α) and Zenith Angle (θz)

The altitude angle is the sun's height above the horizon.

$$\sin \alpha = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega$$

$$\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$$

### Angle of Incidence on Tilted Surface (θi)

$$\cos \theta_i = \sin \delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos \delta \cos \omega \cos(\phi - \beta)$$

### Beam Radiation on Tilted Surface (G$_{B,T}$)

$$G_{B,T} = G_{B,n} \times \cos \theta_i$$

where $G_{B,n}$ is the **direct normal irradiance** measured by a pyrheliometer.

## 2.3 Total Solar Radiation on a Tilted Surface

The total radiation falling on a tilted collector has three components:

$$G_T = G_{B,T} + G_{D,T} + G_R$$

- **$G_{B,T}$** = Beam radiation on tilted plane
- **$G_{D,T}$** = Diffuse radiation on tilted plane (estimated via **isotropic sky model**)
- **$G_R$** = Ground-reflected radiation

### Isotropic Diffuse Model (Liu & Jordan)

$$G_{D,T} = G_D \times \left(\frac{1 + \cos \beta}{2}\right)$$

### Ground Reflected Component

$$G_R = G \times \rho \times \left(\frac{1 - \cos \beta}{2}\right)$$

where $\rho$ = ground albedo (≈ **0.2 for grass, 0.8 for fresh snow**).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{ph} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy from wavelength $\lambda$ (nm) | eV |
| 2 | $I_{ph} = q \cdot G \cdot A$ | Photocurrent proportional to irradiance $G$ | A |
| 3 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = FF \times V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$ | Maximum power extractable from a PV module | W |
| 4 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor (typ. **0.65 – 0.80** for silicon) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_{cell} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \times A_{cell}}$ | Cell conversion efficiency | % |
| 6 | $CUF = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed} \times 8760}$ | Capacity Utilization Factor | % |
| 7 | $PAF = \dfrac{\text{Available hours}}{8760}$ | Plant Availability Factor | % |
| 8 | $\text{PR} = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{ideal}}$ | Performance Ratio (typ. 0.75 – 0.85) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_{gen} = P_{installed} \times CUF \times 8760$ | Annual Energy Generation | kWh |
| 10 | $T_{cell} = T_{amb} + (NOCT - 20) \times \dfrac{G}{800}$ | Cell temperature from ambient | °C |
| 11 | $\eta_{plant} = \eta_{PV} \times \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{trans} \times \eta_{misc}$ | Overall plant efficiency chain | % |
| 12 | $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left(\dfrac{360(284+n)}{365}\right)$ | Solar declination | degrees |
| 13 | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum C_t / (1+r)^t}{\sum E_t / (1+r)^t}$ | Levelized Cost of Electricity | ₹/kWh |
| 14 | $Q_{useful} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Useful heat from solar collector | J/s or W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MNRE Benchmark Values for India:**
> - CUF target for **Solar PV** = **19%**
> - Module degradation = **≤ 0.5% per year** (linear, 25-year warranty)
> - Performance Ratio (PR) = **≥ 0.80** (acceptable), **≥ 0.85** (excellent)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Utility-scale SPV plants** (e.g., Bhadla Solar Park, Rajasthan — **2,245 MW**) form the backbone of India's **500 GW non-fossil target by 2030**.
- **Floating solar** (e.g., Omkareshwar, MP — **600 MW**) reduces land footprint and improves module efficiency by 5–10% due to water cooling.
- **CSP with storage** enables **dispatchable renewable power** beyond sun-hours, addressing the duck-curve problem.
- **Agrivoltaics** combines crop cultivation with PV generation, optimizing **Land Equivalent Ratio (LER) > 1.4**.
