# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define slip ratio. Why is its accurate estimation critical in the Traction Control System of an Electric Vehicle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Slip ratio $\lambda$ is defined as the normalized difference between the wheel contact-patch velocity $V_w$ and the vehicle chassis velocity $V_v$: $\lambda = \vert V_w - V_v \vert / V_v$. It is a dimensionless number in the range $[0, 1]$.
- **EV-specific criticality (1.5 marks):** Because EV motors deliver **peak torque at zero rpm**, the wheel can jump from $\lambda = 0$ to $\lambda = 1$ in milliseconds on a low-μ surface. An over-estimation of $\lambda$ triggers false intervention (jerky acceleration, torque hole); an under-estimation fails to prevent slip. The slip ratio is also the **only** feedback signal that directly measures the operating point on the μ–λ curve, so the entire control law depends on its accuracy.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional I]**
**List and briefly explain THREE methods used by an EV Traction Control System to limit wheel slip.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Motor Torque Limitation (MTC):** The VCU sends a torque-reduction request to the Motor Control Unit over CAN, reducing the motor's electromagnetic torque until slip falls below threshold. Smooth, non-mechanical, no wear.
2. **Selective Wheel Braking (BTCS):** The brake-hydraulic modulator applies pressure **only** to the spinning wheel, transferring some of the torque demand to the brake pad. Fastest response; recovers a fully spinning wheel.
3. **Differential Torque Vectoring (DTV):** In a dual-motor EV, torque is redistributed between left and right driven wheels so that the slower (gripping) wheel takes more torque and the faster (slipping) wheel takes less. Combines stability with energy efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ▶ QUESTION A — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester, Module 4 Set A]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a Traction Control System in a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)**. Clearly identify the sensor layer, computation layer, and actuator layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EV with rear-wheel drive has the following data: vehicle mass $m = 1800$ kg, wheel effective radius $r_w = 0.33$ m, gear ratio $i_g = 8.5$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_d = 0.92$, motor torque $T_m = 350$ N·m. The driven wheels spin at $\omega_w = 95$ rad/s while the non-driven wheels indicate a vehicle speed of $V_v = 18$ m/s. The road has peak friction $\mu_{peak} = 0.90$ at optimal slip $\lambda^* = 0.10$.
**(i)** Calculate the slip ratio and determine if TCS must intervene. **(ii)** If a proportional controller with $K_p = 1800$ N·m is used, compute the corrective torque command. **(iii)** What is the resulting longitudinal propulsive force? **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the diagram in SECTION 4.1 above. Marks awarded for:
- Correct identification of **sensors**: 4 wheel speed sensors + IMU + yaw rate sensor + steering angle sensor. (1 mark)
- **Computation layer**: VCU/TCS ECU, slip calculator, vehicle reference speed estimator, PID controller. (1 mark)
- **Actuator layer**: Motor Control Unit (inverter), EBD/hydraulic modulator, regen brake controller, traction motor. (1 mark)

**Explanation (4 marks):**
- Wheel speed sensors feed into the VRS estimator, which selects the **slowest non-driven wheel** as the kinematic reference (since driven wheels may be slipping and therefore untrustworthy). (1 mark)
- The slip calculator computes $\lambda = (V_w - V_{v,ref})/V_{v,ref}$ for each driven wheel every 10 ms. (1 mark)
- The PID controller compares $\lambda$ to $\lambda^* = 0.10$ and generates a torque-correction request $\Delta T_m$. (1 mark)
- The actuator layer applies correction via **three hierarchical paths** (in order of preference): regen torque cut → motor torque reduction → hydraulic brake. This minimizes brake wear and energy loss. (1 mark)

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Slip ratio (2 marks):**

$$
V_w = r_w \cdot \omega_w = 0.33 \times 95 = 31.35 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{V_w - V_v}{V_v} = \frac{31.35 - 18}{18} = 0.7417
$$

Since $\lambda = 0.7417 \;\gg\; \lambda^* = 0.10$, the wheel is in **deep slip** on the unstable falling slope of the μ–λ curve → **TCS must intervene aggressively**. (1 mark for the comparison and decision.)

**(ii) Corrective torque (2 marks):**

$$
e_\lambda = \lambda - \lambda^* = 0.7417 - 0.10 = 0.6417
$$

$$
\Delta T_m = K_p \cdot e_\lambda = 1800 \times 0.6417 = 1155.06 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
T_{m,cmd} = T_m - \Delta T_m = 350 - 1155.06 = -805.06 \text{ N·m}
$$

The negative command indicates that **motor alone cannot recover grip**; the controller must also activate **regenerative braking** of 805 N·m, and if regen authority is insufficient, also command **hydraulic brake intervention** at the slipping wheel.

**(iii) Propulsive force (3 marks):**

If we assume the controller fully corrects the slip (i.e., system reaches $\lambda^*$), the operating adhesion coefficient is $\mu(\lambda^*) = \mu_{peak} = 0.90$. The normal load on the driven axle is approximately $F_z = mg/2 = 1800 \times 9.81 / 2 = 8829$ N (ignoring load transfer).

$$
F_{x,max} = \mu_{peak} \cdot F_z = 0.90 \times 8829 = 7946.1 \text{ N}
$$

Equivalently, the maximum allowable motor torque at the wheel is:

$$
T_{w,max} = F_{x,max} \cdot r_w = 7946.1 \times 0.33 = 2622.2 \text{ N·m}
$$

which corresponds to a motor-side torque of $T_{m,max} = T_{w,max}/(i_g \cdot \eta_d) = 2622.2/(8.5 \times 0.92) \approx 335.2$ N·m — confirming that the driver's 350 N·m command was **slightly too aggressive** for the available grip, hence the TCS intervention.

---

### ▶ QUESTION B — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester, Module 4 Set B]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **optimal slip ratio $\lambda^*$** in terms of Pacejka's Magic Formula parameters $B$ and $C$. Why do production EV Traction Controllers deliberately operate at a slip threshold **below** $\lambda^*$? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **μ–λ adhesion characteristic** of a pneumatic tire. Mark the stable region, the unstable region, the optimal point, and the typical operating envelope of an EV TCS controller. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 marks

**Derivation (5 marks):** Use the simplified Pacejka form $\mu(\lambda) = D \sin(C \arctan(B\lambda))$. Take $d\mu/d\lambda$, set to zero, solve the trig equation.

The full derivation is presented in **SECTION 3.2** of these notes. The final result is:

$$
\boxed{\lambda^{*} = \frac{1}{B}\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2C}\right)}
$$

**Why production EVs operate below $\lambda^*$ (2 marks):**
1. **Robustness to estimation error:** The slip-ratio estimator has inherent inaccuracy (especially at low vehicle speeds where $V_v \to 0$ makes $\lambda$ ill-conditioned). Operating on the rising slope means a small over-estimation of $\lambda$ still gives safe operation, whereas operating near the peak means a small over-shoot sends the controller into the unstable region.
2. **Transient safety margin:** Tyre properties change with temperature, pressure, water film, and wear. The peak shifts. A conservative $\lambda_{th} \approx 0.08$ to $0.10$ ensures operation remains stable even on a degraded surface.
3. **Comfort:** Operating at peak $\mu$ requires high-frequency torque modulation, which passengers perceive as "judder". A lower threshold gives smoother torque delivery.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 marks

**Diagram (4 marks):** Draw the μ–λ curve as in SECTION 4.2. Marks for:
- Correct shape: monotonically rising to a single peak, then falling. (1 mark)
- Labelling of axes: $\lambda$ horizontal ($0$ to $1$), $\mu$ vertical ($0$ to ~$1.2$). (1 mark)
- Marking the **stable region** (rising slope, $\lambda < \lambda^*$), **unstable region** (falling slope, $\lambda > \lambda^*$), peak point $(\lambda^*, \mu_{peak})$, and the **TCS operating envelope** as a shaded band on the rising slope just below $\lambda^*$. (2 marks)

**Description (3 marks):**
- The curve originates at $\mu(0) = 0$ (free-rolling) and rises with **positive slope** until $\lambda = \lambda^*$ where $\mu = \mu_{peak}$ — this is the **stable region** where small disturbances in $\lambda$ are self-correcting by the tire. (1 mark)
- Beyond $\lambda^*$, the curve descends — this is the **unstable region** where any increase in slip reduces friction, which further increases slip, leading to runaway wheel spin. (1 mark)
- The EV TCS controller aims to keep the operating point in the **shaded envelope** on the stable rising slope (typically $\lambda \in [0.05, 0.12]$ for dry tarmac), which maximises the trade-off between traction force and safety margin. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on Traction Control Questions in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to clamp $\lambda$ to $[0, 1]$** — when $V_v$ is small or zero (launch from rest), $\lambda$ mathematically tends to infinity. The ECU and the board-examiner expect the student to explicitly state the **clamping rule**. Lose 1 mark if omitted.
>
> 2. **Confusing the two slip-ratio sign conventions** — driving vs braking. In KTU, always state the convention first, then write the equation.
>
> 3. **Treating $V_v$ as the wheel speed** — a shocking number of scripts compute $V_v = r_w \omega_w$ for the chassis. The chassis speed is **estimated** from the slowest non-driven wheel, not the driven wheel under regulation.
>
> 4. **Ignoring drivetrain efficiency $\eta_d$** — the propulsive force is $F_x = T_m \eta_d i_g / r_w$. Skipping $\eta_d$ costs a full mark.
>
> 5. **Writing "TCS prevents wheel slip" as a definition** — this is circular. TCS **regulates slip ratio to a target value**, it does NOT eliminate it. Use the precise phrase: *"TCS modulates motor torque to maintain the slip ratio at an optimal operating point $\lambda \le \lambda_{th}$."*
>
> 6. **Skipping the closed-loop block diagram in 7-mark questions** — even a hand-drawn ASCII or labelled rectangle diagram is mandatory. Examiners allocate 2–3 marks purely for the diagram.
>
> 7. **Ignoring regenerative braking** — EV-specific TCS MUST discuss coordination with regen. Failing to mention this loses 1 mark in any 14-mark question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Carry):**
>
> - [ ] **Definition:** TCS is a closed-loop controller that regulates wheel slip ratio $\lambda$ to an optimal value $\lambda^*$ to maximize traction force and prevent instability.
> - [ ] **Slip Ratio Formula:** $\lambda = \vert V_w - V_v \vert / V_v$, clamped to $[0, 1]$.
> - [ ] **Wheel velocity:** $V_w = r_w \omega_w$.
> - [ ] **Pacejka optimum:** $\lambda^* = (1/B)\tan(\pi/(2C))$, with typical $\lambda^* \approx 0.08$–$0.15$ for passenger-car EV tires.
> - [ ] **Operating philosophy:** Always operate on the **stable rising slope** of the μ–λ curve, never on the falling slope.
> - [ ] **Three intervention paths** (in order of preference): **regen torque cut → motor torque reduction → selective hydraulic brake**.
> - [ ] **VRS estimation:** Use the **slowest non-driven wheel** as the chassis-speed reference; never trust a slipping driven wheel.
> - [ ] **Control law:** PID with $e_\lambda = \lambda - \lambda^*$, and the corrective torque $\Delta T_m = K_p e_\lambda + K_i \int e_\lambda \, dt + K_d \dot{e}_\lambda$.
> - [ ] **EV-specific dangers:** Instant peak torque at zero rpm; no clutch/torque-converter slip buffer; high regen-induced wheel lock risk.
> - [ ] **Block diagram:** Sensors → VCU (VRS estimator + slip calculator + PID) → Actuators (MCU + EBD + Regen) → Motor & Brakes → Tire-Road → back to sensors.
> - [ ] **Stability proof:** Use Lyapunov candidate $V = \tfrac{1}{2}J_w \omega_w^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}K_p e_\lambda^2 \ge 0$ with $\dot V \le 0$.
> - [ ] **Industry references:** UNECE R13-H braking regulation; ISO 26262 ASIL-D functional safety for EV TCS.
> - [ ] **Production systems:** Bosch, Continental, ZF, Tesla in-house implementations; threshold $\lambda_{th} \approx 0.10$.
> - [ ] **KTU keyword phrases to memorize:** *"modulates motor torque"*, *"closed-loop slip regulation"*, *"optimal adhesion coefficient"*, *"Pacejka's Magic Formula"*, *"vehicle reference speed estimation"*, *"regen-coordinated intervention"*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forward Motoring and Generating Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forward Motoring and Generating Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a **Chopper-Fed DC Motor Drive**, the **Forward Motoring Mode** and **Forward Generating Mode** (also called **Forward Regenerative Braking Mode**) refer to the two principal operating states of the motor in the **positive speed-positive torque** (Quadrant I) and **positive speed-negative torque** (Quadrant II) regions of the four-quadrant torque-speed plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forward Motoring Mode (Quadrant I):** The DC motor rotates in the forward (positive) direction, draws electrical energy from the DC source via the chopper, and delivers mechanical power to the load. Both armature voltage $E_a$ and armature current $I_a$ are **positive**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forward Generating Mode (Quadrant II):** The motor continues to rotate in the forward direction, but its back-EMF exceeds the source voltage. The motor acts as a **DC generator**, and the kinetic energy stored in the rotating mass (and load) is returned back to the DC source through the chopper. Armature voltage $E_a$ is positive, but current $I_a$ is **negative**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **battery-powered electric bicycle** going downhill:

- **Forward Motoring (Uphill Ride):** You press the throttle. Current flows **from the battery → motor**, the motor spins the wheel forward, helping you climb uphill. Battery energy is being *spent*.

- **Forward Generating (Downhill Ride):** You stop pedaling on a steep downhill. The wheel keeps spinning forward (due to gravity), the motor is now being *driven by the wheel*, and instead of consuming current, it *pushes current back into the battery*. The motor acts as a generator, and the bike decelerates — this is regenerative braking.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme assessment questions, students frequently confuse the **polarity of $I_a$** during motoring vs. generating. Always remember:
> - Motoring: Source delivers energy → $I_a$ flows **out** of the +ve terminal of source.
> - Generating: Motor delivers energy → $I_a$ flows **into** the +ve terminal of source.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities Involved

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Source / Supply Voltage | $V_s$ | Volts (V) |
| Average Armature Voltage | $E_a$ (or $V_a$) | Volts (V) |
| Back-EMF Constant | $K_b$ or $K_E$ | V·s/rad |
| Torque Constant | $K_t$ | N·m/A |
| Armature Current | $I_a$ | Amperes (A) |
| Motor Speed | $N$ (or $\omega$) | rpm (rad/s) |
| Field Flux | $\phi$ | Weber (Wb) |
| Duty Cycle of Chopper | $\alpha$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Four-Quadrant Operation Concept

The torque–speed characteristic of a separately excited DC motor is divided into **four operating quadrants**:

| Quadrant | Speed ($\omega$) | Torque ($T$) | Mode | Power Flow |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | $+$ | $+$ | Forward Motoring | Electrical → Mechanical |
| **II** | $+$ | $-$ | Forward Generating | Mechanical → Electrical |
| **III** | $-$ | $-$ | Reverse Motoring | Electrical → Mechanical |
| **IV** | $-$ | $+$ | Reverse Generating | Mechanical → Electrical |

> [!NOTE]
> For **Module 4 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, focus is on Quadrants I and II, realized by **Class A and Class B choppers** in their basic forms, and combined in the **Class C (two-quadrant) chopper**.

## 2.2 Class A Chopper — Forward Motoring Mode

The **Class A chopper** consists of a single controlled switch ($T$), a freewheeling diode ($D_{FW}$), and is connected between the DC source $V_s$ and the DC motor armature (L–R–E load).

**Operating Principle:**
1. When $T$ is **ON** for time $T_{on}$: Source $V_s$ is connected across the armature. The armature current $I_a$ rises through $L_a$ and $R_a$, and the motor develops forward torque.
2. When $T$ is **OFF** for time $T_{off}$: The source is disconnected. The inductive armature current freewheels through $D_{FW}$, and the energy stored in $L_a$ circulates until $T$ is turned ON again.

**Average Output Voltage:**

$$
E_{a,avg} = \alpha \cdot V_s
$$

where $\alpha = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T}$ is the **duty cycle**.

**Average Armature Current:**

$$
I_{a,avg} = \dfrac{E_{a,avg} - E_b}{R_a}
$$

**Motor Back-EMF:**

$$
E_b = K_b \cdot \phi \cdot \omega
$$

**Developed Torque:**

$$
T = K_t \cdot \phi \cdot I_a
$$

## 2.3 Class B Chopper — Forward Generating Mode

The **Class B chopper** (also called the **step-up chopper** or **boost converter in motoring terms, but here used as a regenerative chopper**) consists of a switch $T$, a diode $D$, and a large inductor (the motor armature itself serves as $L$). The polarity of the motor back-EMF $E_b$ is arranged such that it acts as the *input source* during regeneration, and $V_s$ acts as the *load* (battery being charged).

**Operating Principle:**
1. When $T$ is **ON**: Motor terminals are shorted through $T$. The motor armature current $I_a$ rises (in magnitude) due to back-EMF $E_b$, and kinetic energy is stored as magnetic energy in $L_a$.
2. When $T$ is **OFF**: The motor current $I_a$ is forced through diode $D$ into the source $V_s$. Since $E_b > V_s$ (required for regeneration), current flows from motor → source, feeding energy back. This is the **regenerative action**.

**Average Voltage Across Motor Terminals (during regeneration):**

$$
E_{a,avg} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot V_s
$$

Since $E_{a,avg}$ is negative (during regeneration in conventional notation), the equation can also be written as:

$$
V_s = E_b \cdot (1 - \alpha)
$$

The **regeneration condition** is:

$$
E_b > \dfrac{V_s}{1 - \alpha}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{a,avg} = \alpha V_s$ | Avg. armature voltage — Class A (motoring) | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$; Volts |
| 2 | $E_{a,avg} = (1-\alpha) V_s$ | Avg. motor terminal voltage — Class B (regen) | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$; Volts |
| 3 | $I_{a,avg} = \dfrac{E_{a,avg} - E_b}{R_a}$ | Steady-state armature current | Amperes |
| 4 | $E_b = K_b \phi \omega$ | Back-EMF equation | V, with $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 5 | $T = K_t \phi I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{V_s - I_a R_a}{K_b \phi} \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi}$ | Motor speed in rpm | rpm |
| 7 | $\omega = \dfrac{V_s(1-\alpha) - I_a R_a}{K_b \phi}$ | Speed during regeneration | rad/s |
| 8 | $P_{regen} = V_s I_{a,regen}$ | Power returned to source | Watts |
| 9 | $E_b > V_s$ (must hold) | Necessary condition for regeneration (without chopper) | Volts |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Regenerative braking recovers kinetic energy and recharges the battery, extending range by **15–30%** (industry-standard claim by **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)**).
- **Elevators and Cranes:** Lowering a loaded elevator requires regenerative braking to prevent free-fall and recover energy.
- **Locomotive Drives:** DC traction motors use Class B/C choppers for regenerative braking when descending gradients.
- **Industrial Servo Drives:** High-precision position control uses regeneration to decelerate quickly without heat dissipation in resistors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of a Class A Chopper

Consider the chopper output waveform over one switching period $T$:

- For $0 \le t \le T_{on}$: $v_o(t) = V_s$
- For $T_{on} \le t \le T$: $v_o(t) = 0$ (freewheeling through $D_{FW}$)

The average output voltage is the integral over one period divided by the period:

$$
E_{a,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_o(t)\, dt
$$

Substitute the piecewise waveform:

$$
E_{a,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_0^{T_{on}} V_s\, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0\, dt \right]
$$

Evaluate the integral — the source contributes only during the ON time:

$$
E_{a,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot T_{on} \right]
$$

Define the duty cycle $\alpha$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{T_{on}}{T}
$$

Substitute into the expression:

$$
\boxed{\;E_{a,avg} = \alpha \cdot V_s\;}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of a Class B Chopper

In Class B, the source $V_s$ appears across the load for the **OFF** duration of the switch, and the load (motor) is shorted during the ON duration.

- For $0 \le t \le T_{on}$: $v_o(t) = 0$ (motor shorted)
- For $T_{on} \le t \le T$: $v_o(t) = V_s$ (current freewheels through diode into source)

$$
E_{a,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_0^{T_{on}} 0\, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} V_s\, dt \right]
$$

Evaluate the integral — the source contributes only during the OFF time:

$$
E_{a,avg} = \frac{V_s}{T} \cdot (T - T_{on}) = V_s \cdot \frac{T - T_{on}}{T}
$$

Recognize that $\dfrac{T - T_{on}}{T} = 1 - \alpha$:

$$
\boxed{\;E_{a,avg} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot V_s\;}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Steady-State Armature Current (Forward Motoring)

Apply KVL across the armature loop when the chopper switch is ON:

$$
V_s = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b
$$

In steady-state, average of $L_a \dfrac{dI_a}{dt}$ over one cycle is zero (volt-second balance). The average KVL becomes:

$$
V_s = I_{a,avg} R_a + E_b
$$

Substitute $V_s = \dfrac{E_{a,avg}}{\alpha}$ from the Class A chopper equation:

$$
\frac{E_{a,avg}}{\alpha} = I_{a,avg} R_a + E_b
$$

Solving for $I_{a,avg}$:

$$
\boxed{\;I_{a,avg} = \frac{\dfrac{E_{a,avg}}{\alpha} - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{E_{a,avg} - \alpha E_b}{\alpha R_a}\;}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Speed Equation in Forward Generating Mode

During regeneration (Class B chopper), the motor acts as a generator. Apply KVL with the current now flowing *back* into the source:

$$
E_b = I_a R_a + E_{a,avg}
$$

The motor terminal voltage $E_{a,avg} = (1-\alpha) V_s$. Substitute:

$$
E_b = I_a R_a + (1-\alpha) V_s
$$

Solve for $E_b$:

$$
E_b = I_a R_a + (1-\alpha) V_s
$$

Using $E_b = K_b \phi \omega$, solve for speed:

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_{regen} = \frac{(1-\alpha) V_s + I_a R_a}{K_b \phi}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **positive sign before $I_a R_a$** during regeneration — this contrasts with the motoring equation where it is negative. This is because the current direction reverses.

## 3.5 Derivation: Condition for Regeneration

For current to actually flow *into* the source (i.e., for true regeneration), the source voltage must be **less than** the motor back-EMF when referred through the chopper. From the Class B equation:

$$
E_b > (1 - \alpha) V_s
$$

Dividing both sides by $(1-\alpha)$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{E_b}{1-\alpha} > V_s \quad \text{(Required for Forward Regeneration)}\;}
$$

This means the chopper acts as a **step-up (boost) converter** from the motor's perspective — boosting $E_b$ to a value higher than $V_s$ so that current can flow back into the source.

## 3.6 Numerical Example (Worked Model Problem)

**Given:**
- Source voltage $V_s = 220$ V
- Armature resistance $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$
- Armature inductance $L_a = 10$ mH
- Back-EMF constant $K_b \phi = 1.2$ V·s/rad
- Duty cycle in motoring $\alpha = 0.6$

**Find:** Average armature voltage and motor speed at $I_a = 20$ A.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute average armature voltage:

$$
E_{a,avg} = \alpha V_s = 0.6 \times 220 = 132 \text{ V}
$$

Step 2 — Compute back-EMF:

$$
E_b = V_s - I_a R_a = 220 - (20)(0.5) = 220 - 10 = 210 \text{ V}
$$

Step 3 — Compute speed:

$$
\omega = \frac{V_s - I_a R_a}{K_b \phi} = \frac{210}{1.2} = 175 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Step 4 — Convert to rpm:

$$
N = \omega \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 175 \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 1671 \text{ rpm}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class A Chopper Circuit (Forward Motoring)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vsrc[DC Source Vs] --- ChopT[Chopper Switch T]
    ChopT --- node1[Node A]
    node1 --- La[Armature La]
    La --- node2[Node B]
    node2 --- Ra[Armature Ra]
    Ra --- node3[Node C]
    node3 --- Eb[Back EMF Eb]
    Eb --- Vsrc
    node1 --- Dfw[Freewheel Diode DFW]
    Dfw --- node3
```

## 4.2 Class B Chopper Circuit (Forward Generating)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vsrc[DC Source Vs] --- node1[Positive Rail]
    node1 --- node2[Node A]
    node2 --- La[Armature Inductance La]
    La --- node3[Node B]
    node3 --- Ra[Armature Ra]
    Ra --- Eb[Back EMF Eb]
    Eb --- node4[Negative Rail]
    node4 --- Vsrc
    node2 --- SwitchT[Chopper Switch T]
    SwitchT --- node4
    node3 --- DiodeD[Diode D]
    DiodeD --- node1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Forward Motoring & Generating Control Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Ctrl[Speed Reference Nref] --> Comp[Comparator]
    FB[Actual Speed N] --> Comp
    Comp --> PI[PI Controller]
    PI --> PWMGen[PWM Generator]
    PWMGen --> GateDrv[Gate Driver]
    GateDrv --> Chopper[Chopper Switch]
    Chopper --> Motor[DC Motor]
    Motor --> Load[Mechanical Load]
    Load --> FB
    Motor --> Sense[Current Sensor Ia]
    Sense --> Protection[Overcurrent Protection]
    Protection --> Comp
    Chopper -.->|Mode Select| Mode[Mode Logic: Motoring or Generating]
    Mode --> Chopper
```

## 4.4 Operating Waveforms — Forward Motoring

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Time Axis
    T1[t0] --> T2[t1 TON]
    T2 --> T3[t2 T]
    end
    T1 --> Vhigh[Vs: Switch ON]
    T2 --> Vlow[0: Switch OFF]
    T3 --> Vhigh2[Vs: Next cycle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The waveform shows that during the **ON** period ($T_{on}$), the armature sees full source voltage $V_s$. During the **OFF** period, the freewheeling diode conducts, and the motor terminal voltage is zero (ideally).

## 4.5 Four-Quadrant Operating Plane

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q2[Quadrant II: Forward Generating] ---|T negative, omega positive| Q1[Quadrant I: Forward Motoring]
    Q3[Quadrant III: Reverse Motoring] ---|T negative, omega negative| Q2
    Q4[Quadrant IV: Reverse Generating] ---|T positive, omega negative| Q3
    Q1 ---|T positive, omega positive| Q4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Distinguish between the forward motoring and forward generating (regenerative) modes of operation of a chopper-fed DC motor drive.**

**Model Answer:**
In forward motoring mode, the chopper connects the DC source to the motor armature, supplying electrical energy. The motor develops positive torque in the forward direction and drives the mechanical load. Both armature voltage and current are positive.

In forward generating mode, the motor continues to rotate in the forward direction, but its back-EMF exceeds the source voltage. The chopper (Class B) allows the motor current to reverse, and the motor acts as a generator, feeding kinetic energy back into the DC source. Armature voltage remains positive, but current becomes negative. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is a Class A chopper unsuitable for regenerative braking of a DC motor?**

**Model Answer:**
A Class A chopper uses a single switch and freewheeling diode arranged such that current can flow only in one direction — from source to motor. During regeneration, the current must reverse direction (from motor to source), but the Class A topology does not provide a path for reverse current. Hence, a Class B or Class C chopper must be used for regenerative (forward generating) operation. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **Class A chopper-fed DC motor drive** in the forward motoring mode. Derive the expression for average armature voltage and average armature current. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V, 1500 rpm, 10 A separately excited DC motor has armature resistance of $1 \, \Omega$ and field constant $K_b \phi = 0.5$ V·s/rad. It is fed from a Class A chopper with source voltage 230 V and duty cycle $\alpha = 0.7$. Compute:
- (i) Average armature voltage
- (ii) Armature current at this operating point
- (iii) Motor speed in rpm **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Class A Chopper Operation:**

The Class A chopper consists of a controlled switch $T$ (IGBT/BJT/MOSFET) and a freewheeling diode $D_{FW}$ connected in anti-parallel with the armature-inductance combination. The motor armature is modeled as $L_a$–$R_a$–$E_b$.

- **During $T_{on}$**: Switch $T$ is ON. Source $V_s$ is applied directly across the armature. Current rises through $L_a$ and produces forward torque.
- **During $T_{off}$**: Switch $T$ is OFF. The motor current freewheels through $D_{FW}$. The armature terminals are shorted (voltage across them is zero).

**Average Armature Voltage:**

$$
E_{a,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt = \alpha V_s
$$

**Average Armature Current:** Applying KVL and using volt-second balance over $L_a$:

$$
I_{a,avg} = \frac{\alpha V_s - E_b}{R_a}
$$

**(7 marks — full marks with circuit diagram + derivations)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_s = 230$ V, $\alpha = 0.7$, $R_a = 1 \, \Omega$, $K_b \phi = 0.5$ V·s/rad.

**(i) Average Armature Voltage:**

$$
E_{a,avg} = \alpha V_s = 0.7 \times 230 = 161 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Armature Current:** Using the rated condition (assume motor rated at 10 A initially):

$$
E_b = V_s - I_a R_a = 230 - (10)(1) = 220 \text{ V (back-EMF at rated condition)}
$$

At $\alpha = 0.7$:

$$
I_{a,avg} = \frac{\alpha V_s - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{161 - 220}{1} = -59 \text{ A}
$$

The negative sign indicates the motor is now operating in **regenerative mode** (since reduced voltage cannot sustain the original back-EMF, the motor returns energy). For motoring mode to hold, we need a smaller speed (or lower back-EMF).

**Solving correctly for steady-state speed:**

In motoring at steady state, $V_s = I_a R_a + E_b$ in average form means $\alpha V_s = I_a R_a + E_b$. For the given chopper voltage:

$$
E_{a,avg} = \alpha V_s = 161 \text{ V}
$$

Assuming motor runs at some current $I_a$ such that back-EMF supports motoring:

$$
E_b = \alpha V_s - I_a R_a = 161 - I_a
$$

For current to be positive (motoring), $E_b < 161$ V. At $I_a = 10$ A (rated), $E_b = 151$ V.

**(iii) Motor Speed:**

$$
\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_b \phi} = \frac{151}{0.5} = 302 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
N = \omega \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{302 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 2884 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Alternative]

**(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the operation of a **Class B chopper** in the **forward generating (regenerative) mode**. Derive the expression for the average armature voltage and state the condition for regeneration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 200 V DC series motor with $R_a = 0.8 \, \Omega$ is fed from a Class B chopper with source voltage 220 V. During regeneration, the back-EMF is 250 V. Determine the minimum and maximum duty cycle for regeneration to take place. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Class B Chopper Operation:**

The Class B chopper uses a controlled switch $T$ in series with the armature and a diode $D$ connected to allow current flow back into the source.

- **During $T_{on}$**: Switch $T$ is ON. The motor terminals are shorted (armature current circulates through $T$ and the motor's $L_a$ and $R_a$). Kinetic energy is stored as magnetic energy in $L_a$.
- **During $T_{off}$**: Switch $T$ is OFF. The motor current is forced through diode $D$ into the source $V_s$. Since the motor's back-EMF $E_b > V_s$, current flows from motor → source, returning energy.

**Average Voltage Across Motor Terminals:**

$$
E_{a,avg} = (1 - \alpha) V_s
$$

**Condition for Regeneration:**

$$
E_b > (1 - \alpha) V_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{E_b}{1-\alpha} > V_s
$$

**(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_s = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.8 \, \Omega$, $E_b = 250$ V.

**Condition:** $E_b > (1 - \alpha) V_s$:

$$
250 > (1 - \alpha)(220)
$$

$$
1 - \alpha < \frac{250}{220} = 1.136
$$

Since $1 - \alpha \le 1$ always, the condition is satisfied for all $\alpha \in [0, 1)$.

**Maximum Duty Cycle:** $\alpha_{max} = 1 - \dfrac{V_s}{E_b} = 1 - \dfrac{220}{250} = 1 - 0.88 = 0.12$

**Minimum Duty Cycle:** $\alpha_{min} = 0$ (regeneration possible at any low duty cycle since $E_b > V_s$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> Students often forget that during regeneration, the current $I_a$ in the formula $E_b = I_a R_a + (1-\alpha) V_s$ has a **negative sign convention** for armature current direction. Reversing the sign convention will cost **2 marks**. Also, students frequently assume regeneration occurs at any duty cycle — but the condition $E_b > (1-\alpha) V_s$ must hold, otherwise the diode $D$ will not conduct (it will be reverse-biased) and no energy is returned.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Forward Motoring (Quadrant I):** Source supplies power. Both $E_a$ and $I_a$ are positive. Implemented by **Class A chopper**.
- ✅ **Forward Generating / Regeneration (Quadrant II):** Motor returns energy. $E_a$ positive, $I_a$ negative. Implemented by **Class B chopper**.
- ✅ **Class A** average voltage: $E_{a,avg} = \alpha V_s$.
- ✅ **Class B** average voltage: $E_{a,avg} = (1 - \alpha) V_s$.
- ✅ **Back-EMF equation:** $E_b = K_b \phi \omega$.
- ✅ **Torque equation:** $T = K_t \phi I_a$.
- ✅ **Regeneration condition:** $E_b > \dfrac{V_s}{1 - \alpha}$ (Class B operation).
- ✅ **Energy recovered** in regenerative braking: $P_{regen} = V_s \cdot I_{a,regen}$.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** EVs, elevators, cranes, traction motors, servo drives.
- ✅ **Sign convention trap:** In regeneration, $I_a$ flows *opposite* to motoring direction. Always mark polarity clearly in circuit diagrams.
- ✅ **Class C chopper** combines Class A and Class B in a single circuit to achieve two-quadrant operation (motoring + regeneration in forward direction).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PWM Control Techniques: Voltage Mode
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - Power Electronics & Drives
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PWM Control Techniques: Voltage Mode

# PWM Control Techniques: Voltage Mode Control (VMC)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Voltage Mode Control (VMC)** is a single-loop Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) control strategy for switch-mode power converters in which **only the output voltage** is sensed, fed back, and compared against an internal reference to produce a duty-cycle command through a single feedback path. The compensator output (control voltage $v_c$) is directly compared with a fixed-amplitude, fixed-frequency **ramp (sawtooth) carrier waveform** to generate the gate drive for the active power switch.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic belongs to **Module 4: PWM Control Techniques** and covers the foundational analog control method that dominated SMPS design before current-mode control became widespread.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The "Thermostat on a Wall"**
>
> Imagine a room with an electric heater (the *converter*) that must keep the room temperature at **24 °C** (the *desired output voltage*). You install a wall thermostat that:
> 1. **Measures** the room temperature (output voltage sensing).
> 2. **Subtracts** it from the set-point of 24 °C to get an *error* (error amplifier).
> 3. **Decides** how hard the heater should run — a little error → small flame, big error → huge flame (PI compensator).
> 4. Sends that "heat intensity" command to a clockwork timer that chops the heater ON/OFF each second (PWM comparator with sawtooth).
>
> If the room gets too hot, the error shrinks, the heat intensity drops, and the heater stays OFF for a longer slice of every cycle. If the room gets too cold, the opposite happens. **Voltage Mode Control works exactly the same way** — but with volts, amps, MOSFETs, and a sawtooth waveform.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|-------------------------|------------------|
| Switching frequency ($f_s$) | **20 kHz – 1 MHz** | Rate at which the converter switch is operated |
| Sawtooth peak ($V_{st,pk}$) | Typically **3–5 V** | Carrier amplitude of the PWM modulator |
| Reference voltage ($V_{ref}$) | Typically **1.2 V / 2.5 V / 5 V** | Internal bandgap reference (TL431, etc.) |
| Modulator gain ($F_m$) | $\mathbf{1/V_{st,pk}}$ | Conversion factor from control voltage to duty cycle |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The Voltage Mode Control loop operates in **four cascading stages**:

1. **Output Voltage Sensing** — A resistor divider scales down the high output voltage to a level compatible with the reference (e.g., $V_{out} = 12$ V scaled to $V_{fb} = 2.5$ V).

2. **Error Generation** — The feedback voltage $V_{fb}$ is subtracted from the precision reference $V_{ref}$ at the inverting input of an op-amp error amplifier:
$$
v_{err}(t) = V_{ref} - V_{fb}(t)
$$

3. **Compensation (PI / Type-II / Type-III)** — The error is shaped by a compensation network to provide adequate phase margin (typically 45°–60°) and DC gain. The output is the control voltage $v_c(t)$.

4. **PWM Modulation** — $v_c(t)$ is compared with a fixed-frequency sawtooth carrier $v_{st}(t)$:
   - When $v_c > v_{st}$ → switch is **ON** (output high)
   - When $v_c < v_{st}$ → switch is **OFF** (output low)
   
   The intersection point sets the duty cycle $D$.

### 2.2 The Modulator Gain — Mathematical Heart of VMC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is VMC called "single-loop"?** Because the modulator directly converts **one signal** (the control voltage) into the duty ratio. There is no inductor current information inside the control loop.

The duty ratio $D$ relates linearly to the control voltage:
$$
D = \frac{v_c}{V_{st,pk}}
$$

Differentiating gives the **modulator gain** $F_m$:
$$
F_m = \frac{\hat{d}}{\hat{v}_c} = \frac{1}{V_{st,pk}}
$$

This gain has **units of V⁻¹** and is *constant* — a key property of voltage mode control that simplifies small-signal modeling.

### 2.3 Voltage Mode Control — The Two Sub-Topics

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 4 specifically requires study of **two configurations** of voltage mode PWM control:

#### A. Single-Loop Voltage Mode Control (Basic VMC)

- **One feedback path**: output voltage only.
- **Advantage**: Simple, requires few components, easy to stabilize.
- **Disadvantage**: 
  - Poor **line regulation** — the loop is slow to react to input voltage changes.
  - Output LC filter introduces a **double pole** at the resonant frequency, complicating compensation.
  - Open-loop audio susceptibility is high at low frequencies.

#### B. Voltage Mode Control with Input Voltage Feedforward

- **Two paths**: 
  1. Output voltage feedback (slow, accurate).
  2. Input voltage feedforward (instant, *open-loop* correction).
- The sawtooth carrier amplitude is **made proportional to the input voltage** $V_g$:
$$
V_{st,pk} = k \cdot V_g
$$
- Resulting duty cycle becomes:
$$
D = \frac{v_c}{k \cdot V_g}
$$
- Since for a buck converter $V_o = D \cdot V_g$, the line-audio-susceptibility is **theoretically zeroed out**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Essential VMC Formulas (Memorize for University Exam)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $D = v_c / V_{st,pk}$ | Duty cycle from control voltage | dimensionless |
| 2 | $F_m = 1 / V_{st,pk}$ | PWM modulator gain | V⁻¹ |
| 3 | $G_{vd}(s) = \dfrac{\hat{v}_o(s)}{\hat{d}(s)}$ | Control-to-output transfer function (converter dependent) | V |
| 4 | $G_{vg}(s) = \dfrac{\hat{v}_o(s)}{\hat{v}_g(s)}$ | Line-to-output (audio susceptibility) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $T(s) = G_c(s) \cdot F_m \cdot G_{vd}(s)$ | Loop gain (single-loop VMC) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $D = v_c / (k V_g)$ | Duty cycle with feedforward | dimensionless |
| 7 | $G_{vg,ff}(s) \to 0$ (ideal) | Audio susceptibility with feedforward | dimensionless |
| 8 | $f_p = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ | LC filter double-pole frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $Q = R\sqrt{C/L}$ | Quality factor of output filter | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f_{ESR} = 1/(2\pi R_{ESR} C)$ | Zero from capacitor ESR | Hz |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse $G_{vd}$ (control-to-output) with $G_{vg}$ (line-to-output). They have **different transfer functions** and **different physical meanings**. $G_{vd}$ governs the closed-loop bandwidth; $G_{vg}$ governs line rejection.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Voltage Mode Control is the **workhorse of industrial SMPS** because:
- It is the natural choice for **multiple-output converters** (one feedback loop regulates all outputs).
- It is robust against noise in current-sensing paths.
- It is the preferred method for **high-voltage DC-DC converters** in renewable energy systems (PV micro-inverters) and telecom power supplies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Modulator Gain $F_m$ from First Principles

We derive the small-signal relationship between the control voltage $v_c$ and the duty cycle $D$ for a trailing-edge modulator.

**Step 1 — Define the sawtooth carrier.**

A sawtooth waveform with peak $V_{st,pk}$, period $T_s$, ramping from $0$ to $V_{st,pk}$ in each cycle is:
$$
v_{st}(t) = \frac{V_{st,pk}}{T_s} \cdot t, \quad 0 \le t \le T_s
$$

**Step 2 — Define the switching instant.**

The switch turns OFF when $v_{st}(t) = v_c$. Solving for the switching time $t_1$:
$$
v_c = \frac{V_{st,pk}}{T_s} \cdot t_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_1 = \frac{v_c \cdot T_s}{V_{st,pk}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the duty cycle.**

The duty cycle is the ratio of ON-time to the switching period:
$$
D = \frac{t_1}{T_s} = \frac{v_c}{V_{st,pk}}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce perturbations.**

Let $v_c = V_c + \hat{v}_c$ and $D = D_0 + \hat{d}$. Substituting and equating the small-signal parts:
$$
\hat{d} = \frac{\hat{v}_c}{V_{st,pk}}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the modulator gain.**

$$
F_m \equiv \frac{\hat{d}}{\hat{v}_c} = \frac{1}{V_{st,pk}} \quad \text{(V}^{-1}\text{)}
$$

> This is a **constant, frequency-independent** gain — a major simplifying assumption of averaged PWM modeling used throughout KTU syllabus derivations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Loop Gain of Single-Loop VMC

**Step 1 — Write the forward path from control voltage to output voltage.**

The forward path consists of three cascaded blocks:

- Compensator: $G_c(s)$
- PWM modulator: $F_m = 1/V_{st,pk}$
- Converter power stage: $G_{vd}(s)$

The output voltage perturbation is:
$$
\hat{v}_o(s) = G_c(s) \cdot F_m \cdot G_{vd}(s) \cdot \hat{v}_{err}(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Write the feedback path.**

The feedback network (resistor divider) has gain:
$$
H(s) = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

For a well-designed converter, $H(s)$ is a pure constant (no dynamics) in the frequencies of interest:
$$
\hat{v}_{fb}(s) = H \cdot \hat{v}_o(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the standard negative-feedback closed-loop equation.**

$$
\hat{v}_o(s) = G_c(s) F_m G_{vd}(s) \left[ \hat{v}_{ref}(s) - H \cdot \hat{v}_o(s) \right]
$$

Solving for $\hat{v}_o / \hat{v}_{ref}$:
$$
\frac{\hat{v}_o(s)}{\hat{v}_{ref}(s)} = \frac{G_c(s) F_m G_{vd}(s)}{1 + G_c(s) F_m G_{vd}(s) H}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the loop gain $T(s)$.**

$$
\boxed{\,T(s) = G_c(s) \cdot F_m \cdot G_{vd}(s) \cdot H\,}
$$

The crossover frequency $f_c$ (where $\vert T(j2\pi f_c) \vert = 1$) determines the closed-loop bandwidth, and the phase of $T$ at crossover determines the phase margin.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Why Feedforward Eliminates Audio Susceptibility

Consider a **buck converter** where, without feedforward:
$$
V_o = D \cdot V_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{v}_o = D \hat{v}_g + V_g \hat{d}
$$

In the open-loop case, with constant $D$:
$$
G_{vg}(s) = D
$$

**Step 1 — Modify the sawtooth to be input-dependent.**

Set the sawtooth peak proportional to the input voltage:
$$
V_{st,pk} = k \cdot V_g
$$

**Step 2 — Express the new duty cycle.**

$$
D = \frac{v_c}{k \cdot V_g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_o = D \cdot V_g = \frac{v_c}{k \cdot V_g} \cdot V_g = \frac{v_c}{k}
$$

The input voltage **cancels out algebraically**! Output is now a function only of $v_c$ (and the constant $k$).

**Step 3 — Take the perturbation to find audio susceptibility.**

$$
G_{vg,ff}(s) = \frac{\hat{v}_o(s)}{\hat{v}_g(s)}\bigg\vert_{\hat{d}=0} = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, finite bandwidth of the input feedforward amplifier and modulation delays leave a small residual. KTU 2024 questions expect the **ideal answer of zero** unless otherwise stated.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Buck Converter VMC Transfer Functions

For a buck converter in CCM with $L$, $C$, $R$ load, $R_{ESR}$, and switching frequency $\omega_s$:

$$
G_{vd}(s) = V_g \cdot \frac{1 + sR_{ESR}C}{1 + s\frac{L}{R} + s^2 LC}
$$

$$
G_{vg}(s) = D \cdot \frac{1 + sR_{ESR}C}{1 + s\frac{L}{R} + s^2 LC}
$$

**Poles and Zeros of $G_{vd}$:**

| Feature | Frequency | Physical Origin |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| DC gain | $G_{vd0} = V_g$ | Conversion ratio derivative |
| Double pole | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Output LC filter resonance |
| Quality factor | $Q = R\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$ | Load-dependent damping |
| ESR zero | $f_z = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{ESR}C}$ | Non-ideal capacitor |

> [!WARNING]
> The double pole at $f_0$ causes the phase to drop by **180° in a narrow band**, making VMC loop compensation more challenging than current-mode control. This is the central design issue KTU expects you to discuss.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Single-Loop Voltage Mode Control

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Reference Voltage Vref] --> B[Error Amplifier / Compensator Gc]
    O[Output Voltage Vo] --> P[Feedback Divider H]
    P --> B
    B --> C[Control Voltage vc]
    C --> D[PWM Comparator]
    Q[Sawtooth Carrier Vst] --> D
    D --> E[Gate Drive to Switch]
    E --> F[Power Converter Stage]
    F --> G[LC Output Filter]
    G --> O
    F -->|Vin| E
    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333
    style O fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#fde68a,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#fecaca,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:** The single loop closes through the **output voltage** only. The sawtooth $V_{st}$ enters the comparator as an *external* fixed signal — it has no dependence on $V_{in}$.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Voltage Mode Control with Input Voltage Feedforward

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Reference Voltage Vref] --> B[Error Amplifier Gc]
    O[Output Voltage Vo] --> P[Feedback Divider H]
    P --> B
    B --> C[Control Voltage vc]
    C --> D[PWM Comparator]
    S[Input Voltage Vg] --> T[Sawtooth Generator]
    T -->|Vst proportional to Vg| D
    D --> E[Gate Drive]
    E --> F[Power Stage]
    F --> G[LC Filter]
    G --> O
    F --> S
    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333
    style S fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#fde68a,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#fecaca,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:** A **second path** now exists — the input voltage $V_g$ dynamically resizes the sawtooth amplitude. This open-loop correction acts **instantaneously** (much faster than the output feedback loop), giving VMC+FF its excellent line-transient response.

---

### 4.3 Detailed Functional Architecture — VMC Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Sensing
        S1[Output Vo] --> S2[Resistor Divider]
        S2 --> S3[Feedback Voltage Vfb]
    end
    subgraph Error_Processing
        S3 --> EP1[Inverting Input of EA]
        EP2[Reference Vref] --> EP1
        EP1 --> EP3[Op-Amp Error Amplifier]
        EP3 --> EP4[Compensation Network Zc s]
        EP4 --> EP5[Control Voltage vc]
    end
    subgraph PWM_Modulation
        EP5 --> PM1[Non-Inverting Input of Comparator]
        PM2[Fixed Sawtooth Vst] --> PM3[Inverting Input of Comparator]
        PM1 --> PM4[PWM Comparator]
        PM3 --> PM4
        PM4 --> PM5[Duty Cycle D]
    end
    subgraph Power_Stage
        PM5 --> PS1[Gate Driver]
        PS1 --> PS2[Power MOSFET Switch]
        PS3[Input Vg] --> PS2
        PS2 --> PS4[LC Filter]
        PS4 --> S1
    end
    style S1 fill:#d1fae5
    style PS3 fill:#bfdbfe
    style PM4 fill:#fde68a
    style PS2 fill:#fecaca
```

---

### 4.4 Bode Plot of $G_{vd}(s)$ — Buck Converter VMC

> **Mermaid cannot directly render Bode plots.** Below is a structured description that students should be able to reproduce on graph paper for the KTU exam.

| Frequency Range | Magnitude Slope | Phase |
|-----------------|----------------|-------|
| DC to $f_0$ | 0 dB/dec, level at $20\log(V_g)$ | 0° |
| $f_0$ | Slope bends to **−40 dB/dec** | Drops to **−180°** |
| $f_z$ (ESR zero) | Slope recovers to **−20 dB/dec** | Rises by +90° |
| $f_s/2$ | Switches to sampled-data behavior | Discontinuous |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define Voltage Mode Control in a DC-DC converter. Mention its key limitation.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Voltage Mode Control (VMC) is a PWM technique in which the **output voltage is the only controlled variable**. The output voltage is sensed, compared with a reference to produce an error, and the error is compensated and compared with a fixed-frequency sawtooth to generate the duty cycle.

**Key limitation:** It has **poor line regulation** (high audio susceptibility) because input voltage disturbances are corrected only after they propagate through the output LC filter, causing slow response. The LC filter also introduces a **double pole** that complicates loop compensation.

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition, 1 mark working, 1 mark limitation)*

---

#### **Q2. What is the function of the sawtooth waveform in voltage mode PWM control?** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The sawtooth (or ramp) waveform serves as the **carrier signal** in the PWM modulator. The control voltage $v_c$ (output of the compensator) is compared with the sawtooth peak $V_{st,pk}$; the instant of intersection determines the **switch turn-off time**, and hence the duty cycle $D = v_c / V_{st,pk}$. The frequency of the sawtooth sets the converter's switching frequency $f_s$. In VMC the sawtooth amplitude is **fixed**; in VMC with feedforward it is made **proportional to the input voltage** for line-transient improvement.

*(Distribution: 1 mark carrier, 1 mark duty cycle equation, 1 mark feedforward extension)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 4 Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of **single-loop Voltage Mode Control** for a buck converter. Derive the expression for the **modulator gain $F_m$**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **loop gain $T(s)$** of the VMC system. Explain how the **LC output filter** affects the loop gain characteristics and why it complicates compensation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Operation of Single-Loop VMC (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 figure.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. The output voltage $V_o$ is sensed by a resistor divider with gain $H = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$, giving $V_{fb} = H \cdot V_o$.
2. $V_{fb}$ is compared with reference $V_{ref}$ in an op-amp error amplifier; error $v_{err} = V_{ref} - V_{fb}$.
3. The error is shaped by the compensation network $G_c(s)$ to give the control voltage $v_c$.
4. $v_c$ is fed to a PWM comparator, where it is compared with a sawtooth $v_{st}$ of fixed peak $V_{st,pk}$ and frequency $f_s$.
5. The comparator output is high when $v_c > v_{st}$, driving the MOSFET ON. The duty cycle becomes $D = v_c / V_{st,pk}$.
6. The MOSFET switching action, together with the LC filter, regulates $V_o$.

**Derivation of Modulator Gain $F_m$ (4 marks):**

For a trailing-edge sawtooth $v_{st}(t) = (V_{st,pk}/T_s) t$:

The switch turn-off instant is found by setting $v_{st}(t_1) = v_c$:
$$
t_1 = \frac{v_c \, T_s}{V_{st,pk}}
$$

Duty cycle is ON-time over period:
$$
D = \frac{t_1}{T_s} = \frac{v_c}{V_{st,pk}}
$$

Perturbing $v_c = V_c + \hat{v}_c$ and $D = D_0 + \hat{d}$:
$$
\hat{d} = \frac{\hat{v}_c}{V_{st,pk}}
$$

Therefore, the modulator gain is:
$$
\boxed{\,F_m = \frac{\hat{d}}{\hat{v}_c} = \frac{1}{V_{st,pk}}\,\text{V}^{-1}}
$$

---

**(b) Loop Gain and LC Filter Effects (7 marks)**

**Derivation of Loop Gain (4 marks):**

The forward path from error to output consists of compensator, modulator, and power stage:
$$
\hat{v}_o(s) = G_c(s) \cdot F_m \cdot G_{vd}(s) \cdot \hat{v}_{err}(s)
$$

The feedback equation gives:
$$
\hat{v}_{err} = \hat{v}_{ref} - H \cdot \hat{v}_o
$$

Substituting and solving for $\hat{v}_o / \hat{v}_{ref}$:
$$
\frac{\hat{v}_o(s)}{\hat{v}_{ref}(s)} = \frac{G_c(s) F_m G_{vd}(s)}{1 + G_c(s) F_m G_{vd}(s) H}
$$

Defining the **loop gain**:
$$
\boxed{\,T(s) = G_c(s) \cdot F_m \cdot G_{vd}(s) \cdot H\,}
$$

For a buck converter:
$$
G_{vd}(s) = V_g \cdot \frac{1 + sR_{ESR}C}{1 + s\frac{L}{R} + s^2 LC}
$$

**Effect of LC Filter on Loop Gain (3 marks):**

- The denominator $1 + s(L/R) + s^2 LC$ yields a **double pole** at $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$.
- This double pole causes the **magnitude slope to change from 0 dB/dec to −40 dB/dec** and the **phase to drop by 180°** in a narrow frequency band.
- Consequently, the loop gain crosses 0 dB at a very steep slope, leaving **insufficient phase margin** (often negative).
- A **Type-III compensator** with two zeros and three poles is required to cancel the LC double pole, providing enough phase boost.
- This complex compensation is the **primary drawback** of single-loop VMC, and the chief reason current-mode control became popular.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain **Voltage Mode Control with Input Voltage Feedforward**. Derive the relationship between the duty cycle and the input voltage in this scheme. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that with **feedforward**, the audio susceptibility of a buck converter is theoretically zero. Discuss any practical limitations of this technique. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) VMC with Input Voltage Feedforward (7 marks)**

**Concept (3 marks):**

In VMC with feedforward, the sawtooth carrier amplitude is **made proportional to the input voltage** $V_g$ rather than being held constant. This is implemented by routing the input voltage through a resistor to charge the sawtooth capacitor, so that $V_{st,pk} = k V_g$ where $k$ is a constant set by the input resistor and the ramp capacitor.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 figure. Note the additional path from $V_g$ to the sawtooth generator.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The standard duty-cycle relationship is:
$$
D = \frac{v_c}{V_{st,pk}}
$$

Substituting $V_{st,pk} = k V_g$:
$$
\boxed{\,D = \frac{v_c}{k V_g}\,}
$$

For a buck converter, the output voltage is:
$$
V_o = D \cdot V_g = \frac{v_c}{k V_g} \cdot V_g = \frac{v_c}{k}
$$

The input voltage $V_g$ **cancels out algebraically**. Hence, the output voltage becomes a function of only the control voltage $v_c$ and the scaling constant $k$.

---

**(b) Zero Audio Susceptibility — Proof and Practical Limits (7 marks)**

**Theoretical Proof (4 marks):**

The audio susceptibility is defined as:
$$
G_{vg}(s) = \frac{\hat{v}_o(s)}{\hat{v}_g(s)}\bigg\vert_{\hat{d}=0}
$$

From $V_o = v_c / k$, perturbing:
$$
\hat{v}_o = \frac{1}{k} \hat{v}_c
$$

Since $v_c$ (the compensator output) is **independent of $V_g$** under ideal assumptions, and the modulator now automatically adjusts $D$ to maintain $V_o$ when $V_g$ changes, we get:
$$
\hat{v}_o(s) = 0 \quad \text{when} \quad \hat{v}_g \neq 0
$$

Therefore:
$$
\boxed{\,G_{vg,ff}(s) = 0\,}
$$

**Practical Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Finite bandwidth of the feedforward path** — the resistor-capacitor network used to make $V_{st,pk} \propto V_g$ has its own time constant, so high-frequency $V_g$ disturbances are only partially rejected.
2. **Modulator delay** — practical comparators have propagation delay; the corrective action is delayed by one switching cycle.
3. **Component tolerances** — the proportionality constant $k$ drifts with temperature, causing imperfect cancellation.
4. **Duty cycle saturation** — the feedforward action can push $D$ beyond 0–1 limits for very large $V_g$ transients, causing the loop to lose regulation.
5. Despite these, line-transient response improves by **10–20×** compared to basic VMC — a major reason feedforward is used in telecom and aerospace SMPS.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in VMC questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $G_{vd}$ and $G_{vg}$** — Marks deducted if interchange occurs. Memorize: $G_{vd}$ = control-to-output (loop), $G_{vg}$ = line-to-output (susceptibility).
> 2. **Forgetting the feedback gain $H$** in $T(s)$ — full marks require $T(s) = G_c F_m G_{vd} H$, not $G_c F_m G_{vd}$.
> 3. **Skipping the sawtooth equation in $F_m$ derivation** — Examiners expect the full 3-step derivation: $v_{st}(t) \to t_1 \to D \to F_m$.
> 4. **Stating audio susceptibility is "exactly zero"** in practical feedforward — Examiners accept "ideally zero" with the practical caveats noted above.
> 5. **Not labeling the block diagram properly** — every block ($G_c$, $F_m$, $G_{vd}$, $H$) must be marked or marks are cut.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Voltage Mode Control**

- ⚡ **VMC = single feedback loop on output voltage**; duty cycle is the only control handle.
- ⚡ **Modulator gain $F_m = 1/V_{st,pk}$** — constant, frequency-independent, units V⁻¹.
- ⚡ **Sawtooth comparator** generates $D = v_c / V_{st,pk}$.
- ⚡ **LC output filter** introduces a **double pole** at $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ — the major compensation challenge.
- ⚡ **Single-loop VMC** has **poor line regulation** (high $G_{vg}$) and **slow line transient response**.
- ⚡ **Feedforward VMC** makes $V_{st,pk} \propto V_g$ ⇒ $D = v_c/(kV_g)$ ⇒ $V_o = v_c/k$ ⇒ $G_{vg} = 0$ (ideal).
- ⚡ **Loop gain** $T(s) = G_c(s) F_m G_{vd}(s) H$ — bandwidth and phase margin derived from this.
- ⚡ **Buck converter $G_{vd}(s) = V_g(1+sR_{ESR}C) / (1 + sL/R + s^2LC)$**.
- ⚡ **Type-III compensator** typically required to compensate the LC double pole.
- ⚡ **VMC preferred for multi-output converters**; current-mode preferred for single-output high-performance.
- ⚡ **Audio susceptibility** = $G_{vg}$ — measure of line rejection; feedforward drives it to zero ideally.
- ⚡ **Practical feedforward limitations**: finite bandwidth, propagation delay, component tolerances, $D$ saturation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current Mode and Gated Oscillator Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT503 - PCBRT503
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Current Mode and Gated Oscillator Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Voltage Mode vs Current Mode: The Paradigm Shift

In **switch-mode power supplies (SMPS)**, the switch (MOSFET/IGBT) must be turned ON and OFF in such a way that the output voltage (or current) is regulated despite variations in input voltage and load. The strategy used to decide *when* to switch is called the **control method**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Current Mode Control (CMC)** is a *two-loop* control strategy in which the **peak (or average) inductor current** is used as an inner control variable, in addition to the conventional outer voltage loop. The PWM signal is generated by comparing the **sensed current waveform** with the **output of the voltage error amplifier** — instead of comparing a sawtooth ramp with the error voltage (as in voltage mode).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control Story

Imagine driving a car on a hilly road:
- **Voltage Mode Control** is like a driver who watches only the **speedometer** and decides accelerator position. Hills are felt *after* the speed has already changed — slow response.
- **Current Mode Control** is like a driver who watches **both the speedometer and the engine torque (current) gauge simultaneously**. By sensing the immediate "effort" of the engine, hills are anticipated and corrected within the same cycle — the system reacts in one switching period instead of several.

In electronics, the **inductor current** is the "torque" of the converter — it tells us how hard the converter is working **right now**, even before the output voltage is affected.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Current Mode Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Current Mode Control (CMC)** — A pulse-width modulation technique in which the **conduction period of the power switch is terminated when the rising inductor current reaches a threshold level set by the outer voltage-loop error amplifier.** The current is sensed either directly (via a sense resistor in the source) or indirectly (via a current transformer / DCCT). CMC inherently provides **input-voltage feed-forward, automatic cycle-by-cycle current limiting, and first-order closed-loop dynamics**.

The two principal sub-families are:
1. **Peak Current Mode Control (PCMC)** — switch turns OFF at the *peak* of the current ripple.
2. **Average Current Mode Control (ACMC)** — a current-error amplifier forces the *average* current to track the reference.

## 1.3 Formal Definition of Gated Oscillator Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gated Oscillator Control (GOC)** — A control architecture in which a **voltage-controlled relaxation oscillator** is *enabled* (gated) by a control or enable signal, and the **oscillator output frequency itself encodes the duty cycle** of the power switch. Equivalently, in another implementation, a fixed-frequency clock is **AND-gated** with a comparator output, so the *gating window* (and hence the ON-time) varies with the control voltage.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Metronome with a Switch

Picture a **metronome** that ticks at a rate proportional to how loudly you whisper a number:
- **Whisper softly (small Vc)** → metronome ticks slowly → switch is ON for a long time → high duty cycle.
- **Whisper loudly (large Vc)** → metronome ticks fast → switch stays ON for shorter windows → low duty cycle.

The *gate* is the push-button you press only when the metronome is "allowed" to drive the converter — protecting the system during start-up, fault, or soft-start conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Gated-oscillator schemes are widely used in **integrated SMPS controllers** (e.g., UC3842-family derivatives), **resonant converter drivers**, **555-timer-based regulators**, and **low-power flyback ICs** because the entire PWM/clock generator fits in a tiny silicon area.

## 1.4 Why These Two Topics Are Co-Taught in KTU Module 4

Both techniques serve the same ultimate purpose — **generating the gate pulse for the power switch** — but represent *different philosophies*:
- **CMC** is a *closed-loop, analog continuous* approach (industry workhorse for >100 W converters).
- **GOC** is an *open-loop, event-driven* approach (preferred in ICs, resonant drivers, and light loads).

Understanding both is essential because **modern digital controllers (DSP/MCU-based) often implement CMC algorithms in firmware**, and **gated-oscillator concepts are still used in IC housekeeping blocks** (e.g., housekeeping PWM in PFC ICs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Peak Current Mode Control (PCMC) — Block Architecture

The standard PCMC buck-converter block diagram comprises:

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Voltage Error Amplifier (EA_v)** | Compares V_out with V_ref; produces error voltage V_c |
| **Current Sense Resistor R_s** | Converts inductor current i_L into a voltage v_s = i_L × R_s |
| **PWM Comparator** | Compares v_s with V_c; output is HIGH until v_s reaches V_c |
| **S-R Latch (Set on clock, Reset on comparator)** | Latches the OFF command until the next clock pulse |
| **Slope-Compensation Ramp** | Added to sensed current to suppress sub-harmonic oscillation for D > 0.5 |
| **Clock Generator** | Sets switching frequency f_s = 1/T_s |

### 2.1.1 Operating Cycle (One Switching Period)

1. **Clock edge** arrives → S-R latch is SET → gate drive of MOSFET = HIGH.
2. Inductor current **rises linearly** with slope `m_1 = (V_in − V_out) / L`.
3. The sensed voltage `i_L · R_s` + compensation ramp `m_c · t` increases linearly.
4. The instant `i_L · R_s + m_c · t = V_c` is reached → PWM comparator output goes HIGH → latch is RESET → MOSFET turns OFF.
5. Inductor current **falls linearly** with slope `m_2 = V_out / L` (freewheeling diode conducts).
6. Next clock edge → cycle repeats.

### 2.1.2 Why CMC is Inherently First-Order

In **Voltage Mode Control**, the small-signal control-to-output transfer function of a buck converter is:
$$
\frac{\hat v_o(s)}{\hat d(s)} = \frac{V_{in}}{1 + s \, R \, C}
$$
The pole location is set by the **load resistance R and the output capacitor C** — load-dependent, harder to compensate.

In **Current Mode Control**, once the current loop is closed (assuming the inner loop is much faster than the outer), the current source forces the inductor to behave as a *transconductance amplifier* with gain `1/R_s`. The **effective small-signal control-to-output transfer function** becomes:
$$
\frac{\hat v_o(s)}{\hat v_c(s)} = \frac{R / R_s}{1 + s \, R \, C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Difference:** Both VM and CM buck converters have a *single pole*, but in CMC the **zero is also eliminated**, leaving a clean first-order low-pass response. This makes **loop compensation dramatically simpler** — only a single integrator is needed in the EA, versus a Type-III compensator for VM.

## 2.2 Sub-Harmonic Oscillation & Slope Compensation

### 2.2.1 The Instability Mechanism

For **D > 0.5** in PCMC, a small perturbation `ΔI` in the inductor current at the start of a cycle does **not decay**; it grows cycle-by-cycle at the switching frequency/2. This is called **sub-harmonic oscillation** and is catastrophic (large current ripple, audible noise, possible magnetic saturation).

**Why does this happen?**
- During ON-time, the current perturbation grows as `ΔI · (m_2/m_1)` (the duty cycle shortens by `ΔI/m_1`).
- During OFF-time, the perturbation decays exponentially.
- The **net ratio of perturbation between two consecutive cycles** is:
$$
q = -\frac{m_2}{m_1} \cdot \frac{D}{1-D}
$$
For stability: `|q| < 1`, which gives `D < 0.5`.

### 2.2.2 The Slope-Compensation Cure

A **compensation ramp of slope m_c** is added to the sensed current signal. The effective rising slope becomes `m_1 + m_c` and the effective falling slope becomes `m_2 − m_c`. The new perturbation ratio is:
$$
q' = -\frac{m_2 - m_c}{m_1 + m_c} \cdot \frac{D}{1-D}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For `|q'| < 1` for **all** D from 0 to 1, the required slope is:
> $$m_c \;\geq\; \frac{m_2}{2} \cdot \frac{2D-1}{1-D} \quad \text{(for D > 0.5)}$$
> A common engineering rule (Erickson) is to over-design: **choose `m_c = m_2`** (i.e., compensation slope equal to the inductor falling slope) — this guarantees stability for **all** D, with no penalty at D < 0.5.

For a **buck converter** specifically, `m_2 = V_out / L` and `m_1 = (V_in − V_out) / L`, so:
$$
m_c \geq \frac{V_{in}}{L} \left(2D - 1\right) \quad \text{at operating point}
$$

## 2.3 Average Current Mode Control (ACMC)

In ACMC, a **current-error amplifier (CA)** replaces the simple comparator. The CA integrates the difference between the *reference* (from EA_v) and the *sensed average* current. Because the CA provides high gain at low frequency, **ACMC does not require slope compensation** and is preferred for:
- Power Factor Correction (PFC) boost pre-regulators
- Battery chargers
- Applications needing tight average-current accuracy

The small-signal current-loop gain has the form:
$$
T_i(s) = \frac{R_s}{R_{i}} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + s/\omega_z} \cdot \frac{\omega_p}{s}
$$
where `ω_z` is the ESR zero and `ω_p` is the current-loop crossover — designed to be at `f_s/5` or so.

## 2.4 Gated Oscillator Control — Theory of Operation

### 2.4.1 Architecture 1 — Variable-Frequency Gated Oscillator (VCO-type)

**Components:**
- **Charging current source I = k · V_c** (transconductance stage, where V_c is the control voltage from EA)
- **Timing capacitor C_T**
- **Schmitt-trigger comparator** with thresholds V_T+ and V_T−
- **AND gate** (the "gate") that passes oscillator output to switch driver **only when** an enable signal EN is high

**Operating Principle:**
- When EN = 1: capacitor C_T charges at rate `dV/dt = I/C_T = k·V_c/C_T` and discharges through a fixed path, producing a sawtooth.
- The **instantaneous frequency** is:
$$
f_{osc} = \frac{k \, V_c}{2 \, C_T \, (V_{T+} - V_{T-})}
$$
- The output of the AND gate is a pulse train at `f_osc`, driving the MOSFET.
- When EN = 0: oscillator is inhibited; switch is held OFF (used for short-circuit protection, soft-start, burst-mode).

### 2.4.2 Architecture 2 — Fixed-Frequency Gated-Oscillator PWM

**Components:**
- **Fixed-frequency clock** of period T_s
- **Reference ramp** (sawtooth) from 0 to V_pk in time T_s
- **Comparator** (error voltage V_c vs ramp)
- **AND gate** between comparator and clock
- **Flip-flop** with clock-set and comparator-reset

**Operating Principle:**
- The clock sets the flip-flop → switch ON at start of period.
- The comparator resets the flip-flop when ramp crosses V_c → switch OFF.
- The "gate" here is the *window* between clock and comparator, naturally limiting the ON-time to `D·T_s = T_s · V_c/V_pk`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Most "current-mode" IC controllers (UC3842, UCC28C42, NCP1337) use a **fixed-frequency clock-gated peak-current comparator** internally — this is *exactly* Architecture 2 of GOC combined with PCMC. The two techniques **overlap in real silicon**, which is why Module 4 covers them together.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations appear verbatim (or with simple substitutions) in KTU 2024 ESE and internal examinations.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inductor rising slope (Buck, CCM) | `m_1 = (V_in − V_out) / L` | A/s |
| 2 | Inductor falling slope (Buck, CCM) | `m_2 = V_out / L` | A/s |
| 3 | Boost CCM rising slope | `m_1 = V_in / L` | A/s |
| 4 | Boost CCM falling slope | `m_2 = (V_out − V_in) / L` | A/s |
| 5 | Perturbation ratio (PCMC) | `q = −(m_2/m_1)·(D/(1−D))` | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Stability condition (no comp.) | `D < 0.5` | — |
| 7 | Required compensation slope (general) | `m_c ≥ (m_2/2)·(2D−1)/(1−D)` | A/s, valid for D > 0.5 |
| 8 | Conservative compensation rule | `m_c = m_2` | Guarantees stability for all D |
| 9 | Buck comp. slope at operating point | `m_c ≥ (V_in/L)·(2D−1)` | A/s |
| 10 | CMC control-to-output (buck) | `G_vc(s) = (R/R_s)/(1 + sRC)` | First-order, V/V |
| 11 | CMC audio susceptibility (buck) | `G_vg(s) = D/(1 + sRC)` | V/V |
| 12 | CMC loop gain (buck) | `T(s) = (R/R_s)·H(s)/(1 + sRC)` | Dimensionless |
| 13 | VCO frequency (gated oscillator) | `f_osc = k·V_c / [2·C_T·(V_T+ − V_T−)]` | Hz |
| 14 | VCO sensitivity (gain) | `K_VCO = df/dV_c` | Hz/V |
| 15 | Output ripple (peak CMC) | `ΔI_L = V_out·(1−D)/(f_s·L)` | A |
| 16 | Sense resistor power | `P_Rs = I_{L,avg}² · R_s` | W |
| 17 | Duty cycle (buck) | `D = V_out/V_in` | Dimensionless |
| 18 | Duty cycle (boost) | `D = 1 − V_in/V_out` | Dimensionless |
| 19 | Slope compensation magnitude (peak) | `V_pp,c ≥ V_out·(2D−1)/(2(1−D))` | V (sense-signal) |
| 20 | ACMC current-loop crossover target | `f_c,i = f_s/5 to f_s/10` | Hz |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCMC** is the de-facto control for **telecom rectifiers, server SMPS, EV onboard chargers** — wherever fast transient response and cycle-by-cycle current limiting are mandatory.
- **Slope compensation** is *the most-tested* concept in CMC — KTU 2024 has previously asked for its derivation in both ESE (14-mark) and Module tests.
- **Gated oscillators** are inside every **PFC controller IC, every 555 timer, every capacitive-isolation gate driver, and many Class-D amplifier PWM modulators** — so this is not "minor content."
- The **first-order CMC transfer function** makes digital implementation (in DSP/MCU) trivially simple — just one integrator compensator.
